paragraphs: Simple harmonic motion is motion in which the acceleration always points back toward equilibrium and grows with displacement. Move the mass to one side and the restoring acceleration points toward the center, trying to pull it back.
The mass does not stop at equilibrium because its speed is greatest there. It passes through the center, overshoots, and the same restoring rule slows it down, reverses it, and repeats the cycle.

The stage and graphs show the same instant. At a turning point, displacement is largest, velocity is zero, and the restoring acceleration has its largest magnitude. At an equilibrium crossing, displacement and acceleration are zero while speed is largest.

Use the controls one idea at a time. Amplitude sets the turning points and the energy scale, Angular frequency changes how quickly the cycle repeats, and Phase changes only the starting point in the cycle.

keyIdeas: At a turning point, displacement is maximum, velocity is zero, and the restoring acceleration has maximum magnitude.
At an equilibrium crossing, displacement is zero, acceleration is zero, and speed is maximum.
Amplitude sets the size of the motion, Angular frequency sets the timing, and Phase sets the starting point in the cycle.

commonMisconception.myth: If the mass is moving to the right, the acceleration must point to the right too.
correction: In simple harmonic motion, acceleration is set by displacement, not by the current direction of motion.
The mass can still be moving away from equilibrium while the acceleration already points back toward the center and slows it down.

workedExamples.title: Worked example from the live state
workedExamples.intro: Use the live oscillator as evidence. First read displacement from the same amplitude, angular frequency, phase, and time used by the stage. Then use the current angular frequency to find the period and connect it to the spacing of peaks on the graph.

items.title: Displacement at this instant
items.prompt: At $t = {{timeValue}}\,\mathrm{s}$, what is the oscillator's displacement?

steps.label: 1. Write the displacement relation
2. Substitute the live values
3. Evaluate the current displacement

applyAction.label: Try turning-point start
applyAction.observationHint: This reference case makes it easier to connect the block position, the equilibrium line, and the displacement graph.

items.title: Period of the current oscillation
items.prompt: For the current angular frequency, how long does one full oscillation take?

steps.label: 1. Write the period relation
2. Substitute the current angular frequency
3. Calculate the cycle time

applyAction.label: Try fast cycle
applyAction.observationHint: The peaks bunch closer together because a larger angular frequency shortens the period without moving the turning points.

miniChallenge.prompt: If Amplitude stays fixed but Angular frequency doubles, what changes first on the stage and displacement graph?
miniChallenge.prediction: Predict whether the turning points move, whether the center crossings happen sooner, or both.
miniChallenge.answer: The center crossings and graph peaks happen sooner, but the turning points stay at the same displacement.
miniChallenge.explanation: Angular frequency changes timing, not the size of the motion. The period shrinks and the graph compresses in time, while Amplitude still fixes the turning points.

quickTest.title: Quick check
quickTest.intro: Use the stage, vectors, and graphs together. These questions test whether you can explain what the oscillator is doing at the same instant.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one mass moving back and forth along a line through an equilibrium point. Optional overlays can show the equilibrium line, a short motion trail, the velocity vector, and the restoring acceleration vector.
Changing Amplitude, Angular frequency, or Phase updates the same motion, readouts, and graphs together. The stage and graphs are synchronized views of one oscillator at the same live time.
The graphs show displacement, velocity, acceleration, and energy as different views of the same oscillation over time.
Together they let you compare turning points, equilibrium crossings, timing changes, and the energy swap within one live cycle.

equations.meaning: The rate of change of displacement. Its magnitude is largest at equilibrium and zero at the turning points.
notes: Velocity is a quarter cycle out of step with displacement.

equations.meaning: The restoring acceleration is proportional to displacement and always points toward equilibrium.
notes: Acceleration is zero at equilibrium and largest in magnitude at the turning points.

equations.meaning: In the idealized model, total energy stays constant while kinetic and potential energy trade back and forth.

variableLinks.description: Sets the turning points of the motion, so it changes the maximum displacement and the energy scale.
Sets how quickly the oscillator repeats its cycle, so the graphs compress or stretch in time.
Shifts the starting point of the cycle without changing the amplitude or the period.

controls.description: Controls how far the oscillator moves from equilibrium.
Controls how quickly one full cycle repeats.
Shifts where the cycle starts without changing its size or period.

presets.label: Turning-point start
presets.description: Starts at maximum positive displacement with zero speed.
Keeps the swing modest but shortens the period noticeably.
Keeps the same oscillation size and repeat rate but starts from a different point in the cycle.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the zero-displacement line.
whatToNotice: Crossing this line means displacement is zero and the oscillator is moving through the center.
overlays.whyItMatters: It anchors the sign of displacement and helps you compare equilibrium crossings with turning points.

overlays.shortDescription: Leaves a short history of recent positions.
whatToNotice: Trail points bunch near the turning points because the mass is moving slowest there.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the changing speed visible on the stage, not just on the graph.

whatToNotice: The vector is longest near equilibrium and flips direction at the turning points.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links the live motion to the velocity graph and to the slope of the displacement curve.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the restoring acceleration toward equilibrium.
whatToNotice: The arrow always points opposite the displacement and becomes zero at equilibrium.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the restoring-rule idea visible on the stage.

graphs.description: Peaks mark turning points, while zero crossings mark passes through equilibrium.
Velocity is zero at the turning points and largest in magnitude at equilibrium crossings.
Acceleration is opposite in sign to displacement and largest in magnitude at the turning points.
Kinetic energy is largest at equilibrium, potential energy is largest at the turning points, and the total stays constant in the ideal model.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice in the oscillation
noticePrompts.intro: Use one live cue at a time while you change the oscillator. The clearest thing to watch depends on whether you are comparing turning points, equilibrium crossings, or timing changes.

items.text: The mass is fastest as it passes equilibrium and slowest at the turning points.
items.tryThis: Watch the block cross the center, then compare that moment with the peak of the velocity graph.
items.whyItMatters: It separates position from speed. Maximum displacement and maximum speed do not happen together.

items.text: The velocity graph peaks when displacement crosses equilibrium, not at the turning points.
items.tryThis: Scrub the velocity graph and compare the marker with the block's position on the track.
items.whyItMatters: It makes the quarter-cycle offset between displacement and velocity visible.

items.text: Acceleration points back toward equilibrium even while the mass may still be moving away from it.
items.tryThis: Turn on the acceleration vector and pause just before a turning point. The mass can still be moving outward while the acceleration already points inward.
items.whyItMatters: That inward acceleration is what slows the mass, stops it, and reverses it.

items.text: Increasing Angular frequency squeezes the graphs in time without moving the turning points.
items.tryThis: Raise Angular frequency and compare the spacing between peaks before you touch Amplitude.
items.whyItMatters: Frequency changes timing, while Amplitude still sets the turning points.

items.text: When you pause and scrub, the stage, graph marker, and current readout all describe the same instant.
items.tryThis: Pause, step once, and compare the block position with the displacement, velocity, and acceleration markers.
items.whyItMatters: It turns the equations into one inspectable state instead of separate facts.

items.text: The setup that reaches the same turning point sooner has the shorter period and the larger angular frequency.
items.tryThis: Keep Amplitude similar in A and B, then change only Angular frequency in one setup and compare when each trace reaches the next peak.
items.whyItMatters: Compare mode makes timing differences obvious without changing the spatial frame.

predictionMode.title: Predict the motion change
predictionMode.intro: Predict the motion before you move the controls, then test the change on the stage and graphs.

items.explanation: Angular frequency changes how quickly the cycle repeats. The graph compresses in time, but the turning points stay at the same displacement when Amplitude is fixed.
items.observationHint: The peaks and zero crossings happen sooner, but the maximum displacement does not move.

items.explanation: Phase changes where the cycle begins. It shifts the same pattern left or right in time without changing the amplitude or the period.
items.observationHint: The graphs keep the same height and spacing, but the motion starts from a different point in the cycle.

paragraphs: Oscillation energy is simple harmonic motion viewed through where the energy is stored. In the ideal model, the total energy stays constant, but it changes form as the motion continues.

When the mass is far from equilibrium, the spring is stretched or compressed and stores most of the energy as potential energy. When the mass passes through equilibrium, the spring is least stretched and the motion carries most of the energy as kinetic energy.

The stage, energy bars, and graphs all show the same instant. At a turning point, the mass stops, so kinetic energy is zero and the spring holds the full total energy. At an equilibrium crossing, speed is greatest, so kinetic energy is largest and spring potential energy is smallest.

Use the controls one idea at a time. Amplitude sets the turning points and the total-energy scale, Spring constant changes both the energy scale and the cycle rate, Mass changes the timing, and Phase changes only where the cycle begins.

keyIdeas: At the turning points, velocity is zero, kinetic energy is zero, and the spring holds all of the total energy as potential energy.
At equilibrium, displacement is zero, potential energy is smallest, and the oscillator carries almost all of its energy as kinetic energy.
In ideal SHM, kinetic and potential energy trade back and forth while the total-energy line stays flat.

Amplitude and spring constant set the total-energy scale. Phase changes only the starting point, and mass changes the timing rather than the total energy when amplitude and spring constant stay fixed.

commonMisconception.myth: If the mass stops for an instant at a turning point, the oscillator has no energy there.
correction: At the turning point, the mass has zero kinetic energy, not zero total energy.
At that instant, the spring stores the maximum potential energy and is ready to pull the mass back through equilibrium.

workedExamples.title: Worked example from the live oscillator
workedExamples.intro: Use the live oscillator as evidence. First read how the total energy is split at the current instant. Then calculate the total energy from the current amplitude and spring constant, and check that the graph keeps that total fixed while kinetic and potential energy trade places.

items.title: Energy split at this instant
items.prompt: At $t = {{timeValue}}\,\mathrm{s}$, how much of the oscillator's total energy is kinetic and how much is spring potential?

steps.label: 1. Write the kinetic- and potential-energy relations
2. Substitute the live values for the same instant
3. Compare the energy split

applyAction.label: Show equilibrium crossing
applyAction.observationHint: At equilibrium, the kinetic-energy bar peaks, the potential-energy bar nearly vanishes, and the velocity graph reaches an extreme.

items.title: Total energy from amplitude and spring constant
items.prompt: With amplitude ${{amplitudeValue}}\,\mathrm{m}$ and spring constant ${{springConstantValue}}\,\mathrm{N/m}$, what fixed total energy should this ideal oscillator have?

steps.label: 1. Write the total-energy relation
2. Substitute the current amplitude and spring constant
3. Calculate the stored total energy

applyAction.label: Show larger amplitude
applyAction.observationHint: The turning points move outward and the flat total-energy line rises because total energy scales with $A^2$.

miniChallenge.prediction: Decide whether changing the starting point changes the total energy, or only changes how the same total is divided between kinetic and potential energy.
miniChallenge.answer: They have the same total energy. The starting point changes only the split between kinetic and potential energy.
miniChallenge.explanation: Amplitude and spring constant set the total-energy scale in ideal SHM. Phase only changes where the cycle begins, so one oscillator can start with mostly kinetic energy while the other starts with purely spring potential energy even though the total is the same.

quickTest.intro: Use the motion and the energy meaning together. These questions ask what must be true about where the energy sits in the cycle.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a single oscillator moving back and forth across an equilibrium point. Optional markers show the turning points, and an energy card shows how the total energy is split between kinetic and spring potential energy at the current instant.
Changing Amplitude, Spring constant, Mass, or Phase updates the same motion, the same energy readout, and the same graph together, so the energy story stays tied to one physical state.

The energy graph shows kinetic, potential, and total energy as synchronized views of the same oscillation. The displacement and velocity graphs sit beside it so you can compare where the mass is, how fast it is moving, and which energy form is dominant.
Pausing or scrubbing keeps the stage, the energy bars, and the graph marker locked to the same instant.

equations.meaning: This gives where the mass is in the cycle. Turning points occur at $x = \pm A$, and equilibrium occurs at $x = 0$.
Mass and spring constant together set how quickly the energy exchange repeats.
Kinetic energy is carried by the moving mass, so it is largest when speed is largest and zero at the turning points.
Spring potential energy is stored in stretch or compression, so it is largest at the turning points and smallest at equilibrium.
In ideal SHM, the total stays constant while kinetic and potential energy trade places.

variableLinks.description: Sets the turning points and the total-energy scale. Larger amplitude widens the motion and raises the total energy through the $A^2$ dependence.
Sets how strongly the spring stores energy for a given displacement and how quickly the energy exchange repeats.
Changes how quickly the oscillator responds. With the same amplitude and spring constant, it changes the timing but not the total stored energy.
Changes where the cycle begins, so you can start at equilibrium, at a turning point, or anywhere between without changing the amplitude or period.

presets.label: Mixed start
presets.description: Starts between equilibrium and a turning point so both energy types are visible immediately.

presets.label: Equilibrium start
presets.description: Starts at equilibrium, where speed and kinetic energy are greatest.

presets.label: Turning-point start
presets.description: Starts at the edge of the motion, where the spring holds all of the energy.

presets.label: Larger amplitude
presets.description: Increases amplitude so the turning points move outward and the total energy rises strongly.

presets.label: Stiffer spring
presets.description: Keeps a similar amplitude but raises spring stiffness, so both the energy scale and the cycle rate increase.

whatToNotice: The kinetic-energy bar peaks as the mass crosses this line in the ideal model.
overlays.whyItMatters: It anchors the instant where the energy balance shifts most strongly toward kinetic energy.

whatToNotice: At these markers, the mass stops, kinetic energy falls to zero, and the spring holds the full total energy.
overlays.whyItMatters: Turning points are the clearest places to see energy fully stored in the spring.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows how kinetic and potential energy share one fixed total.
whatToNotice: The two bars trade height while the total stays constant in the ideal model.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the energy exchange visible on the stage instead of only on the graph.

whatToNotice: A longer velocity arrow matches a taller kinetic-energy bar.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the link between speed and kinetic energy visible.

whatToNotice: The motion lingers near the turning points because the speed is smallest there.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you connect where the mass spends time to where the energy sits.

graphs.description: Kinetic and potential energy trade places while the total stays flat in the ideal model.
Peaks mark the turning points, while zero crossings mark equilibrium.
Speed is largest at equilibrium and drops to zero at the turning points.

noticePrompts.intro: Use one cue at a time. Each prompt connects where the mass is, how fast it is moving, and where the energy is stored.

items.text: The mass is fastest as it crosses equilibrium, so the kinetic-energy bar is tallest there.
items.tryThis: Apply Equilibrium start, then compare the center crossing with the peak of the velocity graph.
items.whyItMatters: It separates position from energy storage. The center is where motion carries the energy most strongly.

items.text: At the turning points, the mass pauses but the total-energy line stays flat.
items.tryThis: Freeze the motion near $+A$ or $-A$ and compare the turning-point marker with the potential-energy peak.
items.whyItMatters: It shows that the energy has moved into the spring rather than disappearing.

items.text: Increasing amplitude raises the total-energy line faster than it moves the turning points outward.
items.tryThis: Increase Amplitude and compare how much the total-energy line rises relative to the wider swing.
items.whyItMatters: Because $E \propto A^2$, amplitude changes the stored energy more strongly than a first glance suggests.

items.text: A stiffer spring raises the energy scale and shortens the period even when amplitude stays the same.
items.tryThis: Keep amplitude fixed, then increase Spring constant and compare the total-energy line with the spacing of the velocity peaks.
items.whyItMatters: The same control changes both how much energy is stored and how quickly that energy is exchanged.

items.text: When you pause and scrub, the stage, energy bars, and graph marker all describe the same instant.
items.whyItMatters: It makes the energy plot a readout of the live motion instead of a separate surface.

items.text: The setup with the higher flat total-energy line stores more energy, even if both masses cross equilibrium at similar moments.
items.whyItMatters: Compare mode makes it obvious which changes alter the stored energy and which only shift the starting point.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict how the energy split or energy scale should change before you move the controls, then test it on the live oscillator.

items.explanation: Amplitude sets the turning-point distance directly, and the total energy follows $E = 	frac{1}{2}kA^2$, so doubling amplitude quadruples the total energy when $k$ stays fixed.
items.observationHint: The turning points spread out, but the flat total-energy line rises much more strongly.

items.explanation: At equilibrium the displacement is zero, so the spring stores almost no potential energy there. The same total energy is carried mainly as kinetic energy.
items.observationHint: The energy bars swing almost entirely to kinetic when the mass passes through equilibrium.

items.explanation: With $E = 	frac{1}{2}kA^2$, total energy does not depend on mass when amplitude and spring constant stay fixed. A larger mass changes the timing, not the stored total.
items.observationHint: The flat total-energy line stays in the same place while the motion slows down in time.

items.title: Half kinetic, half potential
items.prompt: Starting from Mixed start, pause at a moment when kinetic and potential energy are nearly equal. Keep the energy graph open so the balance stays honest.
items.successMessage: You found a state where the oscillator shares its total energy almost evenly between motion and spring stretch.

setup.note: Start from the mixed-energy case, then scrub until the two colored energy traces nearly match.

hints: This does not happen at equilibrium or at a turning point. Look between those extremes.
Use inspect mode so the stage, bars, and graph marker all stay on the same instant.

items.title: Reach about 5 J
items.prompt: From the Mixed start baseline, raise the stored energy to about $5\,\mathrm{J}$ without making the oscillator heavier than about $1.2\,\mathrm{kg}$.
items.successMessage: You increased the energy scale by changing the spring or the swing, not by hiding the effect inside a heavier mass.

setup.note: Use the energy graph as the target readout while you adjust amplitude or spring stiffness.

hints: Total energy grows with amplitude and with spring constant.
Mass changes timing, but it does not directly set the ideal total-energy scale.

paragraphs: Start with the Wavelength guide on the stage and the source and probe graph below it. The guide marks one crest-to-crest spacing in space, while the graph shows how the source point and one downstream probe repeat in time. These are two views of the same wave: the probe is showing the same oscillation later, after the disturbance has had time to travel through the medium.

Now change Wave speed while keeping Wavelength fixed. The crest pattern crosses the ruler faster, the source and probe traces complete each cycle in less time, and the Delay guide shrinks because the same distance is covered more quickly. Instead, keep Wave speed fixed and increase Wavelength. The crests spread farther apart, fewer cycles fit into each meter, and the source frequency falls.

The phase-map graph turns that timing idea into a distance picture. Every extra wavelength of downstream distance adds one full cycle of lag, so a probe one wavelength farther away returns to the same phase, just one period later. Moving the probe does not change the wave being launched. It only changes the delay and phase difference between the source and that location.

That is why v = f lambda is so useful: spacing, timing, and travel must all agree. The same relation later helps explain sound arrival delays, interference, and standing waves. Next, keep the probe fixed and compare a pure Wave speed change with a pure Wavelength change, then move the probe by one full wavelength and check that the phase repeats while the delay increases.

keyIdeas: Wavelength is spacing along the medium; frequency and period describe timing at one point.
At fixed Wavelength, increasing Wave speed makes the motion cycle faster. At fixed Wave speed, increasing Wavelength makes it cycle slower.
Moving the probe changes when that point responds and how far it lags in phase. It does not change the wave launched by the source.

commonMisconception.myth: If a crest moves faster through the medium, every point must automatically oscillate faster for that same reason.
correction: Wave speed describes motion through space: how quickly a crest travels. Frequency describes motion in time: how often one location repeats.
They rise together here only because Wavelength is held fixed. If frequency were fixed instead, the wavelength would have to change.

workedExamples.intro: These examples use the live values on the stage, so the algebra stays connected to the wave you can actually see.

items.title: Find frequency and period from v and lambda
items.prompt: For the current traveling wave with v = {{waveSpeedValue}}\,\mathrm{m/s} and lambda = {{wavelengthValue}}\,\mathrm{m}, what must the source frequency and period be?

steps.label: 2. Substitute the live wave values
3. Convert frequency into period

applyAction.label: Show shorter crest spacing
applyAction.observationHint: Shorter wavelengths pack more cycles into the same distance, so the phase map gets steeper.

items.title: Find the probe delay and phase lag
items.prompt: At the current probe position x_p = {{probeXValue}}\,\mathrm{m}, how much later does the wave reach the probe, and how many cycles behind the source is it?

steps.label: 2. Substitute the live probe position

applyAction.observationHint: A probe one wavelength away has the same phase as the source, but it occurs one period later.

miniChallenge.prompt: You must keep the probe fixed, and Wave speed cannot change. What should you change to make the source oscillate more slowly while the wave still reaches the probe?
miniChallenge.prediction: Choose between Amplitude, Wavelength, and Probe position before you test it.
miniChallenge.explanation: With Wave speed fixed, f = v / lambda. Making Wavelength larger means each cycle takes up more distance, so fewer cycles pass each second and the period becomes longer.

quickTest.intro: Use the live wave relation, not guesswork. These checks ask how spacing, timing, and downstream delay fit together.

questions.prompt: On the phase map, what does y = 1 cycle tell you about a downstream point?
questions.explanation: One cycle of lag means the point is one full wavelength downstream of the source. It reaches the same phase again, but later in time.

questions.prompt: Setup A keeps lambda the same but raises v. Setup B keeps v the same but shortens lambda. Which setup makes the time graph cycle faster while leaving the phase-map slope unchanged?

showMeAction.observationHint: In compare mode, one case changes only speed and the other changes only spacing.

whatToNotice: Changing lambda changes the crest spacing on the stage and the slope of the phase map at the same time.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps wavelength visible as a distance on the wave, not just a symbol in a formula.

whatToNotice: Moving the probe does not reshape the wave train. It only changes how long that point waits and how far it lags behind the source.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links downstream distance to both travel time and phase lag on the same live view.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows that one period of source motion sends a crest forward by one wavelength.
whatToNotice: The distance a crest travels in one period matches the wavelength spacing marked on the stage.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the link between time and distance visible instead of leaving it hidden in algebra.

graphs.description: Shows source motion and probe motion on the same time axis, so you can read travel delay and phase difference directly from the live wave.
Shows how cycle lag grows with downstream distance. Every extra wavelength adds one more cycle.

noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time and match it to something you can actually see on the stage or graphs.

items.text: Notice that the graph and the stage tell the same story. When the crest pattern moves faster or the spacing changes, the timing at the source and probe changes with it.
items.tryThis: Keep the probe fixed, then change wave speed or wavelength one at a time and compare the stage, time graph, and readout card.
items.whyItMatters: The graph is the same traveling wave measured at two positions, not a separate math display.

items.text: Notice that hovering the phase map moves the live probe to that downstream position. The graph is pointing to the same wave, not a separate preview.
Notice that shortening lambda packs more cycles into the same distance. The phase map gets steeper, and if v stays fixed the source frequency must increase.
items.tryThis: Lower lambda while keeping v fixed, then compare the crest spacing on the stage with the frequency readout.
items.whyItMatters: Wavelength is a spatial quantity, but through v = f lambda it also sets the timing.
Changing wave speed moves the pattern through the medium faster, which raises frequency when the spacing is held fixed.

items.text: Notice that the time rail changes the instantaneous wave shape and the source-probe displacements, but the phase map stays the same because it depends on position, not on which instant you paused.
items.whyItMatters: It separates a snapshot in time from the distance-based lag relation.

items.text: Notice which setup packs more cycles into the same distance and which setup cycles faster in time. Those are different questions.
items.whyItMatters: Compare mode helps you separate spacing changes from speed changes.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the effect before you test it. Each prompt changes one cause at a time so you can connect the stage, graph, and equation.

items.explanation: With lambda fixed, v = f lambda means a larger wave speed requires a larger frequency. Since T = 1 / f, the period must become shorter.
items.observationHint: Watch the crest pattern move faster while both time traces complete each cycle sooner.

items.explanation: A larger wavelength means fewer cycles fit into each meter, so x / lambda grows more slowly with position. With v fixed, f = v / lambda, so frequency decreases.
items.observationHint: Compare the wider crest spacing with the flatter phase map and slower time graph.

items.explanation: Adding one full wavelength adds one full cycle of lag. The probe therefore reaches the same phase again, but it does so one period later.
items.observationHint: On the phase map, move one wavelength to the right and watch the source and probe line up in phase again.

paragraphs: Watch one parcel in the tube. It moves left and right parallel to the wave direction, then returns near its starting position. The sound disturbance still moves to the right because what travels onward is a pattern of compressions and rarefactions, not a clump of matter being carried down the tube.

This bench keeps the piston, parcel row, pressure ribbon, and movable probe tied to the same wave. The displacement graph shows when the source parcel and probe parcel repeat their motion. The probe-pressure graph shows whether the local region is compressed or rarefied. Read both together, because parcel position alone does not tell you the full local sound state.

keyIdeas: In a longitudinal wave, parcels oscillate parallel to the direction the disturbance travels.
A compression is a crowded region of the medium, while a rarefaction is a more spread-out region.
The disturbance and its energy move onward through the medium even though each parcel mainly oscillates around its own equilibrium position.

commonMisconception.myth: If the sound wave moves to the right, the air itself must stream to the right with it.
correction: The wave pattern and its energy move to the right, but each parcel mainly oscillates back and forth around equilibrium.
Sound is passed onward by local pushes and pulls between neighboring parcels, not by one parcel traveling all the way down the tube.

workedExamples.intro: These checks use the live source, probe, and compression state already shown on the stage and graphs, so the calculation stays tied to the same sound wave you are inspecting.

items.title: Frequency and probe delay from v_wave and λ

steps.label: 1. Use the wave-speed and delay relations
2. Substitute the live values
3. Interpret the timing at the source and probe

applyAction.label: Show a farther downstream probe
applyAction.observationHint: The same compression pattern reaches the farther parcel later, even though each parcel still only oscillates locally.

items.title: Read compression at the probe
items.prompt: At the current probe position $x_p = {{probeXValue}}\,\mathrm{m}$ and time $t = {{timeValue}}\,\mathrm{s}$, what does the probe parcel tell you about the local sound state? Is the region compressed, rarefied, or near its resting spacing?

steps.label: 1. Link parcel displacement to local spacing
2. Read the live probe parcel
3. Interpret the local state

applyAction.observationHint: The probe parcel can be near its center position while the local compression cue is strongly positive.

miniChallenge.prompt: The medium stays the same, so the wave speed is fixed. What single change packs the compressions closer together while keeping the motion longitudinal?
miniChallenge.prediction: Choose between increasing amplitude, shortening wavelength, and moving the probe farther right.
miniChallenge.explanation: Compression spacing is set by wavelength. Shortening λ packs more compressions and rarefactions into the same distance without changing the back-and-forth nature of parcel motion.

quickTest.intro: Use the particle view and pressure view together. These checks are about what a longitudinal sound wave means physically.

questions.explanation: In a longitudinal sound wave, parcels oscillate around equilibrium while the disturbance and its energy are passed onward through local pushes and pulls.
Compression means neighboring parcels are crowded together. One parcel can be near equilibrium while the local compression cue is strongly positive.
At fixed wave speed, a shorter wavelength means more cycles per meter. That packs compressions closer together and raises the source frequency.
Adding one wavelength adds one whole cycle of lag. The travel time grows, but the same phase relation repeats after every full cycle.

equations.meaning: Describes parcel motion along the tube. Each parcel oscillates around equilibrium instead of traveling across the whole medium.
Links the spacing between compressions to how quickly the source must oscillate.
A parcel farther from the source responds later because the disturbance needs time to travel there.
Positive values mean local crowding, while negative values mean local spreading. This is about neighboring parcel spacing, not just one parcel's position.

variableLinks.description: Changes how far each parcel swings from equilibrium and how strong the compression and rarefaction cue becomes.
Changes how quickly the compression pattern and its energy reach downstream positions.
Changes the spacing from one compression to the next along the medium.
Moves the tracked parcel so you can compare local motion, local crowding, and travel delay at different positions.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows back-and-forth parcel motion together with the right-moving disturbance.
whatToNotice: The parcel arrows reverse left and right, but the disturbance arrow keeps pointing right.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates local particle motion from wave propagation.

overlays.shortDescription: Labels one local compression and one local rarefaction on the ribbon.
whatToNotice: The ribbon marks crowded and spread-out regions of the medium, not just where one parcel happens to be.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties sound to density changes in the medium instead of only to a sinusoidal graph.

whatToNotice: Energy moves with the right-moving disturbance even though no parcel travels all the way down the tube.
overlays.whyItMatters: It distinguishes energy transfer from bulk matter flow.

graphs.description: Compares source and probe parcel motion on the same time axis, so you can read the travel delay directly.
Compares one parcel's displacement with the local compression cue, so you can see that strongest compression does not necessarily happen when that parcel is farthest from equilibrium.

noticePrompts.intro: Use one cue at a time and connect it to something visible on the stage or graph.

items.text: Notice that the parcel arrow is left-right while the disturbance arrow remains rightward. The parcels oscillate locally, but the wave pattern propagates through the medium.
items.whyItMatters: This is the core physical idea of a longitudinal sound wave.

items.text: Notice that the compression cue is often strongest when the probe parcel is near its center position, not when it is farthest left or right.
items.whyItMatters: Compression depends on local spacing between neighboring parcels, not on one parcel's displacement alone.
Wavelength is a spacing in the medium, but it also sets the timing through the wave relation.

items.text: Notice that increasing the wave speed shortens the probe delay, but the parcel motion is still back-and-forth rather than one-way drift.

items.whyItMatters: Bigger parcel swings do not automatically mean faster propagation.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the consequence before you test it. Each prompt changes one cause at a time so you can connect parcel motion, compression spacing, and timing.

items.explanation: With λ fixed, $v_{wave} = f\lambda$ means a larger wave speed requires a larger frequency. At the same probe position, the larger speed also makes $\Delta t = x / v_{wave}$ smaller.
items.observationHint: Watch the timing graph cycle faster while the same probe position is reached sooner.

items.explanation: A shorter wavelength means more compressions fit into each meter. With wave speed fixed, the source must oscillate faster to produce that tighter spacing.
Moving the probe one wavelength farther adds one full cycle of lag. The arrival time increases, but the local phase relation repeats after a whole cycle.

paragraphs: Pitch and loudness are different sound ideas. Pitch comes from frequency: higher frequency means more cycles each second, a shorter period, and tighter compression spacing at fixed sound speed. Loudness comes from amplitude and this bench's intensity cue, so a sound can get louder without getting higher in pitch.
Use the bench one knob at a time. Raise frequency to hear and see a pitch change, raise amplitude to strengthen the loudness cue, and move the probe only to change delay at the listening point.

keyIdeas: Pitch is set by frequency and period, not by amplitude.
At fixed medium speed, higher frequency means a shorter wavelength and more closely packed compressions.
Loudness follows amplitude and the intensity cue, while moving the probe changes delay rather than the pitch or loudness produced by the source.

commonMisconception.myth: A taller sound wave must have a higher pitch because the graph looks bigger.
correction: Amplitude changes how strong the motion is, not how many cycles happen each second.
Frequency changes pitch. Amplitude changes the loudness / intensity cue.

workedExamples.title: Live pitch and loudness checks
workedExamples.intro: These checks use the same sound-bench relationships from the lesson, so the numbers stay tied to one coherent setup instead of a detached worksheet.

items.title: Wavelength and period from frequency
items.prompt: For a generated sound setup with $v_{wave} = {{waveSpeedValue}}\,\mathrm{m/s}$ and $f = {{frequencyValue}}\,\mathrm{Hz}$, what wavelength and period go with that pitch?

steps.label: 1. Use the pitch relations
2. Substitute the setup values
3. Connect the result to the sound you hear

applyAction.observationHint: The traces cycle faster and the compression spacing gets tighter while amplitude stays fixed.

items.title: Intensity cue from amplitude
items.prompt: For a generated sound setup with amplitude $A = {{amplitudeValue}}\,\mathrm{m}$, what intensity cue does this bench use, and what does that mean for loudness if frequency stays fixed?

steps.label: 1. Start from the loudness cue
2. Substitute the setup amplitude
3. Connect it to loudness without changing pitch

applyAction.observationHint: Amplitude and the intensity-response graph rise while the frequency readout stays the same.

miniChallenge.prompt: You want the same pitch but a louder sound. What single change should you make?
miniChallenge.prediction: Choose between raising amplitude, raising frequency, and moving the probe farther downstream.
miniChallenge.explanation: Keeping the pitch the same means keeping frequency fixed. Raising amplitude increases the loudness / intensity cue without changing pitch.

equations.meaning: Amplitude sets the size of the parcel motion, while frequency sets how quickly that motion repeats.
Higher frequency means more cycles each second, a shorter period, and a higher pitch.
At fixed medium speed, a higher frequency means a shorter wavelength and more closely packed compressions.
This bench uses amplitude squared as a loudness cue, so larger amplitude means a stronger loudness cue without changing pitch by itself.

variableLinks.description: Changes how strong the parcel motion and loudness cue are without changing pitch by itself.
Sets how many cycles happen each second, so it controls pitch and, at fixed medium speed, the spacing between compressions.
Moves the tracked parcel downstream so you can compare delay without changing the pitch or loudness produced by the source.

controls.description: Changes how strong the loudness / intensity cue is without changing pitch by itself.
Controls pitch while the medium speed stays fixed, so higher frequency also packs the compression pattern closer together.
Moves the tracked parcel without changing the sound produced by the source.

graphs.description: Shows how quickly the sound repeats at the source and probe, so pitch stays tied to frequency and period.
Separates one parcel's displacement from the local compression state of the medium.
Shows the amplitude-squared loudness cue so amplitude can be separated from pitch.

paragraphs: Beats happen when two sounds are almost, but not exactly, the same frequency. You do not hear the sources taking turns getting louder. You hear one rapid oscillation whose amplitude rises and falls because the two waves keep drifting between reinforcement and cancellation.

This bench shows the two source traces, their resultant, and a bounded loudness cue from that same superposition. The slow envelope rate is set by $|f_2-f_1|$, while the faster carrier follows the average of the two frequencies. That is why you can change the beat rate without changing amplitude, or keep the same beat rate while shifting both source frequencies upward or downward together.

keyIdeas: Beat frequency is set by $|f_2-f_1|$, not by amplitude.
The loud-soft pulse comes from changing phase alignment in the resultant, so neither source has to change its own amplitude.
The average frequency sets the fast carrier, while the frequency difference sets the slow envelope.

commonMisconception.myth: If you hear beats, each source must be turning its own loudness up and down.
correction: In this bench, each source keeps a steady amplitude the whole time.
The pulsing appears only in the resultant because the two waves drift between in-phase reinforcement and near-opposite cancellation.

workedExamples.intro: These checks use a selected source pair, source amplitude, and setup time from the same superposition model, so the algebra stays tied to the envelope and carrier cues.

items.title: Beat rate and carrier from the source pair

applyAction.label: Show slow beats
applyAction.observationHint: The rapid oscillation is still present, but the envelope graph makes the slower beat cycle easy to track.

items.title: Envelope amplitude at a setup time

applyAction.label: Inspect the envelope
applyAction.observationHint: Step through time and watch the source traces stay steady while the resultant moves between strong reinforcement and near-cancellation.

miniChallenge.explanation: Beat frequency is set by the difference $\Delta f$. When $f_1$ and $f_2$ become equal, $\Delta f$ goes to zero, so the separate envelope stops pulsing and the resultant becomes one steady-frequency oscillation.

graphs.description: Shows the two source traces and their sum so you can see the fast carrier inside the slower beat envelope.
Shows the slow envelope ratio and the bounded loudness cue, so beat rate can be separated from the faster carrier.

noticePrompts.intro: Use one cue at a time. The goal is to separate the fast carrier, the slow envelope, and the loudness cue on the same superposition bench.

items.text: Notice that the rapid oscillation never disappears. Beats are the slow growth and fade of the envelope wrapped around that faster carrier.
items.tryThis: Start from Near unison and watch the resultant trace before you look at the envelope graph.
items.whyItMatters: It prevents the loud-soft pulse from being mistaken for a separate low-frequency source.

items.text: Notice that matching the two source frequencies removes the separate beat envelope. The resultant can still oscillate strongly, but it no longer swells and fades.
items.whyItMatters: Beat frequency comes from the difference, so making that difference zero removes the beat cycle.

items.text: Notice that increasing $|f_2-f_1|$ speeds up the envelope directly. The carrier can stay in a similar range while the beat rate changes a lot.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps the pulse rate tied to the actual frequency difference.

predictionMode.intro: Predict which part of the motion changes: the fast carrier, the slow envelope, or both.

items.explanation: When $f_1=f_2$, the frequency difference is zero, so there is no separate beat envelope. The two sources reinforce as one steady-frequency resultant instead of pulsing in and out.
Beat frequency follows $|f_2-f_1|$, so widening the split speeds the envelope directly even when amplitude stays fixed.
Both cases keep the same $0.3\,\mathrm{Hz}$ difference, so the beat rate stays the same. Lowering both frequencies together lowers the average carrier without changing the envelope rate.

paragraphs: The Doppler effect does not mean the source changes how often it emits. In this model the emitted frequency stays fixed. What changes first is the spacing laid down in the medium: ahead of a moving source, wavefronts are packed closer together, while behind it they are spread farther apart.

Observer motion then changes how quickly those wavefronts are met on the chosen side. A listener moving toward the source encounters them more often, while a listener moving away encounters them less often. This bench keeps the circles, spacing guides, and heard-frequency trace tied to the same pass-by scene, so a higher heard pitch means a higher arrival rate, not a hidden change in the emitted tone.

keyIdeas: A moving source changes the spacing laid down in the medium: compressed ahead, stretched behind.
A moving observer changes how quickly wavefronts are encountered, but it does not change the spacing already present in the medium.
In this classical sound model, a higher heard pitch means wavefronts arrive more often, not that the source started emitting faster.

commonMisconception.myth: A higher heard pitch means the source must actually be emitting a higher frequency.
correction: The emitted frequency stays fixed here. What changes is the spacing in the medium and the rate at which the observer meets those wavefronts.
Source motion changes front and rear spacing. Observer motion changes arrival rate on the chosen side.

workedExamples.title: Live worked examples
workedExamples.intro: These checks use the same moving-source setup shown on the stage and graphs, so each formula stays tied to one honest pass-by situation.

items.title: Wavefront spacing ahead and behind
items.prompt: For this moving-source setup with $v_{wave} = {{waveSpeedValue}}\,\mathrm{m/s}$, $f_s = {{sourceFrequencyValue}}\,\mathrm{Hz}$, and $v_s = {{sourceSpeedValue}}\,\mathrm{m/s}$, what spacing is laid down ahead of the source and behind it?

items.title: Heard frequency on the selected side
items.prompt: On the {{sideLabelValue}} side of this setup, what observed frequency should the listener hear when $f_s = {{sourceFrequencyValue}}\,\mathrm{Hz}$ and observer speed is $v_o = {{observerSpeedValue}}\,\mathrm{m/s}$, with positive values meaning toward the source on that side?

miniChallenge.prompt: You want the same siren to sound lower without changing the emitted frequency. On which side of the moving source should you listen?
miniChallenge.explanation: Behind the source, the wavefront spacing is stretched rather than compressed. Larger spacing means fewer arrivals each second, so the heard pitch is lower in this classical model.

quickTest.intro: Use the moving-source setup, not memory alone. These checks separate what changes in the medium from what changes at the observer.

questions.explanation: Observer motion changes the arrival rate. It does not rewrite the wavefront spacing already launched into the medium.
Behind the moving source, each later crest is emitted from a more forward position than the one before it, so the rear spacing is larger and the heard pitch is lower.
Ahead of the source, the observer samples compressed spacing. Behind the source, the observer samples stretched spacing. The emitted frequency is the same, but the arrival rate is different.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one sound source moving through a horizontal medium and one observer listening either ahead of the source or behind it. Expanding wavefronts, spacing markers, motion arrows, and arrival labels all stay linked on the same bench.
Changing source speed changes the spacing laid down in the medium. Changing observer speed changes how fast the observer meets those wavefronts. Both effects update the same bench and graphs.
The first graph compares the emitted source signal with the observer signal over time.

The second graph shows how source speed changes front and rear spacing, and the third graph shows how observer speed changes heard frequency on the chosen side relative to the emitted-frequency baseline.

equations.meaning: This is the wavelength laid down in the medium when the source is stationary.
Ahead of the moving source, each new crest is emitted from a more forward position, so the spacing in the medium is smaller.
Behind the moving source, each new crest is emitted farther ahead than the previous one, so the spacing in the medium is larger.
The denominator comes from the spacing laid down by the moving source on the chosen side, while the numerator comes from how quickly the observer meets those wavefronts.

variableLinks.description: Sets how often the source emits wavefronts. In this model that emitted rate stays fixed even when motion changes the heard pitch.
Changes the spacing laid down in the medium: tighter ahead of the source and wider behind it.
Changes how quickly the observer meets the existing wavefronts on the chosen side without changing the spacing already in the medium.

controls.description: Sets how often the source emits new wavefronts.
Moves the source through the medium, which compresses spacing ahead and stretches spacing behind.
Positive values move the observer toward the source on the chosen side; negative values move away and change only the arrival rate.
Choose whether the observer listens on the compressed front side or the stretched rear side of the moving source.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks source and observer motion on the same pass-by scene.
whatToNotice: Source motion changes the spacing laid down in the medium, while observer motion changes how quickly those wavefronts are encountered.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps two different Doppler causes from being blended into one.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows one spacing ahead of the source and one behind it at the same moment.
whatToNotice: As soon as the source moves forward, spacing ahead becomes smaller and spacing behind becomes larger.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties the pitch shift to a visible change in the medium rather than to the source somehow emitting faster.

overlays.shortDescription: Labels the current arrival rate on the observer's chosen side.
whatToNotice: Changing observer speed changes the heard arrival rate even when the chosen-side spacing stays the same.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates spacing in the medium from the rate at which the observer encounters those wavefronts.

graphs.description: Compares the emitted signal with the received signal, so you can see that the source keeps one emitted frequency while the observer receives a different arrival rate.
Shows how source speed changes the spacing in the medium ahead of the source and behind it while the emitted frequency stays fixed.
Shows how observer motion changes heard frequency on the chosen side relative to the emitted-frequency baseline.

noticePrompts.intro: Change one cause at a time and link it to something visible on the pass-by bench.

items.text: Notice that faster source motion compresses the front markers and stretches the rear markers at the same time. The source is not emitting faster; it is moving between emissions.
items.whyItMatters: The Doppler shift begins with a spacing change laid down in the medium.

items.text: Notice that changing observer speed moves the heard-frequency trace even though the front and rear spacing markers do not move. The observer is changing the encounter rate, not the medium.
items.whyItMatters: It separates source-created spacing changes from observer-created arrival-rate changes.

items.text: Notice that switching from ahead to behind changes which spacing the observer samples. Ahead is compressed and sounds higher; behind is stretched and sounds lower.
items.whyItMatters: The pitch shift depends on which side of the moving source the observer listens from.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the pitch and spacing change before you test it. Each prompt changes one cause at a time so you can separate emitted frequency, spacing in the medium, and arrival rate.

items.explanation: A faster source does not change its emitted frequency here. It emits each crest from a more forward position, which compresses the front spacing and raises the arrival rate for an observer ahead.
Observer motion changes how quickly the observer meets wavefronts on the chosen side. It does not redraw the spacing already laid down by the source.
Behind the source, the observer samples stretched rear spacing, so fewer crests arrive each second and the heard pitch drops.

challengeMode.intro: Use the same pass-by bench to separate two ideas people often mix up: changes to spacing in the medium and changes to arrival rate at the observer.

paragraphs: Keep the Path-difference guide and Combined-amplitude guide visible, and fix the probe at one screen height. The probe graph traces Source A, Source B, and their combined wave at that one point over time, while the pattern graph shows the long-run brightness at every screen height. A bright band is not one frozen crest in time. It is a position where the combined wave stays large cycle after cycle.

The key quantity is the total phase difference: how far out of step the two arrivals are when they meet. The Path-difference guide shows where part of it comes from. When you move the probe, r1 and r2 change, so the extra distance Delta r changes too. If Delta r is 0, one wavelength, two wavelengths, and so on, equal sources reinforce. If Delta r is about half a wavelength, one and a half wavelengths, and so on, equal sources arrive nearly opposite in phase and the screen becomes dark.

The source controls let you test which part of that phase difference changed. Source phase offset can turn the center from bright to dark without changing the geometry at all, because it adds phase directly. Changing Wavelength also leaves the geometry alone, but it changes how much phase each meter of path difference represents, so the fringes squeeze together or spread apart on the pattern graph.

One last check matters before you trust a dark band: destructive phase does not guarantee a perfect zero unless the source amplitudes are matched. If Source A and Source B are unequal, the combined-amplitude guide—the outline of how large the added wave can get—does not fully close. The same idea later reappears in standing waves, noise cancellation, and optical interference. Next, make a dark band by moving Probe height, return to the center and darken it again with Source phase offset alone, then unbalance the amplitudes and watch the null fill back in.

keyIdeas: The pattern graph shows brightness across the whole screen, while the probe graph traces the wave at one chosen point over time.
A bright or dark band depends on total phase difference: whole-wavelength path differences reinforce, while half-wavelength differences cancel for equal sources.
A perfect dark fringe needs both destructive phase and equal source amplitudes.

correction: A dark fringe is produced by two waves arriving at the same point with nearly opposite phase.
The waves still overlap there. What disappears is the combined amplitude, not one of the waves.

workedExamples.intro: These examples use the live probe position and source settings, so each calculation stays tied to the bright or dark region you are actually inspecting.

items.title: Turn path difference into phase difference
items.prompt: For the current probe position at y = {{probeYValue}}\,\mathrm{m}, what total phase difference reaches this screen point?

steps.label: 2. Substitute the live path difference
3. Reduce it to an equivalent comparison angle

applyAction.observationHint: The path difference is close to half a wavelength, so the probe sits near a dark fringe on the screen.

items.title: Find the combined amplitude at the probe
items.prompt: For the current source amplitudes and phase difference, how large can the combined wave get at the probe point?

steps.label: 3. Interpret the combined amplitude

applyAction.observationHint: The phase split is still destructive, but unequal amplitudes leave leftover combined motion instead of a perfect null.

miniChallenge.prompt: Your probe is on a bright region, and you may not change Source A amplitude or Source B amplitude. What is one reliable way to make that same point dark?
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide whether changing Probe height, Wavelength, or Source phase offset can shift the point to destructive interference.
miniChallenge.explanation: A dark point needs the total phase difference to shift by about pi. You can do that by changing geometry, because Probe height changes path difference, or by adding source phase directly.

quickTest.intro: Use geometry, phase, and amplitude together. These questions ask what the screen point must do, not which formula you remember.

questions.explanation: Bright regions are places where the arrivals reinforce over repeated cycles. That happens when the total phase difference is near 0, 2π, 4π, and so on, not only when the path difference is exactly zero.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: Zero path difference is one way to get constructive interference, but any whole-number multiple of the wavelength can do it.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: The probe still oscillates in time. The bright pattern comes from a larger combined amplitude, not from the displacement being frozen.

questions.explanation: Interference requires both arrivals to reach the same point. A dark fringe is created by overlapping waves with nearly opposite phase, not by one wave missing the point.

Geometry sets the path difference, but wavelength determines how much phase that path difference represents. Shorter wavelength means more phase change per meter, so the fringes crowd closer together.

paragraphs: The simulation shows two coherent sources on the left, a shared screen on the right, and one adjustable probe point on that screen. Two wavy paths run from the sources to the probe so the user can compare the path lengths, the local phase split, and the resulting probe motion in the same picture.

The probe point also appears on a vertical screen strip that brightens or darkens according to the time-averaged combined amplitude. Optional overlays label the path difference, the combined-amplitude guide, and an arrow diagram for phase addition.
The probe graph plots Source A, Source B, and their combined wave at one selected screen point as functions of time.
The screen-pattern graph plots average brightness against screen height, so it stays position-based even while the time rail is used to inspect the instantaneous probe motion.

overlays.shortDescription: Labels the two travel distances and the extra distance between them.
whatToNotice: Moving the probe changes both path lengths, but interference depends on their difference, not on the two lengths separately.
overlays.whyItMatters: It shows how screen position turns into phase difference instead of making bright and dark bands look mysterious.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the maximum size of the combined motion at the probe.
whatToNotice: The instantaneous displacement can cross zero even at a bright point, but the guide still shows whether that point is mostly bright or dark over time.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the fast up-and-down motion from the longer-run interference pattern.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the two source contributions as arrows that add to the final result.
whatToNotice: As the phase difference changes, the two arrows swing apart or together, and the final arrow shrinks or grows with that angle.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns the amplitude formula into a picture of vector addition instead of algebra alone.

graphs.description: Traces Source A, Source B, and their combined wave at one chosen screen point over time, so you can see how bright or dark regions still come from time-varying waves.
Shows the average brightness at every screen height, so you can compare one chosen probe point with the full interference pattern.

noticePrompts.intro: Use one cue at a time. Each prompt should point to something you can actually see change on the stage or graphs.

items.text: Notice that the probe point and the screen band tell the same story: a larger combined-amplitude guide at the probe means a brighter region on the screen.
items.whyItMatters: The pattern is the long-run result of the same wave addition you are watching at one point.

items.text: Notice that hovering the pattern graph moves the live probe to that same screen height. The graph and stage are linked to the same point.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps the graph, geometry, and probe motion synchronized.

items.text: Notice that shortening the wavelength makes the same path difference count for more phase, so the bright and dark bands pack closer together.
items.whyItMatters: Wavelength sets fringe spacing because it converts path difference into phase difference.
A perfect null needs both the right phase difference and equal amplitudes.

items.text: Notice that the time rail changes the instantaneous probe motion, but the pattern graph stays the same because intensity is an average over time, not a time trace.
Notice which setup keeps the bright peaks farther apart on the screen. That setup has the larger wavelength or the smaller phase change per meter of path difference.
items.whyItMatters: Compare mode makes fringe-spacing changes easy to see without hiding the geometry underneath.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the outcome before you test it. Each prompt changes one cause at a time so you can connect geometry, phase, and brightness.

items.explanation: At the center, the path difference is zero, so geometry does not create a phase split. But a source phase offset of pi still makes the two arrivals oppose each other at that same point.
items.observationHint: Watch the center band dim even though the path lengths stay equal.

items.explanation: Shorter wavelength means the same extra distance produces a larger phase change, so the fringes crowd closer together.
Destructive phase alone does not create a full null. If one source is stronger, part of its contribution is left over instead of being completely canceled.
items.observationHint: The phase split is still destructive, but the combined-amplitude guide opens back up because the amplitudes are unmatched.

paragraphs: At first a standing wave can look frozen, but the string is not motionless. It forms when two equal waves travel in opposite directions on the same string and keep interfering. At some points the cancellation is permanent, so those points stay at zero displacement and become nodes. Halfway between them, reinforcement is strongest, so those points become antinodes with the largest oscillation.

This lab keeps the stage, mode-shape graph, and probe trace tied to the same harmonic. The graph shows where the string can have zero motion or maximum motion, the stage shows that pattern across the whole string, and the probe trace shows what one chosen point does in time. When you change the mode number, you are not just changing the picture. You are choosing a new allowed wavelength and frequency for a string with fixed ends.

keyIdeas: Nodes are fixed positions where destructive interference persists. The wave has not vanished from the whole string.
Antinodes are the centers of the loops, where the local oscillation is largest and the time trace reaches its biggest amplitude.
Higher harmonics fit more half-wavelength segments into the same length, so node spacing shrinks and frequency rises.

commonMisconception.myth: Because the pattern stays in place, the string must stop moving once the standing wave forms.
correction: The pattern in space stays fixed, but most points between the nodes still move up and down in time.
What stays fixed are the node positions and the overall mode shape, not the instantaneous displacement of the string.

workedExamples.intro: These examples use the current harmonic, string length, probe position, and inspected time from the live bench, so each calculation refers to the same string you are watching.

items.prompt: For the current mode number and string length, what wavelength and oscillation frequency are allowed on this fixed string?

steps.label: 1. Start from the fixed-end relations
2. Substitute the current harmonic and string length
3. Calculate the allowed wavelength and frequency

applyAction.label: Show third harmonic
applyAction.observationHint: More half-wavelength segments fit on the same string, so the nodes move closer together and the oscillation gets faster.

items.title: Displacement at the current probe point
items.prompt: At the current probe position $x_p = {{probeXValue}}\,\mathrm{m}$ and time $t = {{timeValue}}\,\mathrm{s}$, what displacement should this point on the string have?

steps.label: 2. Evaluate the position factor
3. Find the displacement at this instant

applyAction.observationHint: At a node, the spatial factor is zero, so the local trace collapses even while nearby parts of the string keep moving.

miniChallenge.prompt: You want one more interior node, but the fixed ends cannot change. What should you change?
miniChallenge.prediction: Choose between moving the probe, raising the mode number, and waiting longer in time.
miniChallenge.explanation: Moving the probe only changes where you observe the string, and waiting only changes the moment you look at the same harmonic. The number of interior nodes is set by the harmonic, so you must raise the mode number.

quickTest.intro: Use the stage, graph, and interference idea together. These checks ask what the string must be doing, not just which formula you remember.

questions.explanation: Nodes are fixed cancellation points created by interference. Nearby points can still oscillate strongly, so the standing wave is still present.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: The full string does not stop. Only the node points stay at zero while neighboring segments continue to move.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: Nodes and antinodes are different positions in the mode shape, not the same point viewed at a different time.

questions.explanation: Zero on the mode-shape graph means the local spatial factor is zero there, so that point is a node and stays fixed.
Interference explains why the fixed pattern of nodes and antinodes exists. Oscillation explains why points between nodes still move up and down in time.
A higher harmonic fits more half-wavelength segments into the same length, so node spacing shrinks and frequency rises. Amplitude does not need to change.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a horizontal string fixed at both ends with a movable probe marker and a ruler underneath. The string oscillates in one selected harmonic, and optional overlays can mark nodes, antinodes, and the two traveling-wave components that combine to make the standing pattern.
Changing amplitude, string length, mode number, or probe position updates the same stage, mode-shape graph, and probe-motion graph together so the standing-wave state stays synchronized.
The mode-shape graph plots the spatial factor of the standing wave against position on the string. Zero crossings correspond to nodes and peaks correspond to antinodes.
The probe-motion graph plots the chosen point's displacement in time together with its local envelope, so a node produces a flat trace while an antinode reaches the largest swing.

equations.meaning: The sine term sets how the amplitude varies along the string, and the cosine term makes each allowed point oscillate in time.
notes: The sine factor fixes the node and antinode pattern in space.
The cosine factor means points between nodes still move up and down.

equations.meaning: Only wavelengths that fit an integer number of half-wavelengths between the fixed ends can form a standing wave.
Once the wave speed and string length are fixed, each harmonic has one matching frequency.
These fixed positions stay at zero because the two traveling waves keep canceling there.

variableLinks.description: Changes how far the antinodes can swing, while the nodes still remain fixed.
Chooses which harmonic fits between the fixed ends, so node count, antinode count, wavelength, and frequency all change together.
Changes the distance between the fixed ends, so the allowed wavelengths, node spacing, and frequencies change with it.
Moves the measurement point along the same string so you can compare a node, an antinode, and an in-between point.

controls.description: Changes how far the antinodes swing without moving the node positions.
Changes the distance between the fixed ends, so the allowed harmonic spacing changes.
Chooses which harmonic fits on the fixed string.
Moves the live measurement point along the string so you can compare node, antinode, and in-between motion. Values past the end clamp to the current string length.

whatToNotice: A node stays on the center line at every instant, even while nearby parts of the string swing above and below it.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes persistent destructive interference visible instead of treating nodes as decorative markers.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows where the string can reach its largest local oscillation.
whatToNotice: An antinode is the center of a loop where the local envelope is largest, not just a high point in one frozen frame.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates maximum possible displacement from the instantaneous shape at one moment.

overlays.shortDescription: Adds the two equal traveling waves that superpose to create the standing pattern.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the interference origin of a standing wave visible instead of treating the pattern as a special separate object.

graphs.description: Shows the spatial factor of the current harmonic. Zero crossings mark nodes, and peaks mark antinodes.
Shows how one chosen point moves in time and how large its local envelope is in the current mode.

noticePrompts.intro: Use one cue at a time. Each prompt should point to something you can actually see on the string or graph.

items.text: Notice that the node markers never leave the center line, even while the neighboring loops cross it every cycle.
items.whyItMatters: A standing wave is not motionless everywhere. What stays fixed are the nodes and the mode pattern.

items.text: Notice that hovering the mode-shape graph moves the same live probe to that position on the string.
items.whyItMatters: The graph and stage are two views of the same standing-wave pattern.

items.text: Notice that raising the mode number adds another half-wavelength segment to the same string, so nodes move closer together and the oscillation gets faster.
items.whyItMatters: Changing mode number changes both the spatial pattern and the time behavior.

items.text: Notice that the standing pattern comes from two equal waves passing through each other. The string has not stopped carrying waves.
items.whyItMatters: This connects standing waves back to interference between traveling waves.

items.text: Notice that the time rail changes the instantaneous string shape and probe displacement, but the mode-shape graph stays the same because it represents the spatial pattern, not one frozen frame.
Notice which setup packs more loops into the same length. That setup is either a higher harmonic or a shorter string.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the consequence before you test it. Each prompt changes one cause at a time so you can connect the string, graph, and equations.

items.explanation: On a fixed string, a higher harmonic fits more half-wavelength segments into the same length. That adds nodes, shortens the allowed wavelength, and raises the oscillation frequency.
items.observationHint: Watch the node spacing shrink and the probe trace cycle faster after the change.

items.explanation: A node is a permanent cancellation point. Moving the probe there makes the spatial factor zero, so that point stops oscillating even though the rest of the string keeps moving.
For the same mode number, shortening $L$ shortens the allowed wavelength through $\lambda_n = 2L/n$. With the wave speed fixed, that shorter wavelength means a higher frequency.

challengeMode.intro: Use the mode-shape graph, overlays, and inspect-time rail to solve short standing-wave tasks on the same live string.

items.prompt: Starting from the third harmonic, move the probe onto a node so its local envelope becomes almost zero.
items.successMessage: You placed the probe on a true node. The rest of the string still shows a standing-wave pattern, but that point stays still.

setup.note: Use the node overlay and the mode-shape graph together as you move the probe.

hints: In the third harmonic, the interior nodes divide the string into three equal loop sections.
A tiny probe envelope matters more than one instant of small displacement. A true node stays quiet at all times.

items.prompt: From the fundamental mode, keep the probe at the center antinode and pause when that antinode crosses through zero displacement.
items.successMessage: You separated instantaneous displacement from envelope. An antinode can cross zero even though it is the point of largest oscillation.

setup.note: Keep the probe at the center antinode, then pause when the probe-motion trace crosses the axis.

hints: The center of the fundamental is an antinode, so its envelope should stay large even when the instantaneous displacement is zero.
This is an inspect-time task. Watch the probe-motion graph and pause at a zero crossing.

paragraphs: An air column resonates only when the standing wave fits the tube and obeys the ends. An open end lets the air move most freely, while a closed wall forces the air there to stay still. Those boundary rules decide which wavelengths can survive, so the resonance frequencies are selected by the tube instead of chosen freely.

This bench shows that same resonance state in several linked views. The tube picture shows where the air moves most and least, the displacement-shape graph shows the standing-wave pattern along the tube, the harmonic ladder shows which frequencies are allowed, and the probe trace shows one parcel in time. Use them together to see why open-open tubes allow every harmonic but closed-open tubes keep only the odd ones.

keyIdeas: At an open end, the air can move most freely, so parcel displacement is largest there. At a closed end, parcel displacement must be zero.
Open-open tubes allow every integer harmonic, but closed-open tubes allow only the odd harmonics because one end must be a node and the other an antinode.
Changing tube length or boundary type changes the allowed wavelengths, and the resonance frequencies follow from the same sound speed.

commonMisconception.myth: An open end should be a displacement node because the air can escape there.
correction: For parcel displacement, an open end is where the air moves most freely, so it behaves like a displacement antinode.
What becomes small at an open end is the pressure variation, which is why pressure nodes line up with displacement antinodes there.

workedExamples.title: Live resonance checks
workedExamples.intro: These examples use the current tube length, boundary condition, resonance order, and probe position from the live bench, so the algebra stays attached to the same resonant pattern you are watching.

items.title: Allowed wavelength and resonance frequency
items.prompt: For the current {{boundaryLabelValue}} with sound speed $v = {{waveSpeedValue}}\,\mathrm{m/s}$, what wavelength and resonance frequency are allowed for the selected mode?

steps.label: 1. Start from the current boundary rule
2. Substitute the live tube and selected mode
3. Compute the allowed resonance

applyAction.observationHint: With one end closed, the next allowed pattern after the fundamental is the third harmonic, not the second, because the tube must keep a node at the wall and an antinode at the mouth.

items.title: Probe motion and pressure cue
items.prompt: At the current probe position $x_p = {{probeXValue}}\,\mathrm{m}$ and time $t = {{timeValue}}\,\mathrm{s}$, what are the parcel displacement and the pressure cue there, and what does that say about the probe's role in the standing wave?

steps.label: 1. Read the local motion and pressure envelopes
2. Read the current time slice
3. Classify the probe's place in the pattern

applyAction.label: Move the probe to the closed wall
applyAction.observationHint: At a closed wall, parcel motion collapses to nearly zero while the pressure cue becomes strongest.

miniChallenge.prompt: You want the same tube length but a lower fundamental resonance. What single change will do that most directly?
miniChallenge.prediction: Choose between closing one end, raising the resonance order, and moving the probe.
miniChallenge.explanation: Closing one end changes the boundary condition from an open-open half-wave fundamental to a closed-open quarter-wave fundamental. That makes the allowed wavelength longer and the fundamental frequency lower, while moving the probe only changes what you inspect and raising the resonance order moves to a higher mode.

quickTest.intro: Use the tube, graph, and boundary rules together. These checks ask what the resonance pattern means physically, not which formula you memorized.
