paragraphs: The simulation shows one horizontal air column with a movable probe parcel inside the tube, a colored pressure ribbon, and a ruler underneath. The left end can switch between open and closed, the right end stays open, and optional overlays mark boundary rules, parcel-motion nodes and antinodes, and the complementary pressure pattern.

Changing tube length, boundary type, resonance order, probe position, or amplitude updates the same tube view, displacement-shape graph, harmonic ladder, and probe-motion graph together so the resonance state stays synchronized.
The displacement-shape graph plots parcel-motion scale against position in the tube, so zero crossings mark displacement nodes and the end behavior changes when the boundary condition changes.

The probe-motion graph plots one selected parcel in time together with its local envelope, while the harmonic ladder shows the allowed resonance frequencies and makes the missing even harmonics of a closed-open pipe visible.

equations.meaning: This standing-wave shape gives displacement antinodes at both open ends, so the air at each mouth can move most freely.
This shape forces a displacement node at the closed wall and a displacement antinode at the open end, so only odd quarter-wave patterns fit.
Because both ends follow the same antinode rule, an open-open tube allows the full sequence of integer harmonics.
Because one end is closed and the other is open, the tube keeps only the odd harmonics.

variableLinks.description: Changes how much standing-wave pattern can fit inside the tube, so all allowed wavelengths and resonance frequencies shift together.
Switches the left end between free-moving and fixed, which changes the whole harmonic family rather than just relabeling the same pattern.
Chooses which allowed mode is shown. In a closed-open tube, stepping the order moves through the 1st, 3rd, 5th, and higher odd harmonic family.
Moves the sampled air parcel so you can compare open-end breathing, interior nodes, and closed-wall stillness on the same resonance.
Scales how large the parcel motion and pressure cue can become without changing which resonances are allowed.

controls.description: Changes the resonating length of the air column and therefore the wavelengths that fit.
Switch between open-open and closed-open boundary conditions on the same tube.
Selects which allowed mode is shown. In a closed-open tube, the order steps through the odd harmonic family.
Moves the live parcel probe along the tube so you can test nodes, antinodes, and boundary behavior.
Scales the size of the parcel-motion and pressure cues without changing the allowed resonance family.

whatToNotice: At an open end, parcel motion is large and pressure variation is small. At a closed wall, parcel motion is zero and the pressure swing is strongest.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the allowed harmonic family tied to a physical end condition instead of a memorized formula.

whatToNotice: Nodes and antinodes stay at fixed positions in the standing-wave pattern, even though the actual parcel displacement changes through time.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the spatial resonance pattern from one frozen instant of motion.

whatToNotice: Pressure and parcel motion swap roles: where one is smallest, the other is often strongest.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps displacement and pressure connected without pretending they peak at the same places.

graphs.description: Shows the standing-wave shape along the tube, so you can see where parcel motion must be zero or largest for the current boundary condition.
Tracks one chosen parcel in time together with its local envelope, so a node gives a flat trace while an antinode gives the largest motion.
Shows the allowed resonance frequencies for the current tube, making the missing even harmonics of a closed-open pipe easy to see.

noticePrompts.intro: Use one cue at a time. Each one points to a real boundary-condition effect already visible on the tube or graph.

items.text: Notice that an open end is where the air moves most freely, so it is a displacement antinode, not a stuck point.
items.whyItMatters: It ties the mouth of the tube to the correct displacement rule before you think about harmonics.

items.text: Notice that closing one end changes the whole harmonic family, not just the label on the same pattern.
items.whyItMatters: Boundary conditions decide which wavelengths can fit in the first place.

items.text: Notice that hovering the displacement-shape graph moves the live probe to that same position in the tube.
items.whyItMatters: The graph and the tube are two views of the same standing-wave pattern.

items.text: Notice that the pressure guides swap roles with the parcel-motion guides: pressure is strongest where parcel motion is smallest, and weakest where parcel motion is largest.
items.whyItMatters: The displacement picture and the pressure picture describe the same resonance from complementary angles.

items.text: Notice that the harmonic ladder skips the even harmonics in a closed-open tube.
items.whyItMatters: The missing even modes are a direct consequence of the end conditions.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the boundary-condition consequence first, then test it on the same tube.

items.explanation: An open-open fundamental fits half a wavelength across the tube, but a closed-open fundamental fits only a quarter wavelength. That makes the allowed wavelength longer and the fundamental frequency lower for the same tube length.
items.observationHint: Watch the ladder drop to a lower fundamental while the closed wall becomes a motion node.

items.explanation: The closed wall is a displacement node and a pressure antinode. Parcels cannot move through the wall, but the compressions and rarefactions pile up there most strongly.
Closed-open tubes skip the even harmonics. After the fundamental, the next allowed pattern is the third harmonic, so the wavelength shortens and the frequency rises.

paragraphs: Uniform circular motion is the clearest example of why constant speed does not mean zero acceleration. The speed stays the same, but the velocity keeps changing because its direction keeps turning.

This lab keeps the orbit, vectors, and time graphs tied to one live state. Change the radius, angular speed, or phase, then compare the circle, the tangent velocity, the inward acceleration, and the x(t) and y(t) projections. You are seeing the same motion in several representations, not learning separate formulas.

keyIdeas: Velocity is always tangent to the circle, not pointed toward the center.
Centripetal acceleration points inward because the velocity direction is changing continuously, even when the speed stays constant.
The x and y coordinates of uniform circular motion behave like sine and cosine oscillations, which is why this motion connects directly to simple harmonic motion.

commonMisconception.myth: If the particle moves at constant speed, its acceleration must be zero.
correction: Speed tells you only how fast the particle moves. Velocity includes direction as well as size.
In uniform circular motion the direction changes at every instant, so there must be a nonzero inward acceleration even when the speed readout stays constant.

workedExamples.title: Worked example from the live motion
workedExamples.intro: Use the current orbit and inspected time, not a separate diagram. First read the x-coordinate from the same radius, angular speed, and phase that the stage is using. Then calculate the inward acceleration from that same circular motion.

items.title: Horizontal position at this instant
items.prompt: At $t = {{timeValue}}\,\mathrm{s}$, what is the particle's x-coordinate?

steps.label: 1. Write the x-projection formula
2. Substitute the live values
3. Evaluate the current x-coordinate

applyAction.label: Turn on projection guides
applyAction.observationHint: Watch the particle, the dashed projection, and the x(t) graph marker update together.

items.title: Inward acceleration for this orbit
items.prompt: What centripetal acceleration is needed to keep the particle on the current circle?

steps.label: 1. Write the centripetal-acceleration formula
2. Substitute the current radius and angular speed
3. Calculate the inward acceleration

applyAction.label: Try a tighter turn
applyAction.observationHint: A smaller radius with a larger angular speed makes the inward acceleration grow quickly.

miniChallenge.prompt: At the very top of the circle, which way does the velocity point, and which way does the centripetal acceleration point?
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide before you turn on the vectors whether either arrow can point straight up at that instant.
miniChallenge.answer: At the top of the circle, the velocity is horizontal because it is tangent to the path, and the centripetal acceleration points straight down toward the center.
miniChallenge.explanation: Velocity follows the tangent, while centripetal acceleration points inward along the radius. In uniform circular motion, those two directions are perpendicular.

quickTest.intro: Use the orbit, vectors, and graphs together. These checks ask whether you can explain what constant speed, tangent velocity, and inward acceleration really mean.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a particle moving around a circle centered on visible x and y axes. Optional overlays can show the radius vector, the tangent velocity vector, the inward centripetal-acceleration vector, the angular marker, and the x and y projection guides.
Changing radius, angular speed, or phase updates the same orbit, vectors, readouts, and graphs together. The stage and graphs are synchronized views of one live circular motion.
The projection graphs show x(t) and y(t), the velocity graph shows the x and y components of the tangent velocity, and the angle graph shows angular position over time.
These are different views of the same circular motion, not separate datasets. The graphs and the orbit stage always describe the same instant.

noticePrompts.intro: Use the orbit, vectors, and graphs as one linked system. The best prompt tells you which representation is clearest for the question you are asking.

items.text: The speed can stay constant while the velocity vector keeps turning.
items.tryThis: Watch the tangent arrow complete a full turn without the speed value on the stage changing.
items.whyItMatters: Acceleration depends on changing velocity, not just changing speed.

items.text: The centripetal acceleration points inward, while the velocity points tangent to the circle.
items.tryThis: Pause at the top, bottom, or side of the orbit and compare the inward arrow with the tangent velocity arrow.
items.whyItMatters: This is the geometry that makes circular motion acceleration nonzero.

items.text: The x and y graphs are the axis projections of the same circular motion.
items.tryThis: Turn on the projection guides, then scrub the graph so the marker and the projected coordinates stay synchronized.
items.whyItMatters: It is the direct visual bridge from circular motion to simple harmonic motion.

items.text: Increasing angular speed makes the particle go around the circle faster and steepens the angle graph without changing the radius.
items.tryThis: Raise Angular speed and compare how much sooner the particle completes a full revolution.
items.whyItMatters: Angular speed changes the timing of the motion even when the circle size stays fixed.

items.text: When you pause and scrub, the orbit position, vector directions, and graph marker all describe the same instant.
items.tryThis: Pause, step once, then compare the particle angle with the x and y projection values.
items.whyItMatters: It turns the formulas into one inspectable state instead of separate facts.

items.text: The setup that completes a revolution sooner has the larger angular speed.
items.tryThis: Keep the radius similar in A and B, then change only Angular speed in one setup.
items.whyItMatters: Compare mode makes timing differences obvious without changing the spatial frame.

predictionMode.title: Predict the effect
predictionMode.intro: Predict what changes before you touch the orbit. Then test the same change on the stage, the vectors, and the graphs.

items.explanation: A larger angular speed means more radians per second, so each revolution takes less time. Because $a_c = \omega^2 R$, the inward acceleration also rises strongly when radius stays fixed.
items.observationHint: The angle graph gets steeper, and the inward arrow grows while the circle size stays fixed.

items.prompt: If angular speed stays fixed and radius increases, what changes most directly?
items.explanation: With angular speed fixed, the period $T = 2\pi/\omega$ stays the same. But the particle has to cover a larger circle in the same time, so its speed rises and the inward acceleration also increases.
items.observationHint: Watch the orbit widen while the angle graph keeps the same slope and the speed readout becomes larger.

paragraphs: Damping and resonance are two different ideas that shape how a real oscillator behaves. Damping removes energy from the motion, so left on its own the oscillation fades. A driver adds energy at a chosen frequency, so the motion can be sustained or amplified.

Resonance is the case where the driver transfers energy especially efficiently because its frequency is close to the oscillator's natural frequency. This module keeps that story clear by separating two views: a time-history view, where you watch one motion fade or settle, and a response view, where you compare steady-state amplitude across many driving frequencies.

keyIdeas: Larger damping removes energy faster, so the transient motion dies away more quickly.
In driven motion, the steady-state response is largest when the driving frequency is close to the natural frequency.
Damping does not remove resonance completely. It lowers the peak and makes the response less sharply concentrated around one frequency.

commonMisconception.myth: Resonance always means the amplitude shoots upward without limit.
correction: In a real damped system, energy is lost every cycle, so the amplitude stays finite even near resonance.
Lower damping makes the resonance peak taller and sharper, while higher damping makes it lower and broader.

workedExamples.title: Worked example from the live settings
workedExamples.intro: Use the mode and parameters already on screen. First evaluate the oscillator's displacement in the currently selected mode. Then calculate the steady-state response amplitude from the drive settings and compare it with the response curve.

steps.label: 1. Choose the formula for the current mode
2. Substitute the live settings
3. Evaluate the displacement

applyAction.label: Try free swing
applyAction.observationHint: In transient mode, damping makes the envelope shrink with time, so later oscillations are smaller even when the motion is still repeating.

items.title: Steady-state amplitude at this drive frequency

steps.label: 1. Write the steady-state response formula
2. Substitute the current drive settings
3. Calculate the response amplitude

applyAction.label: Try near resonance
applyAction.observationHint: In response mode, the largest steady-state amplitude appears when the drive frequency sits close to the peak of the response curve.

miniChallenge.prompt: If you increase damping while keeping the natural frequency and driving frequency fixed, what happens to the resonance peak?
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide whether the peak becomes taller or shorter, sharper or broader.
miniChallenge.answer: The peak becomes shorter and broader.
miniChallenge.explanation: More damping means more energy is removed each cycle, so the oscillator cannot build as much steady-state amplitude. The system also becomes less selective about the exact driving frequency.

quickTest.intro: Use the stage and the graph axes together. One graph follows a single motion through time, while the other compares steady-state amplitude across driving frequencies.

questions.explanation: The transient view follows one oscillator as time passes. The response view is not a time history at all. It compares the steady-state amplitude the oscillator would have at different driving frequencies.

paragraphs: The simulation alternates between two views of the same damped, driven oscillator: a time-based motion view and a frequency-response view. In the motion view, the displacement changes through time and can decay or settle. In the response view, the horizontal axis becomes driving frequency, and the graph shows steady-state amplitude instead of a time history.
The controls make the physical meaning explicit, so it stays clear whether you are changing damping, the natural frequency, or the rate and strength of the external driver.
The transient graph shows one oscillator through time and makes damping visible as a shrinking envelope.

The response graph is not a later-time continuation of that motion. It shows how steady-state amplitude changes across driving frequencies and how damping changes the height and width of the resonance peak.

equations.meaning: The restoring term, damping term, and driving term act on the same oscillator. The motion you see comes from the balance between energy lost and energy added.
This is the frequency the system prefers when it is displaced and released without driving. Resonance is judged by how close the driver is to this value.
This gives the steady-state amplitude for each driving frequency. Lower damping makes the peak taller and sharper, while higher damping makes it lower and broader.

variableLinks.description: Sets how quickly energy is removed from the motion, so it controls both how fast the transient dies away and how tall and sharp the resonance peak can become.
Sets the oscillator's preferred rate. Resonance happens when the driver is close to this frequency, not just when the driving force is large.
Changes how well the external driver stays in step with the oscillator. Near resonance the energy transfer is most effective, and away from resonance the response is smaller.
Sets how strongly the driver pushes. It scales the overall response, but it does not decide where resonance occurs.

starterExploreTasks: Raise damping in the damped-motion view and watch the envelope collapse faster.
Switch to the response graph and move the driving frequency toward the peak marker.
Compare light and heavy damping to see the resonance peak become taller/narrower or shorter/broader.

controls.description: Switch between following one oscillation through time and scanning steady-state amplitude across many driving frequencies.

whatToNotice: This arrow shows the external driver, which can add energy even while damping is removing energy.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the source of the forcing from the oscillator's own preferred motion.

whatToNotice: In transient mode, stronger damping makes the envelope tighten more quickly.
In response mode, the visible amplitude stays bounded instead of growing without limit.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you read damping as energy loss rather than just as a smaller-looking motion.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the driving frequency that gives the strongest steady-state response for the current damping and natural frequency.
whatToNotice: This marker belongs to the response curve, not to a later moment in time.
Changing damping can lower the peak and shift its sharpness without removing it.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the resonance condition readable as a frequency comparison rather than as a mystery spike.

graphs.description: This follows one oscillator through time and shows how damping makes the envelope shrink from cycle to cycle.
This is not a time trace. Each point shows the steady-state amplitude for one driving frequency, so the peak shows where resonance is strongest.

noticePrompts.intro: Use the prompt to match the current view. In transient mode you are following one motion through time. In response mode you are comparing many possible steady-state responses across different driving frequencies.

items.text: Damping removes energy each cycle, so the envelope shrinks even while the oscillator keeps crossing equilibrium.
items.tryThis: Stay in the damped-motion view and compare the first few swings with the later ones instead of looking only at one instant.
items.whyItMatters: This is the clearest sign that the system is still oscillating while steadily losing energy.

items.text: Increasing damping makes the time trace die away faster and makes the resonance peak lower and broader.
items.tryThis: Increase Damping ratio, then compare the tighter transient envelope or the shorter, wider response peak.
items.whyItMatters: It connects one control to both the time-history view and the frequency-response view.

items.text: The response graph is not a later part of the motion. Its horizontal axis is driving frequency, so each point is a different operating condition.
items.whyItMatters: Without this distinction, resonance can look like a time-based spike instead of a frequency-dependent effect.

items.text: The response is largest when the driving frequency sits close to the natural frequency.
items.tryThis: Move the drive frequency toward the peak and watch the response-curve marker and the stage amplitude strengthen together.
items.whyItMatters: Resonance is efficient energy transfer near a matching frequency, not just “big motion” for any reason.

items.text: When you pause late in the motion, the stage and graph marker still refer to the same instant even though the envelope has already shrunk.
items.whyItMatters: Stepping through time makes damping visible as a drop in amplitude from cycle to cycle.

items.text: The setup with the taller response or the slower decay is usually the one with lower damping or a drive frequency closer to resonance.
items.whyItMatters: Compare mode helps separate changes caused by damping from changes caused by frequency matching.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict whether the change mainly affects time decay or the steady-state resonance peak, then test it on the live graph.

items.explanation: Lower damping means less energy is lost each cycle, so the oscillator can build a larger steady-state response near resonance. That makes the peak taller and narrower.
items.observationHint: In response mode, compare both the height of the peak and how tightly it is concentrated around one frequency.

items.explanation: Moving the drive away from the natural frequency makes the driver and oscillator less well matched, so the energy transfer becomes less effective and the response falls.
items.observationHint: The peak stays near the natural frequency, but the marker moves down the curve as the drive frequency moves away from it.

paragraphs: A diagonal vector is not a special new object. It is one vector split into a horizontal part and a vertical part, so you can analyze it by tracking two perpendicular components.

In this model the vector stays constant, so the component graph stays flat, the $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ graphs stay linear, and the point follows a straight path. One magnitude and one angle control every representation, which is why component thinking carries directly into later mechanics.

keyIdeas: The horizontal and vertical components are exact projections of one vector, not two separate motions you invent afterward.
At fixed magnitude, changing angle redistributes the same vector between $v_x$ and $v_y$.
If $v_x$ and $v_y$ stay constant, the point moves on a straight line, the component graph stays flat, and $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ remain linear.

commonMisconception.myth: A diagonal vector needs its own special rule, so components are only rough estimates.
correction: The components are exact perpendicular projections of the same vector. Using trigonometry and the Pythagorean relation takes you back to the original magnitude and angle.
A negative component tells you direction along an axis, not that the whole vector became smaller.

workedExamples.title: Work the live vector
workedExamples.intro: Read the current magnitude and angle from the controls, then check the calculation against the stage and graphs. In the time-based example, the values follow the current inspected time unless you freeze it.

items.title: Current vector components
items.prompt: For the current vector, what are $v_x$ and $v_y$?

applyAction.label: Apply a steeper angle
applyAction.observationHint: Watch the 1 s reference step and the component graph together: the total vector stays the same size, but more of it shifts into the vertical component and less into the horizontal component.

items.title: Position at the current time
items.prompt: After $t = {{timeValue}}\,\mathrm{s}$, where is the point on the stage?

applyAction.label: Apply a downward angle
applyAction.observationHint: The point still moves along one straight path. What changes is the sign of the vertical component, so the path slopes downward while the horizontal motion stays to the right.

miniChallenge.prompt: Keep the vector magnitude fixed and rotate the direction closer to the vertical axis. What happens to the horizontal and vertical components, and how does the path on the stage change?
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide which component grows, which shrinks, and whether the straight path becomes flatter or steeper before you test it.
miniChallenge.answer: The vertical component grows in magnitude, the horizontal component shrinks, and the path tilts closer to vertical.
miniChallenge.explanation: The total vector length stays the same, so changing angle only redistributes that length between the horizontal and vertical legs of the triangle. Because the components are still constant, the path stays straight.

quickTest.intro: Use the stage, the component graph, and the position graph together. These questions check whether you can explain the motion from the representations, not just recall formulas.

questions.prompt: If the angle is $90^\circ$, what must be true?
questions.explanation: At $90^\circ$, the vector points entirely along the vertical axis. That makes the horizontal projection zero, but the vector can still have a nonzero magnitude.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: Pointing straight up says nothing about the vector's size. Only the horizontal projection becomes zero at $90^\circ$.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: At $90^\circ$, the vector aligns with the vertical axis, so the vertical component is as large as it can be for that magnitude.

showMeAction.observationHint: The path becomes vertical on the stage, the $x(t)$ line stays at zero, and the $v_x$ line sits on the axis while $v_y$ stays positive.

questions.prompt: On the position graph, the $x(t)$ line slopes upward while the $y(t)$ line slopes downward. What motion does that represent?
questions.explanation: An upward slope in $x(t)$ means the point keeps moving right. A downward slope in $y(t)$ means it keeps moving downward. Both happen at the same time.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: The sign or slope of $y(t)$ does not decide horizontal motion. The upward slope of $x(t)$ shows the motion is still to the right.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: A downward $y(t)$ line only means the vertical component is negative. The vector can still have a large magnitude.

showMeAction.observationHint: The $x(t)$ line rises, the $y(t)$ line falls, and the path heads into the fourth quadrant on the stage.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a coordinate plane with a vector starting at the origin and a point that moves in the vector direction as time passes. Optional overlays can show the angle, the one-second reference step, and guides that split the motion into horizontal and vertical parts.
Changing magnitude or angle updates the stage and every graph together, so the same vector decomposition stays visible across the path, the position plots, and the component readout.
The path graph is a straight line because the horizontal and vertical components stay constant. Hovering or scrubbing the path graph moves the stage point to the matching place on that line.

The position graph shows straight-line $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ trends, while the component graph shows flat $v_x$ and $v_y$ lines. Together they show the difference between constant components and the displacement those components build over time.

overlays.shortDescription: Drops perpendicular guides from the current point to show the horizontal and vertical parts.
whatToNotice: The guide lines form the same right triangle described by the component equations.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the link between the stage position, the component values, and the triangle geometry visible.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the vector angle from the positive x-axis.
whatToNotice: Crossing below the x-axis or past $90^\circ$ can change a component sign without changing the sine and cosine rules.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the angle convention visible, so sign changes and quadrant reasoning are easier to track.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the one-second displacement and splits that single step into horizontal and vertical parts.
whatToNotice: The 1 s step stays the same while the point keeps repeating that component split as time increases.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the constant vector from the growing displacement it produces over time.

graphs.description: Shows the straight-line track of the moving point in the plane. Its direction is set by the current component ratio.
Shows $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ separately, so their slopes reveal the size and sign of the horizontal and vertical components.
Shows $v_x$ and $v_y$ as constant lines because the vector itself does not change with time in this model.

noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to connect one control change to one visible result. Check the stage, overlays, and graphs together rather than treating them as separate views.

items.text: Notice that one diagonal displacement is exactly one horizontal change and one vertical change happening together.
items.tryThis: Keep the magnitude fixed, rotate the vector, and compare the triangle on the stage with the $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ graphs.
items.whyItMatters: This same decomposition move is used later for displacement, velocity, and force.

items.text: Notice that the stage point and the path graph show the same position at the same time.
items.tryThis: Hover or scrub the path graph and watch the stage point move to the matching place on the straight path.
items.whyItMatters: It helps you read the graph as a map of the motion, not as a separate picture.

items.text: Notice that rotating at fixed magnitude makes one component grow while the other shrinks.
items.tryThis: Rotate from about 35 degrees toward 75 degrees and compare the $v_x$ and $v_y$ lines before you look back at the path.
items.whyItMatters: This shows that angle redistributes the same vector instead of creating extra length.

items.text: A negative component means that projection points in the negative axis direction. It does not mean the whole vector became smaller.
items.tryThis: Keep the magnitude the same and drag the vector below the x-axis. The vertical component flips sign while the total vector keeps the same size.
items.whyItMatters: You need that sign idea later when forces or velocities point leftward or downward.

items.text: Pausing changes the accumulated position, but it does not change the vector components themselves.
items.tryThis: Pause and step through time. The markers on $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ move, while the $v_x$ and $v_y$ lines stay flat because the components are constant.
items.whyItMatters: It separates accumulated displacement from the constant rate that creates it.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the outcome before changing the vector. Then test it and use the stage, component graph, and path graph to explain what happened.

items.prompt: If the angle rotates from 35 degrees to 75 degrees while magnitude stays fixed, what changes must you see?
items.explanation: At fixed magnitude, rotating toward vertical gives more of the same total vector to $v_y$ and less to $v_x$. That is why the path becomes steeper.
items.observationHint: Watch $v_x$ drop, $v_y$ rise, and the straight path tilt upward.

items.prompt: If you keep the same magnitude but rotate the vector to -30 degrees, what must be true about the components?
items.explanation: A negative angle points below the x-axis but still to the right, so $v_y$ becomes negative while $v_x$ stays positive.
items.observationHint: Check the signs on the component graph and then confirm them from the path direction on the stage.

challengeMode.intro: Use the controls, overlays, and inspect rail as measuring tools. The goal is to build a vector and justify it from the live readout, not just guess a picture.

items.prompt: Build a vector whose horizontal and vertical components are nearly the same size. Keep the component graph open so you can match the two lines, not just eyeball the angle.
items.successMessage: You made the two components nearly equal. The graph values and the stage triangle now agree.

setup.note: Use the component graph and the guides together. One shows equal values, the other shows equal triangle legs.

hints: A good first try is near $45^\circ$, because equal legs come from a balanced angle.
If one component is still larger, rotate the vector toward the smaller component's axis.

items.prompt: At the end of the $4\,\mathrm{s}$ walk, make the point land near $(16\,\mathrm{m}, 12\,\mathrm{m})$.
items.successMessage: You matched the target endpoint by coordinating angle and magnitude. The stage position and the graphs point to the same final location.

setup.note: Choose the vector, let the motion run, then inspect the final sample to fine-tune both coordinates.

hints: Use inspect mode near the end of the motion window so you are checking the correct time.
A shallower angle increases horizontal reach faster than vertical reach for the same magnitude.

paragraphs: Torque tells you how strongly a force tries to rotate an object about a pivot. It depends on both where the force is applied and how much of the force points perpendicular to the bar.

This bench keeps the bar and rotational inertia fixed so you can isolate the geometry of torque. Change the distance from the pivot, force size, or force angle relative to the bar, then compare the stage with the moment arm, line of action, and graphs. For one fixed setup, the torque stays constant, so the torque graph is flat while angular speed and rotation angle build up.

keyIdeas: Read torque as $\tau = rF\sin\phi = rF_\perp$: a longer distance from the pivot or a larger perpendicular component gives a larger turning effect.
A force can be large and still produce almost no torque if its line of action passes through the pivot, because the moment arm is then near zero.
On this bench, a fixed setup gives constant torque, so angular acceleration is constant, angular speed changes linearly, and rotation angle curves.

commonMisconception.myth: The biggest force always makes the bar turn the most.
correction: Not unless it also has a large moment arm. A smaller force farther from the pivot can create more torque than a bigger force applied close in.
Not unless enough of it is perpendicular to the bar. A large force aimed through the pivot can have almost zero turning effect.

workedExamples.title: Worked example from the live bench
workedExamples.intro: Start from the geometry you can see. First calculate torque from distance and the perpendicular component of the force. Then use the same constant-torque setup to find angular speed and rotation angle at a chosen time.

steps.label: 1. Write the torque relation
2. Find the perpendicular force component
3. Calculate torque and angular acceleration

applyAction.label: Try line through pivot
applyAction.observationHint: Turn on line of action and moment arm. The force is still large, but the moment arm shrinks to nearly zero and the torque collapses.

steps.label: 1. Use the constant-torque motion rules
2. Substitute the current time and angular acceleration
3. Read angular speed and rotation angle

applyAction.label: Try clockwise push
applyAction.observationHint: Pause near the end of the clip and compare the signs of torque, angular speed, and rotation angle. A clockwise setup should make all three negative.

miniChallenge.prompt: Can you roughly halve the distance from the pivot but keep the same torque?
miniChallenge.prediction: Shorten the distance from the pivot, then predict how the perpendicular part of the force must change if the torque is to stay the same.
miniChallenge.answer: Yes. If $r$ gets smaller, then $F_\perp$ must get larger so that the product $rF_\perp$ stays unchanged.
miniChallenge.explanation: Torque is set by the product of distance from the pivot and perpendicular force. If one factor gets smaller, the other must get larger to keep the same turning effect.

quickTest.intro: These checks ask whether you can reason with torque using distance, direction, and line of action, not just quote the formula.

questions.prompt: Why can a large force still produce almost zero torque about the pivot?
questions.explanation: Torque depends on the moment arm. If the line of action passes through the pivot, the perpendicular distance is nearly zero, so the turning effect is nearly zero.

showMeAction.label: Try line through pivot
showMeAction.observationHint: Turn on the line of action and watch the moment arm collapse while the force arrow stays large.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a single bar hinged at its left end. A force is applied at a chosen distance from the pivot and at a chosen angle relative to the bar, so the bar rotates from rest according to the resulting torque.

Optional overlays can show the perpendicular component, the line of action, and the moment arm. The linked readout and graphs display torque, angular speed, and rotation angle for the same fixed setup.

For a fixed setup, the torque graph stays flat because the geometry relative to the bar does not change. The angular-speed graph is linear and the rotation-angle graph is curved because the bar starts from rest under constant angular acceleration.

The torque-versus-angle map shows a sine-like response for the current force size and distance, with the largest positive and negative torques near plus or minus ninety degrees and near-zero torque when the line of action passes through the pivot.

equations.meaning: Torque increases when the force acts farther from the pivot and when more of the force is perpendicular to the bar.
Only the perpendicular component twists the bar. The component along the bar does not add torque.
With fixed rotational inertia, the sign and size of torque set the sign and size of angular acceleration.
For one fixed setup on this bench, torque and $\alpha$ stay constant, so $\omega$ changes linearly and $	heta$ changes quadratically.

variableLinks.label: Distance from pivot
variableLinks.description: Moving the same push farther from the pivot lengthens the lever arm and increases torque.
Increasing force changes torque only through the part of the force that is perpendicular to the bar.

variableLinks.label: Angle relative to the bar
variableLinks.description: Changing the angle changes both the size and the sign of the perpendicular component, so it can strengthen, cancel, or reverse the torque.

controls.description: Set how hard the push is without changing its angle or position.

controls.label: Angle relative to bar
controls.description: Rotate the force relative to the bar. This changes the perpendicular component and can flip the torque sign.

controls.label: Distance from pivot
controls.description: Move the push point closer to or farther from the pivot. The same force gives a different torque when the lever arm changes.

presets.description: Reference case: a perpendicular push at the handle.
Same force and angle, half the distance from the pivot, so the torque is halved.
A bigger force at $30^\circ$ keeps the perpendicular component and torque matched to the reference case.
Large force, but almost zero torque because the line of action passes through the pivot.
A perpendicular push in the opposite direction produces clockwise torque.

overlays.shortDescription: Separates the force into the twisting part and the part along the bar.
whatToNotice: The perpendicular part is what sets the torque.
A large component along the bar can look dramatic while adding little or no turning effect.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns $\tau = rF\sin\phi = rF_\perp$ into something you can see.

overlays.shortDescription: Extends the force direction so you can see whether it misses the pivot or passes through it.
whatToNotice: When this line passes through the pivot, the moment arm is zero and the torque vanishes.
Changing only the angle can move the same push from a strong-torque case to a near-zero-torque case.
overlays.whyItMatters: Torque depends on the perpendicular distance from the pivot to the force line, not just on arrow length.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the shortest distance from the pivot to the line of action.
whatToNotice: The same force produces more torque when this distance is larger.
The moment arm can shrink to nearly zero even when the hand stays far from the pivot.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the geometry behind $r\sin\phi$ visible instead of leaving it as a memorized formula.

graphs.description: For one fixed setup, torque stays constant because distance from the pivot, force size, and angle relative to the bar stay fixed.
With constant angular acceleration from rest, angular speed changes linearly.
Rotation angle curves because the bar keeps gaining angular speed as it turns.
Shows how torque changes with force angle for the current force size and distance. The turning effect is largest near $\pm 90^\circ$ and near zero when the line of action passes through the pivot.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice about torque
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts while changing one control at a time. Match what you see on the bar to the moment arm, line of action, and graphs.

items.text: Notice that the same perpendicular push twists much more at the handle than near the pivot.
items.tryThis: Switch between the handle right-angle push and the near-hinge version. The force arrow stays the same, but the torque and final rotation should drop by about half.
items.whyItMatters: This isolates distance from every other factor.

items.text: On the angle map, torque is largest near $\pm 90^\circ$ and near zero at $0^\circ$ or $180^\circ$.
items.whyItMatters: It makes the $\sin\phi$ factor visible instead of leaving it as a rule to memorize.

items.text: A large force can still make almost no torque when its line of action passes through the pivot.
items.tryThis: Turn on the line of action, then aim the force back along the bar. The moment arm should collapse even though the force readout stays large.
items.whyItMatters: It breaks the mistake that a bigger force automatically means a bigger turning effect.

items.text: Changing only the force angle can weaken, remove, or reverse the torque without moving the hand.
items.tryThis: Slide the angle from $90^\circ$ through $0^\circ$ to $-90^\circ$. Watch the perpendicular component cross through zero and then flip sign.
items.whyItMatters: Direction decides both the size and the sign of the turning effect.

items.text: Changing only the application point changes torque immediately, even if the force arrow looks the same.
items.tryThis: Keep the same right-angle push and slide the application point inward. The moment arm, torque graph, and final angle should shrink together.
items.whyItMatters: This is why a door opens more easily at the handle than near the hinge.

predictionMode.title: Predict the turning effect
predictionMode.intro: Predict the torque first, then test it on the bar and graphs. The aim is to connect distance, force direction, and rotational response.

items.explanation: Keeping the same perpendicular force while cutting $r$ in half cuts the torque in half, so angular speed and rotation build more slowly.
items.observationHint: Half the distance should halve the torque, so the bar gains angular speed more slowly and turns less by the end of the clip.

items.prompt: If you replace the handle right-angle push with a stronger but shallower push that keeps $F_\perp$ the same, what should stay almost unchanged?
items.explanation: Torque depends on the perpendicular component, not on total force alone. If $F_\perp$ and the distance from the pivot stay matched, the turning effect stays matched too.
items.observationHint: Compare the torque graph and end-of-clip angle with the reference case. Matching $F_\perp$ should match the turning effect.

paragraphs: Static equilibrium means the plank stays at rest without starting to rise, sink, or rotate. That requires both vertical force balance and torque balance. For supported objects, the cleanest way to track the turning effect is to ask where the combined weight acts.

In this bench, the plank has its own weight, one cargo block can move, and the support region can shift or narrow. Each change updates the stage, support reactions, and graphs from the same state, so you can connect mass distribution to centre of mass, torque balance, and tipping without switching models.

keyIdeas: The combined centre of mass tells you where the total weight acts: $x_{\mathrm{CM}} = \dfrac{\sum m_i x_i}{\sum m_i}$.
Static equilibrium needs both $\sum F_y = 0$ and $\sum \tau = 0$. A plank can have balanced upward and downward forces and still tip if there is a leftover torque.
The vertical line through the combined centre of mass must stay inside the support region. At an edge, the margin is zero. Beyond an edge, tipping begins.
Changing support width does not move the combined centre of mass. It changes only how much room remains before the plank tips.

commonMisconception.myth: If the total upward force equals the total downward force, the plank must be stable.
correction: Force balance alone is not enough. Equal upward and downward forces can still leave a net torque that starts rotation.
For a supported plank, the combined centre-of-mass line must also fall inside the support region. Otherwise one required reaction would have to become negative, which is not physically possible.

workedExamples.title: Worked example from the live setup
workedExamples.intro: Read the current plank state rather than an abstract diagram. First find where the combined weight acts and how large it is. Then check whether the current support region can provide physically possible reactions and still leave a positive margin before tipping.

items.title: Combined centre of mass right now

steps.label: 1. Write the weighted-average rule
2. Insert the live masses and cargo position
3. Read the combined weight line and total weight

applyAction.label: Try heavy right load
applyAction.observationHint: Keep the combined centre-of-mass and support-region overlays on. The important change is where the total weight acts: its line moves beyond the right support edge.

items.title: Does this support region work?
items.prompt: For the current support region, can the plank stay in static equilibrium, and what support reactions would be needed if it can?

steps.label: 1. Mark the support edges
2. Check the reactions required by force and torque balance
3. Read the margin to tipping

applyAction.label: Try barely stable setup
applyAction.observationHint: The centre-of-mass line is still inside the support region, but only just. A small extra shift would make one required reaction impossible.

miniChallenge.prompt: Can you make the support region narrower without changing the total weight or the combined centre of mass, and still keep the plank stable?
miniChallenge.prediction: Narrow the support while keeping the same cargo and support centre. Predict what changes: the total weight, the centre of mass, or only the margin to the nearest edge.
miniChallenge.answer: Yes, as long as the combined centre-of-mass line still falls inside the support region. Narrowing the support does not change the total weight or the centre of mass by itself; it only reduces the margin before tipping.
miniChallenge.explanation: Static stability depends on geometry as well as force balance. The total weight line can stay in exactly the same place while the allowed support region around it becomes smaller.

quickTest.title: Quick check
quickTest.intro: Answer from the same centre-of-mass, torque, and support-region logic the bench is using. These questions test whether you can separate mass distribution from support geometry cleanly.

questions.prompt: The combined centre-of-mass line falls just to the right of the support region. Which conclusion is best supported?
questions.explanation: Once the combined centre-of-mass line crosses the support edge, one required reaction would have to become negative. The support can no longer provide the needed balance, so the plank tips right.

showMeAction.label: Show tipping right
showMeAction.observationHint: Track the combined centre-of-mass line and the right support edge together. The tipping verdict comes from that geometry and the negative reaction.

questions.explanation: Narrowing the support region changes the allowed stability window, not the location of the combined centre of mass.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a horizontal plank with its own weight and one movable cargo block. A support region under the plank can shift left or right and become narrower or wider. Optional overlays can show the separate weight lines, the single combined centre-of-mass line, the support region, the reactions at the two edges, and the torque arm from the support centre to the combined centre-of-mass line.

Changing cargo mass or cargo position changes the weight distribution. Changing support centre or support width changes how that weight distribution compares with the support. The stage, readouts, and graphs all update from the same static-equilibrium model.

The support-torque graph shows how the torque from the total weight changes as the support centre moves. The zero crossing is the support position directly under the combined centre of mass. The support-reactions graph shows the left and right reactions that would be required for equilibrium, including when one would need to go negative.

The cargo-stability graph shows the distance from the combined centre-of-mass line to the nearest support edge for the current support geometry. Positive margin means stable, zero means the line is exactly on an edge, and negative margin means tipping.

equations.meaning: Treat the total weight as acting through this weighted-average position.
The support reactions must add up to the total weight.
Zero net force is not enough; the torques must also cancel if the plank is to stay at rest.
The vertical line through the combined centre of mass must stay between the support edges if both reactions are to remain upward.

variableLinks.description: Increasing cargo mass both increases the total weight and pulls the combined centre of mass toward the cargo.
Sliding the cargo shifts the combined centre of mass and changes the torque about the support.
Moving the support region changes the torque arm and redistributes the reactions, but it does not change the weight distribution.
Changing support width changes the allowed region for stability, not the location of the combined centre of mass.

controls.description: Increase or decrease the cargo mass to see how it changes both total weight and centre-of-mass position.
Slide the cargo left or right to move the combined centre of mass.
Move the support region under the plank without changing the mass distribution.
Widen or narrow the support region to change only the stability margin.

overlays.shortDescription: Draws the single vertical line where the total weight acts.
whatToNotice: Changing cargo mass or cargo position moves this line because the mass distribution changes.
Changing support centre or support width does not move this line. It only changes how the support compares with it.
overlays.whyItMatters: This line is the bridge between centre-of-mass calculations and torque balance.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the two support edges and shows how much room the combined centre-of-mass line still has before tipping.
whatToNotice: The plank is stable only while the combined centre-of-mass line falls between the two edges.
If the line reaches an edge, the margin is zero. If it crosses the edge, tipping begins.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns the abstract stability rule into one visible geometric test.

whatToNotice: When the combined centre-of-mass line sits over the support centre, the two reactions are most balanced.
If the line crosses an edge, one required reaction would have to become negative, so that side can no longer support the plank.
overlays.whyItMatters: This connects the support-region rule back to force balance.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the horizontal distance from the support centre to the combined centre-of-mass line.
whatToNotice: This offset is the lever arm of the total weight about the support centre.
When the offset is zero, the torque of the total weight about that point is zero.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the centre-of-mass story tied to the torque idea instead of turning it into a separate rule.

graphs.description: Shows how the torque from the total weight changes as you move the support centre. The zero crossing marks the support position directly under the combined centre of mass.
Shows the left and right reactions required for equilibrium. A negative value means that side would have to pull downward, so the plank tips instead.

Shows the distance from the combined centre-of-mass line to the nearest support edge for the current support geometry. Positive values are stable, zero is the edge case, and negative values mean tipping.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice in the balance setup
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to connect four views of the same situation: centre of mass, torque, reactions, and support-region geometry.

items.text: Notice that stability is a geometry test: the combined centre-of-mass line must land inside the support region.
items.tryThis: Keep the combined centre-of-mass and support-region overlays on while you slide the cargo block.
items.whyItMatters: It turns the tipping question into a visible condition instead of a memorized phrase.

items.text: Notice that the support-torque graph crosses zero when the support centre sits directly under the combined centre of mass.
items.tryThis: Drag the support centre until the graph marker lands on zero, then compare the support centre with the combined centre-of-mass line.
items.whyItMatters: This is the same torque idea from a pivot, now applied to the total weight.

items.text: Changing support width changes the margin before tipping, but it does not move the combined centre of mass.
items.tryThis: Keep cargo and support centre fixed, then narrow the support width. The same centre-of-mass line now has less room inside the region.
items.whyItMatters: It separates mass distribution from support geometry.

items.text: If one required reaction becomes negative, the plank tips instead of remaining in static equilibrium.
items.tryThis: Move the support centre away from the combined centre-of-mass line and watch one reaction curve drop below zero.
items.whyItMatters: This is the force-balance version of the same support-region rule.

items.text: In compare mode, a heavier cargo placed closer in can share the same combined centre of mass as a lighter cargo placed farther out.
items.tryThis: Keep Setup A on Support under load, then tune Setup B to Heavier but closer in and compare the centre-of-mass lines.
items.whyItMatters: It shows that centre of mass is a weighted average, not just a visual midpoint.

predictionMode.title: Predict the balance
predictionMode.intro: Predict the balance result before you change the setup. Then check the same idea on the stage, the reaction arrows, and the graphs.

items.prompt: Starting from the tipping-right setup, what should happen if you move the support centre directly under the combined centre-of-mass line while keeping the cargo fixed?
items.explanation: Moving the support changes the torque arm, not the mass distribution. When the support centre lines up with the combined centre of mass, the torque from the total weight about that point becomes zero.
items.observationHint: Watch the torque marker reach zero and both reactions become non-negative.

items.explanation: Narrowing the support region does not change the total weight or the combined centre of mass. It only brings the support edges closer to the same weight line, so the margin shrinks.
items.observationHint: The centre-of-mass line stays put while the support region closes in around it.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the same bench to show that you can coordinate centre of mass, support region, torque, and reactions instead of treating them as separate rules.

items.title: Recover equilibrium
items.prompt: Starting from Tips right, move the support centre until the plank is stable again.
items.successMessage: You put the support centre under the combined centre of mass. That made the weight torque about the support vanish and made both reactions physically possible.

setup.note: Use the torque zero crossing together with the support-region overlay.

hints: Do not move the cargo. Fix the problem by changing only the support geometry.
The target position is where the support centre lines up with the combined centre-of-mass line.

targets.label: Place the support centre under the combined centre-of-mass line.
Make the torque about the support centre essentially zero.
Restore a clear positive margin inside the support region.

items.title: Edge of stability
items.prompt: Starting from Support under load, shift the support region left until the plank is only just stable.
items.successMessage: You kept the combined centre-of-mass line inside the support region, but only by a very small margin.

setup.note: Keep the heavy cargo fixed. Use support centre and the stability-margin graph together.

hints: A small positive margin is the goal. Zero is the exact tipping edge.
Keep the support width fixed so you are testing support position, not support size.

targets.label: Keep the support width at 1.0 m.
Shift the support region left, but not past the tipping point.
Leave only a small positive margin to the nearest edge.

items.title: Match the same centre of mass
items.prompt: Open compare mode. Keep Setup A on Support under load, then tune Setup B so a heavier cargo placed closer in gives the same combined centre of mass.
items.successMessage: You matched the combined centre of mass in two different mass distributions, so the balance point stayed the same.

setup.note: Clone Setup A, then make Setup B heavier but move the cargo closer to the middle.

hints: Heavier cargo does not need to sit as far out to produce the same weighted average.
You are matching $x_{\mathrm{CM}}$, not just making the two cargo blocks look visually similar.

targets.label: Keep Setup A at the reference combined centre-of-mass position.
Make Setup B clearly heavier than Setup A.
Move the heavier cargo closer to the middle.
Match Setup B to the same combined centre of mass.

paragraphs: Rotational inertia, or moment of inertia, measures how hard it is to change an object's spin about an axis. Two rotors can have the same total mass and still respond very differently if one keeps more of that mass far from the axis.

This bench keeps the comparison clean. The same six equal masses and the same motor-style torque stay in play while you slide the masses inward or outward. That means the main thing changing is mass distribution, so you can see directly how layout changes spin-up.

keyIdeas: Moment of inertia depends on where the mass is, not just how much mass there is. Mass farther from the axis counts much more strongly because each contribution is weighted by $r^2$.
For the same applied torque, angular acceleration follows $\alpha = \tau/I$. Larger moment of inertia means slower spin-up, a shallower angular-speed graph, and less rotation at the same time.
Rotational inertia is the rotational analogue of mass in linear motion. The same total mass can behave like an easy-to-spin compact rotor or a hard-to-spin spread-out rotor.

commonMisconception.myth: If two rotors have the same total mass, the same torque should make them spin up the same way.
correction: Equal total mass does not guarantee equal rotational response. A spread-out rotor can have a much larger moment of inertia than a compact one.
What matters is both how much mass there is and how far that mass sits from the axis, because the resistance to spin-up is weighted by $r^2$.

workedExamples.title: Worked example from the live layout
workedExamples.intro: Use the current mass radius and torque as evidence. First calculate the moment of inertia from the live layout, then use that same setup to predict angular acceleration, angular speed, and rotation angle.

items.title: Moment of inertia of this layout
items.prompt: For the current layout, what moment of inertia does this equal-mass rotor have, and what angular acceleration should the current torque produce?

steps.label: 1. Write the inertia model
2. Insert the current radius
3. Use $\tau/I$ to find the spin-up

items.resultLabel: Moment of inertia and angular acceleration

applyAction.label: Try wide rim
applyAction.observationHint: Keep the radius guide and compact-reference overlays visible. The total mass and torque stay fixed, but spreading the same masses outward makes the spin-up much slower.

items.title: Angular speed and angle at this time
items.prompt: At $t = {{timeValue}}\, \mathrm{s}$, what angular speed and rotation angle has the current layout reached?

steps.label: 1. Use the constant-torque motion rules
2. Insert the current time and angular acceleration
3. Read the spin-up state

applyAction.label: Try compact core
applyAction.observationHint: Pause near the end of the clip and compare this faster spin-up with the mid-radius and wide-rim cases.

miniChallenge.prediction: Push the same masses outward while keeping the motor torque fixed, then predict whether the same spin-up can survive when the distance from the axis gets much larger.
miniChallenge.answer: Not with the same torque. Moving the same mass farther from the axis raises the moment of inertia, so keeping the same angular acceleration would require a larger torque.
miniChallenge.explanation: Moment of inertia is really about mass distribution. If the same mass sits farther from the axis, the rotor becomes harder to spin. With torque fixed, the angular acceleration must drop.

quickTest.title: Quick check
quickTest.intro: These checks ask whether you can reason with mass distribution and spin-up, not just repeat the phrase moment of inertia.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a rotor viewed from the front, with six equal masses attached to spokes around a central hub. A curved torque arrow near the hub indicates a steady motor-style twist, and the masses can slide inward or outward while the total mass stays fixed.

Optional overlays can show the current mass radius, mark the equal masses, and add a ghost compact reference layout. The linked readout and graphs report moment of inertia, angular acceleration, angular speed, and rotation angle for the same live layout.

The angular-speed graph is linear and the rotation-angle graph curves upward because each fixed layout experiences one constant angular acceleration under the current torque. Compact layouts rise faster, while spread-out layouts rise more slowly.

The moment-of-inertia map rises strongly with mass radius, while the angular-acceleration map falls when the torque is fixed. Together they show that spreading the same mass outward increases rotational resistance and slows spin-up.

equations.meaning: Each piece of mass counts more when it sits farther from the axis because the weighting grows like $r^2$.
On this bench the same moving mass sits on one ring, so sliding that ring outward makes the rotational inertia rise quickly.
For the same torque, a larger moment of inertia gives a smaller angular acceleration.
For each fixed setup, the rotor starts from rest under one constant torque, so angular speed grows linearly while rotation angle curves upward.

variableLinks.description: Sliding the same masses outward raises the rotational inertia sharply because each mass contribution is weighted by $r^2$.
Increasing the motor torque raises angular acceleration, but it does not change the rotor's moment of inertia.

controls.description: Change the motor torque without changing the total mass or its layout.
Slide the same six equal masses inward or outward while keeping the total moving mass fixed.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the current distance of the equal masses from the axis.
whatToNotice: The same masses can move far from the axis without adding any extra mass.
Distance from the axis is the key geometry that moment of inertia depends on.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes mass distribution visible instead of leaving it as an abstract formula.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks that each puck has the same mass, so only the distribution is changing.
whatToNotice: The rotor is not gaining extra mass when the pucks move outward.
What changes is where the same mass is placed, not how much mass there is.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps separate total mass from mass distribution.

whatToNotice: The ghost reference keeps the same number of masses but packs them closer to the axis.
As the live masses move away from that reference, the inertia and spin-up responses separate quickly.
overlays.whyItMatters: It gives an immediate compact-versus-spread comparison without changing total mass or torque.

graphs.description: For each fixed layout, angular speed rises linearly because one constant torque produces one constant angular acceleration.
Rotation angle curves upward because the rotor keeps gaining angular speed while it turns.
For this equal-mass rotor, moment of inertia rises quickly as the same mass is pushed farther from the axis.
With torque held fixed, moving the same masses outward lowers the angular acceleration because the moment of inertia increases.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice while it spins up
noticePrompts.intro: Keep one comparison in view at a time: the same total mass, the same torque, and a changing distance from the axis.

items.text: The torque can stay the same while the rotor spins up much more slowly when the same masses move outward.
items.tryThis: Switch between the compact-core and wide-rim presets. The motor torque stays at $4\, \mathrm{N\,m}$, but the angular-speed graph becomes much shallower when the same masses sit farther out.
items.whyItMatters: This isolates moment of inertia from torque. The cause stays the same while the rotational resistance changes.

items.text: On the inertia map, spreading the masses outward raises $I$ faster than the radius itself because each contribution is weighted by $r^2$.
items.tryThis: Move along the inertia map from about $r = 0.3\, \mathrm{m}$ toward $r = 1.0\, \mathrm{m}$ and watch how quickly the curve rises.
items.whyItMatters: The square dependence is why even modest outward shifts can matter a lot.

items.text: In compare mode, a compact rotor and a wide-rim rotor can start under the same torque yet separate immediately in angular speed.
items.tryThis: Clone the compact-core setup, then move only Setup B outward while keeping the torque matched.
items.whyItMatters: The difference comes from moment of inertia, not from a weaker motor.

items.text: Increasing torque can partly recover the spin-up, but it does not change the moment of inertia of the layout.
items.tryThis: Start from the wide-rim setup, then raise the torque. The spin-up map shifts upward, but the inertia map stays exactly where it was.
items.whyItMatters: Moment of inertia is a property of the mass layout, not of the motor.

predictionMode.title: Predict the spin-up
predictionMode.intro: Predict the response before you move the masses or change the motor torque. Then compare the live rotor with the readout and graphs.

items.explanation: Moving the same masses outward raises the moment of inertia. With the same torque, the angular acceleration becomes smaller, so the rotor builds angular speed and rotation more slowly.
items.observationHint: Watch the inertia map rise while the angular-speed graph becomes shallower.

items.prompt: If you keep the masses on the wide rim but give the rotor a stronger torque, what should change?
items.explanation: A stronger motor changes the response through $\alpha = \tau/I$, but the mass layout and therefore the moment of inertia stay the same.
items.observationHint: The inertia map should stay fixed while the spin-up map shifts upward.

paragraphs: Rolling without slipping links the motion of the center of mass to the spin of the object. As the roller speeds up down the incline, it must also spin so that the point touching the ramp does not slide relative to the surface.

On this bench, every setup feels the same downhill component of gravity on the same incline. What changes is how the object's mass is distributed about its axis. That distribution changes how the motion is shared between translation and rotation, which is why different shapes reach the bottom at different times.

keyIdeas: No-slip rolling means $v_{\mathrm{cm}} = r\omega$ and $a_{\mathrm{cm}} = r\alpha$, so the linear and angular motions are locked together.
The inertia factor $k = I/(mr^2)$ tells you how hard the shape is to spin for its mass and radius. Larger $k$ means more of the same downhill pull must go into building rotation.
On a fixed incline, $a_{\mathrm{cm}} = g\sin\theta/(1 + k)$, so smaller inertia factor gives larger center-of-mass acceleration and a faster run.
Changing radius for the same shape mostly changes the spin rate through $\omega = v/r$. In this bounded model, it does not by itself change the center-of-mass acceleration.

commonMisconception.myth: The heavier or larger roller must always win the race down the incline.
correction: In this bounded no-slip model, the key race variable is not total mass by itself. It is how the mass is distributed about the axis, summarized by the inertia factor $k$.
Changing radius changes how fast the object must spin for a given center speed, but for the same shape on the same incline it does not by itself change the center-of-mass acceleration formula.

workedExamples.title: Worked example from the live incline
workedExamples.intro: Use the live slope, shape, and inspected time as evidence. First predict the center-of-mass acceleration and full-ramp travel time from the current slope and inertia factor. Then read the linked linear and angular speeds at one instant and check how the kinetic energy is split.

items.title: Acceleration of the current roller
items.prompt: For the current setup, what center-of-mass acceleration should this {{shapeLabelValue}} have, and how long should it take to travel the full ${{trackLengthValue}}\,\mathrm{m}$ ramp?

steps.label: 1. Write the rolling-acceleration rule
2. Insert the live slope and inertia factor
3. Use the acceleration to find the ramp time

applyAction.label: Try hoop contrast
applyAction.observationHint: Keep the acceleration map and mass-layout cue visible. The slope stays the same, but the larger inertia factor makes the trip slower.

items.title: Linked speed and energy state
items.prompt: At $t = {{timeValue}}\,\mathrm{s}$, what are the current center-of-mass speed and angular speed, and how is the kinetic energy divided between translation and rotation?

steps.label: 1. Write the no-slip link
2. Read the live linear and angular speeds
3. Read the translational and rotational energy

applyAction.label: Try small fast-spin sphere
applyAction.observationHint: Keep the no-slip overlay and speed-link graph visible. The smaller radius does not change the same sphere-like acceleration curve, but it does require a larger angular speed.

miniChallenge.prompt: Can you make one roller reach the bottom sooner without changing the slope angle?
miniChallenge.prediction: Keep the incline fixed, then move from a rim-heavy shape toward a more center-loaded shape. Predict whether the travel time changes because of gravity, radius, or inertia factor.
miniChallenge.answer: Yes. Lowering the inertia factor lets more of the same downhill pull accelerate the center of mass, so the travel time shrinks even though the no-slip link still has to be satisfied.
miniChallenge.explanation: On the same incline, every setup gets the same downhill gravity component. The faster roller is the one that needs less of that pull to build rotation, so more is left for center-of-mass acceleration.

quickTest.title: Quick check
quickTest.intro: These questions test whether you can explain what sets rolling acceleration, spin rate, and energy split on the same incline.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one roller descending an incline from left to right. The interior graphic changes to represent a solid sphere, solid cylinder, hoop, or custom mass distribution, while the center marker and spoke rotate with the motion.

Optional overlays can show the no-slip link between center speed and angular speed, a mass-layout cue for the inertia factor, an energy-split bar, and an uphill static-friction arrow with its torque cue. The same live state drives the stage, readouts, and graphs.

The distance graph curves upward because the center of mass accelerates down the incline. The speed-link graph plots center speed and $r\omega$, which should overlap whenever rolling without slipping holds.

The energy graph shows translational, rotational, and total kinetic energy together. The acceleration map falls as inertia factor rises, which is why a hoop rolls more slowly than a sphere on the same incline.

sections.title: Carry rolling ideas into later mechanics

equations.meaning: This bench summarizes shape or custom mass distribution with the dimensionless factor $k = I/(mr^2)$.
On the same incline, a larger inertia factor means more of the gravitational pull is tied up in spinning the object, so the center accelerates less.
The contact point does not slide, so the linear and angular motions must stay linked.
The drop in gravitational potential energy is shared between translational and rotational kinetic energy.

variableLinks.description: Steeper slopes increase the downhill component of gravity for every shape on the same bench.
This measures how far the mass is distributed from the axis. Larger $k$ means more rotational inertia and slower center-of-mass acceleration.
Radius sets the conversion between center speed and spin rate. For the same shape and slope, it mostly changes the required angular speed rather than the center-of-mass acceleration.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the live relation $v_{\mathrm{cm}} = r\omega$ beside the rolling object.
whatToNotice: The center speed and angular speed are not independent once the object rolls without slipping.
For the same center speed, smaller radius requires larger angular speed.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps translation and rotation tied to the same physical state.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows how sphere, cylinder, hoop, or custom mass distribution changes the inertia factor $k$.
whatToNotice: The slope and total mass can stay the same while only the distribution about the axis changes.
More rim-loaded layouts mean larger inertia factor and slower center-of-mass acceleration.
overlays.whyItMatters: Different shapes roll differently because rotational inertia changes, not because gravity acts differently on them.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows how the same drop in gravitational potential energy is split between translation and rotation.
whatToNotice: All of the energy comes from the same drop in height.
Larger inertia factor keeps a larger fraction of that energy in rotation.
overlays.whyItMatters: It explains the race outcome in energy language, not just as a kinematics rule.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the uphill static-friction force and the torque it supplies at the contact point.
whatToNotice: Static friction points uphill for an object accelerating down the ramp in this no-slip model.
That same friction force supplies the torque needed to build angular acceleration.
overlays.whyItMatters: It corrects the common mistake that friction must always oppose the motion of the center of mass.

graphs.description: Distance curves upward because the center of mass accelerates as the object rolls down the incline.
The center-of-mass speed and $r\omega$ stay on top of each other whenever the no-slip condition is satisfied.
The loss of height feeds both translational and rotational kinetic energy, and the split depends on shape.
For the current slope angle, larger inertia factor means smaller center-of-mass acceleration.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice while it rolls
noticePrompts.intro: Keep one question in view at a time: what the no-slip rule locks together, and what changing the shape actually changes.

items.text: Sphere, cylinder, and hoop feel the same incline, but the one with smaller inertia factor still reaches the bottom sooner.
items.tryThis: Switch between the solid-sphere, solid-cylinder, and hoop presets without changing the slope angle.
items.whyItMatters: This isolates rotational inertia as the reason the race order changes.

items.text: On the speed-link graph, the two curves lie on top of each other because no-slip rolling ties the linear and angular motions together.
items.tryThis: Turn on the no-slip overlay, then reduce the radius and watch the same center speed require a larger angular speed.
items.whyItMatters: It shows that spin is not a separate extra story. It is locked to the translation.

items.text: The energy split changes with shape even though the same drop in height supplies the whole budget in every case.
items.tryThis: Compare the solid sphere and the hoop on the same slope. The hoop keeps a larger share of the energy in rotation.
items.whyItMatters: This explains why equal gravity does not mean equal race times.

items.text: In compare mode, changing only the inertia factor is enough to separate the finish times on the same ramp.
items.tryThis: Clone the solid-sphere preset into compare mode, then turn only Setup B into a hoop.
items.whyItMatters: It makes the key idea explicit: shape matters because it changes rotational inertia.

predictionMode.title: Predict the rolling response
predictionMode.intro: Predict what changes before you touch the controls. Then test the same prediction on the stage, readouts, and graphs.

items.prompt: If the slope angle and radius stay the same but you switch from a hoop to a solid sphere, what should change?
items.explanation: Both still obey rolling without slipping, but the sphere has smaller inertia factor, so less of the same downhill pull is used to spin it up and more is available for center-of-mass acceleration.
items.observationHint: Watch the acceleration map and distance graph after the switch. The sphere should build center speed more quickly.

items.prompt: If you keep the same solid sphere but increase the slope angle, what should change?
