items.explanation: Shape still matters through $k$, but a steeper incline makes $g\sin\theta$ larger for every setup. The no-slip link still holds, so both center speed and spin build faster.
items.observationHint: Compare the distance curve and the speed-link graph after applying the steeper sphere.

items.explanation: For the same shape, the acceleration formula is unchanged, but the no-slip link requires $\omega = v/r$. Smaller radius means larger angular speed for the same center speed.
items.observationHint: Keep the no-slip overlay visible and compare the live center speed with the larger spin rate.

paragraphs: Angular momentum is rotational motion packaged into one quantity: $L = I\omega$. Just as linear momentum uses mass and speed, angular momentum uses rotational inertia and angular speed.

On this bench, the total moving mass stays the same while you change only two things: how far the masses sit from the axis and how fast the rotor spins. That makes two key comparisons visible: keeping the same spin does not always keep the same angular momentum, and keeping the same angular momentum does not always mean keeping the same spin.

keyIdeas: Angular momentum combines rotational inertia and angular speed through $L = I\omega$. A rotor can have large angular momentum because it spins quickly, because its mass sits far from the axis, or because both happen together.
Moving the same mass outward raises $I$, so the same angular momentum can be carried with a smaller angular speed.
If external torque is negligible and $I$ decreases, $\omega$ must increase so that $L$ stays the same.

commonMisconception.myth: A faster-spinning rotor must always have more angular momentum than a slower one.
correction: Angular momentum depends on both $I$ and $\omega$. A wide, slow rotor can carry the same angular momentum as a compact, fast rotor.
Holding angular speed fixed while moving mass outward does not conserve angular momentum. It creates a new, larger $L$ because the moment of inertia increased.

workedExamples.title: Worked example from the live rotor
workedExamples.intro: Use the current rotor state as evidence. First compute the moment of inertia and angular momentum from the live radius and spin rate. Then ask what spin rate a compact reference layout would need to carry that same angular momentum.

items.title: Angular momentum of the current rotor

steps.label: 1. Build the current moment of inertia
2. Insert the live radius
3. Multiply by the live angular speed

items.resultLabel: Current moment of inertia and angular momentum

applyAction.label: Try wide, same speed
applyAction.observationHint: Keep angular speed fixed and move the masses outward. The angular-momentum map rises because the moment of inertia rises.

items.title: Compact layout carrying the same angular momentum
items.prompt: If the current angular momentum were carried by the compact reference layout, what angular speed would that layout need?

steps.label: 1. Keep the current angular momentum fixed
2. Find the compact-reference inertia
3. Solve for the matching spin rate

items.resultLabel: Compact-reference angular speed for the same angular momentum

applyAction.label: Try wide, same L
applyAction.observationHint: Use the same-L overlay and response map together. The wide layout can keep nearly the same angular momentum while spinning much more slowly.

miniChallenge.prediction: Compare the compact same-L and wide same-L presets before you answer. Predict whether a wider layout can trade larger moment of inertia for smaller angular speed.
miniChallenge.answer: Yes. If moving the mass outward increases $I$, then $\omega$ can decrease while the product $I\omega$ stays the same.
miniChallenge.explanation: Conservation of angular momentum is a product story, not a speed story. A wider layout can carry the same $L$ with less spin because its moment of inertia is larger.

quickTest.title: Quick check
quickTest.intro: These questions test whether you can reason with angular momentum as the product $I\omega$, not just use the word conservation.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a face-on rotor with six equal masses attached to spokes around a central hub. The masses can move inward or outward together, and the rotor spins at an adjustable angular speed while a curved arrow near the hub marks the direction of rotation.

Optional overlays can show the current radius, a tangential-speed arrow on one mass, equal-mass labels, and a compact reference ring that reports the angular speed needed to carry the same angular momentum there. The same live state drives the readout and the graphs.

The rotation-angle graph is linear in time because each fixed setup keeps one constant angular speed through the clip. Faster setups make steeper lines, while slower wide same-L setups sweep out less angle in the same time.

The angular-momentum map rises with radius when angular speed is held fixed because the moment of inertia increases. The same-L spin map falls with radius because keeping angular momentum fixed requires lower angular speed at larger radius.

equations.meaning: Linear momentum pairs mass with speed. Angular momentum pairs rotational inertia with angular speed.
On this bench the same moving mass stays on one ring, so sliding the masses outward changes angular momentum by changing the moment of inertia.
Angular momentum becomes larger when either the moment of inertia or the angular speed becomes larger.
If angular momentum stays fixed while moment of inertia changes, angular speed must change in the opposite way.
Rim speed helps you compare the straight-line speed of one mass with the rotor's angular speed, but it is not the same thing as angular momentum.

variableLinks.description: Moving the same masses outward raises the moment of inertia sharply, so the same angular momentum can be carried with a smaller angular speed.
Angular speed is only one part of angular momentum. Two rotors can spin at the same rate and still have different angular momentum if their mass layouts differ.

controls.description: Slide the same six equal masses inward or outward while total mass stays fixed. This changes the moment of inertia even before you change the spin rate.
Set the spin rate so you can compare same-speed changes with same-angular-momentum changes.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the current distance of the equal masses from the axis.
whatToNotice: The total mass is unchanged while the radius changes.
That changing radius is why compact and wide same-L states can need very different spin rates.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the inertia change visible instead of leaving it as an abstract variable.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the straight-line speed of one mass at the rim.
whatToNotice: Rim speed depends on both radius and angular speed.
A faster spin in angular terms does not automatically mean a larger rim speed or a larger angular momentum.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps straight-line speed from being confused with angular momentum.

whatToNotice: The rotor is not gaining extra mass when the pucks move outward.
A change in angular momentum can come from changing the moment of inertia, not only from changing angular speed.
overlays.whyItMatters: This keeps the cause tied to mass distribution instead of extra mass.

overlays.shortDescription: Adds a compact reference ring and shows the spin rate that would carry the same angular momentum there.
whatToNotice: The same angular momentum can require a very different spin rate at a different radius.
The compact reference is not a second simulation. It is the same $L$ translated to a different moment of inertia.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns conservation of angular momentum into a live comparison you can read directly.

graphs.description: At the current angular speed, moving the same masses outward increases the moment of inertia and therefore increases angular momentum.
For the current angular momentum, this curve shows how angular speed would have to change if mass radius changed while angular momentum stayed fixed.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice about angular momentum
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to separate two different stories: holding angular speed fixed, and holding angular momentum fixed.

items.text: In compare mode, two rotors can carry nearly the same angular momentum even while the compact one spins much faster.
items.tryThis: Start from the compact same-L preset, clone it, then move only Setup B outward until the angular-momentum badges still match but the angular-speed badges separate sharply.
items.whyItMatters: This is the clearest same-L comparison on the page: same angular momentum, different moment of inertia, different spin.

items.text: Holding angular speed fixed while moving the masses outward creates a larger angular momentum. That is not conservation.
items.tryThis: Apply the wide same-speed preset and compare it with the baseline. The angular speed readout stays the same, but the angular-momentum map jumps upward.
items.whyItMatters: It shows that changing radius changes the moment of inertia and therefore changes $L$ even when $\omega$ is unchanged.

items.text: On the same-L map, angular speed falls as radius grows because the same angular momentum is being carried by a larger moment of inertia.
items.whyItMatters: This is the conservation relationship in graph form.

items.text: On the angular-momentum map, a wider rotor at the same angular speed carries more angular momentum.
items.tryThis: Keep the angular speed slider fixed and drag across the momentum map. The current marker should climb as radius increases.
items.whyItMatters: It separates same-speed comparisons from same-angular-momentum comparisons.

items.text: Rim speed and angular momentum are different ideas. Both depend on radius and angular speed, but only angular momentum includes the full inertia story.
items.whyItMatters: It stops straight-line speed cues from replacing the actual $L = I\omega$ idea.

items.text: The same-L compact-reference label updates immediately from the current live state.
items.tryThis: Turn on the same-L reference overlay and move the radius slider slowly. The compact-reference speed changes right away because it is being computed from the same live angular momentum.
items.whyItMatters: It makes conservation a live comparison instead of a separate imagined example.

predictionMode.title: Predict the same-L tradeoff
predictionMode.intro: Predict whether a change is a same-speed change or a same-angular-momentum change before you apply it. Then compare the stage, readouts, and maps.

items.explanation: If $L$ stays fixed while $I$ increases, $\omega$ must decrease. A wide same-L rotor therefore spins more slowly than a compact same-L rotor.
items.observationHint: Watch the same-L response map and the angular-speed badges together. The wide same-L state should trade larger inertia for slower spin.

items.explanation: Holding $\omega$ fixed does not conserve the old angular momentum. It creates a new, larger $L$ because the wider layout has a larger moment of inertia.
items.observationHint: Watch the angular-momentum map and the same-L map together. One shows the new larger $L$, while the other shows what conserving the old $L$ would have required.

paragraphs: Momentum measures motion with direction: in one dimension, $p = mv$. A heavier cart or a faster cart has more momentum, and reversing direction changes the sign of the momentum.

Impulse is the total effect of a force acting over time. In this module, one bounded force pulse acts on one cart, so you can compare the force graph, the accumulated impulse, and the momentum graph directly. The key idea is that the same signed force-time area produces the same change in momentum.

keyIdeas: Momentum depends on mass, speed, and direction, so the same speed change does not mean the same momentum change for every cart.
Impulse is the signed area under the force-time graph. A taller, shorter pulse and a lower, longer pulse can produce the same impulse if their areas match.
For the same cart, impulse changes momentum directly through $J = \Delta p$, so the accumulated-impulse graph and the momentum change should match.

commonMisconception.myth: A bigger force must always produce a bigger momentum change, even if it acts for much less time.
correction: Momentum change depends on total impulse, which is the signed force-time area, not on force size alone. A large force over a short time can match a smaller force acting longer.
Mass changes how much the velocity changes for a given impulse, but the momentum change itself is still set by $J = F\Delta t$ for a constant pulse.

workedExamples.title: Worked example from the live pulse
workedExamples.intro: Use the current pulse as evidence. First find the cart's momentum at the instant shown. Then use the same force and pulse duration to predict the impulse and final momentum, and compare that prediction with the graphs and the stage.

steps.label: 1. Write the momentum relation
2. Substitute the live values
3. Calculate the momentum

applyAction.label: Try short hard push
applyAction.observationHint: Compare the cart's speed, the momentum bar, and the momentum graph at the same moment. They should all tell the same story.

steps.label: 1. Use the impulse-momentum relation
2. Insert the current pulse values
3. Predict the final momentum

applyAction.label: Try long gentle push
applyAction.observationHint: This pulse is lower and wider, but the final rise on the impulse and momentum graphs can still match the short hard push.

miniChallenge.prompt: Can you choose a different force-duration pair that gives the same final momentum?
miniChallenge.prediction: Make the pulse shorter and taller, or longer and gentler. Predict which new pairs keep the same signed area under the force-time graph.
miniChallenge.answer: Any force-duration pair with the same signed force-time area gives the same momentum change for the same cart.
miniChallenge.explanation: This is the impulse idea in one step. If the signed area stays the same, then the impulse stays the same, so the momentum change stays the same even when the pulse shape looks different.

quickTest.intro: Use the stage, pulse window, and graphs together. These questions test whether you can connect force, time, and momentum, not just recite a formula.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one cart moving on a fixed horizontal track while a single force pulse turns on during a shaded time window. The cart label shows mass, a horizontal arrow shows velocity, and overlays can mark the force direction, the pulse interval, and momentum bars for initial, current, and final momentum.

Changing mass, initial velocity, force, or pulse duration updates the same cart, readouts, and graphs without changing the track scale. The stage and graphs are meant to be read together: the force graph shows the pulse, the momentum graph shows the cart's momentum, and the impulse graph tracks the accumulated impulse.
The force graph shows a rectangular pulse that can point positive or negative. The momentum graph is flat before and after the pulse and changes only while the force acts.
The impulse graph and the change-in-momentum graph should overlap because they represent the same signed quantity for the same cart.

equations.meaning: Momentum combines mass with velocity, so direction matters as well as size.
For a constant force, impulse is the signed area under the force-time graph.
Impulse changes momentum directly, which is why the accumulated impulse and the momentum change should match.

variableLinks.description: Changing mass does not change the impulse from a chosen pulse, but it does change how much the cart's velocity responds.
Sets the cart's starting direction and starting momentum before the pulse begins. The same pulse shifts momentum by the same signed amount from whatever starting value you choose.
Sets the height and sign of the pulse, so it controls how quickly momentum changes while the pulse is on.
Sets how long the force acts. Increasing duration widens the force-time area and can increase impulse even if the force is smaller.

controls.description: Change the cart's mass and compare how the same impulse changes velocity less or more.
Set the cart's starting direction and starting momentum before the pulse begins.
Set the height and sign of the pulse. Positive force pushes right; negative force pushes left.
Set how long the force stays on. A longer pulse can produce more impulse even if the force is smaller.

presets.description: A tall, narrow pulse with the same impulse as the long gentle push.
A lower, wider pulse with the same impulse as the short hard push.
The same pulse on a heavier cart, so the momentum change is the same but the speed change is smaller.
A leftward pulse that reduces the cart's rightward momentum and can even reverse it.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the direction and size of the force while the pulse is active.
whatToNotice: The arrow matters only during the shaded pulse window. Before and after it, the cart coasts.
Reversing the arrow changes the sign of both the impulse and the momentum change.
overlays.whyItMatters: Impulse is signed, so direction matters as much as size.

overlays.shortDescription: Shades the time interval when the force is on.
whatToNotice: The shaded region marks time, not position on the track.
A wider pulse can give the same impulse as a taller one if the signed area matches.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you read impulse as force multiplied by time.

overlays.shortDescription: Compares the initial, current, and final momentum on one signed scale.
whatToNotice: The current bar changes only while the force is on, then it stays fixed.
The gap from $p_i$ to $p_f$ matches the total accumulated impulse.
overlays.whyItMatters: The bars make $J = \Delta p$ visible on the same stage as the motion.

graphs.description: Shows the sign, height, and duration of the pulse, so the area under the graph represents impulse.
Shows momentum staying constant before and after the pulse and changing only while the force acts.
Shows accumulated impulse alongside change in momentum so you can compare the two directly.

noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts while you change one control at a time. Match what happens on the track to the force, impulse, and momentum graphs.

items.text: Notice that the signed area of the force pulse is the same quantity as the momentum change.
items.tryThis: Watch the force pulse and accumulated-impulse graph together, then compare the final shift on the momentum graph.
items.whyItMatters: This is the impulse-momentum theorem in one connected visual loop.

items.text: Notice that momentum changes only while the force pulse is actually on.
items.tryThis: Pause before the pulse, during it, and after it. The momentum graph should be flat, changing, then flat again.
items.whyItMatters: It separates motion that continues from momentum that is currently changing.

items.text: Notice that a lower wider pulse can end at the same final momentum as a taller narrower pulse.
items.tryThis: Compare the short hard push with the long gentle push. The force graphs look different even when the final momentum matches.
items.whyItMatters: It breaks the mistake that only force size matters.

items.text: Notice that increasing pulse duration widens the force-time area even when the force height stays fixed.
items.tryThis: Lengthen the pulse without changing the force. The graph gets wider and the final momentum shifts farther.
items.whyItMatters: Impulse depends on time as directly as it depends on force.

items.text: Notice that mass changes the velocity response more than the momentum change for the same impulse.
items.tryThis: Keep the pulse the same and compare a light cart with a heavy cart. The momentum change matches, but the heavy cart's speed changes less.
items.whyItMatters: It helps separate $\Delta p$ from $\Delta v$, which students often merge.

items.text: Notice that the impulse graph stops rising exactly when the force pulse ends.
items.tryThis: Scrub near the end of the pulse and compare the time cursor with the shaded pulse window.
items.whyItMatters: Accumulated impulse is a running total, not a force that keeps acting.

predictionMode.title: Predict the pulse
predictionMode.intro: Predict what the pulse will do before you run it. Then check the stage against the force, impulse, and momentum graphs.

items.explanation: Both presets have the same signed force-time area, so they give the same impulse and the same momentum change to the same cart, even though the pulse shapes differ.
items.observationHint: Compare the end of the impulse graph, the momentum graph, and the final momentum bar after the long gentle pulse.

items.prompt: If mass increases while the same short hard pulse acts, what should change on the stage and graphs?
items.explanation: The force-time area is unchanged, so the impulse and momentum change are unchanged. A heavier cart turns the same $\Delta p$ into a smaller $\Delta v$.
items.observationHint: Watch the momentum graph rise by the same amount while the cart's speed changes less.

paragraphs: Conservation of momentum is about the total momentum of a system, not about each object keeping its own momentum. If no external impulse acts on the two-cart system, the sum of the carts' momenta stays constant even while they push or pull on one another internally.

In this module, two carts interact on one track during one internal-force window. Change the masses, system drift, force, or interaction time, then compare the stage with the force graph, momentum graph, and center-of-mass marker. The cart momenta can redistribute, but the system total and center-of-mass motion should stay consistent.

keyIdeas: Momentum conservation applies to the whole isolated system. On the momentum graph, that means the total line stays flat even when the cart lines change.
Internal forces come in equal-and-opposite pairs, so they give the carts equal-and-opposite momentum changes without changing the system total.
Equal momentum changes do not imply equal speed changes. The lighter cart usually changes speed more because $p = mv$.

commonMisconception.myth: If two carts push on each other, one of them can win more of the system momentum by pushing harder or by having more mass.
correction: Inside an isolated system, the force pair is equal and opposite, so the momentum changes are equal and opposite too. The system total does not have a winner.
Mass changes the velocity response, not whether one cart gets to keep the total. A heavier cart can change speed less even while taking part in the same momentum exchange.

workedExamples.title: Worked example from the live system
workedExamples.intro: Use the live stage and graphs as evidence. First compute the system total momentum at the instant shown. Then use the current interaction to predict the final momentum split and compare that prediction with the momentum bars, force graph, and center-of-mass motion.

steps.label: 1. Write the system total
2. Substitute the live momenta
3. Add them

applyAction.label: Try moving system
applyAction.observationHint: The total-momentum line stays flat and the center of mass keeps the same drift even while the carts separate.

items.title: Final momenta after the interaction
items.prompt: For the current internal interaction, what final momentum should each cart have?

steps.label: 1. Write the equal-and-opposite momentum changes
2. Apply them to the initial momenta
3. Compute the final split

applyAction.label: Try long gentle split
applyAction.observationHint: Compare this with the short strong split. The force-time shape changes, but the final momentum split can stay the same.

miniChallenge.prompt: Can you make cart A move left after the interaction while the system total momentum is still positive?
miniChallenge.prediction: Give the whole system a rightward drift, then increase the internal push. Predict whether cart B can keep enough rightward momentum for the total to stay positive.
miniChallenge.answer: Yes. Cart A can end up moving left while the system total momentum remains positive.
miniChallenge.explanation: Momentum conservation constrains the sum of the two cart momenta, not the direction of each cart separately. Cart B can keep enough rightward momentum that the total stays positive while cart A reverses.

quickTest.intro: Use the force pair, momentum graph, and center-of-mass motion together. These questions test how an isolated system behaves, not just whether you know the vocabulary.

equations.meaning: One cart's momentum depends on both its mass and its velocity.
Add the cart momenta to get the system total. In this one-dimensional lab, the sign shows direction.
An internal interaction moves momentum from one cart to the other by equal and opposite amounts.
With zero net external impulse, the system total momentum is the same before and after the interaction.

variableLinks.description: Changing A's mass changes how much its speed changes for the same momentum exchange.
Changing B's mass changes its speed response, even though the equal-and-opposite momentum rule stays the same.
Sets the initial drift of the whole system, so it changes the total momentum and center-of-mass motion before the internal push.
Sets how quickly momentum is transferred between the carts during the interaction.
Sets how long the force pair acts, so together with force it sets the momentum transferred.

controls.description: Change A's mass and compare how the same momentum change appears on the velocity graph.
Change B's mass and compare its velocity response with A's.
Give the whole isolated system a leftward or rightward drift before the internal push begins.
Set the size and direction of the internal force pair. Positive values push A left and B right; negative values reverse that.
Set how long the internal force pair acts during the interaction window.

presets.description: A short, strong internal push from zero total momentum.
A longer, weaker push that gives the same total momentum transfer.
A symmetric case that starts with zero total momentum.
Cart B is much heavier, so the same momentum change produces a smaller speed change for B.
The whole isolated system drifts right before the carts start exchanging momentum.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks which two carts belong to the isolated system.
whatToNotice: The conservation statement applies to both carts together, not to one cart at a time.
Changing what happens inside the boundary redistributes momentum internally, but it does not create an external impulse.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the word system concrete when you read total momentum.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the equal-and-opposite internal forces during the interaction window.
whatToNotice: The arrows appear together with the same size and opposite directions.
On the force graph, one force line is the negative of the other.
overlays.whyItMatters: This is the local reason the momentum changes cancel in the system total.

overlays.shortDescription: Tracks the center of mass as the carts exchange momentum.
whatToNotice: The center of mass keeps one steady drift even while the individual carts speed up or slow down.
If the total momentum is zero, the center of mass stays fixed even though the carts can move apart.
overlays.whyItMatters: It gives a visual check that the net external force on the system is zero.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows cart A, cart B, and total momentum on one signed scale.
whatToNotice: The cart bars change in opposite directions while the total bar stays fixed.
Different masses can turn the same momentum change into different speed changes.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the system total visible while you compare what happens to the two carts.

graphs.description: Shows the equal-and-opposite internal force pair and the zero external-force baseline.
Shows opposite changes in the cart momenta while the total momentum stays constant.
Shows how the same momentum exchange can produce different speed changes, while the center-of-mass speed stays constant.

noticePrompts.intro: Use these short prompts while you change one control at a time. Match what you see on the stage to the graph or overlay that explains it.

items.text: Notice that the total-momentum line stays flat while the individual momentum lines tilt in opposite directions.
items.tryThis: Scrub through the interaction window and compare the moving $p_A$ and $p_B$ lines with the flat total line.
items.whyItMatters: This is the graph form of system-level momentum conservation.

items.text: Notice that the force graph shows an equal-and-opposite pair, not one force overpowering the other.
items.tryThis: Watch the force arrows on the stage and the force graph together. One line should mirror the other at every moment.
items.whyItMatters: Those equal-and-opposite internal forces are why the system total momentum does not change.

items.text: Notice that making one cart heavier changes the velocity split more than the momentum split.
items.tryThis: Increase only cart B's mass. The momentum bars still change by equal and opposite amounts, but B's velocity changes less.
items.whyItMatters: Students often mix up momentum change with speed change. The heavier cart usually gets the smaller speed change.

items.text: Notice that a moving system can keep positive total momentum even while one cart ends up moving left.
items.tryThis: Give the whole system a rightward drift, then strengthen the internal push until cart A reverses.
items.whyItMatters: Conservation constrains the sum. It does not require every object to move in the same direction.

items.text: Notice that the center-of-mass marker keeps a steady drift even while the carts speed up and slow down around it.
items.tryThis: Leave the center-of-mass overlay on while you watch the carts separate. The CM marker should move at one steady speed.
items.whyItMatters: The center of mass gives another compact check that the system has no net external force.

items.text: Notice that a shorter, stronger interaction and a longer, gentler interaction can produce the same final momentum split.
items.tryThis: Compare the short strong split with the long gentle split. The force graph shapes differ even when the final momentum bars match.
items.whyItMatters: This links the force-time interaction to momentum transfer without losing sight of system conservation.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the system total, the momentum split, and the center-of-mass motion before you run the setup. Then test the prediction on the stage and graphs.

paragraphs: In a collision, each cart can change velocity very quickly, so the motion may look as if it changes all at once. But if the two-cart system is isolated, the total momentum cannot jump at contact even when the individual cart velocities do.

Here you change only mass, incoming speed, and elasticity, then watch the same collision on the track and on the linked velocity, momentum, and energy graphs. That makes it easier to separate three ideas: momentum of the whole system, how the motion is split between the carts, and whether kinetic energy is preserved or lost.

keyIdeas: Read momentum conservation on the momentum graph: the cart momentum lines jump at contact, but the total-momentum line stays flat.
Elasticity controls the relative speed after contact. Lower elasticity means less separation speed and more kinetic energy lost.
Mass changes how the same momentum transfer appears as a velocity change, so a heavier cart usually changes speed less.

commonMisconception.myth: If momentum is conserved, then the collision should keep the same total kinetic energy and produce basically the same rebound every time.
correction: Momentum conservation fixes the system total, not the total kinetic energy. In an inelastic collision, the total momentum can stay the same while the total kinetic energy decreases.
Changing mass or elasticity can produce very different outcomes. The carts might rebound, move off together, or one cart might reverse direction, all while total momentum is still conserved.

workedExamples.title: Worked example from the live setup
workedExamples.intro: Use the current setup as evidence. First find the system's total momentum at the instant shown on the stage. Then use the same masses, incoming speeds, and elasticity to predict the velocities just after contact and compare that result with the stage and graphs.

items.title: Total momentum at this instant
items.prompt: At $t = {{timeValue}}\,\mathrm{s}$, what is the total momentum of the two-cart system?

steps.label: 1. Write the system-momentum relation
2. Insert the live masses and velocities
3. Evaluate the sum

applyAction.label: Try moving target
applyAction.observationHint: Both carts are already moving, but the total-momentum graph still stays flat through contact.

items.title: Velocities just after contact
items.prompt: For the current masses, incoming speeds, and elasticity, what should each cart's velocity be immediately after contact?

steps.label: 1. Use momentum conservation and restitution
2. Insert the current values
3. Solve for the two final velocities

applyAction.label: Try sticky collision
applyAction.observationHint: In the sticky case, both carts leave with one shared velocity. The momentum total stays flat, but the energy graph drops at contact.

miniChallenge.prompt: Can you make cart A rebound to the left while the system's total momentum still points to the right?
miniChallenge.prediction: Try a light cart A, a heavier cart B, and high elasticity. Predict whether B can carry enough rightward momentum after contact to keep the total positive even though A reverses.
miniChallenge.answer: Yes. Cart A can rebound left if cart B leaves with enough rightward momentum for the system total to remain positive.
miniChallenge.explanation: Momentum conservation constrains the sum, not the direction of each cart separately. A light cart can reverse while a heavier cart carries most of the rightward momentum after the collision.

quickTest.title: Quick check
quickTest.intro: Use the stage, overlays, and graphs together. These questions test whether you can explain the collision, not just name a rule.

questions.prompt: For this isolated two-cart system, which quantity must be the same before and after the collision?
questions.explanation: Momentum conservation applies to the whole isolated system. Total kinetic energy stays the same only in elastic collisions, and the individual cart velocities can change a lot.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: Total kinetic energy can decrease in an inelastic collision even while total momentum stays fixed.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: A collision changes the individual cart velocities at contact; conservation does not mean each cart keeps the same velocity.

showMeAction.label: Run the sticky collision
showMeAction.observationHint: Even when the carts stick and move together, the total-momentum line stays flat.

questions.prompt: If the total-momentum line stays flat but the total-energy line drops at contact, what kind of collision is it?
questions.explanation: That pattern means momentum is conserved but some kinetic energy is lost, so the collision is inelastic.

showMeAction.label: Run the energy-drop case
showMeAction.observationHint: Line up the contact instant with the flat momentum total and the downward step in energy.

questions.explanation: A heavier cart usually changes speed less because the same momentum transfer produces a smaller velocity change.

showMeAction.label: Run light into heavy
showMeAction.observationHint: The heavier cart does move, but its velocity changes much less than the lighter cart's.

questions.explanation: Changing elasticity changes the rebound and the energy loss, but it does not change the total momentum of the isolated two-cart system.

showMeAction.label: Run the elastic case
showMeAction.observationHint: Now compare it with the sticky case to see that momentum stays the same while the final relative speed and energy do not.

paragraphs: The simulation shows two carts moving along one fixed horizontal track toward a single collision point. Each cart has a mass label and a horizontal velocity arrow, and optional overlays can mark the collision zone, center of mass, momentum bars, and the relative speed before and after contact.

Changing mass, incoming speed, or elasticity updates the same stage, readouts, and linked graphs without changing the track scale. When elasticity is zero, the carts leave together; with higher elasticity, they separate more strongly after contact.

The velocity graph shows each cart's velocity changing at contact and includes a steady center-of-mass velocity line. The momentum graph shows the cart momentum lines changing while the total momentum line stays flat.
The energy graph shows whether total kinetic energy is preserved or drops at contact, which is the main visual difference here between elastic and inelastic collisions.

equations.meaning: This gives one cart's momentum from its mass and velocity.
If no external horizontal force acts on the two-cart system, the total momentum before contact must equal the total momentum after contact.
This compares the separation speed after contact with the closing speed before contact.
Use kinetic energy to distinguish elastic and inelastic collisions. Total kinetic energy stays the same only in an elastic collision.

variableLinks.description: Changing A's mass changes how strongly a given momentum exchange changes A's velocity at contact.
Changing B's mass changes how much B's velocity responds even though the same system momentum rule still applies.
Sets how fast cart A approaches from the left and how much it contributes to the initial system momentum.
Sets how fast cart B approaches from the right and how much it contributes to the initial system momentum.
Sets how much of the closing speed is recovered as separation speed after contact. Lower values mean less bounce and more kinetic-energy loss.

controls.description: Increase or decrease A's mass and watch how the same collision changes A's velocity jump.
Change B's mass and compare how its post-collision speed changes on the velocity and momentum views.
Set how fast cart A approaches the contact point from the left.
Set how fast cart B approaches from the right. Zero means B starts at rest.
Lower values make the carts separate less after contact. Zero means perfectly inelastic; one means perfectly elastic.

presets.description: A classic elastic case where motion transfers from A to B.
A perfectly inelastic case where the carts move together after contact.
A light cart A hits a heavy cart B and is more likely to rebound.
A heavy cart A keeps moving forward while a lighter cart B speeds away.
Both carts are already moving, so you can test momentum conservation without a stationary target.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the position and instant of contact so you can line up the stage with the graph jumps.
whatToNotice: The jump in velocity and momentum happens at one instant, exactly when the carts meet.
Use this marker to align the stage with the step or jump you see on the graphs.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you connect the physical contact on the track to the sudden changes on the velocity, momentum, and energy graphs.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the system's center of mass as the carts approach, collide, and separate.
whatToNotice: The center of mass keeps one steady drift through the collision even when the carts' individual velocities jump.
When the total momentum is close to zero, the center of mass stays nearly fixed.
overlays.whyItMatters: A steady center-of-mass motion is a compact visual check that the system total momentum has not changed.

overlays.shortDescription: Compares the closing speed before contact with the separation speed after contact.
whatToNotice: Changing elasticity does not alter the closing speed before impact in the same setup, but it does change the separation speed after impact.
At elasticity zero, the separation speed becomes zero because the carts leave together.
overlays.whyItMatters: This makes the restitution equation visible as a before-and-after speed comparison instead of just a symbol.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows cart A, cart B, and total momentum on one shared scale.
whatToNotice: The two cart bars can change sharply at contact while the total bar stays fixed.
Changing mass changes how the system momentum is split between the carts, even when the total is unchanged.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the system total visible while you compare the two individual outcomes.

graphs.description: Shows each cart's velocity changing at contact and the steady center-of-mass speed that reflects the system total momentum.
Shows momentum being exchanged between the carts while the system total stays constant.
Shows whether total kinetic energy is preserved or reduced at the collision instant.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice in the collision
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts while you change one control at a time. Each one should help you connect what the carts do on the track to what the graphs and overlays show.

items.text: Notice that the total-momentum line stays flat while the individual cart momentum lines jump at contact.
items.tryThis: Pause just before and just after contact, or scrub across it. The cart lines jump, but the total line should stay level.
items.whyItMatters: This is the clearest graph sign that momentum conservation applies to the whole system, not to each cart separately.

items.text: Notice that lowering elasticity can make the energy graph step downward at contact even while the total-momentum line stays flat.
items.tryThis: Lower elasticity, then compare the energy and momentum graphs at the same contact instant.
items.whyItMatters: It separates two ideas students often merge: momentum can be conserved even when kinetic energy is not.

items.text: Notice that a heavier cart changes speed less, even though the same collision still obeys the momentum rule.
items.tryThis: Make cart B much heavier, then compare the size of B's velocity jump with A's.
items.whyItMatters: Momentum change and velocity change are not the same thing. Mass controls how much a given momentum transfer changes speed.

items.text: Notice that elasticity changes the separation speed after contact, not the closing speed before contact.
items.tryThis: Turn on the relative-speed overlay, then lower elasticity from 1 toward 0 and watch only the after-collision value change.
items.whyItMatters: Elasticity is a ratio of before-and-after relative speeds, so the overlay helps you read what the equation means.

items.text: Notice that the center-of-mass marker keeps one smooth drift even though the individual cart velocities jump at contact.
items.tryThis: Scrub through the collision with the center-of-mass overlay on. The marker should keep the same smooth motion.
items.whyItMatters: A smooth center-of-mass motion is another sign that the system total momentum stays constant.

items.text: Notice that two setups can have the same total momentum but very different total kinetic energy after contact.
items.tryThis: Compare the elastic equal-mass case with the sticky collision. The momentum totals match, but the energy graphs do not.
items.whyItMatters: Compare mode helps separate what momentum conservation guarantees from what elasticity changes.

predictionMode.title: Predict before you test
predictionMode.intro: Make the prediction first, then test it on the stage and graphs. Use momentum conservation together with mass and elasticity rather than guessing from memory.

items.prompt: Equal-mass carts collide elastically and cart B starts at rest. What should happen immediately after contact?

choices.label: Cart A stops, and cart B leaves with A's original speed.
Both carts move right together with the same speed.
The total momentum becomes zero because one cart started at rest.

items.explanation: With equal masses in an elastic collision, the moving cart transfers its speed to the cart that was at rest. The total momentum stays the same throughout.
items.observationHint: Watch the velocity graph right after contact: A drops to zero while B rises to A's original speed.

items.prompt: If you keep the same equal-mass setup but set elasticity to zero, what should happen after contact?
items.explanation: In a perfectly inelastic collision, the carts share one final velocity. Momentum is still conserved, but total kinetic energy decreases.
items.observationHint: Watch the carts leave together and compare the flat momentum total with the step down in total energy.

items.prompt: A light cart A hits a much heavier cart B in a fairly elastic collision. What outcome is most likely?
items.explanation: A heavier cart needs more momentum change to get the same speed change, so B usually speeds up only a little while the lighter cart can rebound.
items.observationHint: Compare the size and direction of the two post-collision velocity changes.

paragraphs: After launch, the projectile is no longer being pushed in the ideal model. Gravity acts only downward, so the motion splits into two simpler parts: constant horizontal velocity and vertical motion with constant downward acceleration.

The stage and graphs are designed to show that same split from several angles. The trajectory is the combined result, while the component and velocity graphs show why the arc forms: the horizontal part stays steady, the vertical part slows on the way up, reaches zero at the apex, then grows downward on the way down.

keyIdeas: Treat projectile motion as two linked motions: horizontal motion with constant velocity and vertical motion with constant downward acceleration.
At the apex, the vertical velocity is zero for an instant, but the horizontal velocity is still nonzero, so the projectile keeps moving forward.
For equal launch and landing height, complementary launch angles give the same range, and 45° gives the maximum range for a fixed speed.

commonMisconception.myth: A steeper launch angle must always give a longer range.
correction: Range depends on the tradeoff between horizontal speed and time in the air, not on launch angle alone.

For equal launch and landing height, a very steep shot stays aloft longer but gives up horizontal speed, so the range can shrink again. The maximum range happens at 45°, and complementary angles share the same range.

workedExamples.title: Worked example from the live launch
workedExamples.intro: Use the current launch as evidence. First compute the range from the same speed, angle, and gravity used by the stage. Then freeze any instant in flight and read the velocity components from the same state shown on the graphs.

items.title: Range predicted for this launch
items.prompt: For the current launch settings, what landing range does the equal-height formula predict?

steps.label: 1. Use the equal-height range formula
2. Insert the current launch values
3. Evaluate the range

applyAction.label: Try Earth shot
applyAction.observationHint: Compare the computed value with the landing marker on the stage. This formula applies when the launch and landing heights are the same.

items.title: Velocity components at this instant
items.prompt: At $t = {{timeValue}}\,\mathrm{s}$, what are the projectile's horizontal and vertical velocity components?

steps.label: 1. Use the component formulas
2. Insert the live values
3. Evaluate each component

applyAction.label: Try shallow arc
applyAction.observationHint: The horizontal component stays constant, while the vertical component decreases linearly, reaches zero at the apex, and then becomes negative.

miniChallenge.prompt: If launch speed stays fixed and you lower the launch angle, what happens to the arc and to the time in the air?
miniChallenge.prediction: Will the path get flatter or taller? Will the projectile stay aloft longer or for less time?
miniChallenge.answer: The arc gets flatter and the flight time decreases.
miniChallenge.explanation: Lowering the angle shifts more of the launch speed into the horizontal component and less into the vertical component. With less upward speed, gravity brings the projectile back down sooner.

quickTest.intro: Use the trajectory and component graphs together. These questions test whether you can explain the flight, not just quote one formula.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a projectile launched from ground level and followed through the air under gravity alone. Optional overlays can show the instantaneous velocity vector, its horizontal and vertical components, the apex, and the landing range.
Changing speed, launch angle, or gravity reshapes the same flight path and updates the same component graphs. The stage and graphs are synchronized views of one launch.
The trajectory graph shows the curved flight path from launch to landing.
The component graphs show horizontal position increasing steadily while vertical position rises and then falls. The velocity graph shows constant $v_x$ and linearly changing $v_y$.

equations.meaning: With no horizontal acceleration in the ideal model, horizontal position changes linearly in time.
Vertical position combines the upward launch part with the downward pull of gravity.
This gives the time to return to the launch height in the ideal equal-height case.
This gives the landing distance for the ideal equal-height case. At fixed speed, complementary launch angles share the same range.

variableLinks.description: Increases both the horizontal and vertical launch components, so the arc can go higher and farther.
Redistributes the same launch speed between sideways motion and upward motion, which changes both the shape of the arc and the time in the air.
Changes only the downward acceleration after launch, so the vertical motion bends more or less sharply while the initial horizontal component is unchanged.

controls.description: Sets the size of the initial velocity vector, so both launch components start larger.
Chooses how the same launch speed is split between horizontal and vertical components.
Sets the downward acceleration after launch. The horizontal part of the motion stays uniform in this ideal model.

presets.description: Reference case on Earth-like gravity with a clear, nearly symmetric arc.
More of the launch speed is horizontal, so the arc is flatter and the flight time is shorter.
The same launch on much lower gravity stays in the air longer and travels farther.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the projectile's instantaneous velocity direction and size.
whatToNotice: The arrow is always tangent to the trajectory.
It tilts downward because gravity changes the vertical component during flight.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links the moving projectile to the component and velocity graphs.

overlays.shortDescription: Separates the velocity into horizontal and vertical parts.
whatToNotice: The horizontal component stays the same in the ideal model.
The vertical component shrinks to zero at the apex and then points downward.
overlays.whyItMatters: It shows exactly why the path curves even though one component stays constant.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the highest point of the flight.
whatToNotice: At the apex, the vertical velocity is zero but the horizontal velocity is still nonzero.
overlays.whyItMatters: It identifies the instant when upward motion ends but forward motion continues.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the horizontal landing distance from the launch point.
whatToNotice: The range depends on both airtime and horizontal speed, not on angle alone.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the landing distance tied to the full component story.

graphs.description: Shows the full arc produced by combining constant horizontal velocity with downward vertical acceleration.
Shows horizontal position increasing steadily while vertical position rises and then falls.
Shows $v_x$ staying constant while $v_y$ decreases linearly, crosses zero at the apex, and becomes negative on the way down.

noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to connect the launch, the vectors, and the graphs. The most useful question is usually either 'which component changed?' or 'which component stayed the same?'

items.text: Horizontal and vertical motion follow different rules even though they belong to the same launch.
items.tryThis: Keep the same launch speed, then compare the steady horizontal motion with the changing vertical motion as gravity bends the path.
items.whyItMatters: Projectile motion becomes easier once you separate the two components.

items.text: The trajectory graph is the same flight shown with horizontal distance and height axes.
items.tryThis: Hover or scrub the trajectory graph and watch the projectile move to the matching point on the stage.
items.whyItMatters: It turns the graph into another view of the same motion, not a separate calculation.

items.text: Changing launch angle redistributes the same launch speed between upward height and forward reach.
items.tryThis: Raise Launch angle and compare how the apex rises while the landing marker stops improving once the shot gets too steep.
items.whyItMatters: It guards against the idea that steeper always means farther.

items.text: The projectile is highest when the vertical velocity has dropped to zero, not when the whole velocity is zero.
items.tryThis: Pause and scrub near the top of the arc, then compare the apex marker with the vertical velocity on the graph.
items.whyItMatters: The apex is about the vertical component only. Forward motion continues.

items.text: On the velocity graph, $v_x$ stays flat while $v_y$ slopes downward and crosses zero at the apex.
items.tryThis: Switch to Velocity components and watch the horizontal line stay flat while the vertical line slopes downward.
items.whyItMatters: It shows exactly why the path curves even though one component is uniform.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the component tradeoff before you touch the launch, then test it on the stage and graphs.

items.explanation: Increasing angle puts more of the same launch speed upward and less sideways. The arc rises higher, but beyond a point the range shrinks because the horizontal component becomes too small.
items.observationHint: The apex rises while the landing marker eventually stops moving outward.

items.explanation: Increasing launch speed makes both the horizontal and vertical components larger, so the projectile goes higher and farther when the angle stays fixed.
items.observationHint: The arc stretches both upward and outward.

paragraphs: A gravitational field tells you the force per kilogram that a tiny test mass would feel at a point. The field exists because of the source mass, whether or not you place a probe there, so field comes before force.

In this lab one source mass sits at the origin, so every live field arrow points inward toward it. Moving the probe changes distance and direction, changing the source mass changes the field itself, and changing the probe mass changes only the force response through F = m_test g. That keeps the stage, graphs, and equations tied to one live model.

keyIdeas: The source mass creates the field. At one chosen point, the probe mass does not change the field direction or strength.
For one source mass, the field points toward the source and its magnitude follows the inverse-square rule |g| = GM/r^2, so doubling distance gives one quarter of the field.
The probe mass turns the existing field into force through F = m_test g, so changing only the probe mass rescales force without rewriting the field.

commonMisconception.myth: A heavier probe makes the gravitational field at that point stronger.
correction: At a point, the gravitational field is set by the source mass and the source-to-probe distance, not by the probe mass.
Making the probe heavier increases the force because $\vec{F} = m_{	ext{test}}\vec{g}$, but the field arrow and the field graph change only if you move the probe or change the source.

workedExamples.title: Worked example from the live field
workedExamples.intro: Use the current source mass, probe position, and probe mass as evidence. First calculate the field vector from the live distance and inward direction. Then use that same field to calculate the force on the chosen probe mass, keeping the field-versus-force distinction explicit.

items.title: Field at the current probe position

steps.label: 1. Find the source-to-probe distance
2. Substitute into the one-source field rule
3. Resolve the field components

applyAction.label: Try double distance on-axis
applyAction.observationHint: Keep the probe on the horizontal axis and compare 1 m with 2 m. At 2 m, the field should be about one quarter of the 1 m value.

items.title: Force on the current probe
items.prompt: For the current probe mass, what force follows from the live gravitational field at that point?

steps.label: 1. Start from the field-to-force rule
2. Substitute the current probe mass and field
3. Compute the force components

applyAction.label: Try heavier probe
applyAction.observationHint: The coral force arrow and force curve grow, but the teal field arrow and field curve stay fixed because only the probe mass changed.

miniChallenge.prompt: Start from Axis near, then move the probe to twice the distance on the same horizontal line. Before you drag, predict what happens to the field magnitude and the force if the probe mass stays fixed.
miniChallenge.prediction: State the ratio first: if distance doubles, should the field become half, one quarter, or stay the same? Then say whether the fixed-mass force follows the same ratio.
miniChallenge.answer: At twice the distance on the same radial line, the field magnitude and the force both drop to about one quarter of their original values.
miniChallenge.explanation: For one source mass, |g| ∝ 1/r^2. Doubling r makes the denominator four times larger, so the field drops to one quarter. With the same probe mass, |F| = m_test |g| drops by the same factor; changing probe mass would change force, not the field.

quickTest.title: Quick check
quickTest.intro: Answer from field logic, not from isolated formulas. Each question asks what the stage and graphs must mean about inward field direction, inverse-square scaling, or force on the probe mass.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one source mass fixed at the origin, a movable probe mass in a bounded two-dimensional region, and optional overlays for a coarse field grid, the live field arrow at the probe, the force arrow on the probe, equal-distance rings, and the horizontal scan line used by the graphs.

Dragging the probe changes the sampled field location directly on the stage. The same location is used by the field readout, the force readout, and the graph cursor, so the stage and graphs always describe the same point.

Very near the source, the display uses a minimum sampling radius so the drawn arrows and graphs stay finite and readable. This keeps the visualization bounded while preserving the correct trend that field strength rises sharply near the source.

The field-components graph plots the horizontal and vertical gravitational field components along the current horizontal scan line. Hovering or scrubbing the graph previews the same x-location on the stage.

The strength-response graph plots field magnitude and probe-force magnitude along that same scan line. Changing probe mass rescales only the force curve, while changing source mass or distance changes the field curve itself.

equations.meaning: This source-to-probe distance sets the field strength in the one-source model.
The minus sign shows that the field points inward toward the source mass.
notes: In this bounded lab, $G = 1$ is built into the displayed units so you can focus on direction and scaling.

equations.meaning: At fixed source mass, doubling distance reduces the field to one quarter.
Probe mass converts the field into force without changing the field itself.

variableLinks.description: Changes the mass creating the field, so both the field and the probe force scale directly with $M$ at every point.
Moves the probe left or right on the stage and on the sampled graph slice, so you can read the same field on both.
Moves the probe to a different horizontal scan line, changing both the field direction and the graph slice.
Changes only the force on the probe. It does not rewrite the field created by the source mass.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows sample field arrows across the stage.
whatToNotice: Every arrow points inward toward the source, but the arrows shrink quickly as the distance grows.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes field direction a whole-region pattern instead of something you infer from only one probe point.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the live field arrow and the radial line from the source to the probe.
whatToNotice: At every point, the teal arrow points back along the line toward the source mass.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties the stage direction directly to the component graph and the field equation.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the force on the chosen probe mass using the same live field.
whatToNotice: Changing only the probe mass rescales the coral force arrow while the teal field arrow stays fixed.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the field itself from the response of the object placed in that field.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks equal-distance circles around the source for inverse-square comparisons.
whatToNotice: The 1 m, 2 m, and 3 m rings make it easier to compare how quickly $|\vec{g}|$ falls as $r$ grows.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns inverse-square reasoning into a geometric comparison you can see before you calculate.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the horizontal slice sampled by both graphs.
whatToNotice: Changing the probe's y-position changes the entire graph because the graph is now sampling a different slice of the field.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the graph-to-stage link honest instead of making the graphs feel detached.

graphs.description: Shows how the inward field splits into x- and y-components along the current horizontal slice.
Separates field strength from force on the chosen probe, so source-mass and probe-mass changes do not get mixed up.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice in the field
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to connect the stage to the graphs. Keep three ideas separate: the source mass creates the field, distance sets the inverse-square size, and probe mass turns the field into force.

items.text: Field direction comes first: the gravitational field always points toward the source mass.
items.tryThis: Drag the probe around the source and keep the teal field arrow visible. The direction should keep rotating inward toward the center.
items.whyItMatters: Direction is part of the field, not an afterthought. Read the geometry before the magnitude.

items.text: On the same radial line, moving from 1 m to 2 m cuts $|\vec{g}|$ to about one quarter.
items.tryThis: Switch between Axis near and Double distance while keeping the rings and strength graph visible.
items.whyItMatters: It turns inverse-square into a visual distance comparison.

items.text: Changing only the probe mass moves the force curve and force arrow, not the field curve or field arrow.
items.whyItMatters: It separates the source-generated field from the response of the chosen object.

items.text: Changing source mass scales the whole field pattern while every arrow still points inward.
items.tryThis: Keep the probe fixed and raise the source mass from 2 kg to 4 kg. Both the field and force magnitudes should double at that point.
items.whyItMatters: It separates the source-strength idea from the distance idea before you combine them.

items.text: Compare mode is strongest when one setup changes the source mass and the other changes the probe mass. One changes the field and force, while the other changes only the force.
items.whyItMatters: It is the fastest honest contrast between cause of a field and response to a field.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the gravitational response before you apply the change. Each scenario isolates one cause: source mass changes the field, distance changes the field by inverse-square scaling, and probe mass changes only force.

paragraphs: Gravitational potential φ tells you the gravitational potential energy per kilogram at a point, measured relative to zero at infinity. For one source mass in this bounded lab, φ is negative everywhere, becomes more negative near the source, and rises back toward zero as the probe moves farther away.

This concept uses the same one-source geometry as Gravitational Fields, so the well map, contour circles, live readout, and scan graphs all describe the same state. That lets you compare three linked ideas without switching models: φ is the well depth, U = m_test φ is the probe's potential energy, and the field points downhill on the same potential landscape. Along the current scan line, the horizontal field component matches the negative slope of the potential graph.

keyIdeas: Gravitational potential φ is a scalar set by source mass and distance. It tells you how deep the well is at a point, not which way the force points.
With zero defined at infinity, one isolated source mass makes φ negative everywhere in this lab, and the well gets deeper as r gets smaller.
Potential energy uses U = m_test φ, so changing only the probe mass rescales U, while the field comes from how φ changes with position.

commonMisconception.myth: A negative gravitational potential means gravity must be pointing outward or “the wrong way.”
correction: The negative sign comes from the reference choice φ = 0 at infinity. Closer to the source, the point sits lower in the gravitational well, so φ is negative.
The field still points inward because gravity follows the downhill direction of the potential landscape. A more negative potential means a deeper well, not an outward force.

workedExamples.title: Worked example from the live well
workedExamples.intro: Use the current source mass, probe position, and probe mass as evidence. First calculate φ from the live distance to the source. Then use that same φ to find the probe's potential energy and compare the result with the well map and the field-slope graph.

steps.label: 1. Find the live distance
2. Substitute into φ = -M/r
3. Read the potential

applyAction.label: Try axis at 2 m
applyAction.observationHint: On the same axis, 2 m gives about half the potential magnitude of 1 m, but only about one quarter of the field magnitude.

steps.label: 1. Use U = m_test φ
2. Insert the current probe mass and potential
3. Read the potential energy

applyAction.label: Try heavier probe
applyAction.observationHint: Changing only the probe mass rescales U and the force, but the potential map and the field-link graph stay the same.

miniChallenge.prediction: State the ratios first: which quantities should halve in magnitude, and which should fall to one quarter?
miniChallenge.answer: At twice the distance, φ and U are about half as negative as before, while |g| drops to about one quarter of its original value.
miniChallenge.explanation: For one source mass, φ ∝ -1/r while |g| ∝ 1/r². So at twice the distance the well is only half as deep, but the field is four times weaker. With the same probe mass, U follows φ and the force follows |g|.

quickTest.title: Quick check
quickTest.intro: Answer from the well map, contours, and scan graphs together. These questions test whether you can separate potential, field, and potential energy.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one source mass fixed at the origin, a movable positive probe in a bounded two-dimensional gravity well, and optional overlays for the potential map, equal-potential contour circles, equal-distance rings, the local field arrow, the force arrow on the probe, and the horizontal scan line used by the graphs.

Dragging the probe changes the sampled location directly on the stage. The same location is used by the readout and the graph cursor, so the map, the arrows, and the graphs always describe the same point.

Very near the source mass, the display uses a minimum sampling radius so the well depth, arrows, and graphs stay finite and readable. This keeps the visualization bounded while preserving the correct trend that potential becomes more negative and the field grows rapidly near the source.

The potential-energy-scan graph plots gravitational potential and the chosen probe's potential energy along the current horizontal scan line. Hovering the graph previews the same x-location on the stage.

The field-link graph plots the horizontal gravitational field component and the matching negative slope of the potential graph along that same scan line. Changing the probe mass rescales the energy curve, but the field-link graph remains a source-mass and geometry readout.

equations.meaning: This distance sets both the well depth and the field strength.
With zero defined at infinity, the potential from one source mass is negative and becomes more negative as r gets smaller.
Probe mass turns the source-set potential into potential energy without changing the potential itself.
The field points downhill on the potential landscape, from higher φ toward lower φ.
On the current horizontal slice, the x-component of the field is the negative slope of φ(x).
Potential changes like 1/r, but field strength changes faster like 1/r².

variableLinks.description: Deepens or shallows the whole well, so φ, U, and |g| all scale from the same source mass at a fixed point.
Moves the probe left or right along the current scan line, so the stage and graphs sample the same x-location.
Moves the probe to a different horizontal slice of the same well, changing both the readout and the graphs.
Changes only the probe response, so U and the force scale while φ and g stay fixed at the same point.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows φ as a gravity well: deeper near the source and shallower farther away.
whatToNotice: Farther from the source, φ rises toward zero.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the sign and depth of potential visible on the stage instead of leaving φ as an isolated number.

whatToNotice: The field arrow crosses the contour circles instead of running along them.
overlays.whyItMatters: Contours help you read potential as one scalar landscape and the field as its downhill direction.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the local gravitational field as the downhill direction on the potential landscape.
whatToNotice: The field arrow always points toward lower φ and therefore toward the source mass.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps field and potential tied to the same live probe state.
It separates the source-generated well from the response of the chosen probe.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the horizontal slice sampled by both graphs.

graphs.description: Shows how φ and the chosen probe's potential energy change along the current horizontal slice.
Shows that g_x matches the negative slope of the potential graph along the same slice.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice in the potential well
noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time. Each one connects the map, the contour circles, and the scan graphs for the same live probe position.

items.text: Moving the probe inward makes φ more negative quickly. The well becomes much deeper near the source.
items.tryThis: Start on Axis 2 m and drag inward to Axis 1 m. Watch the readout, map, and scan curve deepen together.
items.whyItMatters: It helps you read potential as well depth before mixing in force or energy.

items.text: Farther from the source, φ rises toward zero while staying negative because this model defines zero at infinity.
items.whyItMatters: It explains the negative sign as a reference choice, not as a change of force direction.

items.text: On this horizontal slice, the g_x curve matches the negative slope of φ(x).
items.tryThis: Find a place where φ increases as x increases. There the slope is positive, so g_x must be negative.
items.whyItMatters: This is the bridge from scalar potential to vector field.

items.text: Changing only m_test leaves φ and g alone but rescales U and the force on the probe immediately.
items.whyItMatters: It cleanly separates the source-generated well from the response of one chosen object placed in that well.

items.text: Compare mode is most revealing when one setup doubles the distance and the other doubles the probe mass. One changes φ and g; the other changes only U and F.
items.whyItMatters: It makes the difference between geometry changes and probe-response changes visible without leaving the same compact model.

predictionMode.title: Predict the well and the response
predictionMode.intro: Predict what changes before you move the sliders. Each scenario tests one cause at a time: source mass, distance, or probe mass.

items.explanation: For one source mass, φ scales like -1/r, so doubling r halves the well depth. With the same probe mass, U halves too. But |g| follows 1/r², so it falls to one quarter.
items.observationHint: Use the distance rings and compare the potential scan with the field-link graph together.

items.explanation: Source mass sets the scale of the whole one-mass model. Doubling M doubles φ, U, and |g| in magnitude at the same point while keeping the same negative-sign and inward-direction meanings.
items.observationHint: The map deepens, the readout becomes more negative, and the field arrow grows without changing its inward direction.

items.explanation: The source mass and distance define φ and g. Changing only m_test multiplies the existing potential to make U and multiplies the existing field to make force, but it does not rewrite the source-generated well.
items.observationHint: The potential map and field-link graph stay fixed while the U curve and force readout grow.

paragraphs: Pressure tells you how much force is applied to each unit area. If the same force is spread over a larger area, the pressure is smaller. The piston at the fluid surface lets you see that $P_s = F/A$ idea directly before depth is added.

A resting fluid transmits pressure through the connected fluid, and at one point that pressure acts equally in all directions. The probe arrows are not only downward. The fluid above explains why pressure increases with depth, but the pressure at a point is still the same in every direction at that point.

Depth adds hydrostatic pressure because deeper points have more fluid above them. On this page, the probe reads the surface pressure from the piston plus the depth-dependent part $ho g h$. That gives one clear chain to follow: change force or area to change $F/A$, change depth, density, or gravity to change the hydrostatic part, then read how both pieces combine in the graphs and the probe.

keyIdeas: Surface pressure from the piston is $P_s = F/A$, so the same force gives less pressure when it is spread over a larger area.
In a resting connected fluid, pressure at one point acts equally in all directions.
At the same depth in the same resting fluid, the hydrostatic pressure is the same.
Hydrostatic pressure increases linearly with depth because each extra meter adds the same pressure increment.
Density and gravity set the slope of the pressure-depth graph, so denser fluids or stronger gravity make pressure rise faster with depth.

commonMisconception.myth: Fluid pressure only pushes downward because the fluid above the point has weight.
correction: The fluid above a point explains why pressure increases with depth, but the pressure at that point acts equally in all directions.
That is why points at the same depth share the same hydrostatic pressure, and why buoyancy later comes from deeper parts of an object having larger pressure than shallower parts.

workedExamples.title: Pressure worked examples
workedExamples.intro: Use the current tank values. First separate the probe reading into surface pressure and hydrostatic pressure, then see how an extra depth change affects the total.

items.title: Find the current probe pressure
items.prompt: For the current tank with $F = {{forceValue}}$, $A = {{areaValue}}$, $ho = {{densityValue}}$, $g = {{gravityValue}}$, and $h = {{depthValue}}$, what surface pressure, hydrostatic pressure, and total probe pressure should you expect?

steps.label: 1. Convert the piston force into surface pressure with $P_s = F/A$
2. Calculate the extra pressure from depth with $P_h = ho g h$
3. Add the two parts to get the probe reading

applyAction.label: Load Deep probe
applyAction.observationHint: The piston load stays the same, but the deeper probe makes the hydrostatic part larger, so the arrows and total reading increase.

items.title: Move the probe deeper
items.prompt: If the same probe were moved {{extraDepthValue}} deeper while the piston load and fluid stayed the same, how much extra pressure would it gain?

steps.label: 1. Read the pressure increase per meter for this fluid and gravity
2. Multiply that gradient by the extra depth
3. Add that increase to the original probe reading

applyAction.label: Open pressure-depth graph
applyAction.observationHint: The graph keeps the surface-pressure line flat while the hydrostatic and total lines rise linearly with depth.

miniChallenge.prompt: Two pressure probes sit at the same depth in the same resting fluid, but one is near the wall and the other is near the center. Which one reads higher?
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide whether sideways position changes the reading when depth, fluid, gravity, and surface loading are all the same.
miniChallenge.explanation: They read the same pressure. In one connected resting fluid, the hydrostatic part depends on depth, density, and gravity, not on sideways position.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the tank and graphs, not from memorised slogans.

questions.explanation: Pressure from the surface load is $F/A$. With the same force, a larger area gives a smaller surface pressure.

showMeAction.label: Load Wide piston
showMeAction.observationHint: The piston widens, the surface-pressure reading drops, and the area graph falls.

questions.explanation: In one connected resting fluid, the same depth means the same hydrostatic pressure.

showMeAction.label: Show same-depth line
showMeAction.observationHint: The horizontal line marks points that share the same hydrostatic part.

questions.explanation: Density appears in the hydrostatic term. A larger density means each meter of depth adds more pressure.

showMeAction.label: Load Dense brine
showMeAction.observationHint: The hydrostatic and total curves rise while the surface-pressure line stays flat.

questions.explanation: Pressure acts in all directions at each point, but deeper points have larger pressure. That pressure difference from top to bottom is what leads to buoyancy.

showMeAction.label: Load Deep probe
showMeAction.observationHint: At the deeper point, the arrows are larger in every direction, not just upward or downward.

questions.explanation: That graph changes only density, so it is the cleanest check of how density changes the hydrostatic and total pressure.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a piston pressing on a fluid surface and a pressure probe at an adjustable depth. The piston width represents area, the downward arrow represents surface force, the depth marker shows how far the probe is below the surface, and equal arrows around the probe show pressure acting equally in all directions at that point.
