The readout card separates surface pressure, hydrostatic pressure, and total probe pressure. Compare mode overlays a second setup on the same tank so you can compare different ways of producing similar total pressures.
Each graph changes one variable at a time in a static fluid: depth, density, force, or piston area. The other variables stay fixed so the cause of each pressure change is easier to read.
The pressure-depth graph is the main hydrostatic graph. The surface-pressure line stays flat, while the hydrostatic and total pressures rise linearly with depth.

The other graphs isolate the remaining causes. Density changes only the hydrostatic part, force changes only the surface-pressure part, and area changes surface pressure by spreading the same force over more or less area.

equations.meaning: Deeper points have more fluid above them, so hydrostatic pressure increases with depth.
The probe reads the surface pressure from the piston plus the extra pressure from depth.
If density and gravity stay fixed, each extra depth step adds the same pressure increase.

variableLinks.description: Changes the push on the piston. At fixed area, it changes only the surface-pressure part of the probe reading.
Spreads the same force over a wider or narrower piston. A larger area gives a smaller surface pressure for the same force.
Sets how much pressure each meter of depth adds. Denser fluids make the pressure-depth graph steeper.
Changes how quickly pressure increases with depth without changing the surface-pressure part $F/A$.
Moves the probe deeper or shallower in the same fluid. The hydrostatic part grows linearly with depth.

whatToNotice: Making the piston wider spreads the same force over more area, so the surface pressure falls.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps pressure tied to force per area instead of making it feel like a mysterious extra quantity.

whatToNotice: At one point in a resting fluid, the arrows stay equal because pressure acts equally in all directions.
overlays.whyItMatters: It prevents the common mistake of treating hydrostatic pressure as only a downward push.

whatToNotice: Points on the same horizontal line have the same hydrostatic pressure in one connected resting fluid.
overlays.whyItMatters: It sets up buoyancy by showing that deeper points feel more pressure than shallower ones.

graphs.description: Change only the probe depth. The surface-pressure line stays flat, while the hydrostatic and total pressures rise linearly.
Change only fluid density. The surface-pressure line stays fixed, while the hydrostatic and total pressures increase with density.
Change only piston force. The hydrostatic part stays fixed, while the surface and total pressures rise together.
Change only piston area. Spreading the same force over more area lowers the surface and total pressures.

noticePrompts.intro: Ask one question at a time: what comes from $F/A$, what comes from depth, and what stays unchanged?

items.text: Move the probe deeper. The surface pressure from $F/A$ stays the same, but the total probe pressure rises because the hydrostatic part $ho g h$ is larger.
Turn on the same-depth line. A point near the wall and a point near the middle have the same hydrostatic pressure if they are at the same depth in one connected fluid.
Increase the piston area while keeping the same force. The piston gets wider, the surface pressure falls, and the total pressure curve drops on the area graph.
Density and gravity do not change the surface-pressure line. They change the slope of the hydrostatic and total depth graphs because they change how much pressure each extra meter adds.
Use compare mode to match the same total pressure with a smaller surface-pressure part but a denser fluid. The same total pressure can come from different combinations of causes.
items.tryThis: Start from Water baseline, open compare mode, leave Setup A unchanged, and tune Setup B until the total pressures match while Setup B uses a smaller $F/A$ and a larger hydrostatic part.

predictionMode.intro: Predict which part changes first: the surface-pressure part, the hydrostatic part, or both.

items.explanation: Moving the probe deeper changes only the $ho g h$ part. The piston force and area have not changed, so the surface-pressure term stays fixed.
items.observationHint: The depth guide grows, the pressure arrows lengthen, and only the hydrostatic and total curves move on the depth graph.

items.explanation: With the same force spread over a larger area, $F/A$ becomes smaller. The hydrostatic part stays the same, so the total pressure falls by the same amount.
items.observationHint: The piston gets wider, the surface-pressure readout drops, and the area graph slopes downward.

items.explanation: Density appears in the hydrostatic term on this page. A denser fluid makes pressure increase faster with depth without changing $F/A$ at the surface.
items.observationHint: The density changes, but the surface-pressure line stays put while the hydrostatic and total curves rise together.

items.explanation: Gravity changes the hydrostatic gradient $ho g$. The surface-pressure part depends only on force and area, so it stays fixed.
items.observationHint: The pressure arrows shorten and the hydrostatic part decreases even though the piston load has not changed.

challengeMode.intro: Use the same tank to hit pressure targets and compare different ways of making the same total pressure.

items.title: Reach 24 kPa by changing depth only
items.prompt: Start from Water baseline and adjust only the probe depth until the total pressure is about 24 kPa while force, area, density, and gravity stay near baseline.
items.successMessage: You reached the target by changing depth only. The surface pressure stayed fixed while the hydrostatic part increased linearly.

setup.note: Keep the surface-loading controls and fluid properties near baseline. Move only the probe depth.

hints: Read the flat surface-pressure line first, then use the total-pressure line to estimate the needed depth.
If force or area changes, you are no longer isolating the hydrostatic part.

items.title: Match the total with less surface pressure
items.prompt: Start from Water baseline, switch to compare mode, leave Setup A unchanged, and tune only Setup B until it reaches the same total pressure with a smaller surface-pressure part and a denser fluid.
items.successMessage: You matched the same total pressure with different causes. Setup B used less surface pressure and more hydrostatic pressure.

setup.note: Keep Setup A fixed as the baseline. Edit only Setup B.

hints: Keep Setup B at about the same depth first, then lower its surface-pressure part and let density make up the difference.
Match the total pressure before worrying about matching the separate parts.

paragraphs: Imagine tracking one short chunk of fluid as it passes two cross sections of a pipe. In steady incompressible flow, fluid is not piling up or disappearing between those sections, so the same volume must pass each cross section each second. That shared volume flow rate is written as $Q = Av$.

A narrow section does not create more flow each second. It gives the same flow less area to move through, so the speed there has to increase. A wider section does the opposite: the same $Q$ can pass at a lower speed.

Use the stage, speed arrows, same-time slices, and graphs as one connected explanation. When the area changes, the speed adjusts, while $Q$ stays matched across the pipe. This is the continuity part of fluid flow, before pressure enters later with Bernoulli.

keyIdeas: In steady incompressible flow, the same volume per second passes every cross section, so $Q = A_A v_A = A_B v_B$.
A smaller area means the fluid must move faster there to keep the same $Q$.
A larger area means the same $Q$ can pass with a lower speed.
The stage and graphs should agree on the same story: area changes cause the speed change, not a change in how much fluid per second is flowing.
Continuity gives the speed-area link that Bernoulli later combines with pressure changes.

commonMisconception.myth: A narrow section must carry more fluid each second because the fluid shoots through it faster.
correction: In steady incompressible flow, one section of the pipe does not carry more volume per second than another.
The narrow section is faster because the same flow rate is passing through less area. Faster flow there does not mean a larger $Q$.

workedExamples.title: Continuity worked examples
workedExamples.intro: Use the live pipe values. Each example starts from the current $Q$ and areas, then links the calculation to what you see on the stage.

items.title: Find the current speeds in sections A and B
items.prompt: Using the current values $Q = {{flowRateValue}}\,\mathrm{m^3/s}$, $A_A = {{entryAreaValue}}\,\mathrm{m^2}$, and $A_B = {{middleAreaValue}}\,\mathrm{m^2}$, what speeds should continuity predict at sections A and B?

steps.label: 1. Calculate the speed at section A with $v = Q/A$
2. Use the same $Q$ to calculate the speed at section B
3. Compare the two speeds and connect them to the pipe shape

applyAction.label: Load Tight throat
applyAction.observationHint: Section B narrows, its speed arrow lengthens, and the graph shows the larger middle speed.

items.title: Compare equal-time fluid slices
items.prompt: For the current stream, how far does a {{sliceDurationValue}} fluid slice travel in sections A and B?

steps.label: 1. Turn section A speed into a distance for the same short time
2. Do the same for section B
3. Interpret why the two distances can differ while $Q$ stays the same

applyAction.label: Load Wide chamber
applyAction.observationHint: In the wider middle section, the equal-time slice covers less distance because that section is slower.

miniChallenge.prompt: A steady incompressible stream enters a section whose area becomes half as large as before. What must happen to the speed there if $Q$ stays fixed?
miniChallenge.prediction: Choose whether the speed stays the same, doubles, or halves, then test it on the pipe.
miniChallenge.answer: The speed doubles.
miniChallenge.explanation: Continuity says $Q = Av$. If $Q$ stays fixed and the area is halved, the speed must double so the same volume still passes each second.

quickTest.intro: Answer from what the pipe, overlays, and graphs show, not from a memorized slogan.

questions.explanation: With the same $Q$, continuity gives $v = Q/A$. Halving the area makes the speed twice as large.

showMeAction.label: Load Tight throat
showMeAction.observationHint: Section B shrinks and its speed arrow grows clearly.

questions.explanation: It shows that continuity keeps $Q$ the same at both sections. Area and speed can change, but the volume per second stays matched.

showMeAction.label: Open flow-balance graph
showMeAction.observationHint: The two $Q$ lines sit on top of each other while the speed arrows can still differ.

questions.prompt: Which result from this page gives the speed-area link that Bernoulli later uses?
questions.explanation: Bernoulli needs continuity first: changing area changes speed in a specific way while the same steady flow rate is conserved.

showMeAction.label: Compare narrow and wide section B
showMeAction.observationHint: In compare mode, keep the same $Q$ and change section B to watch the speed difference appear.

variableLinks.description: Changes how much volume passes through the pipe each second. Increasing $Q$ raises both section speeds, but it does not change which section is faster.
Changes section A. At the same $Q$, making section A larger lowers $v_A$ because the same flow is spread across more area.
Changes section B. At the same $Q$, making section B smaller raises $v_B$, while making it larger lowers $v_B$.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the two cross sections whose areas appear in the continuity equation.
whatToNotice: When section B is smaller than section A, section B must have the larger speed to keep the same $Q$.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties the equation to the visible pipe geometry.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the speeds at sections A and B directly on the pipe.
whatToNotice: The smaller-area section gets the longer speed arrow when $Q$ stays fixed.
overlays.whyItMatters: It lets you see the speed change before reading the graph or calculation.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows that the same $Q$ applies at both sections.
whatToNotice: Both sections keep the same $Q$ even when their areas and speeds are different.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates conservation of flow rate from changes in speed.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows how far equal-time fluid slices extend in each section.
whatToNotice: Over the same short time, the faster section covers more length.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns volume flow rate into a visible same-time comparison.

graphs.description: Change section A only. Read how $v_A$ changes while section B and $Q$ stay fixed.
Change section B only. Read the inverse area-speed link in the middle section while section A and $Q$ stay fixed.
Change $Q$ only. Both section speeds rise together because both must carry more volume each second.
The two lines stay matched because continuity says both sections carry the same steady incompressible flow rate.

noticePrompts.intro: Change one thing at a time so the pipe shape, arrows, readouts, and graphs all tell the same continuity story.

items.text: Make section B smaller. The matched $Q$ labels stay the same, but the section B speed arrow grows because the same flow must pass through less area.
Make section B wider than section A. The section B speed drops because the same $Q$ is spread across more area there.

Open the flow-balance graph. A flat matched line does not mean the pipe variables stopped mattering. It means the same volume per second is still passing both sections, while the speed changes show up elsewhere.
Use compare mode to keep the same $Q$ but make section B much smaller in one setup. That speed difference is the part continuity gives to Bernoulli later.
items.tryThis: Start from Baseline stream. In compare mode, leave Setup A unchanged and shrink section B in Setup B until the speed contrast is obvious while the two $Q$ labels still match.

predictionMode.intro: Choose the speed change first, then run the setup. Focus on which quantity stays fixed and which one must adjust.

items.prompt: Starting from Baseline stream, you make section B smaller while keeping section A and $Q$ fixed. What should happen?
items.explanation: Continuity keeps the same $Q$ through the pipe. With less area at section B, the fluid there must move faster.
items.observationHint: Section B narrows, its speed arrow grows, and the flow-balance graph still keeps the two $Q$ lines matched.

items.prompt: Starting from Baseline stream, you raise the volume flow rate $Q$ while keeping both areas fixed. What changes next?
items.explanation: With both areas fixed, each section speed is proportional to $Q$. The smaller-area section remains the faster one because the area ratio did not change.
items.observationHint: Both speed arrows grow, and the speed-flow-rate graph shifts both speeds upward together.

items.prompt: Starting from Baseline stream, you make section A wider while keeping section B and $Q$ fixed. What happens next?
items.explanation: Section A follows $v_A = Q/A_A$, so a larger entry area lowers the entry speed. Section B keeps the same speed because its area and $Q$ did not change.
items.observationHint: The section A arrow shortens while the section B arrow stays almost unchanged.

challengeMode.intro: Use the live pipe to hit continuity targets. The checks read the actual areas, speeds, and flow-rate values on the bench.

items.title: Make section B about twice as fast
items.prompt: Start from Uniform pipe. Adjust only section B until the middle speed is about twice section A's while the baseline flow rate stays unchanged.
items.successMessage: You hit the continuity target. A smaller section B made the middle speed about twice section A's without changing the flow rate.

setup.note: Leave the baseline flow rate and section A nearly unchanged. Tune only section B.

items.title: Same flow, slower wider middle section
items.prompt: Start from Baseline stream, switch to compare mode, leave Setup A unchanged, and widen section B in Setup B until it keeps the same flow rate but runs slower there.
items.successMessage: You built a same-flow comparison. Both setups carry the same $Q$, but the wider middle section in Setup B moves more slowly.

setup.note: Keep Setup A as the baseline narrow middle section. Edit only Setup B.

paragraphs: Track the same fluid from section A to throat B. In this ideal steady incompressible-flow model, no pumps or viscosity are adding or removing energy along that streamline, so pressure, speed, and height trade with one another. That is the idea behind $P + 	frac{1}{2}ho v^2 + ho g y = 	ext{constant}$.

Continuity still comes first. The same volume flow rate must pass section A and throat B, so a narrower throat has a larger speed. Bernoulli then tells you what that larger speed and any extra height do to the static pressure.

This does not mean fast flow always gives low pressure in every fluid situation. On this page, lower throat pressure happens for a specific reason: in the same ideal-flow budget, more of the total is tied up in speed and, if the throat is raised, height, so less is left as static pressure.

keyIdeas: Along one steady incompressible streamline in this ideal-flow bench, $P + 	frac{1}{2}ho v^2 + ho g y$ stays nearly constant from section A to throat B.
Continuity sets the speed first: if the throat area is smaller, the throat speed must be larger for the same $Q$.
A faster throat means more of the same total sits in the kinetic term, so the throat's static pressure can be lower.
If the throat is also higher, the height term takes another share of the same total, so the throat pressure can drop further.
Continuity and Bernoulli do different jobs together: continuity links area to speed, and Bernoulli links speed and height to pressure.

commonMisconception.myth: Bernoulli means fast flow automatically creates low pressure everywhere.
correction: On this page, the lower throat pressure comes from one specific model: steady incompressible flow along the same streamline, with pumps and viscosity ignored.
The pipe geometry and continuity set the speed change first. Bernoulli then explains how that speed change, together with height, changes the static pressure.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one steady pipe with section A at the entry and throat B farther along. Pipe thickness represents cross-sectional area, tracer dots show the flow, and speed arrows show which section is moving faster.
Pressure gauges show the static pressure at section A and throat B. Energy bars split each state into pressure, kinetic, and height terms so the trade between them stays visible.
Compare mode overlays a second pipe state on the same bench so you can compare two Bernoulli setups without switching to a different page.
The speed-throat-area graph isolates the continuity speed change that Bernoulli builds on.
The pressure graphs show how throat width, flow rate, and throat height change the throat pressure within the same Bernoulli model.

equations.meaning: Along one ideal steady streamline, static pressure, kinetic term, and height term share the same total.
A faster or higher throat can have lower static pressure because more of the same total is tied up in speed or height.

variableLinks.description: Sets the starting static pressure at section A. Raising it lifts the throat pressure too, but the speed and height trade still decide how much lower $P_B$ is than $P_A$.

Changes how much fluid volume passes through the pipe each second. Raising $Q$ increases both speeds and usually lowers the throat pressure because the kinetic term grows most where the flow is fastest.
Changes section A. It sets the entry speed that Bernoulli compares with the throat state.
Changes the throat width. A smaller throat makes the fluid move faster there and usually lowers the throat pressure, while a wider throat slows the throat flow and can recover pressure.
Raises or lowers the throat relative to section A. A higher throat uses more of the same Bernoulli total in the height term, so the throat pressure can drop even if the speeds stay the same.

overlays.whyItMatters: It reminds you that Bernoulli is responding to a continuity-defined speed change, not an arbitrary one.
It shows the continuity part of the story before you interpret the pressure change.

whatToNotice: The throat gauge can be lower because some of the same total is showing up as speed and height instead of static pressure.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the pressure trade visible on the same pipe.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows how pressure, kinetic, and height terms share the same total at section A and throat B.
whatToNotice: The throat can gain kinetic and height share while losing static pressure share, with the total staying nearly the same.
overlays.whyItMatters: It shows Bernoulli as a trade between terms, not as a memorized slogan.

graphs.description: Change throat area while keeping the entry state fixed. Read the continuity speed change first.
As the throat gets narrower, the throat pressure drops because more of the same total sits in the kinetic term there.
Increase $Q$ with the same geometry. The faster throat usually means a larger pressure drop.
Raise the throat with the same speeds and areas. The throat pressure falls linearly because the height term grows with $\Delta y$.

noticePrompts.intro: Change one thing at a time so the arrows, gauges, energy bars, and graphs all tell the same pressure-speed-height story.

items.text: Make the throat smaller. The same $Q$ stays attached to both sections, the throat speed arrow grows, and the throat pressure gauge falls because more of the total is tied up in the kinetic term there.
Raise the throat while keeping the areas the same. The speed picture barely changes, but the throat gauge drops further because the height term now takes a larger share.
Open the pressure-flow-rate graph. As $Q$ rises, both sections move faster, but the faster throat loses more static pressure, so the pressure drop grows.

Use compare mode to keep the same entry pressure and flow rate, then widen only setup B's throat. Continuity explains why the throat speed drops in B, and Bernoulli explains why the throat pressure recovers there.

paragraphs: Buoyancy comes from a pressure difference, not from a separate mysterious upward force. In a fluid, pressure is larger at deeper points, so the bottom of an immersed object is pushed more strongly than the top. This page deliberately reuses the hydrostatic-pressure idea so buoyancy grows out of the same fluid-statics story.

Archimedes' principle packages that pressure-difference story into one compact rule: the buoyant force equals the weight of the displaced fluid. On this bench, the displaced-fluid column, the buoyant-force arrow, and the graphs all come from the same submerged volume, so the equation stays tied to something the learner can see.

Floating and sinking depend on density balance, not mass by itself. A large object can still float if its average density is below the fluid density, because both weight and buoyant force scale with volume. Once the whole block is submerged, moving it deeper raises the pressure everywhere on it but does not change the pressure difference between top and bottom, so the buoyant force stays the same.

keyIdeas: Archimedes' principle says the buoyant force equals the weight of the displaced fluid, so displaced volume is the key quantity to track.
While the block is only partly submerged, pushing it deeper increases displaced volume and therefore increases the buoyant force.
A freely floating block settles where weight and buoyant force balance, so the required submerged fraction is the density ratio $\rho_{obj}/\rho_f$.
If $\rho_{obj} > \rho_f$, even full submersion cannot provide enough buoyant force, so the block sinks.
Once the whole block is submerged in one uniform fluid, moving it deeper raises both the top and bottom pressures together but does not change their difference, so the buoyant force stays the same.

commonMisconception.myth: Heavy objects sink because buoyant force depends only on the object's mass.
correction: Buoyant force depends on fluid density, gravity, and displaced volume, not on the object's mass alone.
A heavy object can still float if it has enough volume to keep its average density below the fluid density.

paragraphs: This page follows one object released from rest through one fluid. The downward weight stays fixed at $mg$, while the upward drag force starts near zero and grows with speed according to $F_d = kAv^2$. That changing force balance is why the fall does not behave like constant-acceleration free fall.

Terminal velocity is the speed where drag has grown enough to balance the weight. The object does not stop there. It keeps moving downward, but once drag and weight are nearly equal, the net force is near zero, so the speed stops increasing much.

Each control changes a different part of that balance. More mass increases the weight, so a higher speed is needed before drag can match it. More area or a larger drag-strength constant makes drag stronger at the same speed, so the object reaches balance sooner and at a lower terminal speed.

keyIdeas: Drag starts small because the object starts with little speed, then grows rapidly as the fall speeds up.
Weight stays constant while drag changes, so the net force is largest early and much smaller near terminal speed.
Terminal velocity means drag and weight are balancing, so acceleration becomes nearly zero.
At terminal velocity the object is still moving downward; what fades is the change in speed, not the motion itself.
A larger mass raises terminal speed, while a larger area or stronger drag lowers it.

commonMisconception.myth: Terminal velocity happens because gravity turns off after the object has been falling for a while.
correction: Gravity is still acting; the weight force stays essentially constant in this model.
The changing part is drag. It grows with speed until it nearly matches weight, leaving very little net downward force.
