paragraphs: The simulation shows one object falling through one fluid column with a distance ruler on the left and a readout card on the right. The object width changes with area, while mass is represented through the force readouts rather than by resizing the object. Optional overlays show a constant downward weight arrow, an upward drag arrow that grows with speed, a net-force arrow, a terminal-speed cue, and a distance guide.
The time rail controls one bounded fall from rest. Compare mode can ghost a second setup behind the current one so two force-balance stories stay on the same scale.
The readout card reports mass, area, drag strength, distance fallen, current speed, terminal speed, drag force, and net downward force.
The speed-history graph is the main motion graph. The speed rises quickly and then bends toward the constant terminal-speed line.
The force-balance graph keeps the forces explicit: weight stays flat, drag rises, and the net downward force falls toward zero.
The response graphs isolate mass, area, and drag strength. The mass sweep rises, while the area and drag-strength sweeps fall because stronger drag-side factors lower terminal speed.

sections.title: Connect drag to force balance next

pageIntro.definition: Drop one object through a fluid, then change its mass, area, and drag strength to see why the fall settles into a steady terminal speed.
pageIntro.keyTakeaway: Terminal velocity happens when upward drag has grown enough to balance weight, so acceleration fades while the object keeps moving downward.

equations.meaning: In this bounded model, drag gets much larger as speed increases.
Terminal speed is the speed where upward drag has grown enough to match the downward weight.
A larger mass raises terminal speed, while a larger area or stronger drag lowers it.

variableLinks.description: Raises the constant weight. With the same area and drag strength, the object must reach a higher speed before drag can balance it.
Sets how much drag the object gets at the same speed. A larger area lowers the speed needed for drag to catch the weight.
Packages how strongly this fluid-and-shape model resists motion. A larger $k$ makes drag stronger at every speed and lowers terminal speed.

controls.description: Changes the object's mass and therefore the constant weight that drag must eventually balance.
Changes how much drag the object gets at the same speed.
Sets how strongly this bounded model resists motion through the fluid.

presets.description: A larger area makes drag catch up sooner, so terminal speed is lower.
Stronger drag lowers the eventual fall speed without changing the mass.
Doubling both mass and area keeps the same terminal-speed ratio when drag strength stays fixed.
Large area and strong drag produce a low terminal speed quickly.

whatToNotice: At first the weight arrow is much larger than the drag arrow, but the drag arrow grows with speed until the two nearly match.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes terminal velocity a force-balance idea instead of a memorized speed limit.

whatToNotice: Near terminal speed the object is still moving downward. What becomes small is the net force and acceleration.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates constant speed from zero motion.

whatToNotice: After the speed curve flattens, the object still keeps covering more distance.
overlays.whyItMatters: It prevents the mistake of confusing constant speed with stopping.

graphs.description: The speed rises quickly at first and then bends toward the flat terminal-speed line as drag catches up to weight.
Weight stays flat, drag rises with speed, and the net downward force falls toward zero.
Heavier objects need a higher speed before drag can balance the larger weight.
More area means more drag at the same speed, so terminal speed falls.
A larger drag-strength constant lowers the speed needed for force balance.

noticePrompts.intro: Keep one force question in view at a time so the stage, time rail, and graphs all tell the same fall story.

items.text: Right after release, drag is still small because the speed is still small. The weight arrow is already there, so the net downward force starts large.
Near terminal speed, the object is still moving downward even though the net force is tiny. Force balance makes the acceleration nearly vanish; it does not erase the speed that is already there.
items.whyItMatters: This is the key difference between terminal velocity and coming to rest.

items.text: On the mass sweep, larger mass raises terminal speed because the weight is larger while the drag law itself is unchanged.
On the area sweep, a larger frontal area lowers terminal speed because the same speed now creates a larger drag force.
Use compare mode to match the same terminal speed with two different setups. Equal terminal speed does not mean mass, area, and drag strength are all the same. It means the ratio $mg/(kA)$ matches.
items.tryThis: Start from Baseline drop, copy it into compare mode, then turn Setup B into Matched ratio. The terminal-speed labels match even though Setup B is both heavier and larger.

predictionMode.intro: Predict which side of the force balance changes before you run the new setup.

items.explanation: Area appears directly in the drag law $F_d = kAv^2$, so a larger area makes drag stronger at every speed and lowers terminal speed.
items.observationHint: The drag line catches the weight sooner, and the terminal-speed-area graph drops as area grows.

items.explanation: Mass changes the weight side of the balance, so a heavier object reaches a higher terminal speed even though the drag law itself is unchanged.
items.observationHint: The speed-history curve approaches a higher flat terminal-speed value when you switch to the heavier setup.

items.explanation: A larger $k$ makes drag stronger for the same $A$ and $v$, so the balance with weight happens at a lower speed.
items.observationHint: The terminal-speed-drag-strength graph slopes downward, and the force-balance graph reaches near-equality earlier.

challengeMode.intro: Use the falling-body bench to prove two terminal-velocity claims: tune a slower fall by changing the drag side of the balance, then pause at the moment drag and weight nearly match.

items.title: Build a slower terminal-speed case
items.prompt: Starting from Baseline drop, keep the mass near $2\,\mathrm{kg}$ and lower the terminal speed by increasing both area and drag strength.
items.successMessage: You lowered the terminal speed by strengthening the drag side of the balance. The object still falls, but it settles into a slower steady-speed regime.

setup.note: Use the terminal-speed graphs and keep the mass close to baseline.

hints: Keep mass near baseline, so the speed change must come from the drag side of the balance.
A larger area and a stronger drag constant both lower terminal speed; combine them instead of moving only one control.

targets.label: Keep the mass near $2.0\,\mathrm{kg}$.
Raise the area to about $0.10\,\mathrm{m^2}$.
Raise the drag strength to about $18$.
Bring the terminal speed into the $3.2$ to $3.5\,\mathrm{m/s}$ range.

items.title: Catch the near-terminal moment
items.prompt: Starting from Draggy disk, pause when drag is almost equal to weight and the remaining net downward force is tiny.
items.successMessage: You found the near-terminal moment. The object is still moving, but the force imbalance is now so small that the speed is hardly changing.

setup.note: Pause the fall and scrub until the drag and weight readouts are almost matched.

hints: Look for the drag line almost touching the weight line while the net-force line sits close to zero.
Late in this low-terminal-speed setup, the speed is still positive even though the force imbalance is tiny.

targets.label: Make the net downward force smaller than about $0.35\,\mathrm{N}$.
Bring the current speed within about $0.10\,\mathrm{m/s}$ of terminal speed.

paragraphs: On this particle bench, temperature tells you how energetic the particles are on average. Internal energy tells you how much microscopic energy the whole sample has altogether.

That is why amount matters. Two samples can show the same temperature and similar motion traces, yet the larger sample can still have more internal energy because that average motion is being counted across more particles.

When you heat the sample, watch both the stage and the graphs. Sometimes added energy raises temperature quickly, and sometimes it mainly fills another internal store, so internal energy rises while temperature changes little.

keyIdeas: Temperature tracks the average microscopic motion per particle, not the total energy of the whole sample.
Internal energy depends on average motion, the amount of substance, and the other microscopic store shown on the bench.
At the same heater power, a smaller sample warms faster because the same input energy is shared across fewer particles.
On the phase shelf, energy can keep entering the sample even while the temperature graph stays nearly flat.

commonMisconception.myth: If two samples have the same temperature, they must have the same internal energy.
correction: Equal temperature means the average kinetic energy per particle is similar.
The larger sample can still have more total internal energy because that average motion is counted across more particles, along with any other internal store.

workedExamples.title: Read the live sample
workedExamples.intro: Use the current sample state as your evidence. The particle motion, energy bars, and graphs all come from the same live setup, so you can connect what you see on the stage to the numbers you calculate.

items.prompt: For the current sample with $N = {{particleCountValue}}$ particles and temperature ${{temperatureValue}}$, use the live readouts to separate per-particle energy from whole-sample energy. What are the thermal kinetic energy and the total internal energy?

steps.label: 1. Read the average per-particle energy
2. Multiply by particle count
3. Add the other internal store

items.resultLabel: Thermal part and total internal energy

applyAction.label: Compare with the larger sample
applyAction.observationHint: The motion traces and temperature chip can stay similar, while the internal-energy bar and amount scan both increase because more particles are being counted.

items.title: Heating across the shelf
items.prompt: At the current inspected time $t = {{timeValue}}\,\mathrm{s}$, how much energy has the heater added so far, and what do the live readouts say about temperature and shelf progress right now?

steps.label: 1. Compute the input energy so far
2. Read the live temperature and internal energy
3. Decide where the added energy is going

items.resultLabel: Input energy and current shelf state

applyAction.label: Inspect the phase shelf
applyAction.observationHint: On the shelf, the temperature graph can flatten while the internal-energy graph and the shelf-progress bar still rise. That means the heater is still adding energy.

miniChallenge.prompt: Two samples start at the same temperature. Sample A has three times as many particles as Sample B, and both receive the same heater power for the same short time. Which sample ends with more internal energy, and which one warms faster?
miniChallenge.prediction: Do not assume that the sample with more total stored energy must also show the faster temperature change.
miniChallenge.answer: The larger sample ends with more total internal energy, but the smaller sample warms faster.
miniChallenge.explanation: Temperature follows the per-particle energy scale, so the same heater input is spread across more particles in the larger sample. Internal energy still ends up larger for that sample because the total counts energy across more particles and internal stores.

quickTest.intro: Answer from what the bench means, not from a memorized slogan. Keep average per-particle motion separate from total internal energy.

questions.prompt: Two samples have the same temperature, but one contains three times as many particles. Which statement is best supported by this model?
questions.explanation: Temperature follows the average per-particle motion. Total internal energy still depends on how many particles and internal stores the whole sample contains.

selectedWrongExplanations.a: That confuses temperature with total internal energy. Here, temperature is an average-scale measure, not the full total.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: Crowding is not the key idea in this model. The relevant pieces are amount and per-particle energy.

showMeAction.label: Compare with the larger sample
showMeAction.observationHint: Look for a similar temperature chip but a larger internal-energy bar as particle count increases.

questions.prompt: On the shelf, the temperature graph is nearly flat while the heater is still on. What must be true in this model?
questions.explanation: The shelf represents an internal store that can absorb energy without a matching immediate temperature rise.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: A flat temperature graph does not automatically mean zero energy input. You need the energy bookkeeping too.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: Temperature and internal energy are different quantities, so a flat temperature does not imply decreasing internal energy.

showMeAction.label: Inspect the shelf
showMeAction.observationHint: The shelf bar and internal-energy curve keep moving even while the temperature graph flattens.

questions.explanation: Temperature is tied to the average microscopic motion scale, not to the whole-sample energy total and not to the heater alone.

showMeAction.observationHint: Watch the motion vectors and the temperature chip change together, while the internal-energy total still depends on particle count.

questions.prompt: A student says, 'If the heater adds energy, temperature must rise immediately every time.' What is the best correction in this model?
questions.explanation: The shelf is the reminder that energy input can feed other microscopic changes, so temperature is not forced to jump every instant.

selectedWrongExplanations.a: That ignores shelf behavior. In this model, temperature is not the only way added energy can appear.
selectedWrongExplanations.b: Particle count is a separate control. Added energy changes internal energy directly and can also change temperature when it goes into average motion.

showMeAction.label: Inspect shelf heating
showMeAction.observationHint: The heater is still active while the shelf bar and internal-energy curve continue to rise.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows motion traces on representative particles so the average microscopic motion stays visible.
whatToNotice: Faster average motion means higher temperature, but it does not by itself tell you the total internal energy.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links temperature to visible particle motion instead of treating temperature as a disconnected meter.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows how the current internal energy is divided between thermal motion and the other microscopic store.
whatToNotice: During shelf heating, the total internal-energy bar can keep growing even when the temperature graph is flat.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you separate temperature from total internal energy on the same bench.

whatToNotice: If the shelf is only partly filled, the heater can keep adding energy while the temperature changes very little.
overlays.whyItMatters: It shows why added energy does not always produce an immediate temperature rise.

whatToNotice: Samples with the same temperature chip can still have different particle counts and different total internal energies.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the difference between amount and average motion visible before you read the graphs.

graphs.description: Use this graph to follow how the sample warms over time. A flat section does not automatically mean energy has stopped entering the sample.
Compare total internal energy with its thermal-motion part and the other microscopic store. This is the clearest graph for seeing what happens on the shelf.
This graph changes only particle count while the temperature-like scale stays fixed, so it cleanly shows how same-temperature samples can have different total internal energies.
This graph changes only particle count at the current heater setting. It shows why larger samples warm more slowly under the same energy input.

noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time. Each one links a control change to what you should see on the stage, overlays, and graphs.

items.text: If you change only particle count, the temperature chip can stay nearly the same while the internal-energy bar grows a lot. That is the clearest sign that temperature and total internal energy are different quantities.
items.tryThis: Switch between the small-warm-sample and large-warm-sample presets. Compare the temperature chip, motion cues, internal-energy bar, and amount scan.
items.whyItMatters: You need this distinction before ideas like heat transfer, specific heat, and phase change can make sense.

items.text: At fixed heater power, the temperature-rise graph falls as particle count increases because the same input energy is shared across more particles.
items.tryThis: Hover a point at small particle count and one at large particle count, then compare the preview values with the particle box.
items.whyItMatters: It shows why warming rate depends on amount, not just on heater power.

items.text: A flat temperature graph does not mean the heater turned off. If the shelf is still filling, internal energy is still increasing even while temperature stays nearly constant.
items.tryThis: Use the on-the-shelf preset, scrub through the flat part of the temperature graph, and compare it with the energy-breakdown graph and the shelf-progress bar.
items.whyItMatters: It prevents the common mistake that flat temperature means no energy is entering the sample.

items.text: Raising starting temperature makes the motion cues more active immediately, but total internal energy still depends on particle count and the other store.
items.tryThis: Raise only the starting temperature and compare how the motion vectors, temperature chip, and internal-energy bar move together but not by the same amount.
items.whyItMatters: It blocks the shortcut that hotter must always mean more total energy in every comparison.

items.text: Compare mode is most useful when Setup A and Setup B have similar temperatures but different particle counts. Then you can isolate average motion from total internal energy.
items.whyItMatters: It lets you test one causal change at a time without losing the live particle picture.

predictionMode.intro: Predict before you apply the change. Each scenario checks whether you can keep average motion, total internal energy, and shelf behavior separate.

items.prompt: From the small warm sample, you increase only particle count and keep the starting temperature the same. Which statement best matches the bench?
items.explanation: Temperature follows the average per-particle motion, while internal energy counts the whole sample. More particles at the same temperature give a larger total internal energy, and the same heater would warm that larger sample more slowly.
items.observationHint: Look for a similar temperature chip but a taller internal-energy bar and a lower temperature-rise graph.

items.prompt: A sample is already on the phase shelf and the heater stays on. What should happen before the shelf is finished filling?
items.explanation: During the shelf, the heater still adds energy. That energy goes into the other microscopic store, so temperature does not need to rise at the same moment.
items.observationHint: The temperature graph stays nearly flat while the internal-energy curve and shelf bar keep moving.

items.prompt: Two samples start at the same temperature and get the same heater power, but one has far fewer particles. Which sample warms faster before any shelf behavior matters?
items.explanation: Heater power sets the input rate, but the temperature response depends on how many particles have to share that incoming energy.
items.observationHint: The amount-heating-rate graph is highest at small particle counts.

challengeMode.intro: Use the live bench to hit targets about amount and shelf behavior. The checks read the current metrics, so you must match the displayed state as well as the idea.

items.title: Same temperature, larger internal energy
items.prompt: Start from the small warm sample, switch to compare mode, and edit only Setup B until it stays at about the same temperature as Setup A but clearly stores much more internal energy.
items.successMessage: You kept the temperatures similar while making Setup B store much more internal energy. That is the key difference between average motion and whole-sample energy.

setup.note: Keep Setup A unchanged and edit only Setup B.

hints: Use particle count to raise the whole-sample total without changing the temperature much.
Check both the compare readouts and the amount scan to see whether the temperatures still match.

items.title: Find a true shelf state
items.prompt: Start from Warming toward a shelf and pause at a moment when temperature is nearly flat even though energy is still entering the sample.
items.successMessage: You found a genuine shelf state. The heater was still adding energy, but that energy was filling the internal store instead of raising temperature immediately.

setup.note: Run the sample until the temperature curve levels off, then pause during that flat region.

hints: Use inspect mode. Let the run reach the shelf, then pause and fine-tune with the time controls.
Use the energy-breakdown graph or the shelf bar to confirm that energy is still increasing while temperature is nearly constant.

paragraphs: On this bench, gas pressure comes from particles colliding with the walls. The ideal gas law is the compact rule that links that pressure to particle number, temperature, and volume.

Temperature sets the average particle-speed scale, particle number sets how many particles can hit the walls, and volume sets how often they get back to the walls. Faster particles, more particles, or a smaller box all make pressure rise.

This is a simplified 2D model rather than a full gas simulator, but it keeps the main ideal-gas patterns honest. It also shows that the same pressure can come from different microscopic situations, so pressure alone does not tell you temperature or particle number by itself.

keyIdeas: At fixed particle number and temperature, reducing volume raises pressure because the particles hit the walls more often in the smaller box.
At fixed particle number and volume, raising temperature raises pressure because particles move faster and transfer more momentum in each collision.
At fixed temperature and volume, adding particles raises pressure even though the average speed of each particle stays about the same.
The same pressure can come from different combinations of particle speed and crowding, so pressure alone does not determine temperature or particle number.

commonMisconception.myth: Pressure only depends on how crowded the gas is, so heating matters only if the number of particles changes.
correction: Crowding matters, but temperature matters too because hotter particles move faster and hit the walls harder.
That is why a hotter gas at the same particle number and volume can have a higher pressure without adding any particles.

workedExamples.title: Read the live gas state
workedExamples.intro: Use the current box as your evidence. The particles, wall-hit cues, pressure gauge, and graphs all come from the same state, so each calculation can be checked against what you see.

items.title: Calculate the current pressure
items.prompt: For the current gas state with $N = {{particleCountValue}}$, $T = {{temperatureValue}}$, and $V = {{volumeValue}}$, use the live readouts and the ideal-gas relation to find the pressure.

steps.label: 1. Read the crowding from $N/V$
2. Apply the ideal-gas relation
3. Check the answer against the wall hits

applyAction.label: Compare with the compressed box
applyAction.observationHint: The particles are not faster, but the smaller box makes wall hits more frequent, so the pressure gauge and pressure-volume graph both rise.

items.title: Explain the current pressure microscopically
items.prompt: For the current state, use the average speed and wall collision rate to explain why the pressure has its present value.

steps.label: 1. Read the temperature-speed link
2. Read the wall-hit rate
3. Link the collisions to the pressure reading

applyAction.label: Compare with a hotter box
applyAction.observationHint: The box size and particle count stay fixed, but the speed cue lengthens, the wall-hit rate rises, and the pressure-temperature graph moves upward.

miniChallenge.prompt: Two sealed boxes have the same particle number and the same volume, but Box A is hotter than Box B. Which box has the higher pressure, and what changes on the particle level explain it?
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide whether heating changes only particle speed, or both particle speed and the wall-hit pattern.
miniChallenge.answer: Box A has the higher pressure.
miniChallenge.explanation: At fixed $N$ and $V$, a hotter gas has faster particles. They hit the walls harder and also cross the box more often, so the pressure rises.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the particle box and graphs, not from a memorized slogan. Each question checks whether you can explain pressure causally.

showMeAction.label: Show the compressed box
showMeAction.observationHint: The wall-hit cue strengthens even though the motion streak length stays about the same.

questions.prompt: At fixed particle count and volume, why does heating the gas raise pressure?

showMeAction.observationHint: Watch the motion streaks, wall-hit rate, and pressure graph rise together.

questions.prompt: Two boxes have the same temperature and the same volume, but Box B has more particles. Which statement is best supported by the model?

questions.explanation: Equal pressure does not fix temperature or particle number. Different combinations of speed, crowding, and box size can produce the same pressure.

questions.prompt: Which graph most directly shows how pressure changes with volume when particle number and temperature stay fixed?

paragraphs: The simulation shows a single gas box with moving particles. Motion streaks show the speed scale, density shading shows how crowded the gas is, wall flashes show collision frequency, and a pressure gauge shows the resulting pressure.

The graphs below each vary one control while holding the other two fixed. They let you connect what changes in the particle box to a clean pressure-volume, pressure-temperature, pressure-particle-count, or collision-temperature trend.
This is a simplified 2D model rather than a full 3D molecular simulation. It is designed to make the ideal-gas patterns and the wall-collision explanation easy to see.

The pressure-volume graph isolates compression: with particle number and temperature fixed, smaller volume gives higher pressure. The pressure-temperature and pressure-particle-count graphs isolate the other two state-variable changes in the same way.
The collision-temperature graph is the kinetic-theory bridge. It does not plot pressure directly; it shows that hotter particles hit the walls more often, helping explain why pressure rises.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows motion streaks and the average-speed chip.
whatToNotice: Raising temperature lengthens the motion streaks before you even look at the graphs.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the temperature-pressure link visible on the stage.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows how packed the box is with density shading and a density chip.
whatToNotice: Reducing volume or adding particles increases density even if the average speed stays the same.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the $N/V$ part of the law visible.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows wall flashes and the wall collision rate.
whatToNotice: Pressure rises when wall hits become more frequent, not only when particles move faster.
overlays.whyItMatters: It connects the pressure gauge to particle-wall collisions.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the current pressure next to the live wall-hit rate.
whatToNotice: Two setups can have similar pressure even when one is hotter and the other is more crowded.
overlays.whyItMatters: It reminds you that pressure is an outcome, not the whole microscopic story.

graphs.description: Change only volume. This is the cleanest graph for seeing how compression raises pressure.
Change only temperature. Use this graph to check that hotter gas gives higher pressure at fixed $N$ and $V$.
Change only particle number. This shows that more particles raise pressure even when the average speed stays about the same.
Change only temperature. This graph shows how faster particles reach the walls more often.

noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time. Change one variable, then check the stage, the pressure gauge, and the matching graph.

items.text: Reducing only the volume raises pressure even though the speed cue stays about the same. In the smaller box, the same particles reach the walls more often.
items.tryThis: Switch between Room baseline and Compressed box. Compare the motion streaks, wall-hit flashes, and the pressure-volume graph.
items.whyItMatters: It separates the volume effect from the temperature effect.

items.text: Raising only the temperature lengthens the motion streaks and increases wall-hit rate, so pressure rises even though box size and particle count stay fixed.
items.tryThis: Hover a cool point and a warm point on the collision-temperature graph, then watch the preview values update on the stage.
items.whyItMatters: It links the pressure-temperature law to particle speed and collisions.

items.text: Adding particles at fixed temperature raises pressure without changing the average-speed cue much. More particles means more wall hits each second.
items.tryThis: Switch between Room baseline and More particles same box, then compare the density cue and the pressure-particle-count graph.
items.whyItMatters: It prevents the shortcut that every pressure change must come from temperature.

items.text: Hotter same box and More particles same box can reach similar pressures. The gauge can match while the speed cue and density cue tell different microscopic stories.
items.tryThis: In compare mode, keep one setup hotter and the other more crowded until the two pressure readings are close.
items.whyItMatters: It shows why pressure alone does not determine temperature or particle number.

items.text: Read the ideal gas law as one summary of speed, crowding, and box size, not as four unrelated symbols.
items.tryThis: Hold two variables fixed, move one slider, and use the matching response graph to explain the pressure change.
items.whyItMatters: It turns the law into a causal model instead of a formula to memorize.

predictionMode.intro: Predict before you move the slider. Each scenario asks which microscopic part of the pressure story is changing.

items.prompt: Starting from Room baseline, you reduce only the volume while keeping the same particle count and temperature. Which statement best matches the bench?
items.observationHint: The motion streaks stay similar, but the wall-hit cue and pressure gauge both increase.

items.prompt: Starting from Room baseline, you raise only the temperature. What should happen to the pressure?
items.observationHint: The motion streaks lengthen, wall hits become more frequent, and the pressure-temperature graph rises.

items.prompt: Starting from Room baseline, you increase only particle count. Which statement best matches the bench?

challengeMode.intro: Use the live gas box to hit pressure targets. The checks read the current state, so you have to create the right pressure for the right reason.

items.title: Double the pressure by compression
items.prompt: Start from Room baseline and lower only the volume until the pressure is about double while temperature and particle count stay near the baseline values.
items.successMessage: You doubled the pressure by compression alone. The particles were not individually faster; they were just reaching the walls more often in the smaller box.

setup.note: Do not use temperature or particle count to help. The point is to raise pressure by compression alone.

hints: Use the pressure-volume graph first. Halving the baseline volume is the cleanest route.
If temperature drifts away from the baseline, you are changing two causes at once.

items.title: Match the pressure with a different cause
items.successMessage: You matched the pressure with a different microscopic cause. Setup A used faster particles, while Setup B used more particles in the same box.

setup.note: Leave Setup A hot. Make Setup B cooler and more crowded.

hints: Keep the box size close to the same in both setups, then let particle count do the compensating work in Setup B.
The pressure badges should match before the speed cue and density cue do.

paragraphs: On this bench, heat means energy crossing from the hotter block to the cooler surroundings. The block stores internal energy, but heat is the transfer itself, so both the direction and the rate come back to the temperature difference.

The same hot block can lose energy through three pathways at once. Conduction uses the solid contact with the bench, convection uses moving air, and radiation uses thermal emission that still works even without solid contact.

Watch how the pathways change as the block cools. The temperature gap shrinks, so the pathway rates usually shrink too. This page is about identifying where the energy goes before later topics ask how that transfer changes temperature.

keyIdeas: Heat is energy transfer caused by a temperature difference, not energy stored inside the block.
Conduction gets stronger when the material is more conductive, the contact is better, the shared area is larger, or the temperature difference is larger.
Convection depends on moving air and exposed area, so stronger airflow can increase transfer without changing the solid-contact path.
Radiation needs no contact and becomes much more important at large temperature contrast.

correction: A hot object contains internal energy, but heat is the energy crossing into or out of it because of a temperature difference.
That is why the same object can gain heat, lose heat, or have almost no net heat transfer depending on its surroundings.

workedExamples.title: Read the live heat-flow state
workedExamples.intro: Use the live block, room, and pathway readouts as your evidence. Each calculation should match what you see in the pathway bars and graphs.

items.title: Read the current pathway split
items.prompt: For the current setup, what temperature difference is driving the transfer, and how much of the total rate comes from conduction, convection, and radiation right now?

steps.label: 1. Read the temperature gap
2. Read the three pathway rates
3. Add them to get the total

items.resultLabel: Current split of the total rate

applyAction.label: Compare with the metal bench
applyAction.observationHint: The conduction channel becomes much larger because the solid-contact path to the cool bench is strong.

items.title: Why the cooling rate drops
items.prompt: At the current inspected time $t = {{timeValue}}\,\mathrm{s}$, how much energy has left the block so far, and why is the total transfer rate lower than it was at the start?

steps.label: 1. Read the energy transferred so far
2. Read the current temperature gap
3. Use the smaller gap to explain the smaller rate

items.resultLabel: Energy lost so far and current cooling rate

applyAction.label: Inspect the windy room
applyAction.observationHint: As the block cools, the temperature gap shrinks, so the pathway curves bend downward even when the airflow setting stays the same.

miniChallenge.prediction: Decide which transfer pathway changes immediately, before either block has had much time to cool.
miniChallenge.explanation: With the same initial temperature difference, the main change is the contact path. Better conductivity and better contact make conduction larger right away, so Block A loses energy faster before the temperatures have had much time to change.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the live bench, not from a slogan. Keep stored energy, temperature difference, and transfer pathway separate.

questions.prompt: On this page, what does heat mean?
questions.explanation: The block stores internal energy, but heat is the energy crossing the boundary because of a temperature difference.

showMeAction.observationHint: The bridge points from hotter to cooler, showing why there is a net transfer.
Improving contact mainly raises the conduction part of the rate split.

showMeAction.label: Show airflow changing convection
showMeAction.observationHint: The convection arrows and blue pathway rate respond first.

showMeAction.label: Show the contrast graph
showMeAction.observationHint: Compare the amber radiation curve with the straighter conduction and convection lines as delta T increases.

questions.explanation: Area changes the boundary size, so all three pathways can increase at the same temperature difference.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a hot block beside a cooler bench and room. A temperature-gap bridge, a solid contact channel, airflow cues, and radiation arcs all come from the same hot-block and room temperatures.
Changing the controls updates one shared heat-flow state. The temperatures, pathway split, readout card, graphs, prediction mode, compare mode, and challenge checks all stay synchronized.

The temperature-history graph compares the hot block with the fixed room temperature so the shrinking temperature gap stays visible. The pathway-rates graph compares conduction, convection, radiation, and the total on one shared time axis.

The contact-response graph changes only contact quality, so it isolates conduction. The contrast-response graph changes only temperature contrast, which is where the stronger upward bend of radiation is easiest to see.

equations.label: Total heat-transfer rate
equations.meaning: On this bench, the outward heat-transfer rate is the sum of the three visible pathways.

equations.label: Conduction trend
equations.meaning: Conduction grows with material conductivity, contact quality, shared area, and temperature difference.
notes: The contact factor chi stands in for how well the two surfaces actually touch.

equations.label: Convection trend
equations.meaning: Convection grows with airflow, exposed area, and temperature difference.

equations.label: Radiation trend
equations.meaning: Radiation works without contact and grows especially quickly at large temperature contrast.
notes: The fourth-power dependence is why the radiation curve bends upward on the contrast graph.

equations.label: Cooling rate of the block
equations.meaning: The block cools fastest when the total outward transfer rate is largest and cools more slowly as the temperature gap collapses.

variableLinks.description: Sets the block's starting temperature, so it sets the initial temperature gap that drives all three pathways.
Changes the surroundings, which changes both the size and the direction of the temperature difference.
Strengthens or weakens only the solid-conduction path.
Represents how well the block actually touches the bench. Better contact means stronger conduction.
Changes the boundary across which energy can leave, so all three pathways respond.
Changes the convection path by making the surrounding air more still or more strongly moving.

controls.description: Sets the block's starting temperature, so it sets the initial temperature gap.
Changes the room and bench temperature around the block.
Changes how strong the solid-conduction path is.
Changes how well the block actually touches the bench.
Changes the size of the boundary through which energy can leave.
Makes the surrounding air more still or more strongly moving.

presets.description: Strong conductivity and good contact make conduction dominate early.
Weak contact reduces conduction, so convection and radiation matter more.
Strong airflow makes convection the largest pathway even though the conduction path still exists.
A very hot block with weak contact and still air can make radiation the largest pathway.

whatToNotice: As the two temperature chips move closer together, all three pathway rates shrink because they share the same driving temperature difference.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps heat tied to a temperature difference instead of treating heat as something stored in the block.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the separate conduction, convection, and radiation channels plus the total built from them.
whatToNotice: The total rate is just the sum of the three visible pathways, not a fourth hidden process.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you see which pathway is actually setting the total at any moment.

whatToNotice: Increasing area raises all three rates because it gives energy more boundary to cross.
overlays.whyItMatters: It gives the area control a visible physical meaning.

whatToNotice: Changing airflow leaves the solid-contact path nearly unchanged while the convection arrows and convection rate respond right away.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates convection through moving air from conduction through the bench.

graphs.description: Track the hot block against the room. As the temperature gap shrinks, the pathway rates below should shrink too.
Compare conduction, convection, radiation, and the total on one axis. The total line should match the sum of the three pathway lines.
Change only contact quality. This isolates how strongly the solid-contact path can affect the total rate.
Change only the starting temperature difference. Radiation curves upward more strongly here than conduction and convection.

noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time. Each one links one control change to the temperature gap and the pathway split.

items.text: Changing material conductivity or contact quality changes only the conduction path directly. That is the clearest sign that conduction depends on the solid connection, not on the airflow.
items.whyItMatters: It separates the contact path from the air path before later topics ask what the transferred energy does.

items.text: Changing airflow leaves the solid-contact path nearly unchanged while the convection arrows and convection curve respond immediately. That is the cleanest way to separate convection from conduction.
A larger area raises all three pathways because it gives energy more boundary to cross. Area is not just a conduction idea.
On the contrast graph, radiation bends upward more strongly than conduction and convection. That is the sign that radiation becomes much more important at large temperature differences.
items.whyItMatters: This gives you the right early intuition for why very hot objects can radiate strongly.

items.text: Compare mode works best when Setup A and Setup B keep nearly the same temperature gap but differ in contact or area. Then any total-rate difference comes from the pathway, not from a hidden delta-T change.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the pathway split before you move the control. Change one cause, then check which part of the rate bar responds.

items.explanation: Better contact strengthens the solid-conduction path immediately. Because the temperature gap is the same at the start, conduction is the part that jumps first.
items.observationHint: Watch the conduction bar grow first while the two temperature chips are still almost unchanged.

items.explanation: Airflow belongs to convection, not to the solid-contact path. With the same temperatures, changing only airflow mainly changes the convection share.
items.observationHint: Watch the convection arrows and blue curve first.

items.explanation: A larger temperature difference increases all three pathways, but radiation increases most strongly at high contrast because of the fourth-power dependence.

challengeMode.intro: Use the live bench to create target heat-flow situations. The checks read the current temperature gap and pathway rates, so the physics stays tied to one visible state.

items.title: Same gap, smaller total rate
items.successMessage: You kept the same driving temperature difference while weakening the transfer path. That cleanly separates delta-T effects from contact-path effects.
hints: Keep the hot-block and room temperatures matched so the delta-T badges stay close.
Use contact quality or material conductivity to change the conduction path without changing the temperature gap.

items.title: Make radiation the biggest path
items.successMessage: You created a case where weak contact and still air keep conduction and convection small enough for radiation to dominate.

setup.note: Keep the pathway-rates graph open so you can see the amber radiation line overtake the others.

paragraphs: Specific heat tells you how much energy each kilogram of a material needs for a 1 degC temperature change. On this bench, the same power input makes a low-c sample warm quickly and a high-c sample warm slowly because the same incoming energy is being spread across different thermal capacities.

Phase change adds a second idea. A sample can keep absorbing or releasing energy while its temperature stays nearly flat if that energy is changing phase fraction instead of temperature. That is why the heating curve can have a real flat shelf even while energy is still being transferred.

Watch the stage, the heating curve, and the energy bars together. Sloped parts of the curve show sensible energy changing temperature. The flat shelf shows latent energy changing phase. One energy bookkeeping explains both.

keyIdeas: The sample's thermal capacity is m c, so the same energy input changes temperature less when mass or specific heat is larger.
Away from the phase-change shelf, temperature change follows Q_sensible = m c delta T.
On the shelf, energy can still enter or leave while temperature stays near the phase-change temperature because the energy is changing phase fraction instead.
Specific heat controls the slope away from the shelf, while latent heat controls how wide the shelf is in energy.

correction: A flat temperature line can still mean real energy transfer if the sample is on the phase-change shelf.
In that case the transferred energy is changing phase fraction, so the latent-energy term changes while temperature stays near the phase-change temperature.

workedExamples.title: Read the live energy bookkeeping
workedExamples.intro: Use the current bench state as your evidence. The stage, heating curve, and energy bars all come from the same sample, so each calculation can be checked against what you see.

items.title: Track where the input energy goes
items.prompt: At the current inspected time $t = {{timeValue}}\,\mathrm{min}$, how much energy has been transferred, and how much of it is changing temperature rather than phase for this sample?

steps.label: 1. Compute the total energy transferred
2. Work out the sample's thermal capacity
3. Split the energy into sensible and latent parts

items.resultLabel: Total input, sensible part, and temperature change

applyAction.label: Compare with the high-c sample
applyAction.observationHint: The same power is still being supplied, but the larger specific heat makes the temperature rise more slowly on the sloped part of the curve.

items.title: Read the flat shelf correctly
items.prompt: At the current inspected time $t = {{timeValue}}\,\mathrm{min}$, how far across the phase-change shelf is the sample, and what does the flat temperature mean right now?

steps.label: 1. Read the full shelf energy
2. Read the current phase progress
3. Explain the flat temperature

applyAction.label: Inspect the shelf state
applyAction.observationHint: The temperature line can stay flat while the shelf bar and latent-energy readout continue to change.

miniChallenge.prediction: Decide whether the same total energy input must produce the same temperature change.
miniChallenge.explanation: The same total input does not force the same temperature change. Away from the shelf, the sample with the smaller thermal capacity m c changes temperature more.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the energy bookkeeping, not from a slogan. Keep specific heat, latent heat, and heating-curve reading separate.

questions.explanation: For the same material, doubling mass doubles thermal capacity, so the same sensible-energy pulse produces only half the temperature change.
Cooling reverses the same shelf logic: latent energy leaves first, so phase fraction can decrease while temperature stays near the shelf.
Specific heat belongs to Q_sensible = m c delta T, while latent heat belongs to Q_phase = m L. One controls slope and the other controls shelf width.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one sample, a heater-or-cooler energy stream, a thermometer, a phase-fraction bar, and a state card. The same mass, specific heat, power, starting temperature, latent heat, and phase fraction drive every visible part of the bench.
Changing the controls updates the same live state for the stage, heating curve, energy-bookkeeping graph, response graphs, compare mode, prediction mode, and challenge checks.

The heating-curve graph compares the live temperature with the fixed phase-change temperature so the sloped stretches and the shelf stay aligned with the stage. The energy-bookkeeping graph compares total transferred energy with the sensible and latent parts on the same time axis.

The specific-heat response graph changes only c, so it shows how the same pulse changes temperature less when thermal capacity is larger. The latent-response graph changes only L, so it shows how a larger latent heat widens the shelf in energy.

equations.label: Sample thermal capacity
equations.meaning: Mass and specific heat together tell you how much energy is needed for each 1 degC of temperature change.

equations.label: Sensible energy change
equations.meaning: Away from the shelf, transferred energy changes temperature in proportion to mass, specific heat, and temperature change.

equations.label: Energy transferred by constant power
equations.meaning: With fixed power, the total transferred energy grows linearly with time.

equations.label: Latent energy for the whole sample
equations.meaning: Crossing the full shelf requires energy that depends on both mass and the material's latent heat.

equations.label: Total energy bookkeeping
equations.meaning: The same transferred energy must be split between temperature change and phase change.
notes: On a sloped segment, most transferred energy is sensible.
On the shelf, the latent term can keep changing while temperature stays nearly fixed.

variableLinks.description: Mass increases both m c and m L, so a larger sample usually warms or cools more slowly and needs more energy to finish the phase change.
Specific heat controls how steep the sloped parts of the heating curve are. Larger c means more energy is needed for each degree of temperature change.
Power sets how fast energy enters or leaves the sample. It changes how fast the sample moves along the heating curve.
Starting temperature decides whether the run begins below the shelf, on the shelf, or above it.
Latent heat sets the shelf width in energy, not the slope away from the shelf.

controls.description: Changes how much sample there is.
Changes how much energy each kilogram needs for each degree of temperature change.
Positive values heat the sample and negative values cool it.
Moves the starting point below the shelf, onto the shelf, or above it.
Changes how much energy the shelf absorbs or releases before temperature starts changing again.

presets.description: Above the shelf with a low specific heat, so the same power produces a fast temperature rise.
Same mass and power as the low-c sample, but the larger specific heat makes temperature rise more slowly.
Starts below the phase-change temperature, warms up to it, and then begins moving across the shelf.
Starts on the flat shelf, so phase fraction changes while temperature stays near 0 degC.
Starts on the shelf with cooling power, so phase fraction falls before temperature drops below 0 degC.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the live m c value and temperature rate so you can connect specific heat to the slope of the curve.
whatToNotice: A larger m c makes the sloped parts of the heating curve less steep under the same power.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties specific heat to a visible rate of temperature change.

whatToNotice: The shelf bar can keep moving while the temperature marker stays near the phase-change temperature.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes latent heat visible instead of leaving the flat part of the curve unexplained.

whatToNotice: Total transferred energy can keep increasing even when most of the change is going into only the latent bar.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links the sloped parts and the flat shelf to one shared energy bookkeeping.

whatToNotice: Reaching the phase-change line does not always mean temperature changes right away. It can mean the run has entered the shelf.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the stage and graph aligned so the flat part of the heating curve has a clear physical meaning.

graphs.description: Compare the live temperature with the phase-change temperature. Sloped parts mean sensible heating or cooling, and the flat shelf means latent-energy change.
Compare total transferred energy with the sensible and latent parts. Use this graph to check whether a flat temperature really means the shelf is active.
Change only the specific heat while keeping the current mass and power. The same 4 minute pulse gives a smaller temperature change when c is larger, unless the run is on the shelf.
Change only the latent heat. A larger latent heat makes the shelf wider in energy, so the sample can spend longer near constant temperature under the same power.

noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time. Each one links one control change to the heating curve and the live energy split.

items.text: Changing only specific heat changes the steepness of the sloped parts, but it does not change the energy width of the shelf. That shows that c controls temperature response, not latent shelf energy.
items.whyItMatters: This connects Q = m c delta T to a visible temperature response.

items.text: Mass changes both the m c chip and the m L shelf-width chip. A larger sample warms more slowly and also needs more energy to finish the phase change.
items.whyItMatters: It shows that mass affects both the sloped parts and the shelf.

items.text: A flat shelf does not mean the heater stopped. Check the energy-bookkeeping graph and the shelf bar: latent energy can still be changing while temperature stays near the phase-change temperature.
items.whyItMatters: This is the key habit for reading a heating curve honestly.

items.text: On the latent-response graph, increasing L makes the shelf wider in energy. Under the same power, the sample can spend longer near constant temperature.
items.whyItMatters: It separates a wide shelf from a large specific heat.

items.text: Compare mode works best when Setup A and Setup B keep the same mass and power but different specific heats. Then any temperature-change difference comes from m c, not from a hidden change in energy input.

predictionMode.intro: Predict where the next bit of energy goes before you change the controls. Does it change temperature, phase fraction, or both?

items.explanation: At fixed mass and power, a larger specific heat increases m c, so the same 4 minute pulse produces a smaller temperature change on the sloped part of the curve.
items.observationHint: For the same pulse, the higher-c setup climbs less on the sloped part of the heating curve.

items.explanation: On the shelf, transferred energy changes phase fraction. Temperature can stay nearly flat even while the latent-energy term keeps changing.
items.observationHint: Watch the shelf bar and the latent-energy line first.

items.prompt: Starting from Cooling on the shelf, you keep removing energy. What changes first before temperature falls below the shelf?
items.explanation: Cooling reverses the same shelf logic: energy leaves the latent store first, so phase fraction falls before the sample drops below the phase-change temperature.
items.observationHint: The shelf fraction shrinks while the temperature marker stays near 0 degC.

challengeMode.intro: Use the same thermal bench for slope and shelf targets. The checks read the live temperature change, phase fraction, and graph state from one simulation.

items.title: Same pulse, smaller temperature change
items.prompt: Start from Low-c warm sample, switch to compare mode, and edit only Setup B until both setups receive the same 4 minute pulse but Setup B warms much less because its specific heat is larger.
items.successMessage: You kept the same energy pulse but changed the thermal capacity. Setup B warmed less because its larger specific heat spread the same energy across a larger m c.
hints: Keep the mass and power the same. Let specific heat do the explanatory work.
The compare badges and the specific-heat-response graph should both show Setup B warming less.

items.title: Find a genuine shelf state
items.prompt: Starting from Warming toward the shelf, pause at a real shelf moment where temperature is near 0 degC but the phase fraction is still between fully solid and fully liquid.

setup.note: Let the run reach the shelf, then pause and fine-tune with inspect mode.

hints: The sample reaches the shelf after several minutes, not immediately.
Use the energy-bookkeeping graph or the shelf bar to confirm that latent energy is changing while temperature stays near the shelf.

paragraphs: A circular orbit is not a place where gravity disappears. It is continuous free fall: gravity keeps pulling inward, and the sideways speed is exactly right so the object keeps missing the central mass at the same radius.

This lab lets you change the source mass, the chosen reference radius, and the launch speed as a fraction of the circular value. Then you compare the live path with the radius, speed, and acceleration-balance graphs. The assumptions stay visible: one central source, circular-orbit targets, displayed G = 1 units, and no atmosphere or other forces. The key check is simple: for a circular orbit, gravity must exactly match the inward acceleration needed to keep turning at that radius.

keyIdeas: For a circular orbit at radius $r$, gravity itself provides the centripetal acceleration: $M/r^2 = v^2/r$ in the displayed units where $G = 1$.

At one chosen radius, a speed below the circular value makes gravity stronger than the turning requirement, so the path bends inward. A speed above the circular value makes the turning requirement larger than gravity, so the path opens outward.
At fixed radius, a heavier source needs a larger circular speed. At fixed source mass, a larger circular orbit needs a smaller speed by a square-root factor and a much longer period.

commonMisconception.myth: A satellite stays in orbit because gravity is almost absent there.
correction: Gravity is still acting all the time in orbit. It is the inward acceleration that keeps the path curved instead of straight.
What makes one orbit circular is not the absence of gravity, but the correct sideways speed at that radius.

workedExamples.title: Worked example from the live orbit
workedExamples.intro: Use the stage and graphs as evidence. First find the circular speed for the chosen reference circle. Then compare the live gravity with the turning requirement v^2/r to decide whether the path should stay circular, bend inward, or open outward. Watch the scaling: doubling radius gives 1/sqrt(2) of the circular speed, not half.

steps.label: 1. Match gravity to the turning requirement
2. Solve for the circular speed
3. Calculate the live circular speed, angular speed, and period

applyAction.label: Try heavier source
applyAction.observationHint: Keep the reference circle and gravity vector visible. At the same radius, a heavier source needs a larger circular speed and gives a shorter period.

items.title: Gravity versus turning requirement right now

steps.label: 1. Read the live radius and speed
2. Calculate the inward gravity
3. Calculate $v^2/r$ and compare the two

applyAction.label: Try too-slow orbit
applyAction.observationHint: When the speed factor is below 1, the gravity curve sits above the turning-requirement curve and the trail bends inside the dashed reference circle.

miniChallenge.prediction: Answer before you run the too-slow case: is gravity now stronger than the turning requirement, weaker than it, or exactly matched?
miniChallenge.explanation: At a fixed radius, lowering the speed lowers $v^2/r$. Gravity is then larger than the inward acceleration needed for a circular turn, so the path curves inward.

quickTest.intro: Use the stage and the linked graphs together. These questions test whether you can explain circular-orbit balance, including the square-root radius scaling, not just recite formulas.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one source mass at the center, one moving satellite launched from the right side of the chosen reference circle, and optional overlays for the dashed reference orbit, the current radius line, the tangent velocity vector, the inward gravity vector, and the trajectory trail.

Changing source mass, reference radius, or speed factor updates the same orbit path, readouts, and graphs together. The main comparison is whether the live path stays on the reference circle or peels inside or outside it because gravity and the turning requirement no longer match.

The radius-history graph shows whether the live orbit stays on the chosen reference radius or drifts inward or outward. The speed-history graph compares the actual speed with the circular speed required at the current radius.
The acceleration-balance graph compares the inward gravity with the turning requirement $v^2/r$ on the same live path. A circular orbit is the case where those two curves match.

equations.meaning: In the displayed units with $G = 1$, this gives the inward gravitational acceleration from the source mass at radius $r$.
Any path with speed $v$ at radius $r$ needs this inward acceleration to keep turning at that radius.
This is the one speed that makes gravity and the turning requirement match at the chosen radius.
Once the circular speed is fixed, this gives how quickly the orbit moves around the circle.
Wider circular orbits take much longer to complete, while heavier source mass shortens the period.

variableLinks.description: A larger source mass makes gravity stronger at every radius, so the circular speed rises and the circular period falls for the same reference circle.
Sets the dashed reference circle. A larger circular orbit needs a smaller speed but a much longer period.
Sets the actual launch speed relative to the circular value. At 1.00 the path can stay circular, below 1 it bends inward, and above 1 it opens outward.

graphs.description: Shows whether the live path stays on the chosen circular radius or drifts inward or outward over time.
Compares the actual speed with the circular speed required at the current radius.
Shows directly whether gravity matches, exceeds, or falls short of the turning requirement $v^2/r$.

noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to compare the live path with the dashed reference circle. The central question is always the same: does gravity match the turning requirement at the current radius?

predictionMode.intro: Predict what the path will do before you run the case. Then use the stage and the balance graph to check whether gravity matches the turning requirement. For radius changes, remember the speed change is square-root scaling, not a direct half-or-double change.

paragraphs: Kepler's third law is the timing consequence of the same circular-orbit balance you met in orbital speed. A larger circular orbit takes longer not just because the path is longer, but also because the allowed circular speed is smaller at larger radius.

This lab keeps one source mass, one chosen reference orbit, and one live path on the same state. That lets you connect the period law to what the simulation is showing: gravity sets the circular speed, and that speed sets the time for one full circular orbit. The law applies only when the speed factor stays at the circular match.

keyIdeas: For circular orbits around one source mass, the period follows $T_c = 2\pi\sqrt{r^3/M}$ in the displayed units with $G = 1$.
At fixed source mass, a larger circular orbit has both a longer circumference and a lower circular speed, so the period grows faster than radius alone.
Kepler's third law describes the circular-match case. If the speed factor moves away from 1.00, the path is no longer the circular orbit whose period the law describes.

workedExamples.title: Worked example from the live circular case
workedExamples.intro: Use the current source mass and reference orbit as evidence. First find the circular period for the chosen radius. Then compare the circular speed and period at $r$ and $2r$ so you can see why a wider circular orbit takes much longer.

items.title: Period of the chosen circular orbit

steps.label: 1. Recall the circular speed at this radius
2. Turn that speed into one full-orbit time
3. Compute the live circular period

applyAction.label: Try outer long-year orbit
applyAction.observationHint: At the same source mass, the wider circular orbit is slower and takes much longer to complete.

items.title: How doubling the radius changes the year

steps.label: 1. Use the period scaling
2. Use the speed scaling
3. Translate the ratios into the current live case

applyAction.label: Try inner vs outer circular orbits
applyAction.observationHint: Compare the two circular cases: the wider orbit has the lower speed curve and the much longer period.

miniChallenge.prediction: Answer before you try the inner and outer circular presets: does the longer year come only from more distance around, or from both more distance and lower circular speed?
miniChallenge.explanation: A larger circular orbit is not just a bigger circle. The allowed circular speed is also lower at larger radius, so the period scales as $r^{3/2}$ rather than directly as $r$.

quickTest.title: Quick check
quickTest.intro: These questions test whether you can explain why larger circular orbits take longer and when Kepler's law does not apply.

paragraphs: The radius-history graph shows whether the live path stays on the chosen circular reference radius or drifts inward or outward. The speed-history graph compares the actual speed with the circular speed required at the current radius.
The acceleration-balance graph compares inward gravity with the turning requirement $v^2/r$ on the same live path. Kepler's third law applies here only when those match in the circular case.

equations.meaning: This is the sideways speed that makes gravity exactly match the turning requirement at the chosen radius.
The circular year grows strongly with radius because wider orbits are both farther around and slower.
At fixed source mass, every circular orbit in this one-source model shares the same $T_c^2/r^3$ ratio.

variableLinks.description: A heavier source strengthens inward gravity, so the circular speed rises and the circular year gets shorter at the same radius.
A larger circular orbit is farther around and slower, so its period increases strongly.
Kepler's third law here describes only the circular-match case. At 1.00 the live path is the circular orbit; away from 1.00 the path stops being the orbit whose circular period the law predicts.

controls.description: Changes the central mass and therefore the gravity that sets the circular speed.
Sets the dashed circular target whose speed and period are being compared.
1.00 means the live speed matches the circular speed. Away from 1.00, the path stops being the circular orbit whose period the law predicts.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the dashed circle for the chosen circular orbit whose period is being discussed.
whatToNotice: Kepler's law here is about staying on this dashed circular path, not about paths that peel away from it.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the chosen circular year separate from cases that are too slow or too fast to remain circular.

whatToNotice: Wider circular orbits have shorter velocity vectors because the allowed circular speed is lower.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the slower-part of the longer-year story visible instead of hiding it in a formula only.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the inward gravitational acceleration that sets the circular speed.
whatToNotice: This same inward gravity is what makes wider circular orbits slower and heavier-source circular orbits faster.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the timing law tied to the actual force picture instead of making period feel detached from gravity.

whatToNotice: At the circular match, the trail keeps tracing one stable circle. Away from that match, the path is no longer the circular orbit whose period Kepler's law describes.
overlays.whyItMatters: It shows whether you are still looking at the circular case or have already left it.

graphs.description: Use this graph to confirm whether you are still looking at the circular orbit whose period the law describes.
Wider circular orbits are slower, so this graph helps explain why their periods are longer.
Kepler's law is the timing consequence of this same gravity-versus-turning balance when the orbit remains circular.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice about orbital period
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to keep the timing law tied to the orbit you are actually watching. The key question is whether you are still looking at an honest circular orbit or a path that has already left the circular case.

predictionMode.title: Predict the timing change
predictionMode.intro: Predict what the circular year should do before you apply the preset. Then use the speed and radius graphs to check whether the path is still circular.

paragraphs: Escape velocity is the minimum outward launch speed from a chosen radius that makes the total specific energy exactly zero. At that threshold, gravity still pulls inward and keeps slowing the launch, but there is no finite turnaround radius. Below that threshold, even a very long outward trip is still bound and must eventually return.

This lab keeps one source mass, one launch radius, one speed factor, and one live radial path on the same state. That lets you compare three ideas students often mix up: the local circular speed, the local escape speed, and the sign of the total specific energy. Going far from the source is not enough by itself. Escape is the case with no finite turnaround radius.

keyIdeas: Escape is decided by total specific energy, not by distance alone.
With displayed units using $G = 1$, the escape speed from launch radius $r_0$ is $v_{\text{esc}} = \sqrt{2M/r_0}$. A heavier source raises the threshold, while a larger launch radius lowers it.
The local circular speed is useful for comparison, but a launch can be faster than circular and still remain bound if its total specific energy stays negative.

commonMisconception.myth: If a launch gets far enough from the source, it must have escaped, and escape means gravity has mostly stopped acting.
correction: Distance alone does not decide escape. The deciding quantity is the sign of $E/m$. Zero or positive total specific energy means there is no finite turnaround radius, while negative total specific energy means the launch is still bound.
Gravity keeps acting after escape. It still points inward and continues reducing the outward speed. The difference is that the object no longer needs to reverse direction.

workedExamples.title: Worked example from the live launch
workedExamples.intro: Use the current source mass, launch radius, and speed factor as evidence. First find the threshold launch speed. Then read the live kinetic, potential, and total specific energies to decide whether the launch must escape or eventually turn around.

items.title: Escape speed from this launch point

steps.label: 1. Set the total specific energy to zero
2. Solve for the threshold speed
3. Compare the live escape and circular speeds

applyAction.label: Try wider launch threshold
applyAction.observationHint: Starting farther out lowers the threshold speed because the launch begins in a shallower part of the well.

steps.label: 1. Read the live radius and speed
2. Compute the specific kinetic and potential terms
3. Add them and decide the outcome

applyAction.label: Try high but bound
applyAction.observationHint: A launch can climb far outward while the total-energy line stays negative, so a finite turnaround still exists.

miniChallenge.prediction: Predict what must happen to the total-energy line and the turnaround marker when the launch just reaches the escape threshold.
miniChallenge.explanation: At fixed source mass and launch radius, only the launch speed changes the starting kinetic term. The threshold case is the point where $E/m = v_0^2/2 - M/r_0 = 0$.

quickTest.title: Quick check
quickTest.intro: Answer from the live launch-and-energy logic. The key question is not how far the object goes, but whether its total specific energy is negative, zero, or positive.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one source mass and one launched object moving along a radial line away from or back toward the source. Optional overlays can mark the launch radius, the predicted turnaround radius for bound cases, the current velocity, the inward gravity vector, and the visited trail.

Changing source mass, launch radius, or speed factor updates the same motion, readouts, and graphs together. Compare mode adds a second launch on a separate dashed track without changing the underlying gravity model.
The displayed units use a one-source model with $G = 1$. The stage has a finite visible range, so bound launches with turnaround beyond that range are labeled instead of being forced into view.

The radius-history graph compares the live radius with the starting launch radius over time so you can see whether the launch keeps expanding or approaches a finite turning point. Hovering or scrubbing previews the same instant on the stage.

The speed-thresholds graph compares the live speed with the local escape and circular speeds, and the specific-energy graph shows the kinetic, potential, and total specific energies together with the zero-energy line.

equations.meaning: The sign of this quantity decides the outcome: negative is bound, zero is threshold, and positive escapes.
This is the boundary between bound and escaping launches at the chosen starting radius.
A deeper well needs a larger threshold speed, while a shallower starting point needs a smaller one.
Local circular speed is a useful benchmark, but exceeding it alone does not guarantee escape.
When $E < 0$, this gives the maximum radius before the bound launch must turn around.

variableLinks.description: Deepens the gravity well, so both the local escape speed and the local circular-speed comparison rise.
Starting farther out means the potential is less negative, so less launch speed is needed to reach the threshold.
Changes only the launch kinetic term relative to the escape threshold, so it directly controls whether the start is bound, threshold, or escaping.

controls.description: Changes the depth of the gravity well and therefore the local escape threshold.
Sets how far from the source the outward launch begins. A larger starting radius means a shallower well at launch.
1.00 means the launch speed exactly matches the escape threshold for the current source mass and launch radius. Below 1 is bound; above 1 escapes.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the starting radius used by the threshold equations and the trail.
whatToNotice: Moving the launch point outward lowers the threshold speed immediately because the starting potential is less negative.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps $r_0$ visible as the launch condition.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the predicted finite turnaround radius for bound launches.
whatToNotice: This marker appears only when the total specific energy is negative.
At threshold or above, there is no finite turnaround radius to mark.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates a long outward trip from a true escape.

whatToNotice: A large outward speed at one instant is not enough to prove escape.
The energy sign still decides whether the launch must reverse later.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the speed comparison tied to the same moving state.

whatToNotice: Gravity keeps pointing inward even for threshold and escape launches.
overlays.whyItMatters: It corrects the idea that escape means gravity disappears.

whatToNotice: A long outward trail is not proof of escape. Bound launches can travel far before turning back.
overlays.whyItMatters: It lets the motion and the energy graph tell the same story.

graphs.description: Shows whether the launch keeps moving outward or approaches a finite maximum radius before turning back.
Compares the live speed with the local escape-speed and circular-speed benchmarks at the same radius.
Shows whether the total specific energy stays below zero, reaches zero, or becomes positive.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice about escape
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to separate three ideas that are easy to mix up: going far from the source, being above circular speed, and actually escaping.

items.text: The total-energy line gives the verdict: below zero is bound, zero is threshold, and above zero escapes.
items.tryThis: Switch between Threshold launch, High but bound, and Clear escape with the specific-energy graph open.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps escape tied to the energy sign instead of to distance alone.

items.text: A launch can be above the local circular-speed curve and still remain bound if it stays below escape speed and keeps negative total specific energy.
items.tryThis: Apply High but bound and compare the speed-threshold graph with the specific-energy graph before deciding whether the launch truly escapes.
items.whyItMatters: It separates the local circular benchmark from the actual escape threshold.

items.text: At the same launch radius, increasing source mass deepens the well and raises the threshold speed.
items.tryThis: Use Heavier source threshold and watch the launch-speed readout rise with the escape-speed curve while the total-energy line stays on zero.
items.whyItMatters: It ties the threshold directly to the depth of the gravity well.

items.text: Launching farther from the source lowers the threshold speed because the starting point sits higher in the gravitational well.
items.tryThis: Use Wider launch threshold and compare the new launch marker with the lower threshold speed and the less-negative starting potential energy.
items.whyItMatters: It turns the radius dependence into an energy-depth story instead of a memorized formula.

predictionMode.title: Predict the escape outcome
predictionMode.intro: Predict the energy verdict before you apply the preset. Then use the speed-threshold and specific-energy graphs to check the result.

items.explanation: With speed factor fixed at 1.00, the actual launch speed updates with the new escape threshold. A heavier source makes the well deeper and raises $v_{	ext{esc}}$, but the total specific energy still stays at zero.
