items.observationHint: The launch-speed readout rises with the escape-speed curve, while the total-energy line stays on zero.

items.explanation: Increasing $r_0$ makes the starting potential less negative, so less launch speed is needed to reach the zero-energy threshold. At speed factor 1.00 the total specific energy still stays at zero.
items.observationHint: The launch marker moves outward, the threshold speed drops, and the total-energy line stays near zero.

items.explanation: Escape depends on the sign of $E/m$, not on whether the launch exceeds the local circular-speed comparison. In this page the motion is radial, not a sideways orbit.
items.observationHint: The speed-threshold graph can show the launch above circular speed while the specific-energy graph still stays below zero.

challengeMode.intro: Use the same one-source launch bench to build bound, threshold, and escaping cases from the live energy logic.

paragraphs: A simple circuit needs three things: a voltage source to provide a push, a closed path so charge can move, and resistance that limits the current. Ohm's law links them: for the same resistance, a larger voltage drives a larger current; for the same voltage, a larger resistance gives a smaller current.

This module stays small on purpose. One battery drives two resistors, and the only structural change is whether they are in one series loop or in two parallel branches. That is enough to show how current, voltage, and equivalent resistance change together while the stage, readout, overlays, and graphs all describe the same live circuit.

keyIdeas: For one fixed topology and resistor setup, current is proportional to voltage and inversely proportional to equivalent resistance.
In series there is only one path, so the same current flows through both resistors and the battery voltage is shared between them.

In parallel both branches connect across the same two nodes, so each branch gets the full battery voltage, the branch currents depend on branch resistance, and the total current is the sum of the branch currents.

correction: Current is not used up by a resistor. In a steady series loop, the same amount of charge per second passes every point in the loop.
What changes across a resistor is voltage, not how much current is left. The resistor causes a voltage drop while the loop current stays the same unless the path splits into branches.

workedExamples.title: Work from the live circuit
workedExamples.intro: Use the values from the circuit now on screen. The same battery, resistor values, and topology determine the stage, readout, overlays, and graphs, so every calculation matches the live setup.

items.prompt: For the current circuit, what equivalent resistance does the battery see, and what total current follows from it?

steps.label: 1. Choose the correct equivalent-resistance rule for this topology
2. Substitute the live resistor values
3. Apply Ohm's law to the whole circuit
4. Calculate the total current

applyAction.label: Load uneven series divider
applyAction.observationHint: The same current passes through both resistors, but the larger resistor takes the larger voltage drop.

items.title: Resistor B current and voltage
items.prompt: For the current setup, what current goes through resistor B, and what voltage is across that resistor or branch?

steps.label: 1. Choose the rule for current through resistor B
2. Substitute the live values for I_B
3. Choose the matching voltage relation
4. Calculate V_B

applyAction.label: Load branch split
applyAction.observationHint: In parallel, the blue branch still has the full battery voltage, but it carries less current because its resistance is larger.

miniChallenge.prompt: Use the matched-resistor presets. Why is the total current larger in the parallel circuit than in the series circuit even though the battery and both resistor values are unchanged?
miniChallenge.prediction: Before switching topology, predict whether the battery will see a larger or smaller equivalent resistance.
miniChallenge.answer: In parallel, the battery sees a smaller equivalent resistance because charge has two paths instead of one long path. With the same battery voltage, Ohm's law then gives a larger total current.
miniChallenge.explanation: Series puts the resistors end to end, so their resistances add. Parallel gives charge two paths between the same two nodes, so the equivalent resistance is smaller. With the same source voltage, smaller equivalent resistance means larger total current.

quickTest.intro: Answer from circuit logic, then use the live setup to check yourself. Each question asks what the stage, readout, or graphs must mean.

equations.meaning: For one fixed resistance, doubling the voltage doubles the current.
Two parallel branches reduce the total load because charge has multiple paths between the same two nodes.
In a steady series loop, the current is the same at every point.
At a junction, the total current equals the sum of the branch currents.

controls.description: Off keeps one series loop. On gives charge two branches across the same battery.

graphs.description: Sweep the battery voltage while keeping the resistor values and topology fixed. Straight lines show that current changes linearly with voltage for one fixed circuit.
Sweep only resistor B while keeping the battery and resistor A fixed. In series the voltage shares depend on resistance ratio, while in parallel both branches stay at the battery voltage.

noticePrompts.intro: Use each prompt as a short investigation. Make the change, then connect what the circuit shows on the stage to what the graphs show.

items.text: For one fixed circuit, the current map stays linear because current is proportional to voltage.
items.whyItMatters: This is the clearest graph version of Ohm's law before extra circuit complexity is added.

items.text: In series, the same loop current passes through both resistors. Changing either resistor changes that same current everywhere in the loop.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the circuit response before you apply the change. Each scenario changes one cause at a time so you can track how voltage, current, and equivalent resistance are linked.

challengeMode.intro: Use the same compact battery-resistor model to build circuit targets. The checks come from the live stage, not a separate answer key.

items.title: Build a full-voltage parallel pair
items.prompt: Starting from the matched series pair, change only the circuit structure needed so each branch gets the full battery voltage and the total current is about 4 A.

paragraphs: Series and parallel circuits can use the same battery and the same two loads, but the path for charge is different. In series there is one loop, so the same current passes through both loads. In parallel there are two branches between the same nodes, so the current splits and recombines while each branch keeps the full battery voltage.

This module keeps just two loads and one arrangement toggle. That is enough to connect what you see on the stage to the main ideas: how equivalent resistance changes, how current and voltage are shared, why brightness follows power, and why the charge counters behave differently in series and parallel.

keyIdeas: In series there is only one path, so the same current passes through both loads and the battery voltage is shared between them.
In parallel both loads connect across the same two nodes, so each branch has the full battery voltage even when the branch currents are different.
Brightness follows power in this bounded model, so identical bulbs are dimmer in series and brighter in parallel because the power in each load changes with the arrangement.

commonMisconception.myth: Parallel bulbs are brighter because the battery somehow sends more voltage into each branch.
correction: The battery voltage does not get stronger when you switch to parallel. Each branch keeps the same battery voltage because both branches connect across the same two nodes.
What changes is the equivalent resistance of the whole circuit. A smaller equivalent resistance lets the same battery drive a larger total current, which changes the power in each load.

workedExamples.title: Work from the live circuit
workedExamples.intro: Use the exact arrangement, resistances, and inspected time shown on screen. The same live circuit drives the loads, counters, overlays, and graphs below, so every calculation matches the stage.

steps.label: 1. Choose the equivalent-resistance rule for the live arrangement
2. Substitute the two live load values
3. Apply Ohm's law to the whole circuit
4. Calculate the total current

applyAction.label: Load equal parallel pair
applyAction.observationHint: The same two loads now connect across the same battery directly, so each load keeps the full battery voltage while the total current rises.

items.title: Load B power and charge at the inspected time
items.prompt: For the current setup and inspected time, how much power is Load B taking right now, and how much charge has passed through Load B by this time?

steps.label: 1. Write the live rules for I_B and V_B
2. Substitute the live circuit values
3. Use P_B = I_B V_B to connect to brightness
4. Use Q_B = I_B t for charge passed

applyAction.label: Load unequal parallel pair
applyAction.observationHint: Load B keeps the full battery voltage in parallel, but its current, power, and charge counter grow more slowly because its resistance is larger.

miniChallenge.prompt: Two identical bulbs are connected to the same battery. Why do they both look dimmer in series than in parallel even though the battery has not changed?
miniChallenge.prediction: Before you switch the equal pair between arrangements, predict whether each bulb gets less current, less voltage, or both in series.
miniChallenge.answer: In series, the battery voltage is shared between the two bulbs, so the loop current is smaller and each bulb gets less power. In parallel, each bulb keeps the full battery voltage and therefore dissipates more power.
miniChallenge.explanation: Brightness in this bounded model follows power. Series raises the equivalent resistance and reduces the loop current, while parallel lowers the equivalent resistance and lets each branch keep the full battery voltage.

quickTest.intro: Answer from circuit logic, then check the stage if needed. Each question asks what the live circuit, counters, or graphs must mean.

questions.explanation: Equal loads in series share the battery voltage and therefore carry a smaller current than the equal parallel case. Less power per load means dimmer bulbs in this bounded model.
Equal branch voltage does not force equal branch current. Ohm's law says the lower-resistance branch draws the larger current.
In series, the same current crosses both loads, so equal time means equal charge passed even if brightness differs. In parallel, different branch currents make the counters diverge.

paragraphs: All three response graphs sweep only load B resistance while the battery voltage, load A, and arrangement stay fixed. The branch-current graph shows total current together with the two load currents, the branch-voltage graph shows how the two load voltages behave, and the load-power graph shows the brightness logic for both loads.

The time rail does not change the graph sweep. It lets the learner inspect one moment on the stage so charge counters and moving packets can be compared without changing the underlying response curves.

graphs.description: Sweep only load B while the battery voltage, load A, and arrangement stay fixed. In series all three current lines move together because one loop current passes through both loads. In parallel, load A stays fixed while load B and the total current respond to the changing blue branch resistance.

Sweep only load B while the battery voltage and load A stay fixed. In series the two load voltages trade off and still add to the battery voltage, while in parallel both branch voltages stay pinned to the battery.
Brightness follows power in this bounded model. Sweep load B to see how changing one resistance changes which load transfers energy faster in series and in parallel.

noticePrompts.intro: Use each prompt as a small investigation. Change one thing, then connect what the stage shows to what the graphs show.

items.text: Switching from series to parallel does not make the battery stronger. It changes the path structure and therefore the equivalent resistance seen by the battery.
items.whyItMatters: This keeps the cause of brighter parallel bulbs honest: the battery is unchanged, but the total load is smaller.

items.text: On the branch-current graph, series keeps one current everywhere, while parallel lets load B change on its own and makes the total current follow the branch sum.
items.whyItMatters: Current splitting becomes something you can read directly instead of memorizing as a detached rule.

items.text: On the branch-voltage graph, parallel branches stay at the battery value because both loads connect across the same two nodes. In series, the two load voltages trade off and still add to the battery.
items.whyItMatters: Equal branch voltage in parallel stops feeling mysterious once the shared-node picture and the graph agree.

items.text: The load-power graph is the brightness story. The arrangement decides whether the loads share the same current or the same voltage, so resistance alone does not predict brightness.
items.whyItMatters: This keeps bulb intuition tied to power rather than to one variable taken out of context.

items.text: Charge counters show the time consequence of current. In series both counters rise together; in parallel the branch with the larger current accumulates charge faster.
items.whyItMatters: It connects a current rule to a time-based outcome without introducing a separate hidden model.

predictionMode.intro: Predict before you change the arrangement or a load. Each scenario changes one idea at a time so you can track what must happen to current, voltage, and brightness.

items.prompt: The circuit starts as the equal series pair: 12 V with 6 ohm and 6 ohm in one loop. If you change only to parallel, which statement must be true?
items.explanation: Putting the same two loads in parallel reduces the equivalent resistance seen by the battery. The battery is unchanged, so the total current rises and each branch still spans the full battery voltage.
items.observationHint: The battery label stays fixed while both branch-voltage labels sit at the full battery value and the total current badge jumps.

items.explanation: Raising one series resistance raises the equivalent resistance, so the same battery drives less loop current. With the same current through both loads, the larger resistance gets the larger voltage drop.
items.observationHint: The current badge shrinks, the blue voltage label grows, and the branch-voltage graph tilts toward load B.

items.explanation: In parallel, each branch keeps the full battery voltage. Raising only R_B lowers only the blue branch current and power, while the amber branch remains set by its own unchanged resistance.
items.observationHint: The amber branch stays unchanged while the blue current, power, and charge rate all shrink.

challengeMode.intro: Use the same two-load circuit to hit branch-current and branch-voltage targets. The checks come from the live stage, so the topology rule stays tied to what you can actually see.

items.title: Give Load B full battery voltage
items.prompt: Starting from Unequal series loads, rewire the circuit so Load B keeps the full battery voltage while its branch current stays around 1 A.

setup.note: Use the branch-voltage graph and the node guide together while you rewire the unequal pair.

hints: Keep the 4 ohm and 12 ohm loads unchanged. The target comes from changing the arrangement, not the resistances.
If Load B really spans the same two nodes as the battery, its voltage should stay at the full battery value.

paragraphs: Equivalent resistance answers a practical question: what single resistor would make the battery behave the same way as this whole resistor group? If the source voltage is unchanged and the total current is unchanged, the replacement is honest.

In this module, R1 always stays outside a highlighted two-resistor group. Reduce that group first, then combine it with R1. That small structure is enough to show why reduction order matters, how series and parallel groups behave differently inside the same circuit, and how the final equivalent resistance changes the total current everywhere.

keyIdeas: Equivalent resistance is not just a shorter expression. It is the one-number load that gives the same battery voltage and the same total current as the original circuit.
Reduce the highlighted group first. Only after that group becomes one resistor should you combine it with the outer resistor R1.
A series group is identified by one shared current path, while a parallel group is identified by two shared nodes and one shared group voltage.

commonMisconception.myth: Equivalent resistance is only an algebra shortcut, so it does not tell you anything physically real about the original circuit.
correction: Equivalent resistance is defined by matching what the source experiences: the same voltage across the circuit and the same total current through it.
What can go wrong is the grouping, not the idea itself. You must first identify a pair that truly shares one series path or the same two nodes before replacing it.

workedExamples.title: Work from the live reduction
workedExamples.intro: Use the exact circuit on screen. The same resistor values, group mode, inspected time, overlays, and graphs drive the reduction steps below, so every calculation matches the live stage.

items.title: Reduce the group, then the whole circuit
items.prompt: For the current circuit, what does the highlighted pair reduce to, what total equivalent resistance does the battery see, and what total current follows?

steps.label: 1. Identify whether the highlighted pair is series or parallel
2. Reduce the highlighted pair first
3. Combine the reduced group with the outer resistor R1
4. Use the final equivalent resistance to find the total current

applyAction.label: Load balanced parallel group
applyAction.observationHint: Reduce the highlighted pair first. Its equivalent resistance drops sharply in parallel, and only then do you add R1 to get the full-circuit result.

items.title: Group voltage, R3 current, and charge passed
items.prompt: For the current setup and inspected time, what voltage is across the highlighted group, what current flows through R3, and how much charge has passed through R3 by this time?

steps.label: 1. Use the reduced block to find the group voltage drop
2. Choose the correct rule for current inside the highlighted group
3. Substitute the live values to find I_3 and V_3
4. Use the inspected time to find the charge through R3

applyAction.label: Load unequal parallel group
applyAction.observationHint: In the parallel group, R3 shares the full group voltage with R2, but its current and charge counter stay smaller because R3 is the larger resistance.

miniChallenge.prompt: The highlighted pair starts as two 6 ohm resistors in series with R1 = 4 ohm on the same 12 V battery. If you switch only the highlighted pair to parallel, why does the total current increase so strongly?
miniChallenge.prediction: Before you switch the group mode, decide which part of the circuit changes: R1, the highlighted pair, or both.
miniChallenge.answer: Only the highlighted pair changes. It goes from 12 ohm in series to 3 ohm in parallel, while R1 stays at 4 ohm. That makes the total equivalent resistance much smaller, so the same battery drives a much larger total current.
miniChallenge.explanation: Equivalent resistance matters because it changes the real source current. Here the big jump comes from changing the grouped pair before R1 is added, not from changing the battery or the outer resistor.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the live grouped circuit, not from detached formulas. Ask what the stage, reduction card, and graphs must all be saying at the same time.

questions.explanation: The highlighted pair is the legitimate first reduction step in this module. Only after it becomes one resistor should R1 be added.
The highlighted pair changes from 12 ohm in series to 3 ohm in parallel, while R1 remains 4 ohm. That lowers the total equivalent resistance and raises the total current.
In the unequal series group, the same current still passes through both grouped resistors. A larger voltage drop across R3 does not mean more charge per second passes through it.
The graph is plotting equivalent resistance. The vertical gap stays equal to the fixed contribution from R1.

paragraphs: All three graphs sweep only R3 while the battery, R1, R2, and the grouped-pair mode stay fixed. The reduction sweep shows the grouped equivalent and the final total equivalent, the current graph shows total current together with the grouped-resistor currents, and the voltage-share graph shows how the outer drop, grouped-block drop, and R3 drop change as R3 varies.
The time rail does not change the graph sweep. It lets the learner inspect one moment on the stage so the grouped charge counters can be compared without changing the response curves.

equations.meaning: Use direct addition only when the highlighted pair lies on one unbranched path and carries the same current.
Use the parallel rule only when the highlighted pair connects across the same two group nodes and shares one group voltage.
After you reduce the highlighted group to one resistor, R1 still adds in series with that reduced block.
The reduced group still has one definite voltage drop in the full circuit, and that drop becomes the shared branch voltage if the group is parallel.

variableLinks.description: R1 stays outside the highlighted pair, so it is never part of the first reduction step.
Changing R3 reshapes the grouped equivalent, the live branch behavior, and every response graph that depends on the highlighted group.

controls.description: This resistor always stays in series with the reduced group.
Off keeps the highlighted pair in series. On makes the highlighted pair parallel before it is combined with R1.

presets.description: The same resistor values now reduce through the parallel rule first, giving a much smaller grouped equivalent before R1 is added.
Both grouped branches share the same group voltage, but the lower-resistance branch draws more current and charge per second.

whatToNotice: R1 always carries the total current. The highlighted pair only splits that current when the pair is parallel.
overlays.whyItMatters: The simplified circuit should still agree with the live charge flow you can see.

overlays.shortDescription: Highlights the only pair you should reduce first and shows its live grouped equivalent.
whatToNotice: The highlighted pair is reduced first. R1 is not combined until that group has already become one resistor.
overlays.whyItMatters: This makes reduction order visible instead of turning it into a symbol trick.
The shared-node picture explains both the parallel reduction rule and the live branch behavior inside the group.

graphs.description: Sweep only R3 while keeping the battery, R1, R2, and the group mode fixed. First read the grouped-equivalent curve, then add the fixed outer resistor R1 to understand the total-equivalent curve.

Sweep only R3 while keeping the rest of the circuit fixed. In a series group, all currents stay locked together. In a parallel group, R2 and R3 split the grouped current while R1 still carries the total current.

Sweep only R3 while keeping the source, R1, R2, and the group mode fixed. The outer-resistor drop and the grouped-block drop always add to the battery voltage. In a parallel group, R3 gets the full group voltage; in a series group, it gets only part of that drop.

noticePrompts.intro: Keep one eye on the highlighted pair and one on the full circuit. The important question is always: what changes when that group is reduced honestly?

items.text: Start with the highlighted pair, not the whole circuit. Reduce only a group that truly shares one current path or one pair of nodes.
items.whyItMatters: Reduction order is the main new skill in this concept.

items.text: The total-equivalent curve stays a fixed amount above the grouped curve because R1 is added only after the highlighted pair has been reduced.
items.whyItMatters: The graph turns a two-step symbolic process into something you can see.

items.text: The current graph is the honesty check. In a series group, all currents match. In a parallel group, only the branch currents split while R1 still carries the total.
items.whyItMatters: Equivalent resistance should agree with the visible current flow, not replace it.

items.text: Charge counters show the time consequence of the group rule. In a series group, Q2 and Q3 rise together. In a parallel group, they separate because the branch currents differ.
items.whyItMatters: The reduction step changes a real time-based outcome, not just a static number.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the honest reduction before you switch the circuit. Each scenario changes one group feature at a time so the cause stays visible.

items.explanation: Only the highlighted pair changes. Switching it from series to parallel makes the grouped equivalent much smaller, so the final total equivalent drops and the battery current rises.
items.observationHint: Watch the grouped equivalent change first, then the total-current badge and graph respond.

items.explanation: In a series group, increasing R3 increases the grouped equivalent and the final total equivalent. The same current still flows through both grouped resistors, so the larger R3 also gets a larger share of the group voltage.
items.observationHint: The current falls everywhere, while the blue share of the grouped voltage grows.

items.explanation: In a parallel group, increasing R3 makes that branch weaker and increases the grouped equivalent. The whole circuit draws less total current, and the R3 branch current falls even though R3 still sees the group voltage.
items.observationHint: The blue branch current and blue charge counter shrink, even while the branch still shares the group's voltage.

challengeMode.intro: Use the same grouped circuit to hit reduction targets. The checks come from the live reduction card, current response, and voltage behavior, not from a separate answer sheet.

items.title: Build the 3 ohm parallel group
items.prompt: Starting from the balanced series group, change only the grouped connection until the highlighted pair is about 3 ohm and the full circuit is about 7 ohm.

setup.note: Use the reduction guide and the equivalent sweep together while you change only the grouped-pair mode.

hints: Keep the resistor values unchanged. The target comes from changing the grouped relationship, not the resistor sizes.
The grouped equivalent should drop below either grouped resistor before R1 is added back in series.

paragraphs: Electrical power tells you how quickly a circuit is transferring energy right now. In this one-load circuit, that rate comes directly from the same voltage, current, and resistance ideas used in Ohm's law.

This page keeps the model deliberately small: one source, one resistive load, and one time readout. That lets you track three linked ideas clearly: voltage and resistance set the current, current and voltage set the power, and a steady power rate builds more and more energy as time passes.

keyIdeas: Power is energy transferred each second, while energy is the accumulated total after that rate acts for some time.
For one fixed load resistance, current increases linearly with voltage, but power rises faster because both V and I increase together.
For one fixed source voltage, increasing the load resistance lowers current, lowers power, and makes the energy graph less steep in this ohmic-load model.

commonMisconception.myth: A larger resistance always means the load must use more power.
correction: For one fixed source voltage and one ohmic load, increasing the resistance limits the current more strongly.
Because the current falls, the power falls too in this model. A larger resistance does not automatically mean the load is transferring energy more quickly.

workedExamples.title: Work from the live circuit
workedExamples.intro: Use the values shown in the current setup. The same circuit state drives the stage, readout card, overlays, and graphs, so every calculation matches what you see on screen.

items.title: Current and power in the live setup
items.prompt: For the current source and load, what current flows and how much power is the load taking right now?

steps.label: 1. Use Ohm's law for the live load
2. Substitute the live voltage and resistance
3. Calculate the current
4. Use the power relation with the same live values
5. Calculate the load power

applyAction.label: Load boosted source
applyAction.observationHint: The same 8 ohm load draws more current, and the power graph rises much more strongly than the current graph.

items.title: Energy transferred by the inspected time
items.prompt: At the current inspected time, how much energy has the source delivered to the load?

steps.label: 1. Start with the energy relation
2. Substitute the live power and inspected time
3. Calculate the accumulated energy

applyAction.label: Load low-resistance heater
applyAction.observationHint: Lowering the resistance makes the energy graph steeper because the circuit is transferring more energy each second.

miniChallenge.prompt: Two runs use the same 12 V source and the same 8 ohm load. One runs for 3 s and the other for 9 s. Which run transfers more energy, and what quantity stays the same in both runs?
miniChallenge.prediction: Before answering, decide whether changing only the time changes the power or only the accumulated energy.
miniChallenge.answer: The 9 s run transfers three times as much energy because the load power stays the same and energy is power multiplied by time.
miniChallenge.explanation: With voltage and resistance fixed, the circuit keeps the same current and power. Letting it run longer does not change the rate; it just allows the same rate to keep adding energy for more seconds.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the live circuit logic, not from detached formulas. Use the stage, meters, and graphs to decide what must be true.

questions.explanation: Power is the transfer rate right now. Energy is the total amount transferred after that rate acts for some time.
At fixed resistance, current rises with voltage, so P = VI grows faster than either one alone.
At one fixed source voltage, larger resistance lowers current, so the load takes less power in this ohmic-load model.
If power stays fixed, energy grows directly with time through E = Pt.

paragraphs: The energy-transfer graph plots delivered energy against time for the current circuit. For one fixed setup, the line stays straight and its slope matches the current power.

The current-voltage and power-voltage graphs sweep source voltage while the load stays fixed, so they show the different current and power responses clearly. The power-resistance graph sweeps only load resistance at one fixed source voltage to show why larger resistance lowers power in this bounded model.

equations.meaning: In this one-load circuit, current is set by the source voltage and the load resistance.
At fixed resistance, doubling the voltage makes the power four times larger.
At fixed resistance, doubling the current makes the power four times larger.
If power stays constant, energy grows linearly with time.

variableLinks.description: At fixed load resistance, raising the source voltage increases current and makes the power graph rise faster than the current graph.
Changing the load resistance changes the current, the instantaneous power, and the slope of the energy graph for the same source.

controls.description: Raises or lowers the source voltage without changing the load itself.
Changes how strongly the load limits current in this one-load ohmic-circuit model.

presets.description: The same 8 ohm load is driven by a larger source voltage, so current and power both increase strongly.
With the same 12 V source, a larger load resistance gives a smaller current and lower power.
A smaller load resistance draws more current, takes more power, and makes the energy rise faster each second.

whatToNotice: For one fixed load, the current changes immediately when you change the voltage or the resistance.
overlays.whyItMatters: Power formulas only make sense when the current stays tied to the same visible circuit.

whatToNotice: For one fixed setup, the power bar stays constant even while the energy meter keeps growing.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the transfer rate right now from the total energy transferred so far.

whatToNotice: At fixed power, the energy meter grows by equal amounts in equal time intervals.
overlays.whyItMatters: Energy is the accumulated total, not a second copy of power.

graphs.description: For one fixed setup, the line is straight and its slope is the power. A steeper line means the circuit is transferring more energy each second.
Sweep the source voltage while keeping the load resistance fixed. The straight line shows the Ohm's-law response for this one load.
Sweep only the source voltage while the load stays fixed. The curve bends upward because power grows like V^2 for one fixed resistance.
Sweep only the load resistance while the source stays fixed. In this model, larger resistance lowers current and therefore lowers power.

noticePrompts.intro: Use each prompt as a short investigation. Make one change, then connect what the stage shows to what the graphs show.

items.text: On the energy graph, the slope is the power. A steeper line means more energy transferred each second, not just more time passing.
items.tryThis: Pause the run, compare the line slope for the steady-glow and low-resistance-heater setups, then compare the stage power bar.
items.whyItMatters: This is the clearest way to separate power from energy without leaving the same circuit.

items.text: With one fixed load, the current-voltage graph is a straight line because the resistance stays constant.
items.whyItMatters: Current is the link between the source setting and the power formulas.

items.text: The power-voltage graph bends upward because raising V also raises I in the same load, so power grows faster than linearly.
items.whyItMatters: It helps you see that power is not just another copy of current.

items.text: At one fixed source voltage, increasing the load resistance lowers both current and power in this model.
items.whyItMatters: This corrects the common mistake that a larger resistance number must mean the component uses more power.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the circuit response before you apply the change. Each scenario changes one cause at a time so current, power, and energy rate stay linked to the same setup.

items.explanation: At fixed resistance, current is proportional to voltage. Since P = VI, doubling both V and I makes the power four times larger.
items.observationHint: The current point doubles, but the power graph rises much more sharply.

items.explanation: With the source fixed, a larger resistance limits current more strongly. Lower current means lower power, so the circuit transfers energy more slowly each second.
items.observationHint: Watch the current label, power bar, and energy-graph slope all shrink together.

items.explanation: Energy transferred each second is power. At one fixed source voltage, the lower-resistance load draws the larger current and therefore the larger power.
items.observationHint: The lower-resistance setup has the steeper energy line because its power bar is higher.

challengeMode.intro: Use the same single-load circuit for compact power targets. The checks come from the live current and power, so rate and total stay connected to one honest setup.

items.title: Build a steady 18 W load
items.prompt: Starting from Gentle glow, keep the 8 ohm load and raise the source until the stage power bar is about 18 W.
items.successMessage: You hit the target without changing the load. The stronger source raised current and power together, exactly as the graph and stage should agree.

setup.note: Use the power-voltage graph and the stage power bar together while you raise the source.

hints: Do not change the load. The target comes from raising the voltage while the same 8 ohm resistor stays in place.
At the target, the current should be about 1.5 A at the same time the power is about 18 W.

paragraphs: An electric field answers a concrete question: if a tiny positive test charge were placed here, which way would it start moving, and how strongly? Source-charge sign sets the field direction, source strength and distance set the size, and the net field comes from adding all source vectors at one shared point.

In this module, one live setup drives everything at once: the stage arrows, scan graphs, worked examples, prediction prompts, quick test, and challenges all use the same source signs, separation, probe position, and test-charge sign. That keeps superposition tied to what you can actually change and observe.

keyIdeas: At any point, the electric field points in the direction a positive test charge would accelerate.
A stronger source or a nearer source contributes a larger field, so nearby charges often dominate the local vector sum.
The net field is found by adding the source contributions at the same probe point. The test charge changes the force through F = q_test E, not the field itself.

commonMisconception.myth: Making the test charge negative flips the electric field at the probe.
correction: The electric field is set by the source charges and the probe location, not by the sign of the test charge you use to sample it.
Changing to a negative test charge flips the force because F = q_test E, but the field arrows and field graphs stay the same until you move the probe or change a source charge.

workedExamples.title: Work from the live setup
workedExamples.intro: Read the values from the setup now on screen, then solve that exact case. The substitutions come from the same source signs, separation, and probe position shown on the stage, and the force example updates immediately when you change the test-charge sign.

items.title: Net field at the current probe point
items.prompt: Using the source pair and probe position now on screen, what is the net field vector at the probe?

steps.label: 1. Read the two source positions from the shared separation
2. Form the vectors from each source to the probe
3. Compute each source's field contribution at the probe
4. Add the two contributions at that same point

applyAction.label: Load the like-charge symmetry case
applyAction.observationHint: Above the midpoint, each positive source contributes an upward part and a horizontal part. The horizontal parts cancel, the upward parts add, and the graphs reflect the same symmetry.

items.title: Force on the current test charge
items.prompt: Using the current $q_{	ext{test}}$ and the net field, what force acts on the test charge?

steps.label: 1. Start with the force rule
2. Substitute the live test charge and net field
3. Compute the force components and magnitude

applyAction.label: Flip only the test-charge sign
applyAction.observationHint: The field arrows and field graphs stay fixed while the force arrow reverses, because only q_test changed.

miniChallenge.prompt: Keep both sources equal and positive. Start with the probe at the midpoint on the source axis, then move it straight upward. What happens to the horizontal contributions from the two sources, and why must the net field point straight up along that symmetry line?
miniChallenge.prediction: Before you drag the probe, predict which components cancel and which components add.
miniChallenge.answer: The horizontal contributions are equal in size and opposite in direction, so they cancel. The vertical contributions both point upward, so they add into a straight-up net field.
miniChallenge.explanation: Along the midpoint line, the probe is the same distance from both equal positive sources. Equal distance and equal charge give equal-magnitude contributions there, so only the shared upward component survives the vector sum.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the field picture, then confirm with the simulation if needed. Each question checks whether you can read direction, symmetry, superposition, or force from the same setup.

questions.prompt: A probe sits to the right of one isolated source charge. If the source changes from +2q to -2q without moving, what must happen to the electric field at the probe?
questions.explanation: At one fixed point, changing only the sign flips the field direction while keeping the same distance-based size trend.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: Distance helps set the size, but charge sign decides whether the field points away from or toward the source.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: A single negative charge does not cancel itself. It still creates a field, but the direction is opposite to a positive source.

showMeAction.label: Show isolated source sign flip
showMeAction.observationHint: With Source B set to zero, sweep Source A through zero and watch the field direction reverse at the same probe point.

questions.explanation: At the midpoint on the source axis, the geometry is perfectly symmetric. Each source contributes an equal horizontal field in the opposite direction, so the net field is zero there.

showMeAction.observationHint: At the midpoint on the axis, the two equal positive-source contributions are equal and opposite, so the net field collapses to zero.

questions.explanation: Above the midpoint of an equal dipole, both source contributions share a rightward component. Their vertical parts oppose, but the horizontal parts reinforce, so the net field points mostly to the right.

showMeAction.observationHint: Above the midpoint, both contributions share a rightward part, so the net field bends from the positive charge toward the negative charge.

questions.explanation: The electric field is set by the source charges and the location. A negative test charge feels a force opposite to the field, but it does not redefine the field.

showMeAction.label: Show force flip without field flip
showMeAction.observationHint: Change only the test charge and watch the force reverse while the field arrows and graphs stay fixed.

paragraphs: The stage keeps two source charges, one movable probe, field arrows, and the test-charge force arrow in one bounded view.

Drag the probe to sample a new point, drag either source to change the shared separation, or use the sliders for source signs, probe position, and test-charge sign. The same controls drive the stage and graphs.
Near a source, the arrows are capped for readability, but the trend remains: field strength rises quickly as distance shrinks.
The horizontal field-scan graph plots Source A, Source B, and net E_x along the current scan line. Where the net curve crosses zero, the horizontal contributions cancel.
The direction-and-strength graph plots net E_y and |E| on that same line. The force arrow can flip with q_test, but the graphs remain field-only readouts from the source charges.

equations.label: Symmetric source placement
equations.meaning: The separation control places Source A and Source B equally far to the left and right of the origin on the same axis.
At the probe, each source creates a radial field. The sign of q_i sets whether the field points away from or toward the source, and the size shrinks with distance.
At one fixed location, a larger |q| gives a larger field, while increasing r weakens the field rapidly.

equations.label: Net field by superposition
equations.meaning: Evaluate each source's field at the same probe point, then add the vectors.
Once the field is known, the test charge sets the force size and can reverse the force direction.

variableLinks.description: Moves the probe left or right and moves the graph marker to the same x-position on the current scan line.
Moves the probe to a different horizontal scan line, so both graphs switch to a new y-level.
Changes the force on the probe and can reverse its direction, but it does not change the source-generated field or the field graphs.

controls.description: Moves the probe to a different horizontal scan line, so both graphs update to a new y-level.
Changes only the force on the probe charge. It does not change the field created by the sources or the field graphs.

presets.description: Equal positive sources show why the horizontal parts cancel and the net field points straight up above the midpoint.
Equal positive sources cancel exactly at the midpoint on the source axis.
The source-generated field stays the same while the test-charge force reverses.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the net field direction at many sample points across the stage.
whatToNotice: Each small arrow already includes both source contributions at that location.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps superposition visible across the whole plane instead of only at the current probe.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows each source contribution and the net field at the probe.
whatToNotice: The two source arrows add to the teal net arrow at the probe.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns superposition into a visible vector addition at one point.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the force on the test charge without changing the source-generated field.
whatToNotice: Changing only q_test can reverse the force arrow while the field arrow stays fixed.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps separate electric field direction from force direction on a negative charge.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the horizontal slice that both graphs are currently plotting.
whatToNotice: Changing probeY changes the whole graph because both graphs are sampling a different y-level.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the graphs tied to the stage instead of making them seem like a separate calculation.

graphs.description: Shows Source A, Source B, and net horizontal field along the current scan line. A zero crossing marks horizontal cancellation at that x-position.

graphs.label: Vertical field and strength
graphs.description: Compares upward/downward field direction with total field strength along the same scan line, so peaks mean stronger fields and sign changes mean direction flips.

noticePrompts.intro: Use each prompt as a small investigation. Make the named change, then connect what you see on the stage to what changes in the graphs.

items.text: Charge sign chooses the radial direction first: positive sources send the field away, while negative sources pull it toward themselves.
items.tryThis: Set Source B to zero, then sweep Source A from positive to negative without moving the probe. Watch the field arrow reverse.
items.whyItMatters: Students often treat sign as a small correction, but it is the rule that sets the field direction.

items.text: The net scan curve is built point by point from the two source curves on the same y-level. It is not a separate extra rule.
items.tryThis: Open the horizontal scan graph, then move the probe along the dashed scan line. The stage marker and graph preview should stay locked to the same x-position.
items.whyItMatters: It turns superposition into something you can read directly from the graph, not just a sentence to memorize.

items.text: Changing only the test-charge sign flips the force arrow, not the field arrow or the field graphs.
items.whyItMatters: This cleanly separates the source-generated field from the force experienced by one chosen test charge.

items.text: Compare mode makes it easier to see which setup has the larger local contribution. A stronger or closer source pulls the net field toward its own direction.
items.whyItMatters: Comparing two real superposition cases makes it easier to judge which source dominates and why.

items.text: Changing source separation changes both source-to-probe distances, so the same probe point can shift from a balanced case to a one-source-dominated case.
items.tryThis: Keep the probe above the midpoint and widen the gap slowly. Watch the field arrows and graph peaks reshape together.
items.whyItMatters: It shows that geometry matters as much as charge size.

predictionMode.intro: Predict before you apply the change. Each scenario changes one cause at a time, so you can test how the stage arrows and linked graphs respond.

items.prompt: At one fixed probe point, what changes when Source B flips from positive to negative while Source A stays positive?

choices.label: Source B's contribution reverses direction, so the net field can rotate sharply even though the probe stays in the same place.

items.explanation: Changing the sign of a source charge reverses that source's radial field direction. The probe stays at the same place, but one contribution now points the opposite way, so the net vector can change sharply.
items.observationHint: Watch Source B's contribution reverse and the net field pivot even though the probe does not move.

items.prompt: If Source A becomes stronger while Source B stays the same and the probe remains between them, which statement is best supported?

choices.label: The net field leans more toward Source A because the larger source contributes a larger vector at the same probe point.

items.explanation: Superposition adds the actual vector contributions at the probe. If Source A becomes stronger, its contribution becomes larger, so the net field can change in both direction and size.
items.observationHint: Compare the two source arrows after the change. The larger Source A contribution should pull the vector sum toward itself.

items.prompt: If the sources and probe stay fixed but the test charge changes from positive to negative, what should you expect?
items.explanation: The source charges generate the field. The test charge only turns that field into a force through F = q_test E, so flipping q_test reverses the force but leaves the field unchanged.
items.observationHint: The force arrow flips, but the field arrow and field graphs do not.

challengeMode.intro: Use the live controls to hit field targets. Success depends on reading symmetry, superposition, and the difference between field and force from the stage and graphs.

items.title: Build a straight-up field
items.prompt: Starting from Dipole reference, adjust the source pair until the probe sees equal positive charges, zero horizontal field, and an upward net field.
items.successMessage: You reached the symmetry case: the left-right parts cancel at the probe, but the upward parts still add into one net field.

setup.note: Keep the direction scan and field vectors visible so you can check cancellation in x and reinforcement in y.

hints: Do not aim for a zero field. You want the sideways parts to cancel while the upward parts still add.
Equal positive charges and a slightly smaller separation than the dipole reference get you there quickly.

items.title: Reverse the force only
items.prompt: Starting from Like-charge arch, reverse the force on the test charge without changing the field at the probe.
items.successMessage: You changed only the test-charge response: the field still points up, but the force now points down because q_test is negative.

setup.note: Use the force overlay and the direction scan together to confirm that the field stayed fixed while only the force changed.

hints: If the field must stay the same, do not move the sources or the probe.
Only the test-charge control can reverse the force without changing the field graphs.

paragraphs: Electric potential tells you how much electric potential energy a positive test charge would have per unit charge at a location. Positive source charges raise the potential, negative source charges lower it, and nearby sources change it more strongly than distant ones.

Because potential is a scalar, you add signed values, not directions. That is why the midpoint between two equal positive charges can have zero electric field but still positive potential. Along the current scan line, the electric field is linked to the potential graph by slope: where V falls as x increases, E_x points in the positive x-direction.

keyIdeas: Electric potential is a scalar, so source contributions add by signed value rather than by vector direction.
A zero electric field means the local slope of potential is zero there. It does not automatically mean the potential itself is zero.
Along a horizontal scan line, the electric field depends on how quickly the potential changes with position: a steeper downhill slope means a stronger field.

correction: Field and potential are related, but they are not the same quantity. A zero field means the potential has zero local slope there, not that the potential value must be zero.

At the midpoint between two equal positive charges, the field cancels because the two field vectors are equal and opposite, but the potential stays positive because both scalar contributions are positive.

workedExamples.title: Work from the live setup
workedExamples.intro: Read the values from the setup now on screen, then solve that exact case. The same source signs, distances, and test-charge sign drive the potential map, the linked graphs, and the numerical results below.

items.title: Potential at the current probe point
items.prompt: Using the current source pair and probe position, what net electric potential is present at the probe?

steps.label: 1. Read the two source positions from the separation
2. Measure the probe's distance from each source
3. Compute each signed potential contribution
4. Add the scalar contributions

applyAction.label: Load the like-charge midpoint case
applyAction.observationHint: At the midpoint between two equal positive charges, the field arrow can vanish while the map stays warm and V remains positive.

items.title: Potential energy of the current test charge
items.prompt: Using the current $q_{	ext{test}}$ and the net potential, what potential energy does the test charge have at the probe?

steps.label: 1. Start with the energy relation
2. Substitute the live test charge and potential
3. Compute the potential energy

applyAction.label: Flip only the test-charge sign
applyAction.observationHint: The potential map and V graph stay fixed when only q_test changes, but U changes sign immediately.

miniChallenge.prompt: Two equal positive charges sit symmetrically on the x-axis, and the probe is exactly halfway between them on that same axis. Why can the potential there stay positive while the net electric field is zero?
miniChallenge.prediction: Before opening the midpoint preset, decide which quantity is controlled by scalar addition and which is controlled by vector cancellation.
miniChallenge.answer: The potential stays positive because both source contributions are positive scalars, but the electric field is zero because the two equal field vectors point in opposite directions and cancel.
miniChallenge.explanation: This midpoint is the cleanest place to separate potential from field without changing the geometry. Potential adds by signed value, while field adds by vector direction.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the live potential picture, not from detached formulas. Use the map, graphs, and field-potential link to decide what must be true.

questions.explanation: For one positive source, V = kq/r stays positive while moving the probe farther away makes the value smaller in magnitude.

showMeAction.observationHint: Move the probe farther from the positive source and watch the warm colors fade while the potential curve flattens toward zero.

questions.explanation: The two positive-source fields cancel as vectors at that midpoint, but the two positive potentials add as scalars.

showMeAction.observationHint: The field arrow disappears while the map and V readout stay positive.

questions.explanation: At the midpoint of an equal dipole, the positive and negative potentials cancel, but the field contributions both point from the positive source toward the negative source and therefore add.

showMeAction.observationHint: The readout shows V near zero while the field arrow still points from the positive source toward the negative source.

questions.explanation: A downward slope means dV/dx is negative, so the minus sign in E_x = -dV/dx makes E_x positive.
The source charges set the potential. The test charge only tells you what potential energy one chosen charge would have there.

showMeAction.observationHint: The map and graphs stay fixed while the potential-energy readout changes sign.

paragraphs: The simulation shows two source charges on a horizontal axis, a movable probe inside a bounded potential region, and optional overlays for a signed potential map, equipotential contours, a field arrow at the probe, and the horizontal scan line used by the graphs.

Dragging the probe changes the sampled location directly on the stage, while dragging either source marker changes the shared source separation symmetrically. Sliders provide the same controls for source-charge sign and size, separation, probe position, and test-charge sign.

Color saturation in the potential map is clipped near a source for readability, but the readout and graphs still preserve the correct trend that the potential magnitude grows rapidly as the probe approaches a charge.

The potential-scan graph plots Source A's contribution, Source B's contribution, and the net potential along the current horizontal scan line. Hovering the graph previews that same x-location on the stage.

The field-link graph plots the net horizontal field and the matching negative slope of the potential graph along that same scan line. The test-charge sign changes the potential-energy readout, but the graphs remain source-only views of V and E.

equations.meaning: Each source contributes a signed scalar potential. Positive charges raise V, negative charges lower V, and the magnitude decreases with distance.

equations.label: Scalar superposition of potential
equations.meaning: At one point, net potential is the signed sum of the source contributions.
The electric field points in the direction of the steepest decrease in potential.
Along the current horizontal slice, the x-component of the field is the negative slope of the potential graph.
Changing the sign of the test charge changes the sign of U without changing the potential set by the source charges.

variableLinks.description: Moves both sources symmetrically on the axis, changing the source-to-probe distances and the slope of the potential landscape.
Moves the probe left or right and moves the graph preview to that same x-location on the current scan line.
Moves the probe to a new horizontal slice, so the map, contours, and graphs all sample a different y-level.
Changes only the potential energy through U = qV. It does not change the potential map, contours, or field graph.

controls.description: Moves both sources symmetrically, changing distances and reshaping the potential landscape.
Moves the probe left or right across both the stage and the linked scan graphs.
Moves the probe to a different horizontal scan line, so the graphs sample a new y-level.
Changes only the potential energy U = qV, not the potential map or the field graph.

presets.description: Equal positive charges show that the field can be zero at the midpoint while the potential stays positive.
Equal and opposite charges make the midpoint a clean check that zero potential does not imply zero field.
The source-generated potential stays fixed while the test charge makes the potential energy change sign.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows signed potential across the stage, with warm colors for positive V and cool colors for negative V.
whatToNotice: Each point on the map already includes both source contributions.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns potential difference into a visible change across one shared landscape instead of a detached number.

overlays.shortDescription: Draws lines of equal potential across the stage.
whatToNotice: The field arrow crosses these contours instead of pointing along them.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you see that the field comes from the slope of the potential, not from a separate unrelated rule.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the net electric field at the probe as the downhill direction on the potential map.
whatToNotice: At a balance point, the field arrow can vanish even while the potential readout stays nonzero.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the link E = -grad V tied to the same live probe state.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the horizontal slice used by the linked graphs.
whatToNotice: Graph hover and stage preview refer to the same x-location on this line.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the graph tied to one real spatial slice through the map.

graphs.label: Potential along the scan line
graphs.description: Source A, Source B, and the net potential are sampled along the same horizontal scan line. The vertical gap between two x-positions is a potential difference.

graphs.label: Field-slope link
graphs.description: Along the current horizontal slice, the net horizontal field matches the negative slope of the potential graph. Hovering previews that same x-location on the stage.

noticePrompts.intro: Treat each prompt as a small investigation. Make the named change, then connect what you see on the map to what changes in the readout and graphs.

items.text: Source sign decides whether a nearby region is positive or negative potential before distance decides how large the value is.
items.tryThis: Turn Source B off, then drag Source A from positive to negative and watch the warm hill switch to a cool well.
items.whyItMatters: Potential-sign mistakes often come from forgetting that a negative source lowers V instead of making a smaller positive value.

items.text: Potential difference is the vertical gap between two points on the same V(x) curve, not a separate object.
items.tryThis: Hover two x-locations on the potential graph and compare the stage preview, the readout, and the height difference on the curve.
items.whyItMatters: Voltage becomes easier to read when you treat it as a change between two positions on one shared graph.

items.text: The field arrow points downhill on the potential map. Along this horizontal slice, the E_x curve follows the negative slope of V(x).
items.whyItMatters: This is the key bridge between the scalar potential picture and the vector field picture.

items.text: At the midpoint between equal positive charges, the field can be zero while the potential stays positive.
items.tryThis: Use the like-charge midpoint preset and compare the warm map color, the positive V readout, and the vanished field arrow.
items.whyItMatters: It separates scalar superposition from vector cancellation in one clean setup.

items.text: Changing only q_test flips the sign of potential energy, not the potential map or the field-link graph.
items.whyItMatters: It separates source-set potential from the energy of one chosen test charge.

predictionMode.intro: Predict first, then apply the change. Each scenario changes one cause at a time so you can test what the map, contours, and graphs should do.

items.prompt: At one fixed probe point, what changes if Source B flips from positive to negative while Source A stays positive?

choices.label: Source B's contribution changes sign, so the net potential can shift sharply and the nearby map can switch from warm to cool.
Only the field changes because potential does not depend on source sign.
Nothing changes because the source stayed the same distance from the probe.

items.explanation: Potential is signed. Flipping a source charge changes that source's contribution from positive to negative or vice versa, so the net potential can change strongly even when the geometry stays fixed.
items.observationHint: Watch the region near Source B flip sign when the blue source changes sign.

items.prompt: If both sources are equal and positive, then the probe moves to the exact midpoint on the source axis, which statement is best supported?
items.explanation: At the midpoint, the two positive-source fields are equal and opposite, so they cancel. But the two potentials are both positive scalars, so they add and leave a positive V.
items.observationHint: This is the cleanest place to see a warm map, a positive V readout, and a vanished field arrow at the same time.

items.prompt: If the sources and probe stay fixed but the test charge flips from positive to negative, what should happen?
items.explanation: The source charges set V and E. The test charge only multiplies that existing potential to produce U = qV, so flipping q_test changes the energy sign without changing the map.
items.observationHint: The potential-energy readout changes sign while the map and graphs stay fixed.

challengeMode.intro: Use the live controls to build a specific potential-field situation. Success depends on reading both the map and the field link from the same setup.

items.title: Build a positive midpoint with zero field
items.prompt: Starting from Dipole reference, adjust the setup until the midpoint has almost zero field but still clearly positive potential.
items.successMessage: You found the clean midpoint case where vector cancellation makes the field vanish while scalar addition keeps the potential positive.

setup.note: Use the field-link graph, the field arrow, and the potential map together while you tune the midpoint.

hints: You need the midpoint between two equal positive charges, not the midpoint of a dipole.
A nearly zero field means the local slope is almost flat. The map can still remain positive there.

paragraphs: A magnetic field around a long straight wire does not point along the wire. It circles around the wire. Current direction sets the circulation sense through the right-hand rule, current size sets the field strength, and distance matters because the field gets weaker as you move farther from the wire.

This module keeps one bounded two-wire stage, one movable probe, and one linked scan line. The same current directions, current sizes, wire spacing, and probe position drive the field loops, probe vectors, scan graphs, worked examples, prediction prompts, and quick test, so the field pattern always stays tied to its sources.

keyIdeas: In this model, positive current means out of the page and gives counterclockwise magnetic circulation, while negative current means into the page and gives clockwise circulation.
At the same distance from a wire, a larger current gives a larger magnetic field. At the same current, points farther from the wire have a weaker field.
The net magnetic field at the probe comes from adding the two wire contributions at that same location.

commonMisconception.myth: The magnetic field near a current-carrying wire points along the wire.
correction: For a long straight wire, the magnetic field circles around the wire. At any point it is tangent to a circle centered on the wire, not parallel to the wire.
Current direction chooses clockwise or counterclockwise circulation through the right-hand rule, but the field direction at the probe still depends on where the probe is around the wire.

workedExamples.title: Work from the live setup
workedExamples.intro: Use the current wire currents, spacing, and probe position directly from the stage. The same live values drive the probe arrows, the scan graphs, and the calculations below.

items.title: Net magnetic field at the current probe point
items.prompt: Using the wire pair and probe position now on screen, what is the net magnetic field vector at the probe?

steps.label: 1. Read the wire positions from the separation
2. Form the wire-to-probe position vectors
3. Find each wire's magnetic-field contribution
4. Add the two contributions at the probe

applyAction.label: Load opposite-current lift
applyAction.observationHint: Above the midpoint, opposite current directions can make the horizontal parts cancel while the vertical parts add into a strong upward field.

items.title: Which wire dominates at the probe?
items.prompt: For the current wire pair, which wire contributes more strongly at the probe, and how does that set the net field direction?

steps.label: 1. Use current direction to set each circulation sense
2. Compare the sizes of the two contributions
3. Read the net direction from the vector sum

applyAction.label: Load same-current midpoint cancel
applyAction.observationHint: At the midpoint between equal same-direction currents, the two tangent directions oppose exactly, so the net field drops to zero even though each wire still makes a field.

miniChallenge.prompt: Keep both currents equal and out of the page, then place the probe exactly halfway between the wires on the same horizontal axis. What must happen to the two magnetic-field contributions there, and why can the net field be zero even though neither wire produces zero field on its own?
miniChallenge.prediction: Before moving the probe, predict which tangent directions oppose and why their magnitudes match.
miniChallenge.answer: The two contributions are equal in size and opposite in direction at that midpoint, so they cancel and the net field is zero there.
miniChallenge.explanation: The midpoint is the same distance from both equal-current wires, so the two contribution magnitudes match. At that particular location their tangent directions point opposite ways, so the vector sum is zero.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the field pattern, not from memorized hand signs. Each question asks what the stage and graphs must mean about circulation, superposition, or field strength.

showMeAction.label: Show one-wire current flip
showMeAction.observationHint: Turn Wire B off, then drag Wire A through zero and watch the field direction reverse at the same probe point.

questions.explanation: At the midpoint on the shared axis, the geometry is perfectly symmetric. Each wire contributes an equal tangential field in the opposite direction, so the vector sum is zero there.
Above the midpoint of equal opposite currents, the horizontal parts cancel on the symmetry line while the vertical parts add, so the net field points straight up.

paragraphs: The horizontal field-scan graph plots Wire A's horizontal contribution, Wire B's horizontal contribution, and the net horizontal magnetic field along the current scan line. Hovering the graph previews the same x-location on the stage.

The direction-and-strength graph plots the net vertical component and the total magnetic-field strength along that same scan line. Both graphs are field-only readouts determined by the wire currents and the current probe location.

equations.meaning: In this model, positive current means out of the page and negative current means into the page, so the right-hand rule sets counterclockwise or clockwise circulation.
Each wire contributes a tangent magnetic field around itself. Current sign sets the circulation sense, and the field stays perpendicular to the radial line from the wire to the probe.
At one point, a larger current gives a larger field, while greater distance makes the field weaker.

equations.label: Net field by superposition
equations.meaning: Add the two wire contributions at the same probe point to get the net magnetic field.

variableLinks.description: Moves the probe left or right and moves the graph preview to that same x-position on the current scan line.
Moves the probe to a different horizontal scan line, so both graphs sample a new y-level.

controls.description: Changing Wire B changes its circulation sense and strength, so the second contribution can reinforce, cancel, or reverse the net magnetic field.
Moves the probe to a different horizontal scan line, so both graphs sample a new field slice.

presets.description: Equal and opposite currents above the midpoint make a clean symmetry case: horizontal parts cancel while vertical parts add.
Equal out-of-page currents above the midpoint show strong horizontal reinforcement.
Reversing both currents keeps the same geometry but flips every field direction.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows circular guide loops around each wire so the right-hand rule has a visible shape.
whatToNotice: Dot markers go with counterclockwise circulation, while cross markers go with clockwise circulation.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes current direction and field circulation part of the same picture.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the net magnetic-field direction at many sample points across the stage.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps superposition visible across the whole plane instead of only at the current probe.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows each wire contribution and the net magnetic field at the probe.
whatToNotice: The two source arrows add to the teal net arrow at the same probe point.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns magnetic superposition into a visible vector addition.
It keeps the graphs tied to a real slice through the stage.

graphs.description: Shows how Wire A and Wire B contribute to B_x along the current scan line, and how the net curve is their point-by-point sum.

graphs.label: Vertical field component and strength
graphs.description: Compares the net vertical component and total magnetic-field strength along the same scan line shown on the stage.

noticePrompts.intro: Treat each prompt as a small investigation. Change one thing, then connect the loop picture, the probe arrow, and the graphs.

items.text: Current direction chooses the circulation sense first: out-of-page current gives counterclockwise circulation, and into-page current gives clockwise circulation.
items.tryThis: Set Wire B to zero, then sweep Wire A from positive to negative without moving the probe. Watch the loop direction and probe arrow reverse together.
items.whyItMatters: This prevents the common mistake of guessing the field direction without first setting the circulation sense.

items.text: The net scan curve is the point-by-point sum of the two wire-contribution curves on the same y-level.
items.tryThis: Open the horizontal scan graph and move the probe along the dashed scan line. Keep the stage marker aligned with the graph preview.
items.whyItMatters: It turns magnetic superposition into something you can read directly, not just remember.

items.text: Above the midpoint, opposite currents can make the horizontal parts cancel while the vertical parts add into a straight-up or straight-down net field.
items.whyItMatters: It is the cleanest symmetry case for reasoning about field direction without guessing.

items.text: Changing wire separation changes both wire-to-probe distances, so the same probe point can move from a balanced case to one-wire dominance.
items.tryThis: Keep the probe above the midpoint and widen the gap slowly. Watch the field arrows and graph peaks change together.
items.whyItMatters: It shows that geometry matters as much as current size.

predictionMode.intro: Predict before you apply the change. Each scenario changes one cause at a time so the stage and graphs can confirm the same magnetic-field story.

items.prompt: At one fixed probe point, what changes if Wire B flips from out of the page to into the page while Wire A stays out of the page?
items.explanation: Changing the sign of one current reverses that wire's circulation sense. The probe stays at the same place, but one contribution now points the opposite way, so the net vector can change sharply.
items.observationHint: Watch Wire B's loop direction and contribution arrow reverse.

items.prompt: If Wire A becomes stronger while Wire B stays the same and the probe remains between them, which statement is best supported?
items.explanation: Superposition adds the actual local vectors at the probe. If Wire A becomes stronger, its tangent contribution becomes larger, so the net field can change in both direction and size.

items.prompt: If both wire currents reverse while the geometry stays fixed, what should happen at the probe?
items.explanation: Each wire contribution is proportional to current. Reversing both currents multiplies both contributions by -1, so the net field reverses direction while keeping the same magnitude at the same probe point.
items.observationHint: The net arrow flips direction while |B| stays the same.

challengeMode.intro: Use the same two-wire stage to build target field patterns. Success depends on reading symmetry, circulation sense, and vector addition from the live stage.

items.title: Build a straight-up field
items.prompt: Starting from Same-current sweep, reverse Wire B so the sideways parts nearly cancel while the net magnetic field points strongly upward above the midpoint.
items.successMessage: You built the symmetry case: sideways parts cancel at the probe, but the upward parts still add.

setup.note: Use the direction scan and the probe vectors together while you tune the two-wire setup.

items.title: Compare upward lift and midpoint cancel
items.prompt: Open compare mode from Opposite-current lift. Keep Setup A on the above-midpoint upward case, but turn Setup B into the midpoint-cancel case where the net field nearly vanishes even though the current magnitudes still match.

setup.note: Open compare mode from the lift case, then edit only Setup B.

paragraphs: Electromagnetic induction is about change. A magnetic field through a loop is not enough by itself. The coil develops an emf only when the magnetic flux linkage through it changes, and Lenz's law fixes the sign so the induced response opposes the change that produced it.

This module keeps one moving-magnet-and-coil picture in charge. One bar magnet passes one coil on one shared axis. The same magnet position, speed, pole orientation, coil turns, and coil area determine the stage, the galvanometer, the current arrows, the flux graph, and the induced-response graph, so Faraday's law and Lenz's law stay tied to one changing setup.

keyIdeas: Induced emf depends on changing flux linkage $\lambda = N\Phi$, not on magnetic field strength alone.
A coil can link strong magnetic flux and still have zero induced emf if the flux linkage is not changing at that instant.
More turns, larger coil area, stronger magnetic coupling, or faster relative motion can increase the induction signal because they increase the flux linkage or how quickly it changes.

The sign of emf and current is a direction label: positive and negative mean opposite loop directions in the stage convention, and Lenz's law chooses the direction that opposes the current flux change.
Reversing the motion direction or flipping which pole faces the coil reverses the sign of the induced emf and current, because Lenz's law makes the response oppose the change in flux.

commonMisconception.myth: If the magnetic field through the coil is large, the induced emf must also be large.
correction: A large field can give a large flux linkage, but induction depends on the rate of change of flux linkage. A flat flux curve gives zero emf even when the field is strong.
That is why the induced-response graph crosses zero when the magnet is centered in a symmetric pass: the flux linkage is momentarily at an extremum, so its slope is zero.

workedExamples.title: Work from the live pass
workedExamples.intro: Use the live magnet pass directly. The same magnet position, speed, pole orientation, coil turns, and coil area set the stage, the galvanometer, the graphs, and the calculations below.

items.title: Flux linkage, emf, and current right now
