items.title: Find the refractive index for the selected wavelength

steps.label: 1. Use the wavelength-dependent index model
2. Evaluate the wavelength factor
3. Calculate the selected refractive index

applyAction.observationHint: Violet uses a larger refractive index than red, so its ray leaves the prism with the greatest downward deviation.

items.title: Find the red-violet angular spread
items.prompt: For the current material and prism angle, how much angular separation should there be between red and violet as they leave the prism?

steps.label: 1. Find the red deviation
2. Find the violet deviation
3. Subtract to get the spread

applyAction.observationHint: A stronger wavelength dependence and a larger prism angle both widen the outgoing color fan.

miniChallenge.prompt: You keep the same prism angle and the same green reference index, but you raise the dispersion strength. Before you touch the controls, which color should separate furthest from the others and why?
miniChallenge.prediction: Answer from the wavelength-dependent refractive index, not from brightness.
miniChallenge.explanation: Raising the dispersion strength increases the difference between short- and long-wavelength refractive indices. Violet then uses the largest refractive index in the visible range, so it bends the most and pulls furthest from red.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the wavelength-dependent refraction story, not from a vague picture of a rainbow beam.

questions.explanation: Dispersion means refractive index depends on wavelength. In this visible range, violet uses the largest refractive index and therefore the greatest prism deviation.
With zero dispersion, all visible wavelengths use the same refractive index. The prism still refracts the beam, but it no longer separates colors.
In the thin-prism model, deviation is proportional to prism angle. A larger prism angle scales every color's deviation and increases the red-violet spread.
A prism separates wavelengths already present in the beam because each wavelength can follow a slightly different refractive index in the same material.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one thin triangular prism with an incoming white beam, one highlighted selected-color ray, and optional red, green, and violet comparison rays leaving the same prism. A readout card lists wavelength, reference index, dispersion strength, selected refractive index, selected deviation, speed fraction, red-violet spread, and prism angle.

Optional overlays show the outgoing color fan, the current red-green-violet index ordering, and the thin-prism guide. The stage slightly magnifies the outgoing fan for visibility, while the card and graphs keep the real angle values.
One graph plots refractive index against visible wavelength for the current material. The other plots prism deviation against the same wavelengths for the current prism angle.
Hovering either graph previews another wavelength on the same prism rather than advancing time.

equations.meaning: Gives one material a wavelength-dependent refractive index while staying anchored at a reference wavelength near green.
If one wavelength uses a larger refractive index, it travels more slowly in the material.
For a thin prism, the total bend for a given color grows with both refractive index and prism angle.
Red-violet separation grows when the material is more dispersive or when the prism angle is larger.

variableLinks.description: Moves the selected color from red toward violet so you can see refractive index, speed, and prism deviation change together.
Sets the baseline refractive index near green, so it shifts the whole bending family up or down together.
Controls how far apart red, green, and violet sit on the refractive-index curve, and therefore how wide the prism fan becomes.
Scales the total bend of every color without changing their wavelength order.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows red, green, and violet leaving the prism together so their angular separation is easy to compare.
whatToNotice: Violet sits below red because it uses the larger refractive index and therefore the larger prism deviation.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns dispersion into a visible difference in refraction angle instead of a vocabulary list about prisms.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows how red, green, and violet are ordered by refractive index in the current material.
whatToNotice: The color order on the stage comes from the ordering of refractive indices, not from a separate color rule.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps one wavelength-dependent refractive-index story behind both the stage and the graphs.

whatToNotice: Changing the prism angle scales every color's deviation while keeping the same red-to-violet ordering.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates overall bending from wavelength-dependent spreading.

graphs.description: Shows how the current material assigns slightly different refractive-index values across visible wavelengths.
Shows how the prism deviation changes across visible wavelengths, so color spread can be read as angle differences instead of only seen on the stage.

noticePrompts.intro: Keep one cause in view at a time. The prism fan, readout card, and graphs are all showing the same wavelength-dependent refraction model.

items.text: Notice that violet bends more than red because violet uses the larger refractive index in this model. The color order on the stage comes from the index curve.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps the prism fan tied to refraction instead of to a separate color rule.

items.text: Notice that a prism can still bend the beam when dispersion is set to zero. What disappears is the color spread, not the overall refraction.
items.whyItMatters: It separates overall bending from wavelength-dependent separation.

items.text: Notice that increasing the prism angle scales the whole bend family upward. The red-violet order stays the same, but every outgoing ray shifts farther.
items.whyItMatters: It shows why geometry and material response both matter in prism spreading.

items.text: Compare mode works best when one setup changes only the material response and the other keeps the same prism geometry. That makes color spread easier to separate from overall bending.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps two different causes from blending into one vague idea of a stronger prism.

predictionMode.intro: Predict how the same prism should respond before you change wavelength, material response, or prism angle.

items.explanation: Raising the dispersion strength increases how much the refractive index changes between red and violet. Because the reference index stays anchored near green, the green bend changes only a little while the color spread widens a lot.
items.observationHint: Watch the spread bracket and the index guide together after you apply the stronger-dispersion setup.

items.explanation: In this bounded model, shorter visible wavelengths use a larger refractive index. The same prism therefore bends violet more than red.
items.observationHint: Apply the violet shift and compare the selected ray with the fixed red and green fan.

items.explanation: Increasing the prism angle scales every deviation, so the whole fan opens more while the color order stays the same.
items.observationHint: Watch the deviation graph rise and the spread bracket lengthen after you widen the prism.

paragraphs: Total internal reflection is not a separate rule that replaces refraction. It is the limiting case of Snell's law when light tries to go from a higher-index medium into a lower-index medium and the transmitted angle would have to be larger than 90°.

Use one live boundary to watch that transition. As you raise the incident angle while $n_1 > n_2$, the transmitted ray bends farther away from the normal, reaches $\theta_2 = 90^\circ$ at the critical angle, and then disappears as a real transmitted ray above that threshold. The stage, readout, and graphs are all showing the same handoff from ordinary refraction to full internal reflection.

keyIdeas: Total internal reflection is only possible when light starts in the higher-index medium and tries to enter the lower-index medium.
At the critical angle, the transmitted ray just skims along the boundary, so $\theta_2 = 90^\circ$.
Above the critical angle, Snell's law gives no real transmitted angle, so the ray stays in medium 1 and reflects.
A larger index contrast lowers $\theta_c$, so the same incident angle is more likely to produce total internal reflection.

commonMisconception.myth: Any large incident angle can produce total internal reflection.
correction: Angle alone is not enough. The light must be traveling from higher $n$ to lower $n$ so that a real critical angle exists.
If $n_1 \le n_2$, this model still gives a real transmitted angle even at very large incident angles, so there is no TIR threshold to cross.

workedExamples.title: Live threshold checks
workedExamples.intro: Use the same indices and incident angle shown on the stage. The numbers you substitute are the same ones controlling the ray diagram and the threshold graphs.

items.title: Does this boundary have a critical angle?
items.prompt: For the current media pair, does a critical angle exist, and if it does, what is its value?

steps.label: 1. Check whether the ray starts in the higher-index medium
2. Use the critical-angle relation if it exists
3. Evaluate the threshold

applyAction.label: Try near-critical glass to air
applyAction.observationHint: The transmitted ray is still real, but it is almost parallel to the boundary.

items.title: Decide whether this setup is below or above threshold

steps.label: 1. Compare $\theta_1$ with $\theta_c$
2. Find how far the setup is from the threshold
3. Decide what outgoing ray can still exist

applyAction.label: Try above-critical glass to air
applyAction.observationHint: Above $\theta_c$, the transmitted branch is gone and only the reflected ray remains in medium 1.

miniChallenge.prompt: Keep the incident angle fixed above the glass-to-air critical angle, then raise $n_2$ closer to $n_1$. Before you test it, what should happen to the critical angle and to the boundary outcome?
miniChallenge.explanation: Raising $n_2$ toward $n_1$ weakens the index contrast. Since $n_2 / n_1$ becomes larger, $\theta_c$ increases, and a setup that used to be above threshold can return to ordinary refraction.

quickTest.intro: Reason from the threshold, not from a slogan. Use the indices, the critical angle, and the outgoing branch that still exists.

questions.explanation: TIR needs both conditions together: the light must try to leave the higher-index medium, and $\theta_1$ must be larger than the critical angle for that media pair.
If $n_2$ gets smaller while $n_1$ stays fixed, the ratio $n_2 / n_1$ gets smaller, so $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2 / n_1)$ also gets smaller. The threshold is easier to exceed.
Just below $\theta_c$, Snell's law still gives a real $\theta_2$. The transmitted ray survives, but it is nearly parallel to the boundary.
When light starts in the lower-index medium, this model never reaches a critical-angle cutoff. The transmitted ray remains real.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one boundary between two media with a dashed normal at the contact point. One ray approaches from medium 1. Below threshold, a transmitted ray enters medium 2; above threshold, only a reflected ray remains in medium 1.
Optional overlays show the angle markers, the critical-angle threshold, the equal-angle reflection cue, and speed information linked to the current refractive indices.

The threshold transition graph plots outgoing angle against incident angle for the current media pair. The refracted branch reaches 90° at $\theta_c$, and beyond that only the reflected branch remains.
The transmitted-angle graph isolates the ordinary-refraction branch below threshold, so hovering it previews different boundary setups rather than later times.

equations.meaning: This is the same refraction law used below threshold, and it is the law that stops giving a real $\theta_2$ above the critical angle.
This is the largest incident angle that still allows a real transmitted ray when light goes from higher $n$ to lower $n$.
In total internal reflection, the ray remains in medium 1 and the reflected angle still equals the incident angle.

controls.description: Measured from the normal. Increasing it pushes the setup toward, and then possibly beyond, the critical angle.
Changing $n_1$ changes the speed contrast. Higher $n_1$ relative to $n_2$ lowers the critical angle.
Changing $n_2$ moves the threshold. Raising $n_2$ toward $n_1$ raises $\theta_c$; lowering it makes TIR easier to reach.

presets.description: The transmitted ray is still real but almost parallel to the boundary.
The incident angle is above $\theta_c$, so no real transmitted ray remains.
The weaker contrast raises $\theta_c$, so the same 50° ray refracts again.
The milder contrast still allows TIR, but at a larger critical angle.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the normal and the angle markers used on this page.
whatToNotice: The incident and transmitted angles are measured from the normal, not from the surface.
overlays.whyItMatters: Many TIR mistakes come from measuring the wrong angle.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the threshold where the transmitted ray would just run along the boundary.
whatToNotice: The guide only exists when light starts in the higher-index medium.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the change from refraction to TIR visible on the same boundary.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the equal-angle reflection that remains after the threshold is crossed.
whatToNotice: Above $\theta_c$, there is no real transmitted ray, so the reflected ray is the only outgoing branch in this model.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links TIR to the ordinary law of reflection without pretending refraction still survives.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the relative light speeds implied by $n_1$ and $n_2$.
whatToNotice: Changing the index contrast changes the speed contrast, and that moves the critical angle.
overlays.whyItMatters: TIR comes from how the two media differ, not from a separate bounce rule.

graphs.description: Below $\theta_c$, the refracted-angle branch climbs toward 90°. Above $\theta_c$, no real transmitted branch remains, so only the reflected-angle branch continues.
Shows only the refracted branch that exists below threshold. As $\theta_1$ approaches $\theta_c$ from below, $\theta_2$ approaches 90°.

noticePrompts.intro: Change one thing at a time. Use the stage and graphs together to see where the refracted branch ends and total internal reflection begins.

items.text: Notice that TIR only appears when the ray starts in the higher-index medium. If $n_1$ is not larger than $n_2$, the critical-angle threshold disappears.
items.tryThis: Start with Glass to air, then raise $n_2$ toward $n_1$ and watch the threshold move upward or disappear.
items.whyItMatters: A large angle alone is never enough. The travel direction across the index contrast matters first.

items.text: Notice that the threshold transition graph is not a time plot. Each point is a different incident-angle setup for the same two media.
items.whyItMatters: That is why the graph can show where the refracted branch ends and the reflected branch takes over.

items.text: Notice that just below $\theta_c$ the transmitted ray is still real, but it is nearly parallel to the boundary as $\theta_2$ approaches 90°.
items.whyItMatters: That is the geometric limit you cross before TIR begins.
It prevents the mistaken idea that the transmitted ray just keeps bending forever.

items.text: Notice that repeated TIR at successive boundaries is the same local event used in fiber-optic guidance.
items.whyItMatters: It gives one concrete application of the boundary event without leaving the current concept.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the threshold outcome first, then apply the change and compare the stage, the critical-angle readout, and the graphs.

items.explanation: At $\theta_c$, the refracted ray reaches 90°. Above it, Snell's law no longer gives a real transmitted angle, so the ray stays in medium 1.
items.observationHint: Watch the critical margin turn positive as the reflected ray becomes the only outgoing branch.

items.explanation: Raising $n_2$ toward $n_1$ weakens the contrast, raises $\theta_c$, and can restore a real transmitted ray at the same $\theta_1$.
Lowering $n_2$ strengthens the contrast, lowers $\theta_c$, and can move the same $\theta_1$ from refraction into TIR.

paragraphs: Every mirror image starts from the same rule: the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection. If the reflected rays really meet, the image is real. If the reflected rays spread apart but their backward extensions meet, the image is virtual.

This bench keeps plane, concave, and convex mirrors on one shared geometry. As you change mirror type, focal-length magnitude, object distance, and object height, the stage, signed image distance, and magnification all update together. That lets you connect what the rays do, where the image forms, and whether it is upright or inverted without treating them as separate rules.

keyIdeas: A plane mirror gives a virtual upright image the same distance behind the mirror as the object is in front.
For a concave mirror, an object outside F gives a real inverted image in front of the mirror, while an object inside F gives a virtual upright image behind it.
A convex mirror always gives a virtual upright reduced image for a real object in front.
The sign of d_i tells which side of the mirror the image is on, and the sign of m tells whether the image is upright or inverted.

commonMisconception.myth: A virtual image is not a real image because no light actually reaches it.
correction: A virtual image is still a real geometric result. The reflected rays do not cross there physically, but their backward extensions meet there consistently.
That is why your eye still receives the reflected light in a way that makes the image appear at a definite location behind the mirror.

workedExamples.title: Live mirror checks
workedExamples.intro: These examples use the current mirror, object position, and object height from the live bench, so each calculation matches the ray diagram you are actually seeing.

items.title: Find the signed image distance
items.prompt: For the current {{mirrorFamilyValue}} mirror, what signed image distance does the mirror equation predict, and what does the sign tell you about the image?

steps.label: 1. Start with the mirror equation
2. Solve for the signed image distance
3. Interpret the sign of the result

applyAction.label: Apply plane-mirror case
applyAction.observationHint: For a plane mirror, the image stays the same distance behind the mirror as the object is in front, so the sign comes from the image being virtual rather than from any crossing of real rays.

items.title: Find the image height from magnification
items.prompt: For the current mirror, what image height and orientation does magnification predict?

steps.label: 1. Start with the magnification relation
2. Calculate the signed magnification
3. Use m to find the image height

applyAction.label: Apply concave mirror inside focus
applyAction.observationHint: Positive magnification means upright, and a value larger than 1 means the virtual image is enlarged.

miniChallenge.prompt: A concave mirror has the object outside F, and you slowly move the object inward. What should happen just before the object crosses inside the focal point, and what changes after it crosses?
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide what happens to the image distance, image type, and orientation as the object passes through F.
miniChallenge.explanation: As the object approaches F from outside, the real image moves farther away and the magnitude of the magnification grows. Once the object crosses inside F, the reflected rays no longer meet in front of the mirror, so the image flips to a virtual upright image behind the mirror.

quickTest.intro: Use the ray behavior, the sign of d_i, and the sign of m together. The goal is to reason from the live mirror model.

questions.explanation: A plane mirror gives a virtual image found by backward extension. The geometry is symmetric, so the image is upright, the same size, and equally far behind the mirror.

questions.prompt: Which mirror setup can project an image onto a screen in front of the mirror?
questions.explanation: Only a real image can land on a screen, and the concave mirror produces that when the object is outside F. The plane and convex cases stay virtual.
Crossing inside F changes the reflected-ray geometry. The rays no longer meet in front of the mirror, and their backward extensions meet behind the mirror instead.

questions.prompt: What is always true for a convex mirror with a real object in front of it?
questions.explanation: A convex mirror always keeps the image on the virtual side for a real object in this setup. The image stays upright and reduced because the reflected rays spread and only their backward extensions meet.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a mirror at the center of the principal axis, an object arrow on the left, and an image arrow that moves according to the selected mirror type. Depending on the setup, the image appears in front of the mirror as a real inverted image or behind the mirror as a virtual upright image.
Optional overlays show the equal-angle cue at the pole, the focal markers for curved mirrors, the principal rays, and the distance-and-height guide used in magnification.
The object-image graph plots signed image distance against object distance for the current mirror family and focal-length magnitude.
The magnification graph plots m against object distance, so the sign gives orientation and the magnitude gives the image-size scale without leaving the ray diagram.

equations.meaning: Relates signed focal length, object distance, and image distance for the current mirror.
notes: For a plane mirror, the focal length is effectively infinite, so the relation reduces to $d_i = -d_o$ and the image sits the same distance behind the mirror as the object is in front.

equations.meaning: The sign of m gives orientation, and the magnitude |m| gives the size ratio.

variableLinks.description: Sets how strongly a curved mirror redirects the rays. In this module, concave uses positive f and convex uses negative f.
Moves the object toward or away from the mirror. For a concave mirror, crossing F changes the image from real and inverted to virtual and upright.
Changes only the object size. The image height scales by the same magnification factor, but the image distance is still set by the mirror geometry.

controls.description: Turn this off for a plane mirror. Leave it on and use Concave mirror to switch between concave and convex cases with the same focal-length magnitude.
With Curved mirror on, turn this off to switch from concave to convex while keeping the same |f|.
Moves the object along the principal axis. For a concave mirror, crossing F changes the image from real to virtual.
Changes only the object size, so the image height changes by the same magnification factor without moving the image point.

presets.description: A concave mirror with the object outside F, giving a real inverted image in front of the mirror.
A concave mirror with the object inside F, giving a virtual upright enlarged image behind the mirror.
A convex mirror with a virtual upright reduced image behind the mirror.

whatToNotice: Every mirror on this page still obeys the same equal-angle reflection rule at the surface.
overlays.whyItMatters: The different image behaviors come from how those reflected rays combine, not from a different reflection law.

whatToNotice: For a concave mirror, crossing F changes the image from real to virtual.
overlays.whyItMatters: The focal markers anchor the standard mirror regions directly on the stage.

whatToNotice: Real images come from reflected rays that actually meet, while virtual images come from backward extensions.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties the sign of d_i to visible ray behavior instead of to a memorized sign rule.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the signed distances and the object-image height comparison used in magnification.
whatToNotice: The sign of m tells orientation, while |m| tells how much the image size has changed.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links the stage directly to the magnification relation.

graphs.description: Shows signed image distance against object distance for the current mirror. Positive values are real images in front of the mirror, and negative values are virtual images behind it.
Shows how m changes with object distance. Negative values are inverted images, positive values are upright images, and |m| gives the image-size scale.

noticePrompts.intro: Use the live prompt to connect the law of reflection, the ray construction, and the graph branch for the current mirror.

items.text: Notice that every mirror on this page still obeys the same equal-angle rule at the surface. The different image behaviors come from where the reflected rays do or do not meet.
items.whyItMatters: Mirror type changes the geometry built from reflection, not the reflection law itself.

items.text: Notice that the mirror graphs are object-distance based, not time based. Hovering a point previews a different geometry immediately, not a later moment.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps the graph honest: you are comparing families of setups, not stepping through time.

items.text: Notice that when a concave-mirror object crosses inside F, both d_i and m change sign together.
items.whyItMatters: That shared sign flip is the clean signal that the image has changed from real inverted to virtual upright.

items.text: Notice that a plane mirror places the image the same distance behind the mirror as the object is in front. The image is not inside the surface; it is set by the backward extensions of the reflected rays.
items.whyItMatters: It separates the geometric image location from the physical mirror material.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the branch or orientation change before you apply it. Then compare the sign of d_i and m with the ray diagram.

items.explanation: At this object distance, the concave mirror is outside the focal length, so the reflected rays meet in front of the mirror and form a real inverted image.
Inside F, the reflected rays no longer meet in front of the mirror. Their backward extensions meet behind the mirror instead, so the image becomes virtual and upright.
items.observationHint: The dashed backward extensions replace the real ray intersection, and both d_i and m flip sign.

items.explanation: A convex mirror always sends the image to the virtual side for a real object in front of it and keeps the image upright with magnification below one.

challengeMode.intro: Use the real mirror controls and signed graphs to hit image targets. The checks read the same d_i and m that the stage is already showing.

paragraphs: Thin-lens imaging starts with one object point. Rays from that point pass through the lens and are redirected. If the refracted rays really meet on the far side, the image point is real. If the rays spread after the lens but their backward extensions meet on the object side, the image point is virtual.

The ray diagram and the thin-lens equation are two views of the same geometry. On this page, changing lens type, focal length, object distance, or object height updates the stage, the signed image distance, and the magnification together. That lets you connect where the image forms, whether it is upright or inverted, and how large it is without treating those as separate facts.

keyIdeas: For a converging lens, an object outside the focal length gives a real inverted image, while an object inside the focal length gives a virtual upright image.
A diverging lens still forms an image for a real object on the left, but that image is always virtual, upright, and reduced.
The sign of the image distance tells which side of the lens the image is on: positive for a real image on the far side, negative for a virtual image on the object side.
The sign of magnification tells orientation, while its magnitude tells how much the image size has been scaled.

commonMisconception.myth: A diverging lens spreads the rays, so it cannot form an image.
correction: A diverging lens still forms an image. The refracted rays separate after the lens, but their backward extensions meet at a virtual image point on the object side.
That is why the image is upright and reduced, even though it cannot be projected onto a screen.

workedExamples.title: Live lens checks
workedExamples.intro: These examples use the current lens, object position, and object height from the live bench, so each calculation matches the ray diagram you are actually viewing.

items.title: Find the image height from magnification

steps.label: 1. Start with the magnification relation
2. Calculate the signed magnification
3. Use m to find the image height

applyAction.label: Apply diverging-lens case
applyAction.observationHint: Positive m keeps the image upright, while |m| below 1 keeps it reduced.

miniChallenge.prompt: For a converging lens, move the object inward toward the focal point but keep it outside F. What should happen to the real image before the object crosses the focus?
miniChallenge.prediction: Predict both the image distance and the image size.
miniChallenge.explanation: As the object approaches F from outside, the refracted rays need more distance to meet. The real image moves farther away, and the magnitude of the magnification grows until the object reaches the focal point.

quickTest.intro: Use the sign of d_i, the sign of m, and the ray behavior together. The goal is to reason from the live lens model.

questions.prompt: Which setup can project a sharp image onto a screen on the right side of the lens?
questions.explanation: Only a real image can land on a screen, and that requires actual ray intersections on the far side. For a converging lens, that happens when the object is outside F.
Crossing inside F changes the ray geometry. The rays after the lens no longer meet on the right, and their backward extensions meet on the left instead, so the image becomes virtual and upright.

questions.prompt: What does a negative magnification tell you immediately about the image?
questions.explanation: The sign of m tells orientation. Negative magnification means the image is inverted, while |m| tells how much the size has changed.

questions.prompt: What is always true for a diverging lens with a real object on the left?
questions.explanation: A diverging lens keeps the image on the virtual side for a real object in this setup. The image stays upright and reduced because the refracted rays separate and only their backward extensions meet.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a thin lens at the center of the axis, an object arrow on the left, and an image arrow that moves according to the signed thin-lens equation. Depending on the setup, the image appears on the far side as a real inverted image or on the object side as a virtual upright image.
Optional overlays show the focal markers, the principal rays, and the distance-and-height guide used in magnification.
The object-image graph plots signed image distance against object distance for the current lens family and focal length.
The magnification graph plots m against object distance, so the sign gives orientation and the magnitude gives the image-size scale without leaving the ray diagram.

equations.meaning: Relates signed focal length, object distance, and image distance for one thin lens.
The sign tells whether the image is upright or inverted, and the magnitude tells the size ratio.
Positive image distance means the refracted rays really meet on the far side, so the image can be projected onto a screen.
notes: Negative image distance means the rays do not meet after the lens and only their backward extensions locate the image.

variableLinks.description: Sets how strongly the lens bends the rays. Converging uses positive f, while diverging uses negative f with the same magnitude.
Moves the object toward or away from the lens. For a converging lens, crossing F changes the image from real to virtual.
Changes the object size only. The image height scales by the same magnification factor, but the image distance stays set by f and d_o.

Sets the focal-point positions and how strongly the lens bends the rays.
Moves the object along the principal axis and can move a converging-lens image from the real branch to the virtual branch when the object crosses F.
Changes the object size so the magnified image height can be compared directly without changing where the image forms.

presets.description: A converging lens with the object outside F, giving a real inverted image on the far side.
A converging lens with the object inside F, giving a virtual upright enlarged image on the object side.
A diverging lens giving a virtual upright reduced image.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows F and 2F on both sides of the lens.
whatToNotice: For a converging lens, crossing F changes the image from real to virtual.
overlays.whyItMatters: The focal markers anchor the standard lens regions directly on the stage.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the construction rays that locate the image point.
whatToNotice: Actual ray intersections give real images, while backward extensions give virtual images.
