overlays.shortDescription: Shows the signed distances and the object/image height comparison used in magnification.
whatToNotice: The sign of m sets orientation, while |m| sets the size change.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links the stage directly to the magnification equation.

graphs.description: Shows how signed image distance changes with object distance for the current lens. Positive values are real images on the far side, and negative values are virtual images on the object side.
Shows how m changes with object distance for the current lens. Negative values are inverted images, positive values are upright images, and |m| gives the size scale.

noticePrompts.intro: Use the live prompt to connect the signed equation, the rays, and the graph branch for the current setup.

items.text: Notice that real images come from real ray crossings, while virtual images come from backward extensions.
items.whyItMatters: That one difference explains why only real images can land on a screen.

items.text: Notice that the object-image graph is based on object distance, not time. Hovering it selects a different geometric setup immediately.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps the graph honest: you are comparing families of lens setups, not stepping through time.

items.text: Notice how the image distance grows very large as a converging-lens object approaches F from outside.
items.whyItMatters: F is the dividing line between the real-image branch and the virtual-image branch.

items.text: Notice that the sign of m tells orientation right away: negative means inverted and positive means upright.
items.whyItMatters: The magnification graph is not only about size. It also tells you image orientation.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the branch change or orientation change first, then apply the setup and compare the ray diagram with the graphs.

items.explanation: For a converging lens with the object still outside F, moving the object inward pushes the real image farther away and increases the magnitude of m. The image stays real and inverted until the object crosses F.
items.observationHint: Watch the image-map point climb the real branch while the inverted image arrow grows.

items.explanation: Inside F, the refracted rays diverge after the lens. Their backward extensions meet on the object side, so the image becomes virtual, upright, and enlarged.
items.observationHint: The dashed backward extensions now locate the image on the virtual branch of the graph.

items.explanation: Changing the sign of the focal length changes the whole branch structure. For a real object on the left, a diverging lens keeps the image virtual, upright, and reduced.
items.observationHint: The graph point stays on the virtual and upright side while the backward extensions locate the image on the left.

challengeMode.intro: Use the real lens controls and signed graphs to hit image targets. The checks read the live distances and magnification from the same bench.

items.title: Build a real inverted image
items.prompt: Starting from the converging reference, tune the setup until the image stays real with d_i between 1.0 and 1.2 m and m between -1.4 and -1.1.
items.successMessage: You matched a real inverted image by steering the object onto the correct real-image branch instead of guessing by eye.

setup.note: Use the image-distance graph and the ray intersection together.

hints: Move the object inward from the reference setup, but keep it outside the focal point.
The magnification should stay negative because the image is still inverted.

items.title: Build a virtual upright image
items.prompt: Starting from the diverging reference, tune the setup until the image stays virtual with d_i between -0.65 and -0.45 m and m between 0.3 and 0.5.
items.successMessage: You used the signed graph honestly: the image stayed virtual and upright while the reduced magnification set the final size.

setup.note: Use the magnification graph and the height guide together.

hints: A diverging lens keeps the image on the virtual side, so do not try to force d_i positive.
Adjust object distance and focal-length magnitude to move both d_i and m.

paragraphs: Even a perfectly focused imaging system does not send one object point to a mathematically perfect point on the detector. Because the aperture is finite, each object point forms a diffraction blur with a real width.

Resolution is therefore a comparison between two distances on the same image plane: how far apart the two image centers are, and how wide each diffraction blur is. On this bench, wavelength and aperture set the blur width, point separation sets the center-to-center spacing, and the detector sample only reads one place on the finished profile.

keyIdeas: A finite aperture turns each object point into a diffraction blur, not a perfect point.
A larger aperture or a shorter wavelength shrinks that blur, while a smaller aperture or a longer wavelength broadens it.
Two points are easier to resolve when their detector spacing is large compared with the blur width.
The Rayleigh limit gives a practical scale for when two equal point sources are only just distinguishable.

commonMisconception.myth: If the lens is focused correctly, two nearby points should always stay perfectly separate.
correction: Correct focus removes defocus blur, but it does not remove diffraction from a finite aperture.
That is why two well-focused points can still merge when the aperture is small or the wavelength is long.

workedExamples.intro: These examples use the live wavelength, aperture, and point spacing from the current image, so the algebra explains the same detector profile you are already seeing.

items.title: Rayleigh limit from the current optical setup
items.prompt: For the current wavelength lambda = {{wavelengthNmValue}} and aperture D = {{apertureMmValue}}, what diffraction-limited Rayleigh scale should you expect for two equal point sources?

steps.label: 1. Start from the diffraction limit
2. Substitute the live wavelength and aperture
3. Turn that angular limit into a detector blur size

applyAction.observationHint: At about the Rayleigh limit, one peak sits close to the first minimum of the other, so the profile is only just split.

items.title: Is the current pair actually resolved?
items.prompt: For the current pair separation Delta theta = {{separationMradValue}}, is the image below the limit, near it, or clearly above it?

steps.label: 1. Compare the point spacing with the Rayleigh scale
2. Compare the same spacing on the detector
3. Decide whether the profile should keep a visible dip

applyAction.observationHint: When the point centers are too close compared with the blur radius, the two peaks merge into one broad hump.

miniChallenge.prompt: You cannot move the object points, but you want the detector profile to show a clearer split. What optical change helps most?
miniChallenge.prediction: Choose between changing aperture, wavelength, or detector sample position.
miniChallenge.answer: Increase the aperture or use a shorter wavelength so the diffraction blur shrinks.
miniChallenge.explanation: The detector sample only reads the existing profile. To really improve resolution, you must shrink the diffraction blur itself.

quickTest.intro: Answer from blur width versus point spacing, not from slogans about focus.

questions.explanation: Resolving power improves when each diffraction blur gets narrower. Moving the detector sample only reads a different place on the same image profile.
The Rayleigh limit scales with wavelength, so a longer wavelength broadens the diffraction blur for the same aperture.
Correct focus removes defocus blur, but a finite aperture still leaves a diffraction-limited blur with a nonzero width.
The detector sample is only a measurement point. The optical limit was already set by wavelength and aperture before you moved the sample.

paragraphs: The simulation shows two distant point sources on the left, one finite lens aperture in the middle, and one detector strip on the right. Each source forms a diffraction-limited blur on the detector rather than a perfect point.

Optional overlays show the aperture diameter on the lens, the current point-spread centers on the detector, and the Rayleigh blur scale around those centers. A movable detector sample marks one detector position and reports the current normalized exposure there.
The graph shows normalized exposure against detector height, with one combined profile and two component profiles for the individual point-spread contributions.
Hovering the graph previews the matching detector position on the stage, so the profile stays linked to the same image plane rather than turning into a time plot.

equations.meaning: Gives a practical angular scale for when two equal diffraction blurs are only just distinguishable.
Turns the object's angular separation into center-to-center spacing on the detector.
Turns the same diffraction limit into the characteristic radius of one point-spread core on the detector.

variableLinks.description: Longer wavelengths broaden each diffraction blur, so the same point pair becomes harder to separate.
A larger aperture narrows each diffraction blur and lowers the Rayleigh limit.
Changes the center-to-center spacing of the two image points, but it does not change the blur width of each point.
Moves the detector sample to a different place on the existing image profile. It does not change the resolution limit.

controls.description: A larger clear aperture narrows each diffraction blur and improves resolution.
Sets the angular gap between the object points. Smaller gaps are harder to preserve when the blur width is fixed.
Moves the live readout along the detector profile without changing the optical image itself.

whatToNotice: Changing D changes the diffraction width itself, not just the brightness of the image.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps resolving power tied to aperture geometry instead of making blur look like a mysterious detector effect.

whatToNotice: At about the Rayleigh limit, one image-point center sits close to the first-minimum scale of the other blur pattern.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns the Rayleigh criterion into a visible comparison on the detector.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the centers of the two individual point-spread patterns.
whatToNotice: Whether the pair is resolved depends on center spacing compared with blur width, not on focus alone.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the merged-versus-separated transition visible as overlap between two real blur patterns.

graphs.description: Shows the combined detector exposure together with the two individual point-spread profiles, so you can see whether their overlap still leaves a central dip.

noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time and keep the aperture, detector strip, and graph tied to the same diffraction-limited image.

items.text: Notice that widening the aperture does not move the point centers. It narrows each diffraction blur, so the gap between the peaks is easier to preserve.
items.whyItMatters: Resolution improves because the blur shrinks, not because the object points move farther apart.

items.text: Notice that increasing the wavelength broadens each blur even though the lens, detector, and point spacing stay fixed.
items.whyItMatters: It turns lambda over D into a visible imaging limit instead of a formula to memorize.

items.text: Notice that reducing point separation does not change the blur width. It only pushes the same two blur centers closer together.
items.whyItMatters: It cleanly separates object spacing from optical blur width.

items.text: Notice that the graph is detector position versus exposure, not a time trace. Hovering it moves the sample along the current image plane.
items.whyItMatters: Resolution lives in the spatial profile across the detector, not in a later-versus-earlier time story.

items.text: Compare mode works best when one setup changes aperture and the other changes wavelength. Then you can see two different ways to change the same diffraction width.
items.whyItMatters: It stops imaging limits from collapsing into one vague blur slider.

predictionMode.intro: Predict whether the image should split more clearly or merge more strongly before you test it. Each prompt changes one cause at a time.

items.explanation: A larger aperture lowers the Rayleigh limit and narrows each diffraction blur. The point spacing itself does not change.
items.observationHint: Watch the Rayleigh guide tighten while the two peaks separate more cleanly.

items.explanation: A longer wavelength raises the Rayleigh limit and broadens each diffraction blur, so nearby points merge more easily.
items.observationHint: The point centers stay where they are, but the middle dip fills in as the blurs widen.

items.explanation: Changing the point separation moves the blur centers closer together or farther apart. The blur width itself stays set by wavelength and aperture.
items.observationHint: Watch the two centers crowd together while the same blur size tries to preserve a smaller gap.

challengeMode.intro: Use the live detector profile to hit resolution targets. The task is to change the optics or the point spacing, not to game the detector sample.

paragraphs: This page asks a simple question: what decides whether light can eject electrons from a metal? The key result is that frequency sets the energy of each photon. If that energy is below the metal's work function, no electrons are emitted, even if you make the beam brighter.

Once the light is above threshold, intensity mainly changes how many electrons are emitted, while frequency changes how energetic the fastest electrons are. The lamp, metal plate, collector plate, readouts, and graphs all stay tied to that same cause-and-effect story.

keyIdeas: Below the threshold frequency, increasing intensity still gives no emitted electrons because each photon is still individually below the work function.
Above threshold, increasing frequency increases the maximum kinetic energy of emitted electrons, while increasing intensity mainly increases the current.
The threshold frequency is set by the work function through f_0 = phi / h.
A negative collector voltage can stop even emitted electrons, and the stopping potential measures the maximum kinetic energy.

commonMisconception.myth: If sub-threshold light is bright enough, electrons can gradually build up enough energy to escape.
correction: In this bounded model, each emitted electron still needs one photon with enough energy to clear the work-function barrier.
Brightness increases how many photons hit the metal, but if each photon is still below threshold, the collector current stays zero.

workedExamples.intro: Use the current light and metal settings from the live bench. The same controls determine the stage, graphs, overlays, and worked result.

items.prompt: For the current frequency {{frequencyValue}} and work function {{workFunctionValue}}, is the light above threshold, and how much energy is left for emitted electrons?

steps.label: 1. Find the photon energy
2. Compare with the threshold
3. Read the remaining electron energy

applyAction.observationHint: Watch the energy-budget card cross the barrier and the emitted electrons appear at the same time.

items.prompt: For the current collector bias {{collectorVoltageValue}}, what stopping potential does this light predict, and how much current still reaches the collector?

steps.label: 1. Use Kmax to find Vstop
2. Compare the bias with Vstop
3. Read the collected current

applyAction.observationHint: Electrons can still be emitted at the surface while a strong enough retarding field prevents them from reaching the collector.

miniChallenge.prediction: Answer from energy per photon first, not from brightness alone.
miniChallenge.explanation: The deciding factor is energy per photon, not just total light power. Intensity changes how many photons arrive each second, but only frequency decides whether a single photon can eject an electron.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the live photon-energy story, not from memorized slogans.

questions.prompt: A bright red beam is still below threshold for the metal. What happens?
questions.explanation: What matters is energy per photon, not total beam brightness.
Above threshold, intensity mainly changes current. Frequency changes energy per photon and therefore Kmax.
The key evidence is that a dim higher-frequency beam can eject electrons while a brighter lower-frequency beam cannot.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a frequency rail from infrared through visible to ultraviolet and a lamp shining on a metal emitter plate toward a collector plate. When emission occurs, electron packets move across the gap toward the collector.

Overlays can mark the threshold frequency, separate intensity from electron energy, show the collector field, and keep hf, phi, and Kmax visible. The readout card reports beam band, photon energy, work function, maximum kinetic energy, stopping potential, available current, collected current, and collector voltage.

The energy-balance graph shows photon energy, work function, and maximum kinetic energy on the same frequency axis, so you can see when light is below threshold, at threshold, or above it. The collector-sweep graph shows how negative collector voltage reduces current to zero at the stopping potential. The intensity-sweep graph shows current increasing with intensity while electron energy stays fixed.

pageIntro.whyItMatters: The effect is the shortest route from wave brightness intuition to a measurable photon-energy story: threshold frequency decides whether emission starts, and stopping voltage measures the leftover electron energy.

equations.meaning: Increasing frequency increases the energy carried by each photon.
This is the minimum frequency that can eject any electrons from the metal.
After the electron escapes, any extra photon energy becomes maximum kinetic energy.
The voltage that just stops the fastest electrons measures their maximum kinetic energy.
Above threshold, increasing intensity mainly increases current, not the energy of each electron.

variableLinks.description: Changes the beam color band and the energy carried by each photon.
Changes how many photons hit the metal each second, so it changes available current without changing energy per photon.
Sets the minimum energy an electron needs to escape the metal surface.
Helps emitted electrons reach the collector when positive and pushes back against them when negative.

controls.description: Changes the energy of each photon and the beam's color band.
Changes photon flux, so above threshold it mainly changes current.
Changes how much energy electrons need to escape the metal.
Helps or opposes electrons after they leave the metal.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the threshold frequency and work-function barrier together.
whatToNotice: Emission starts only when the frequency marker crosses f0 for the chosen metal.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the threshold visible before you calculate it.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows that brightness changes photon flow and current, not energy per photon.
whatToNotice: Above threshold, the electron stream gets denser when intensity rises, but the energy budget changes only when frequency or work function changes.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates electron count from electron energy.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows whether the collector voltage helps, reduces, or stops the collected electrons.
whatToNotice: The cutoff voltage depends on the emitted electrons' energy, not just on how bright the light is.
overlays.whyItMatters: It connects the collector graph to the same energy story shown on the stage.

overlays.shortDescription: Keeps hf, phi, and Kmax visible in one place near the stage.
whatToNotice: Changing intensity does not move hf, phi, or Kmax. Only frequency and work function do.
overlays.whyItMatters: It is the clearest way to see why threshold depends on photon energy rather than brightness alone.

graphs.description: Shows when photon energy is below the work function, equal to it, or above it, and how much is left as Kmax.
Shows how a more negative collector voltage reduces collected current until the stopping point is reached.
Shows that changing intensity changes current size while the per-electron energy stays fixed.

noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to separate threshold, intensity, and collector-voltage ideas while staying on the same bench.

items.text: Below threshold, making the lamp brighter still does not produce collected electrons. The missing ingredient is photon energy, not total brightness.
items.tryThis: Switch from Red below threshold to Bright but still below threshold while keeping the energy-budget card in view.
items.whyItMatters: It directly tests the idea that brightness alone should eject electrons.

items.text: Once the frequency is above threshold, raising intensity mainly raises the current, not the maximum kinetic energy.
items.whyItMatters: It separates how many electrons are emitted from how energetic the fastest ones are.

items.text: Making the collector voltage more negative reduces the collected current, and cutoff happens near the displayed stopping potential.
items.tryThis: Open the collector-voltage sweep and hover near the cutoff point.
items.whyItMatters: It turns stopping potential into a measurable quantity on the same bench.

items.text: Compare mode works best when one setup changes frequency and the other changes intensity. That lets you separate threshold from brightness.
items.whyItMatters: Side by side, it becomes clear that frequency and intensity do different jobs.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the cause-and-effect change before you move the control.

items.explanation: Increasing intensity changes photon flux, not photon energy, so below-threshold light still cannot eject electrons.
items.observationHint: Watch the beam brighten while the energy-budget card stays below the work-function barrier and current stays zero.

items.explanation: Frequency changes energy per photon. Crossing threshold is what turns emission on and makes Kmax and stopping potential nonzero.
items.observationHint: Watch hf cross phi on the energy graph, then read Kmax and Vstop.

items.explanation: The collector field changes how many emitted electrons reach the collector. It does not change the surface energy budget set by hf and phi.
items.observationHint: Track the collector-sweep graph while the energy-budget card stays fixed.

challengeMode.intro: Use the same bench to test threshold and stopping-potential ideas with concrete targets.

items.successMessage: You found the stopping region, where the retarding field just cancels the fastest emitted electrons.

setup.note: Use the collector-voltage sweep together with the stage and readout.

items.prompt: Starting from Bright but still below threshold, keep the beam bright while showing that the collector current can still stay essentially zero.

setup.note: Use intensity as a distraction, but keep checking the threshold barrier.

paragraphs: On this page, an atom does not emit or absorb every color. It can change only between allowed energy levels, so it can emit or absorb only photons whose energies match those level gaps. That is why the spectrum appears as discrete lines instead of a smooth rainbow.

The energy ladder, spectrum strip, and wavelength graph are all showing the same allowed transitions. When you change a gap or switch between emission and absorption, the line positions, overlays, worked examples, and checks stay tied to that same energy-gap story.

keyIdeas: Each spectral line comes from one allowed energy difference between levels.
Larger energy gaps produce shorter-wavelength photons, while smaller gaps produce longer-wavelength photons.
Emission and absorption use the same allowed wavelengths because both are set by the same level differences.
This simplified ladder is here to explain line positions and energy gaps clearly, not to model full atomic structure.

workedExamples.intro: Use the current ladder and mode directly from the live bench. The same allowed gaps control the arrows, the strip, the graph, and the worked result.

items.prompt: With gaps {{gap21Value}}, {{gap32Value}}, and {{gap43Value}}, which spectral lines from the current ladder fall in the visible range?

steps.label: 1. Find the 2 -> 1 wavelength
2. Find the 3 -> 2 wavelength
3. Count the visible lines

applyAction.observationHint: Watch each visible line stay linked to its ladder arrow on both the strip and the graph.

items.title: Current mode and unchanged wavelength
items.prompt: For the current mode {{modeLabelValue}}, how does the largest-gap line appear, and why does its wavelength stay the same?

steps.label: 1. Identify the largest gap
2. Convert it to a wavelength
3. Separate position from appearance

applyAction.observationHint: Bright peaks turn into dark notches at the same x positions because the allowed gaps did not change.

miniChallenge.prediction: Answer from the allowed energy gaps, not from whether a line looks bright or dark.
miniChallenge.explanation: Discrete level gaps allow only discrete photon energies, so only certain wavelengths appear. This page focuses on that line-pattern logic without trying to model full atomic structure.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the energy-gap story shown on the ladder and graph, not from disconnected color names.

controls.description: Set the energy difference between levels 2 and 1.
Set the energy difference between levels 3 and 2.
Set the energy difference between levels 4 and 3.
Switch between bright emission lines and dark absorption notches while keeping the same allowed wavelengths.

whatToNotice: The active ladder arrow, strip line, and graph position move together because they represent the same energy change.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you connect energy-level changes to spectral lines instead of treating them as separate pictures.

whatToNotice: Changing one gap can move several labeled lines because some larger jumps include that same gap.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the line pattern easier to explain from the ladder.

whatToNotice: Bright peaks become dark notches at the same wavelengths because the allowed level differences are unchanged.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates where a line appears from how it appears.

whatToNotice: Empty wavelength regions are expected whenever no allowed energy gap lands there.
overlays.whyItMatters: It is the clearest visual cue that the spectrum is discrete, not continuous.

graphs.description: Shows the current line pattern across ultraviolet, visible, and infrared wavelengths. In absorption mode, the continuum stays in place while dark notches mark the same allowed wavelengths.

noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time so the ladder, strip, and graph keep telling the same energy-gap story.

items.text: Most wavelengths stay empty because this ladder allows only a few energy differences. The spectrum is sparse because of the model, not because the graph is hiding anything.
items.tryThis: Start on Hydrogen-like emission and look between the visible lines on the graph.
items.whyItMatters: It is the clearest reason this page shows lines instead of a smooth rainbow.

items.text: Changing one gap can move more than one line because some larger jumps include that same gap.
items.whyItMatters: It helps you read the spectrum as a map of level differences, not a list of unrelated colors.
It keeps emission and absorption tied to the same energy-level structure.

items.text: Compare mode works best when one setup changes a gap and the other changes only the mode. That lets you separate changes in line position from changes in appearance.
items.whyItMatters: Side by side, you can tell whether a difference came from the energy ladder or just from emission versus absorption.

predictionMode.intro: Commit to what the line pattern should do before you move a gap or flip the mode.

challengeMode.intro: Use the same ladder and spectrum bench for line-pattern targets, not a separate spectroscopy tool.

paragraphs: de Broglie proposed that a particle with momentum has a wavelength, given by \(\lambda = h / p\). On this page, that means wavelength is controlled by momentum: if the particle is faster or heavier, its momentum is larger and its wavelength is shorter. That momentum-first view is why electron-scale wavelengths can shape spectra and atomic models while everyday matter waves stay too tiny to notice.

The bench shows this in two linked ways. The strip shows one local wavelength spacing, and the fixed loop shows how many of those wavelengths fit around the same path. When the fit is close to a whole number, the wrapped wave matches up cleanly at the seam. That gives a simple bridge from wave spacing to early quantum ideas without pretending this page is a full quantum-mechanics solver.

keyIdeas: de Broglie wavelength is tied to momentum by \(\lambda = h / p\), so the main inverse relation is between wavelength and momentum.
On this bounded non-relativistic page, \(p = mv\), so increasing either mass or speed increases momentum and shortens the wavelength.
The strip and the loop are two views of the same \(\lambda\): when the spacing shrinks on the strip, more wavelengths fit around the loop.
A whole-number loop fit is a useful bridge toward quantized behavior and the Bohr model, but it is not the full modern quantum picture.

correction: de Broglie wavelength is not set by distance traveled per second. It is set by momentum, so larger momentum means smaller wavelength.
This page uses matter wavelength as a limited bridge between wave ideas and early quantum behavior. It is not treating the particle like an ordinary water wave or a complete quantum state.

workedExamples.intro: Use the live mass and speed values from the bench. The same settings determine the local spacing strip, the loop-fit count, and both graphs.

items.prompt: For the current particle mass \({{massMultipleValue}}\,m_e\) and speed \({{speedValue}}\,\mathrm{Mm/s}\), what momentum and de Broglie wavelength does the page show?

steps.label: 1. Find the momentum
2. Convert momentum to wavelength
3. Match the numbers to the bench

applyAction.observationHint: Watch the spacing on the strip tighten while the loop count rises. Both panels are responding to the same smaller wavelength.

items.title: Current loop fit and nearest whole number
items.prompt: With the fixed loop circumference \(L = {{loopCircumferenceValue}}\,\mathrm{nm}\), how many wavelengths fit around the loop now, and how close is that to a whole-number fit?

steps.label: 1. Use the fit-count relation
2. Substitute the live wavelength
3. Check the nearest whole number

applyAction.observationHint: Watch the seam become cleaner as the fit count moves close to one whole wavelength.

miniChallenge.prediction: Reason from momentum and wavelength first, not from how long the path looks.
miniChallenge.answer: Electron B is closer to fitting more whole wavelengths because its greater speed gives greater momentum, and greater momentum means a shorter de Broglie wavelength.
miniChallenge.explanation: The loop length stays fixed, so a smaller \(\lambda\) makes \(N = L / \lambda\) larger. The faster electron therefore fits more wavelengths around the same loop.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the momentum-wavelength link shown on the bench, not from loose wave analogies.

paragraphs: The simulation has two main stage views: a local strip showing the current matter-wave spacing and a fixed loop showing how many of those wavelengths fit around the same path. Changing mass or speed updates both views together.

Optional overlays mark one wavelength on the strip, show how mass and speed combine into momentum, and compare the loop fit with the nearest whole number. The readout card gives mass, speed, momentum, wavelength, loop length, and fit count.

One graph shows wavelength decreasing as momentum increases. The other shows the loop-fit count increasing as momentum increases because more wavelengths fit around the same fixed path. Hovering a graph previews the corresponding bench state.

pageIntro.whyItMatters: This momentum-first view explains why electron-scale wavelengths can shape spectra and atomic models while everyday objects have matter waves too tiny to notice.

equations.meaning: On this page, mass and speed combine into one momentum before wavelength is determined.
More momentum means a shorter matter wavelength.
At fixed mass, increasing speed shortens the wavelength. At fixed speed, increasing mass also shortens it.
The loop closes neatly only when a whole number of wavelengths fits around the fixed path.

variableLinks.description: At the same speed, increasing mass increases momentum and shortens the matter wavelength.
At the same mass, increasing speed increases momentum, so the spacing shrinks and more wavelengths fit around the loop.

controls.description: Set the particle mass in electron-mass units.
Change the speed and watch momentum, wavelength, and loop fit update together.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks one crest-to-crest spacing on the local matter-wave strip.
whatToNotice: When momentum increases, the marked spacing shrinks on the strip and the loop fit count rises.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes wavelength visible as a spacing on the stage, not just as a number in an equation.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows mass and speed combining into one momentum before wavelength is set.
whatToNotice: Different mass-speed pairs can produce the same momentum and therefore the same wavelength.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the focus on momentum as the variable behind the wavelength.

overlays.shortDescription: Compares the current loop count with the nearest whole-number match.
whatToNotice: At whole numbers the wrapped wave meets itself cleanly; between them the seam does not match.
overlays.whyItMatters: It gives a compact visual bridge from wavelength to early quantized behavior.

graphs.description: Read the inverse de Broglie relation directly: moving to larger momentum means moving to shorter wavelength.
As momentum increases, the fixed loop holds more wavelengths because the wavelength gets shorter.

noticePrompts.intro: Use one cue at a time so the strip, loop, and graphs all stay tied to the same momentum-based state.

items.text: The strip and the loop are showing the same wavelength. When the spacing on the strip shrinks, the loop fit count rises.
items.tryThis: Start from Near one-fit electron, then raise the speed and watch both panels together.
items.whyItMatters: It helps you read the bench as one connected story instead of two separate pictures.

items.text: Hovering either graph previews the same bench at that momentum, not a separate example.
items.tryThis: Hover farther to the right on either graph and compare the tighter spacing with the larger loop count.
items.whyItMatters: The graphs and the bench are two views of the same momentum-based state.

items.text: At fixed mass, increasing speed raises momentum, shortens the wavelength, and pushes the loop toward a larger fit count.
items.tryThis: Move from Near one-fit electron toward Near two-fit electron.
items.whyItMatters: It turns \(\lambda = h / p\) into something you can see on the stage.

items.text: At fixed speed, increasing mass still shortens the wavelength here because mass changes momentum too.
items.tryThis: Compare Near one-fit electron with Heavier same speed.
items.whyItMatters: It prevents the common mistake that only speed matters for de Broglie wavelength.

items.text: Compare a heavier slower case with a lighter faster case that keeps the same momentum. The wavelength should stay the same.
items.tryThis: Put Setup A on Near one-fit electron and Setup B on Same momentum swap.
items.whyItMatters: It shows that momentum, not mass or speed alone, is the control variable behind the wavelength.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the momentum and wavelength change before you test it on the bench.

challengeMode.intro: Use the same bench to hit whole-number-fit targets by reading momentum, wavelength, and loop count together.

items.prompt: Starting from Slow electron, tune only the speed until the fixed loop is close to one wavelength long without changing the particle mass.
items.successMessage: You found the near one-fit case where the loop is almost exactly one wavelength around.
hints: Keep the mass at the electron value.
Aim for \(N \approx 1\), not just for a shorter wavelength.

items.prompt: Starting from Near one-fit electron, keep the speed nearly unchanged but increase the mass until roughly two wavelengths fit around the loop.
items.successMessage: You used mass, not speed, to raise momentum and reach a loop count near two.
hints: Keep the speed close to the starting one-fit value.
A shorter wavelength here must come from greater momentum through mass.

paragraphs: In the Bohr model of hydrogen, the electron is allowed to occupy only certain energy levels. That means it can change energy only by certain amounts, so hydrogen emits or absorbs specific wavelengths instead of a continuous rainbow. The model is historically important because it links observed line spectra to quantized energy levels, even though modern quantum mechanics gives the fuller picture.

On this page, the radius map, energy ladder, line strip, and spectrum graph all describe the same live transition. When you change the upper or lower level, the selected jump, wavelength, series family, reverse-excitation case, and worked-example readouts update together.

keyIdeas: In the Bohr model for hydrogen, the allowed energies follow E_n = -13.6 eV / n^2, so only certain transition gaps are possible.
Keeping the lower level fixed groups the lines into named series such as Lyman, Balmer, and Paschen.
Within one series, increasing the starting level makes the lines crowd toward a series limit because the upper energy levels get closer together.
The Bohr model is useful for understanding hydrogen spectral lines, but it is not the final quantum-mechanical description of atoms.

commonMisconception.myth: If the electron circles the nucleus, it should be able to drop between any two radii and produce any color.
correction: In the Bohr model, only specific energy levels are allowed, so only specific photon energies and wavelengths appear.
Modern quantum mechanics replaces the literal orbit picture, but quantized energy levels still explain why hydrogen has discrete spectral lines.

workedExamples.intro: Read each result from the same live bench. The level pair, energy gap, wavelength marker, graph, and overlays are all describing the same transition.

items.prompt: For the current transition {{activeLabelValue}}, what photon energy and wavelength match this jump?

steps.label: 1. Read the active level pair
2. Read the energy gap
3. Convert the gap to a wavelength

applyAction.observationHint: Watch the ladder arrow, wavelength marker, and graph stay aligned as the red Balmer line appears.

items.title: Current series and limit
items.prompt: With lower level {{lowerLevelValue}}, which hydrogen series is active, and why do its lines crowd toward a limit?

steps.label: 1. Identify the series
2. Compare the orbit sizes
3. Explain the crowding

applyAction.observationHint: Notice the active line move toward the Balmer limit while the reverse-excitation wavelength still matches it.

miniChallenge.prediction: Base your answer on the size of the energy gap, not just on whether the arrow points up or down.
miniChallenge.explanation: The direction only changes whether the atom emits or absorbs the photon. The level spacing between the same two allowed levels stays the same, so the wavelength stays the same.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the live level-and-spectrum story, not from memorized phrases about Bohr.

paragraphs: The simulation is arranged as one Bohr-model hydrogen bench: a radius map on the left, an energy ladder in the middle, a line strip on the right, and a spectrum graph below. The highlighted transition appears as an arrow between two levels, with the same wavelength marked on the strip and graph.

Overlays can mark the series family, line labels, the n squared radius scaling, and the matching reverse-excitation case. The readout card reports the current mode, series name, selected levels, photon energy, wavelength, series limit, and radius ratio.

The graph shows relative line strength versus wavelength for the selected hydrogen series across ultraviolet, visible, and infrared regions. Each narrow peak is an allowed spectral line, and the active transition is highlighted on the same wavelength axis as the line strip.

equations.meaning: This gives the size of the allowed energy gap between two hydrogen levels.
Once you know the gap, you know the photon wavelength: larger gaps give shorter wavelengths.
Higher allowed levels sit much farther from the nucleus because radius scales with n^2.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows that allowed Bohr radii grow like n^2.
whatToNotice: Even a one-step increase in n can make the allowed orbit much larger.
overlays.whyItMatters: It connects the orbit picture to the same quantized levels shown on the energy ladder.

overlays.shortDescription: Labels the level pair and wavelength for the lines in the selected series.
whatToNotice: As you raise the starting level in one series, the lines squeeze toward the series limit instead of staying evenly spaced.
overlays.whyItMatters: It lets you read the series pattern directly from the bench instead of treating it like a separate table.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the current hydrogen series and its wavelength limit.
whatToNotice: Changing n_f switches the whole family of lines, not just the active one.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links the name of a series to what you actually see on the graph.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows that the matching excitation uses the same wavelength as the emission transition.
whatToNotice: Reversing the arrow changes the direction of energy flow, not the size of the gap.
overlays.whyItMatters: It prevents the common mistake that upward and downward transitions between the same levels need different wavelengths.

graphs.description: Shows the selected hydrogen series as narrow spectral lines across wavelength, with the active transition highlighted on the same axis used by the line strip.

noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time so you can connect the radius map, energy ladder, line strip, and spectrum graph.

items.text: Only certain hydrogen level gaps are allowed, so the graph shows separate lines rather than a continuous rainbow.
items.tryThis: Start on Balmer alpha, then look between the peaks on the graph.
items.whyItMatters: It makes quantization visible in the graph, not just in the equation.

items.text: Within one series, raising n_i makes the wavelength shorter and pushes the lines toward the same series limit.
items.tryThis: Compare Balmer alpha with High-n Balmer and watch the active wavelength shift.
items.whyItMatters: It explains why a series bunches up near one limit instead of staying evenly spaced.

items.text: Switching to reverse excitation flips the arrow but keeps the same wavelength because the energy-gap size is unchanged.
items.tryThis: Flip from Balmer alpha to Balmer reverse excitation and compare the readout.
items.whyItMatters: It ties emission and absorption to the same level spacing.

items.text: Compare mode works best when one setup changes n_i within a series and the other changes n_f to a different series. That separates crowding within one family from switching families.
items.tryThis: Keep Setup A on High-n Balmer and Setup B on Lyman alpha.
items.whyItMatters: Side-by-side comparison helps you see which changes come from upper-level crowding and which come from changing the lower level.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the spectral change before you move the control.

items.explanation: For a fixed lower level, increasing the starting level increases the energy gap and pushes the wavelength toward the Balmer limit.
items.observationHint: Watch the active wavelength marker on the strip and graph together.

items.explanation: Dropping to a lower final level creates a larger energy change, so the wavelength becomes much shorter and moves into the ultraviolet.
items.observationHint: Watch the family label change while the active line shifts on the graph.

items.explanation: The same two levels set the same energy-gap size in either direction.

challengeMode.intro: Meet each target by reading the level pair, wavelength, and series together on the same live bench.

items.prompt: Starting from Balmer beta, adjust the live state until the active transition is the classic red Balmer line and the page still shows emission.
items.successMessage: You found Balmer alpha in emission and kept the Balmer series consistent.

setup.note: Use the active level pair, wavelength readout, and graph together.

hints: Keep n_f = 2 so you stay in the Balmer series.
The red Balmer line is the smallest visible drop in that series.

items.prompt: Starting from Lyman alpha emission, switch to the matching reverse excitation from the ground level without changing the ultraviolet wavelength.
items.successMessage: You matched the reverse Lyman-alpha case and kept the same UV wavelength while reversing the transition.

setup.note: Use the reverse-transition overlay to check that only the direction changes.

hints: Keep n_f = 1 so you stay in the Lyman series.
You need the first upward jump from the ground level.

paragraphs: Radioactive decay is random for any single nucleus, but regular for a large sample. A half-life does not mean each nucleus lasts that long. It means that after one half-life, the expected number of undecayed nuclei is half the starting sample. Individual nuclei may decay early, late, or not yet at all.

The tray, readout card, and time graphs all describe the same live sample. Changing sample size or half-life updates the one-by-one decays in the tray, the smooth expectation curve, and the checkpoint markers together, so you can connect random single events to the overall exponential trend.

keyIdeas: Half-life means the expected sample is multiplied by one half after each equal half-life interval. It does not assign every nucleus its own lifetime.
A single nucleus either decays or survives. A large sample averages many of those yes-or-no events into a smoother exponential trend.
The actual sample should fall in steps because nuclei disappear one at a time, while the expectation curve stays smooth.
The expected curve approaches zero without going negative. Real samples can sit above or below it and still follow the same law overall.

commonMisconception.myth: A half-life of 2 seconds means each nucleus survives for 2 seconds, and then half of the sample disappears.
correction: Half-life describes the expected behavior of a sample, not a personal timer for each nucleus.
Individual nuclei decay at unpredictable times. The actual tray falls in steps, while the smooth curve shows the exponential expectation.

workedExamples.intro: Use the current sample and half-life directly from the live bench. The tray, graphs, and readout card are all describing the same actual-versus-expected decay story.

items.prompt: For a sample of {{sampleSizeValue}} nuclei with half-life {{halfLifeValue}}, what should the smooth expectation curve show at time {{timeValue}}?

steps.label: 1. Find how many half-lives have passed
2. Apply repeated halving to the expectation
3. Compare the tray with the expectation

applyAction.observationHint: Use the half-life markers and smooth expectation together, then scrub the time rail.

items.title: Current sample versus expectation
items.prompt: At time {{timeValue}}, how far is the live sample from the smooth expectation, and what does that tell you about randomness in decay?

steps.label: 1. Read the single-nucleus survival chance
2. Compare the tray with the smooth curve
3. Interpret the difference

applyAction.observationHint: Keep the single-versus-sample cue open and watch the stepped count pull away from the smooth curve.

miniChallenge.prediction: Answer from the difference between an expected curve and actual single decays.
miniChallenge.explanation: The smooth curve is the average you expect from many random decays. The tray must fall in steps because nuclei decay one by one, but it should still follow the same overall law.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a tray of nuclei and two linked time graphs. Each nucleus is either still present or already decayed, so the tray changes one nucleus at a time.

Overlays can highlight recent decays, mark equal half-life checkpoints, and compare single-nucleus language with sample-level expectations. The readout card shows sample size, half-life, time, actual remaining count, expected remaining count, remaining fraction, deviation, and single-nucleus survival probability.

The remaining-count graph shows both the stepped actual count and the smooth expected count over time. The remaining-fraction graph shows the same idea scaled by the starting sample, so different sample sizes can be compared more fairly.

equations.meaning: After each equal half-life interval, the expected count is halved.
This is the same decay law written in exponential form.
For one nucleus, this gives the probability of still being undecayed at time t.

overlays.shortDescription: Highlights nuclei that decayed recently so the tray does not look as if it fades smoothly.
whatToNotice: The tray changes one nucleus at a time even when the expected curve is smooth.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the one-by-one nature of decay visible.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the stepped actual count and the smooth expectation together.
whatToNotice: The actual tray can sit above or below the smooth curve while still following the same decay law overall.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates random single events from predictable large-sample behavior.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks equal half-life checkpoints so repeated halving stays visible.
whatToNotice: Each equal checkpoint halves the expectation again instead of subtracting a fixed number.
overlays.whyItMatters: It prevents half-life from being misread as a linear drop.

overlays.shortDescription: Keeps one-nucleus language beside the sample curve.
whatToNotice: One nucleus is either still present or gone, while the sample curve is an average expectation.
overlays.whyItMatters: It prevents 50 percent survival from being misread as half a nucleus or a countdown timer.

graphs.description: Shows the stepped actual count and the smooth expected count together so one-by-one decays can be compared with the exponential trend.
Shows the same decay story as a fraction of the start, so samples of different sizes can be compared without changing the law.

noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time so the tray, readout card, and graphs stay tied to the same decay story.

items.text: At one half-life, a single nucleus is not 'half there'. It is either still present or gone. The 50 percent value is a survival probability for one nucleus and an expectation for a sample.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps probability honest at the single-nucleus level.

items.text: Equal half-life intervals repeatedly halve the expected sample. They do not remove a fixed number each second, so the curve bends instead of staying straight.
items.whyItMatters: It helps you read half-life as repeated multiplication, not linear loss.

items.text: Small samples can land noticeably above or below the expectation, but larger samples hug the same curve more closely because many independent decays are being averaged.
items.whyItMatters: It links randomness in single events to regularity in large samples.

items.text: Compare mode works best when the half-life stays the same but the sample size changes. That separates randomness in the tray from the shared decay law in the graphs.
items.whyItMatters: Side by side, you can see the same decay law with different amounts of noise.

predictionMode.intro: Predict what should happen before you scrub time or change the sample.

items.explanation: After one half-life, the expectation is 50 percent of the start, but the actual tray still changes by whole nuclei at random times.
items.observationHint: Use the half-life markers, then compare the stepped count with the smooth expectation.

items.explanation: Changing sample size changes how noisy the actual result looks, not the half-life law itself. The fraction expectation stays the same, but the larger sample looks steadier.
items.observationHint: Jump between the presets or use compare mode, then focus on the fraction graph.

items.explanation: After a second equal interval, the expectation is halved again, so it becomes one quarter of the starting sample.
items.observationHint: Watch the half-life markers and the fraction graph together.

paragraphs: Graph transformations make more sense when one reference graph stays visible and every parameter acts on that same shape. This module keeps the base curve on screen while horizontal shift, vertical shift, vertical scale, and optional y-axis reflection build the transformed graph in the same coordinate plane.

The safest reading is landmark-first. Pick a known point on the base graph, such as the vertex, then track how its x-location changes under the inside move and how its y-value changes under the outside move. That is what separates horizontal placement from vertical stretching or reflection.

keyIdeas: Inside changes decide where a chosen base x-landmark ends up on the transformed graph.
Outside changes act on y-values after the horizontal placement has been determined.
A negative vertical scale reflects the graph across the x-axis, while the optional inside reflection reverses left-right orientation across the y-axis before the horizontal shift places the graph.

commonMisconception.myth: A positive horizontal shift must move the graph left because the sign inside the brackets looks backwards.
correction: Do not guess from the sign alone. Track a known base landmark and ask where the inside input must equal that same base x-value again.
On this bench, the base vertex starts at $x = 1$. If the inside reflection is on, that landmark flips to $x = -1$ first, and only then does the horizontal shift place the transformed vertex.

workedExamples.title: Track one landmark through the transformation
workedExamples.intro: Use the current transform and follow the base vertex. The same controls move the graph, the vertex markers, and these calculations.

items.title: Track where the vertex lands
items.prompt: The base graph has a vertex at $( {{baseVertexXValue}}, {{baseVertexYValue}} )$. For the current transform, where does that same landmark end up?

steps.label: 1. Work out the inside landmark
2. Place that landmark on the x-axis
3. Apply the vertical scale and shift

applyAction.label: Show reflection, then shift right
applyAction.observationHint: Watch the base vertex reflect across the y-axis first. Then the horizontal shift places it, and the negative vertical scale flips its height before the vertical shift sets the final y-value.

items.title: Read how $a$ and $k$ change the height
items.prompt: How do the current vertical scale and vertical shift change the base vertex height?

steps.label: 1. Start with the base vertex height
2. Apply the vertical scale
3. Add the vertical shift

applyAction.label: Show the upward stretch
applyAction.observationHint: Compare the base and transformed vertices: $a$ changes the distance from the x-axis first, then $k$ moves the result up or down.

miniChallenge.prompt: Set the graph so the transformed vertex is left of the y-axis but above the x-axis, and make sure the graph has been reflected across the x-axis.
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide first which controls must change the x-location, which controls must change the y-location, and which sign creates the x-axis reflection.
miniChallenge.answer: You need the transformed vertex x-coordinate to be negative, the transformed vertex y-coordinate to be positive, and the vertical scale to be negative so the graph reflects across the x-axis.
miniChallenge.explanation: The x-location comes from the inside reflection together with the horizontal shift. The height comes from the vertical scale and vertical shift. The x-axis reflection comes from the sign of the vertical scale.

quickTest.intro: Reason from the live graph and the landmark formulas together. These checks are about where the graph goes, not just about matching symbols.

questions.prompt: Which single change moves the graph up without changing its opening or left-right placement?
questions.explanation: Changing only $k$ lifts or lowers every output value without changing the graph's left-right position or opening direction.

showMeAction.label: Show a pure vertical shift
showMeAction.observationHint: The transformed graph keeps the same shape and orientation while the whole curve moves upward.

questions.prompt: A student says a negative $a$ only makes the graph steeper. What important effect are they missing?
questions.explanation: The sign of $a$ controls whether the outputs stay upright or reflect across the x-axis. The size of $a$ controls stretch or compression.

showMeAction.label: Show the x-axis reflection
showMeAction.observationHint: The graph opens downward because the outputs have been reflected across the x-axis.

questions.prompt: Two setups have the same $h$, $k$, and $a$, but only one turns on the y-axis reflection. What changes first?
questions.explanation: Turning on the y-axis reflection changes the inside input only, so the first visible difference is the left-right orientation of the graph.

showMeAction.label: Turn on the y-axis reflection
showMeAction.observationHint: Compare the reflected graph with the reference curve and watch the landmark flip to the opposite side first.

paragraphs: A coordinate grid shows the base graph and the transformed graph together. Sliders and a reflection toggle change the horizontal shift, vertical shift, vertical scale, and optional y-axis reflection, while vertex markers keep the main landmark visible.
A readout card reports the current values of $h$, $k$, $a$, the transformed vertex, and the y-intercept so the graph and the algebra can be checked together.

The first graph tab compares the base and transformed curves on one set of axes. The second graph tab shows how the current vertical shift changes the transformed vertex height as the vertical scale varies.
These graph views stay tied to the same landmark story: one view shows where the graph is, and the other shows how the transformed vertex height responds to $a$.

featuredSetups.description: Open the stretched graph with the base curve and transformed vertex visible together.

predictionMode.title: Predict the landmark move

items.prompt: If $h$ increases while everything else stays fixed, what happens first to the transformed vertex?
items.explanation: Changing only $h$ moves the graph horizontally. The vertex height stays fixed until $a$ or $k$ changes.
items.observationHint: Track the transformed vertex marker rather than the sign in the formula by itself.

items.prompt: If $a$ changes from positive to negative with $|a| > 1$, what is the clearest visual result?
items.explanation: A negative vertical scale reflects the graph across the x-axis, and a magnitude greater than 1 makes that reflected graph steeper.
items.observationHint: Watch the opening direction and the vertex height together.

items.prompt: If you turn on the y-axis reflection without changing any other control, what happens first?
items.explanation: The inside input reverses left-right orientation. It does not change the vertical scale or vertical shift by itself.
items.observationHint: Use the reference curve and the current vertex markers together.

challengeMode.intro: Use the graph like a landmark-matching bench: keep the reference curve visible, then place a reflected and scaled vertex deliberately instead of guessing from the symbols.

items.title: Land a reflected vertex
items.prompt: Build a reflected graph whose transformed vertex lands near $(-1, 2.4)$. Keep the reference curve and shift guide on so the move stays tied to the base landmark.
items.successMessage: You matched the reflected landmark while keeping the graph anchored to the base curve. The sign choices now read as visible moves instead of symbol tricks.

setup.note: Start from the base graph, then use the reference curve, vertex markers, and shift guide together.

hints: A vertex near $x=-1$ with almost no horizontal shift means the leftward landing must come mainly from the inside reflection.
A negative vertical scale flips the height first, and then the vertical shift finishes the placement.

paragraphs: This bench treats a rational function as one shifted reciprocal family you can read directly from the graph. As you change h, k, and a, the vertical asymptote x = h, the horizontal asymptote y = k, the branches, and the intercepts move together on one plane. You can also turn on one removable hole at x = p without changing the main asymptote structure.

Read the graph in this order: first find the forbidden x-value and the long-run y-level, then check which side of y = k each branch occupies, then decide whether an extra missing point is a true asymptote or only a removable hole. A vertical asymptote makes nearby values grow without bound. A removable hole leaves a finite nearby height but deletes the point itself.

keyIdeas: The denominator x - h creates the true vertical asymptote at x = h, so that x-value is excluded from the domain.
The constant k sets the horizontal asymptote y = k, so the graph settles toward that level far to the left and far to the right.
The sign of a decides which side of y = k each branch occupies, and the magnitude of a controls how sharply the graph bends near x = h.
A removable hole adds another excluded x-value without creating blow-up there, so it must be read differently from a vertical asymptote.

commonMisconception.myth: A removable hole is basically another vertical asymptote because both come from the denominator.
correction: Near a vertical asymptote, the function values grow without bound as x approaches the forbidden value.
Near a removable hole, the nearby curve still approaches one finite height.
So a hole deletes a point from an otherwise finite curve, while a true asymptote marks a place where the graph blows up.

workedExamples.title: Read the main landmarks first
workedExamples.intro: Use one plain shifted reciprocal and one hole case. In each example, identify the forbidden x-value, the long-run y-level, and any extra missing point before worrying about smaller details.

items.title: Read one shifted reciprocal from its landmarks
items.prompt: For the preset $f(x) = -1 + 2/(x - 1)$, what are the first landmarks to identify on the graph?

steps.label: 1. Find the forbidden x-value
2. Find the long-run y-level
3. Check the intercepts on the same graph

items.title: See why the hole is not an asymptote
items.prompt: In the removable-hole preset $f(x) = -0.5 + 3(x - 1)/((x + 1)(x - 1))$, what stays the same, and what changes?

steps.label: 1. Identify the true vertical asymptote
2. Separate the canceled factor from the asymptote
3. Read the missing point from the nearby curve

miniChallenge.prompt: Set the family so the true vertical asymptote is left of the y-axis, the right branch sits below the horizontal asymptote, and a removable hole appears at a positive x-value.
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide first which control moves the asymptote, which one flips the branch orientation, and which one only adds a removable missing point.
miniChallenge.answer: You need a negative h-value, a negative branch scale, and the removable-hole view turned on with the hole placed to the right of the y-axis.
miniChallenge.explanation: The asymptote position comes from h, the branch orientation comes from the sign of a, and the removable hole adds a second excluded x-value without changing the true asymptotes.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the graph itself: look for the forbidden x-value, the long-run y-level, and whether a missing point is infinite or finite.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one rational-function graph with dashed asymptote guides, two movable probe markers near the vertical asymptote, visible intercept markers when they exist, and an optional open-circle hole marker.

Sliders move the vertical asymptote, the horizontal asymptote, the branch scale, the probe distance, and the optional hole location so the same reciprocal family can be read from several landmarks at once.
The response graph shows the left-hand and right-hand values against distance from the vertical asymptote, together with the horizontal-asymptote level.
That makes it easier to compare blow-up near the forbidden x-value with the finite long-run level the family approaches far from it.

equations.meaning: Shows one reciprocal family with vertical asymptote x = h, horizontal asymptote y = k, and branch behavior controlled by a.
Lists the x-values the function cannot use: x = h for the true asymptote, and x = p as well when a removable hole is shown.
Far from the vertical break, the reciprocal term becomes small, so the graph approaches y = k.

variableLinks.description: Moves the true vertical asymptote x = h left or right without changing the horizontal asymptote level.
Moves the horizontal asymptote y = k up or down and shifts the long-run level of the whole family with it.
Controls which side of y = k each branch occupies and how sharply the graph bends near x = h.

controls.description: Move the true vertical asymptote x = h left or right.
Move the horizontal asymptote y = k up or down.

presets.description: A plain shifted reciprocal with one vertical asymptote, one horizontal asymptote, and visible intercepts.
The same asymptote layout, but the sign of a swaps which side of y = k each branch occupies.
The same reciprocal family, plus one finite missing point.

whatToNotice: Changing h moves x = h, and changing k moves y = k.
overlays.whyItMatters: These two guides give the fastest read of where the family breaks and where it levels off.

whatToNotice: As d shrinks, the y-values near x = h grow rapidly if there is a true vertical asymptote.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties asymptote language to nearby function values instead of only to an undefined x-value.

whatToNotice: Intercepts can move even when the asymptotes stay fixed.
overlays.whyItMatters: They give extra landmarks on the same family instead of replacing the asymptotes.

whatToNotice: Near the hole, the curve still approaches one finite height.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates a removable missing point from blow-up at x = h.

noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to keep forbidden x-values, long-run levels, and removable holes on one graph story.

items.text: Shrink the probe distance d and watch the y-values near x = h grow much faster. That is the visible difference between a true asymptote and an ordinary missing point.
Raise or lower k and watch the whole family move with y = k while the forbidden x-value stays fixed unless h changes too.
Turn on the removable hole and compare it with the true asymptote. The hole removes one finite point, but the asymptote layout and the long-run level stay the same.

predictionMode.title: Predict the graph behavior
predictionMode.intro: Decide what should move before you touch the controls.

challengeMode.title: Separate the hole from the asymptote
challengeMode.intro: Use the graph as a domain-break bench. The goal is to keep a true asymptote and a removable hole clearly different while still reading branch orientation from the same family.

items.title: Keep the true break and the removable break separate
items.prompt: Build a reciprocal family where the true vertical asymptote sits near $x=-1$, the removable hole sits at a positive x-value, and the right branch stays below the horizontal asymptote.
items.successMessage: You separated the true asymptote from the removable hole while keeping the branch orientation consistent. The two excluded x-values now tell different graph stories for visible reasons.

setup.note: Start from the clean shifted reciprocal, then turn the hole on and move the family until the true asymptote and the removable hole behave differently on the same graph.

hints: The sign of a decides which side of the horizontal asymptote the right branch occupies.
A removable hole should still have one finite nearby height, so do not place it on the asymptote itself.

paragraphs: Exponential change is easier to understand when growth or decay stays attached to one live curve. This bench keeps the starting value, the continuous rate, the target line, and the target-hit time on the same graph so every algebra step matches a visible crossing.

The key idea is multiplicative change, not adding the same amount each step. Equal time steps multiply the amount by the same factor, which is why growth can double and decay can halve on a fixed schedule. The logarithm appears when you reverse the question and solve for the time needed to reach a chosen target.

keyIdeas: In an exponential model, the amount follows y(t) = y_0 e^{kt}, and the sign of k decides whether the curve grows or decays.
Doubling time and half-life are fixed time scales because exponential change repeats by multiplication, not by adding a constant amount each step.
The inverse question is logarithmic: compare the target to the start with T / y_0, then solve for time with ln(T / y_0) / k.

commonMisconception.myth: Exponential change always means rapid growth, and logarithms belong to a separate chapter.
correction: A negative rate gives honest exponential decay, so the same model can fall toward zero instead of rising away from the start.
A logarithm appears naturally when you solve y_0 e^{kt} = T for time.
So the logarithm is not a disconnected new object here. It is the inverse question for the same exponential curve.

workedExamples.title: Read target time from the curve
workedExamples.intro: Use the growth-to-target preset and keep the target marker and log guide visible. First compare the target to the start, then solve for the hit time, then check that time against the graph.

items.title: Find the target-hit time
items.prompt: A population starts at 3 units and grows at a continuous rate of 0.25 until it reaches 12 units. How long should the curve take to hit the target?

steps.label: 1. Compare the target to the start
2. Solve for time with the log rule
3. Check the answer against doubling time

miniChallenge.prompt: Build a decay case where the target lies below the start and the hit time is longer than one half-life.
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide whether the target should sit just below the start or much lower than the halfway mark.
miniChallenge.answer: The target must sit well below half of the start so the curve needs more than one half-life to reach it.
miniChallenge.explanation: One half-life only cuts the amount in half. To need more than one half-life, the target must lie below that first halfway level.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the curve, the target line, and the inverse-time readout together.

showMeAction.label: Show growth turned into decay
showMeAction.observationHint: The same bench now falls toward zero because k is negative.

showMeAction.label: Show a target on the wrong side of the start
showMeAction.observationHint: The target line sits above the start while the curve keeps falling away from it.

showMeAction.label: Show why the logarithm appears

paragraphs: The simulation shows an exponential amount-versus-time curve with a starting value, a target line, an opposite-rate comparison curve, a doubling-time or half-life guide, and a smaller log view that straightens the inverse target question.

One graph shows the current exponential curve, the opposite-rate comparison, the target crossing, and the doubling-time or half-life cue when it exists. A second graph shows ln(amount / initial) as a straight line with the matching target-log line.

featuredSetups.description: Open a clean growth case with the target marker, opposite-rate comparison, and log guide visible on the same curve.

equations.label: Exponential model

equations.label: Solve for the target-hit time

equations.meaning: Shows that doubling time for growth and half-life for decay depend on the rate, not on the current amount.

variableLinks.description: Sets the starting amount y_0 at t = 0 and changes the reference height for both the curve and the target ratio.

controls.description: Set the starting amount y_0 at time 0.

A decay case where the target sits below the start and the half-life cue is immediately useful.

overlays.shortDescription: Show the target line and the crossing point when the curve can actually reach it.
whatToNotice: The hit point only appears when the target lies on the side of the start that matches growth or decay.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties the inverse-time question to one visible crossing on the curve.

whatToNotice: Keeping the same start but flipping the sign of k turns growth into decay, or decay into growth.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes growth and decay two versions of the same model instead of two separate topics.

overlays.whyItMatters: It makes multiplicative cadence visible instead of leaving doubling time and half-life as slogans.

noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to keep the curve, the target line, and the inverse-time readout on the same story.

items.text: Flip the sign of k while keeping the same start. One curve rises and the other falls, so the same target can become reachable or unreachable.
In the log view, the curved target question becomes a straight-line crossing because ln(y / y_0) = kt.
A fixed positive k gives one doubling cadence. A fixed negative k gives one half-life cadence.
Try the unreachable-target preset and check which side of the start the target lies on before you look for a hit time.
items.tryThis: Apply the unreachable-target preset, then compare the target line with the direction of the decay curve before reading the inverse-time card.

predictionMode.title: Predict the target behavior
predictionMode.intro: Commit to what the curve and the hit time should do before you change the rate or the target.

items.explanation: Changing only the sign of k flips growth into decay, so a target above the start stops being reachable on this bench.
items.observationHint: Watch the target marker and the opposite-rate comparison together.

items.explanation: With the same positive rate, a larger target ratio means a larger logarithm and therefore a larger hit time.
items.observationHint: Compare the target-log line before and after the target changes.

items.explanation: Doubling time is ln 2 / k, so a larger positive k makes that fixed time shorter.
