challengeMode.title: Read a quarter-target as two half-lives
challengeMode.intro: Use the curve, the target line, and the half-life guide together. The goal is to see a quarter-target as two multiplicative steps, not just as a number in a formula.

items.title: Make the target one quarter of the start
items.prompt: Build a decay case where the target is about one quarter of the start, so the curve reaches it in about two half-lives.
items.successMessage: You built a decay case where two half-life steps land on the target, so the ratio, the half-life guide, and the hit time all agree.

setup.note: Start from a simple decay case, then lower the target until it sits at one quarter of the start.

hints: A quarter of the start means two halvings, not one.
If the hit time is only one half-life, the target is still too high.

paragraphs: The derivative is the slope the curve has at one chosen point. This module keeps the point, the tangent line, the secant line, and the derivative graph linked together so you can see that local slope on the original curve before reading it on a second graph.

A secant uses two points and gives an average rate of change across an interval of width Δx. As Δx shrinks, the second point moves toward the first, the secant slope approaches the tangent slope, and that limiting local slope is the derivative.

keyIdeas: The derivative at one point is the tangent slope there: the local rate of change on the original curve.
A secant slope is an average rate of change across a finite interval, so shrinking Δx moves that average toward the tangent slope.
The derivative graph records those tangent slopes as the point moves along the original curve.

commonMisconception.myth: Once you have a derivative graph, it no longer has anything to do with the original curve.
correction: The derivative graph is built from tangent slopes on the original curve.
When the tangent rises, the derivative is positive. When the tangent is flat, the derivative is near zero. When the tangent falls, the derivative is negative.

workedExamples.title: Read the live slopes
workedExamples.intro: Use the current point and Δx on the live curve. First read the secant as an average rate over an interval, then compare it with the tangent and the derivative graph.

items.title: Compute the average rate from the secant
items.prompt: For the current point and Δx, what average rate of change does the secant line represent over that interval?

applyAction.label: Shrink Δx
applyAction.observationHint: As Δx gets smaller, the second point moves closer, the secant pivots toward the tangent, and the secant-slope graph moves toward the derivative value.

items.title: Read the local slope from the derivative
items.prompt: At the current point, what local rate of change does the derivative give?

applyAction.label: Move near a turning point
applyAction.observationHint: Near the turning point, the tangent becomes almost horizontal and the derivative graph moves close to zero even if the curve itself is not near $y=0$.

miniChallenge.prompt: Move the point to a place where the tangent is rising, but keep Δx large enough that the secant still shows a noticeably different average rate.
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide first whether you need a larger or smaller Δx to keep that gap visible.
miniChallenge.answer: You need a point where the tangent slope is positive and a Δx that is still large enough for the secant to show a visibly different average rate.
miniChallenge.explanation: The tangent uses only the local steepness at one point. The secant averages over a wider interval, so a larger Δx keeps the two slopes visibly different.

quickTest.intro: Use the tangent line, secant line, and derivative graph together. Each question is really asking what slope means on the original curve.

questions.explanation: A horizontal tangent has slope 0, so the derivative there is 0 or very close to 0.

showMeAction.label: Show the almost flat tangent
showMeAction.observationHint: The tangent is nearly horizontal and the derivative graph sits close to zero.

questions.prompt: Which statement best separates a secant slope from a tangent slope?
questions.explanation: A secant slope measures average change across an interval, while a tangent slope measures local change at one point.

showMeAction.label: Show average slope over a wider interval
showMeAction.observationHint: The secant still averages over a noticeable interval, so it does not yet match the tangent exactly.

questions.prompt: If the derivative graph is below zero at a point, what does that mean about the original curve there?
questions.explanation: A negative derivative means the tangent slope is negative, so the original curve is falling there.

showMeAction.label: Show a falling tangent
showMeAction.observationHint: The tangent falls and the derivative graph lies below zero.

paragraphs: A single coordinate grid shows the original curve, one movable point on the curve, an optional secant point, and the tangent line at the main point. Optional guides show the horizontal and vertical changes used in the difference quotient.
Moving the chosen point changes the local slope and the derivative reading together. Changing Δx moves the secant point and changes the interval used for the average rate.
The derivative graph plots tangent slope against x, so it acts as a slope map for the original curve.
The difference-quotient graph plots secant slope against Δx, so it shows how the average rate approaches the tangent slope as the interval shrinks.

equations.meaning: Measures the average rate of change across a finite horizontal interval.
Defines the tangent slope as the value the secant slope approaches when Δx shrinks to 0.
Uses the derivative at one point to write the line with the same local slope through that point.

variableLinks.description: Slides the chosen point along the curve, changing the tangent line, the secant comparison, and the derivative-graph reading together.
Sets the horizontal gap to the secant point. Smaller Δx makes the average rate move closer to the local slope.
Keeps the two-point average-rate comparison visible while you compare it with the tangent slope.

controls.description: Move the chosen point along the curve.
Set the horizontal spacing to the secant point used for the average rate.
Hide or show the secant comparison so you can compare average and local slope.

presets.description: A point where the curve is decreasing and the tangent slope is negative.

overlays.whyItMatters: It shows the derivative as a visible slope on the original curve.

overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the average-rate idea visible before the limit definition takes over.

overlays.whyItMatters: It connects the drawn interval to the algebra of the difference quotient.

graphs.description: Shows how the tangent slope changes across the curve: positive values mean the curve rises there, negative values mean it falls there, and values near zero mean a nearly flat tangent.
Shows how the secant slope changes as Δx shrinks and how it approaches the tangent slope at the chosen point.

noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to connect the curve, the lines, and the slope graphs. Read the geometry first, then use the graph as a slope map.

items.text: Move the point and watch the tangent switch from falling to rising. The derivative crosses zero where the tangent becomes flat.
Shrink Δx and compare the secant slope with the tangent slope. The average rate approaches the local rate.

challengeMode.intro: Use the point and Δx as a slope-comparison bench. The goal is to make the tangent and secant tell the same near-flat story for a geometric reason, not just to hit a number.

items.title: Match the near-flat slopes
items.prompt: Move to the right-hand turning point so the tangent is almost flat, then shrink Δx until the secant slope is almost flat there too.
items.successMessage: You found a point where both the local slope and the small-interval average slope are nearly zero. The derivative and difference quotient are now telling the same near-flat story.

setup.note: Use the secant graph and the Δy/Δx guide while you move toward the right-hand turning point.

hints: The right-hand turning point sits a little past $x=1.3$.
If the secant is still noticeably tilted, shrink Δx further.

paragraphs: A limit asks what the function values do as x gets close to a target, not what happens exactly at the target. On this bench the target stays fixed at $x = 0$, and the case slider plus the distance control $h$ let you watch whether the left-hand and right-hand values approach one shared height, split toward different heights, or fail to settle to any finite height at all.

Continuity is the second question, not the first. First decide whether both sides approach one finite value. Then compare that limiting value with the actual plotted point at $x = 0$: if they match, the function is continuous there; if they do not, you may have a removable hole, a jump, or no finite limit.

keyIdeas: Always read the left-hand and right-hand behavior separately first. A two-sided limit exists only when both sides approach the same value.
A removable hole and a continuous graph can have the same finite limit. The difference is whether the actual function value at the target matches that limit.
A jump fails because the two sides approach different heights, while a blow-up fails because the values do not settle to any finite height.

commonMisconception.myth: The filled point at the target decides the limit.
correction: The limit is about what nearby x-values are doing as they approach the target, not about the value at one point by itself.
A removable-hole case shows this clearly: the nearby values can approach one number even when the actual function value is different or missing.

workedExamples.title: Read the limit from the graph
workedExamples.intro: Use the case slider, the distance $h$, and the one-sided markers together. First read what the two sides are doing. Then compare that limit story with the actual point at $x = 0$.

items.title: Read the one-sided behavior
items.prompt: For the current {{caseLabelValue}} case with $h = {{hValue}}$, what do the left-hand and right-hand markers suggest about the limit?

steps.label: 1. Read the case and the sample distance
2. Read the left-hand and right-hand values
3. Decide whether a two-sided limit is possible

applyAction.label: Show the removable hole
applyAction.observationHint: The two sides are already approaching the same height even though the actual point at $x = 0$ does not sit there.

items.title: Compare the limit with the actual value
items.prompt: In the current {{caseLabelValue}} case, does the function value at $x = 0$ match the value the graph approaches?

steps.label: 1. Read the two-sided limit story
2. Read the actual value at the target
3. Decide whether the function is continuous

applyAction.label: Show the jump
applyAction.observationHint: The left and right sides approach different heights, so there is no single two-sided limit for the point at $x = 0$ to match.

miniChallenge.prompt: Choose the case where both sides approach the same finite height, but the function is still not continuous at the target.
miniChallenge.prediction: Before switching cases, decide whether the failure should come from disagreeing sides, no finite limit, or the actual point not matching the limit.
miniChallenge.answer: Choose the removable-hole case. The limit exists, but the actual function value does not match it.
miniChallenge.explanation: This separates the two ideas cleanly: limits describe nearby behavior, while continuity also requires the function value at the target to agree with that limit.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the one-sided markers, the limit guide, and the actual point. Each question is really asking which of those three pieces matters.

questions.explanation: A finite two-sided limit requires both sides to approach the same finite value.

showMeAction.label: Show matching sides
showMeAction.observationHint: Both sides approach the same height, and in this case the actual point lands there too.

questions.explanation: Continuity at a point means the limit exists and equals the actual function value there.

showMeAction.label: Show a limit that does not match the point
showMeAction.observationHint: The limit guide and the actual point are separated, so the function is not continuous even though the limit exists.

questions.explanation: If the values never settle to one finite number as $h$ shrinks, there is no finite two-sided limit.

showMeAction.label: Show no finite limit
showMeAction.observationHint: As the sample points move inward, the values do not settle to one finite height.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one coordinate graph with a target at $x = 0$, a function curve, and two movable sample points approaching the target from the left and the right. Depending on the selected case, the graph can be continuous, show a removable hole with a separate filled point, split into a jump, or rise and fall sharply near the target like a vertical asymptote.

A side readout reports the current left-hand value, right-hand value, limit reading, and actual function value at the target. Optional guides mark the sample points, the finite limiting height when one exists, and the actual point or undefined status at $x = 0$.

The one-sided-approach graph plots value against distance to the target, so the left-hand and right-hand traces show whether the two sides are converging to the same finite number, separating into a jump, or failing to settle at all.
Optional guides keep the finite limit and the actual function value visible as separate objects, which makes removable holes and true continuity easy to compare.

featuredSetups.label: Matching limit and point
featuredSetups.description: Open the case where both sides approach the same height and the actual point lands there too.

setup.note: Use this as the baseline continuity case before comparing holes, jumps, and blow-up behavior.

featuredSetups.label: Same limit, different point
featuredSetups.description: Open the case where both sides approach one height but the actual point is somewhere else.

setup.note: Use this to separate the limit from the actual function value at the target.

featuredSetups.label: Sides disagree
featuredSetups.description: Open the case where the left-hand and right-hand sides approach different heights.

setup.note: Use this to see why a two-sided limit needs agreement from both sides.

featuredSetups.label: No finite landing height
featuredSetups.description: Open the asymptote-like case where the nearby values do not settle to any finite value.

setup.note: Use this to compare a true no-finite-limit case with holes and jumps.

equations.meaning: Describe what the graph approaches from the left and from the right of the target.
A finite two-sided limit exists only when both one-sided limits approach the same number $L$.
Continuity at the target means the limit exists and equals the actual function value there.

variableLinks.description: Switches between continuous, removable-hole, jump, and blow-up behavior while keeping the same target x-value.
Moves the sample points closer to or farther from $x = 0$ so you can see whether both sides are settling to the same value.

controls.description: Switch between a continuous graph, a removable hole, a jump, and an asymptote-like blow-up.
Move the left-hand and right-hand sample points closer to or farther from the target at $x = 0$.

overlays.shortDescription: Show the left-hand and right-hand sample points and where they sit on the graph.
whatToNotice: As $h$ shrinks, the two markers reveal whether the sides are moving toward one value, different values, or no finite value at all.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the idea of approach visible instead of leaving it as a definition to memorize.

overlays.shortDescription: Show the shared finite limiting height when the case has one.
whatToNotice: The continuous and removable-hole cases can share the same finite limit guide.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the limit from the separate question of whether the actual point matches it.

overlays.shortDescription: Show the actual function value at $x = 0$, or show that no value is defined there.
whatToNotice: Continuity depends on this point matching the limiting value when a finite limit exists.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps $f(0)$ separate from the nearby approach behavior.

graphs.description: Shows how the left-hand and right-hand values behave as the sample points move toward the target from each side.

noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to keep three questions separate: what happens from the left, what happens from the right, and what happens exactly at the target.

items.text: Switch between the continuous and removable-hole cases. The nearby values can approach the same height in both cases, even though only one has the actual point at that height.
In the jump case, making $h$ smaller does not make the two sides agree. Each side approaches a different height.
In the blow-up case, the values do not settle to any finite height as $h$ shrinks. Their magnitudes keep growing instead.

predictionMode.title: Predict the limit story
predictionMode.intro: Predict what the graph should do before you switch cases or move the sample points inward.

items.prompt: If both sides head toward the same finite height but the actual point sits somewhere else, what type of behavior is that?
items.explanation: That is a removable discontinuity. The limit exists, but the function value at the target does not match it.
items.observationHint: Keep both the limit guide and the actual point visible so you can compare them directly.

items.prompt: If the left-hand and right-hand values approach different heights as $h$ shrinks, what happens to the two-sided limit?
items.explanation: A two-sided limit needs agreement from both sides. If the sides approach different heights, no single two-sided limit exists.
items.observationHint: Use a small $h$ and compare the two markers side by side.

challengeMode.intro: Use the graph as a classification bench. The goal is to separate “the limit exists” from “the function is continuous.”

items.title: Limit exists, continuity fails
items.prompt: Switch to the case where the left-hand and right-hand values agree on one finite limit, but the function is still not continuous at $x = 0$.
items.successMessage: You found the removable-hole case. The limit exists, but the actual point does not match it.

setup.note: Start from the continuous case, then look for the smallest change that breaks continuity without breaking the limit.

hints: A jump is not the right case because the two sides stop agreeing.
You want the case where the limit guide stays the same but the actual point moves away.

paragraphs: Optimization becomes easier to understand when the shape, the constraint, and the objective graph all respond to one choice. On this bench the perimeter is fixed at 24 meters, so choosing the width automatically fixes the height, the rectangle, the area, and the point on the graph.

That is why the square maximum is not a trick to memorize. On the narrow side, gaining width helps more than the lost height hurts. Past the square, the lost height hurts more than the extra width helps. The best rectangle sits at the balance point where the objective curve reaches its peak and the local area slope is zero.

keyIdeas: With perimeter 24 m, width and height are linked by h = 12 - w, so one width choice already determines the whole rectangle.
The objective graph A(w) = w(12 - w) is the same rectangle problem written in one variable, so its peak is the best possible area under this constraint.
A'(w) tells you which direction improves the area: positive means making the rectangle wider still helps, negative means you have gone past the best shape, and zero marks the turning point.

correction: On this bench, making the width larger always forces the height smaller because the perimeter stays fixed at 24 meters.
Area is width times height, so a longer base stops helping once the lost height becomes too costly.
That is why the objective curve eventually turns downward even while width is still increasing.

workedExamples.title: Read the tradeoff live
workedExamples.intro: Use the width slider, the rectangle, and the graphs together. First read the forced height from the perimeter, then read the area, then use the slope graph to decide whether wider would help or hurt.

items.title: Read the forced height and area
items.prompt: At the current width of ${{widthValue}}$ meters, what height does the fixed perimeter force, and what area does that give?

steps.label: 1. Use the fixed-perimeter constraint
2. Find the matching height
3. Compute the area from width times height

applyAction.label: Show a still-improving rectangle
applyAction.observationHint: On this side of the graph, the area is still increasing. The extra width is helping more than the lost height is hurting.

items.title: Use the slope to decide which way to move
items.prompt: At the current width of ${{widthValue}}$ meters, what does the local area slope say about making the rectangle a little wider?

steps.label: 1. Start from the one-variable area rule
2. Evaluate the slope at the current width
3. Use the sign to decide what happens next

applyAction.label: Show the best square
applyAction.observationHint: At the square, the objective curve reaches its peak and the slope graph hits zero at the same width.

miniChallenge.prediction: Decide first whether you should approach the square from the narrow side or from the wide side.
miniChallenge.answer: You must move close to the square, because only near $w = 6$ do the width and height nearly balance and the area slope become almost zero.
miniChallenge.explanation: This challenge keeps the geometry and derivative story together. The square is best not because squares are automatically special, but because the width-height tradeoff stops improving the area there.

quickTest.intro: Use the rectangle, the objective graph, and the slope graph together. These checks ask why the maximum happens, not just what number it is.

equations.meaning: Fixes the total perimeter, so choosing a width immediately fixes the matching height.
Rewrites the rectangle area using the perimeter constraint so the whole optimization problem depends on one variable.
Tells whether making the rectangle a little wider would increase area, decrease area, or land at the turning point.

variableLinks.description: Moves the one free design choice. Once w changes, the fixed perimeter forces the height, area, and local slope to update together.

graphs.description: Shows the constrained area A(w) for the 24 meter perimeter. Its highest point is the maximum possible area.
Shows whether making the rectangle wider would help or hurt area at the current width: positive helps, negative hurts, and zero marks the peak.
Shows the fixed-perimeter tradeoff h = 12 - w, so every extra meter of width costs one meter of height.

noticePrompts.intro: Use the rectangle, the tradeoff line, and the area graph as one linked story.

items.text: This rectangle is still too narrow. A little more width gives back more area than the lost height removes.
Near the square, the area graph flattens. That near-zero slope is the sign that the best rectangle is here, not at one of the extreme widths.
Past the square, extra width hurts because the height is now dropping too fast.
The constraint line h = 12 - w shows the one-for-one tradeoff directly: every extra meter of width costs one meter of height.

challengeMode.title: Optimization challenge
challengeMode.intro: Use the rectangle, the objective graph, and the slope graph together. The goal is to reach the maximum because the tradeoff balances there, not just to guess 36.

items.title: Find the maximum-area rectangle
items.prompt: Move the width until the rectangle reaches the maximum area and the local area slope is essentially zero.
items.successMessage: You found the square maximum. The rectangle, the peak of A(w), and the zero of A'(w) all agree on the same width.

setup.note: Start from the narrow rectangle, then move toward the square while watching the height tradeoff and the objective curve flatten.

hints: The best rectangle is the square under this fixed perimeter.
The slope should settle near zero at the same width where the area graph reaches its peak.

paragraphs: This bench treats an integral as a running total, not a frozen shaded picture. As you move the upper bound, the signed area on the source graph and the matching point on the accumulation graph update together, so you can watch the total build, level off, or fall.

The key distinction is local height versus accumulated amount. The source value f(x) tells you whether the next thin slice adds or subtracts right now. The accumulation A(x) remembers all signed area from 0 to the current bound, so it can stay positive even after the source curve has dropped below the axis.

keyIdeas: A(x) is the signed area from 0 to the current upper bound, so moving the bound changes the running total rather than creating a separate static picture.
When f(x) is above the axis, new slices add to A(x); when f(x) is below the axis, new slices subtract from A(x).
Because A'(x) = f(x), the current source height gives the local slope of the accumulation graph, but that slope is not the same thing as the total accumulated so far.

commonMisconception.myth: If f(x) is negative at a bound, then A(x) must already be negative there.
correction: A negative source height only tells you that the running total is decreasing at that moment.
Earlier positive signed area may still keep A(x) above zero, which is why the source graph and accumulation graph have to be read together.

workedExamples.title: Read the running total live
workedExamples.intro: Use the upper-bound slider, the signed-area shading, and the accumulation point together. First read the source height at the current bound, then read the total that has been built so far.

applyAction.label: Try a near-cancel case
applyAction.observationHint: The running total is close to zero because earlier positive area and later negative area are nearly balancing, not because the current source height is near zero.

items.title: Separate height from total
items.prompt: At the current bound, how do the source height and the accumulated total represent different information?

applyAction.label: Try a negative-tail case
applyAction.observationHint: Here f(x) is below zero, so the accumulation graph slopes downward locally. But the total A(x) can still stay positive because of earlier positive area.

miniChallenge.prompt: Move the bound to a place where the source curve is below the axis, but the running total has not fallen below zero yet.
miniChallenge.prediction: Before you drag, decide whether you need a point just after the source crosses below the axis or much farther into the negative region.
miniChallenge.answer: You need a bound where the curve has already dropped below the axis but the earlier positive signed area has not been fully cancelled yet.
miniChallenge.explanation: This is the clearest way to separate local change from total accumulation. New slices are negative, but the earlier positive area has not been fully cancelled yet.

quickTest.intro: Use the source graph, the shaded area, and the accumulation graph together. These questions are really checking whether you can separate current height from total accumulation.

questions.explanation: Because A'(x) = f(x), a zero source height means the accumulation graph is locally flat there.

showMeAction.observationHint: The source height is near zero, so the accumulation graph is nearly flat at the matching x-value even though A(x) itself is not zero.

showMeAction.label: Show a negative height but a positive total
showMeAction.observationHint: The source height is negative there, but the total A(x) depends on all the signed area collected earlier too.

questions.explanation: Area below the axis counts negatively, so extending the bound through that region subtracts from the running total.

showMeAction.observationHint: The source curve is below the axis, so each new slice subtracts from A(x).

paragraphs: The simulation shows a source curve with a movable upper bound. The signed area from 0 to that bound is shaded on the source graph, and a second graph shows the corresponding accumulated amount A(x).
Moving the bound updates the current source height, the shaded area, and the matching point on the accumulation graph together, so local height and running total can be compared directly.
The source-function graph shows whether the next slice adds or subtracts from the total. The accumulation graph shows the running total built from all signed area between 0 and x.
The two graphs share the same x-value: the source height sets the local slope of the accumulation graph, while the y-value on the accumulation graph gives the total collected so far.

equations.meaning: Defines A(x) as the signed area collected from 0 to the current bound x.
The local slope of the accumulation graph at x is exactly the current source height f(x).
Over a small step in x, the change in the running total is approximately height times width.

variableLinks.description: Moves the endpoint on both graphs, changing how much signed area has been accumulated from 0 and where the matching point sits on A(x).

controls.description: Move the endpoint x that determines how much signed area has been accumulated from 0.

overlays.shortDescription: Shade the signed area collected from 0 to the current bound.
whatToNotice: Area above the axis adds to the total, while area below the axis subtracts from it.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the integral visible as accumulation, not just as notation.

overlays.shortDescription: Mark the current upper bound on the source graph.
whatToNotice: The source height at this x-value tells you how the running total is changing right now.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the current source value from the whole accumulated amount.

overlays.shortDescription: Mark the matching point on the accumulation graph.
whatToNotice: The y-value is the running total, and the local slope there is set by the current source height.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the link A'(x) = f(x) visible on the graph.

graphs.description: Shows the source curve that supplies the signed area being accumulated.
Shows the running total built from the signed area between 0 and x.

noticePrompts.intro: Use the same x-value on both graphs. One graph tells you what the next slice is doing now, and the other tells you how much has been collected so far.

items.text: While the source curve stays above the axis, moving the bound to the right adds positive slices and makes the running total rise.
Once the source drops below the axis, the accumulation graph can still sit above zero even while its local slope turns negative.
Compare f(x) with A(x) directly: one is the current height at the bound, and the other is the total signed area collected up to that bound.

predictionMode.intro: Predict whether the running total should rise, flatten, or fall before you move the bound.

items.explanation: If the source height stays positive, each extra thin slice adds positive signed area, so the running total increases.
items.observationHint: Use the shaded area and the accumulation point as the same event shown on two graphs.

items.explanation: Because A'(x) = f(x), a negative source height means the accumulation graph slopes downward at that x-value, even if A(x) is still above zero.
items.observationHint: Keep the graph's current height and its local slope separate.

challengeMode.intro: Use the two graphs together. The goal is to keep local height and total accumulation separate, even when the signs tempt you to treat them as the same thing.

items.prompt: Move the bound to a place where the source height is negative, but the running total is still positive.
items.successMessage: You found the key split between local rate and accumulated amount. The source is subtracting locally, but the earlier positive area still keeps the running total above zero.

setup.note: Watch the current height on the source graph and the total on the accumulation graph at the same x-value.

hints: Move past the right-hand zero of the source curve, but not so far that the earlier positive area has been fully cancelled.
The accumulation graph can stay above zero even while its local slope turns negative.

paragraphs: A 2D vector is easiest to understand when you can see both the arrow and its components at the same time. This module keeps A, B, the scaled vector sA, and the resultant on one plane so addition, subtraction, and scaling can all be read from one shared picture.

The key habit is to switch between arrow language and component language without treating them as different objects. When a vector changes on the plane, its ordered pair and the graphs change with it, so the geometry and the algebra should always agree.

keyIdeas: A 2D vector is one object with two equivalent descriptions: an arrow on the plane and an ordered pair of components.
Addition and subtraction both work component by component, and the same tip-to-tail picture still works if subtraction is treated as adding the opposite vector.
A scalar changes the size of A, and a negative scalar also reverses its direction through the origin.

commonMisconception.myth: Vector subtraction needs a completely different picture from vector addition.
correction: Subtraction is still vector addition, but with the opposite of the second vector.
That is why the same tip-to-tail construction still works after B is reversed.

workedExamples.title: Work from the live plane
workedExamples.intro: Use the current vectors and scalar from the plane. The same live state drives the arrows, the graphs, and these substitutions, so every step should match something you can see.

items.title: Find the resultant components
items.prompt: For the current setup, what are the x- and y-components of the resultant vector?

applyAction.label: Try subtraction
applyAction.observationHint: Watch B reverse before it is moved tip-to-tail. The resultant is still built by addition, but now you are adding the opposite vector.

items.title: Read the scaled version of A
items.prompt: Before combining with B, what vector does the current scalar make from A?

applyAction.label: Try a negative scalar
applyAction.observationHint: When the scalar becomes negative, the scaled copy of A flips through the origin before any combination happens. Then the resultant is built from this flipped arrow.

miniChallenge.prompt: Adjust the vectors until the resultant is small even though the visible vectors are still clearly nonzero.
miniChallenge.prediction: Before testing, decide whether the cancellation needs to happen in x, in y, or in both.
miniChallenge.answer: Both the x-components and the y-components must nearly cancel, so the resultant ends close to the origin.
miniChallenge.explanation: A small resultant does not require small vectors. It happens when positive and negative component contributions nearly balance in each direction.

quickTest.intro: Use the plane, the component readout, and the graphs together. The right answer should fit what the bench shows, not just a memorized rule.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes a 2D vector?
questions.explanation: A 2D vector is one object with two equivalent descriptions: an arrow on the plane and an ordered pair of components.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: Components are exact coordinate descriptions, not rough estimates.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: Geometry and algebra are two views of the same vector, not separate systems.

showMeAction.label: Show one vector in both forms
showMeAction.observationHint: The arrow, component guides, and live component values all describe the same vector state.

questions.prompt: What is the most accurate way to think about vector subtraction on this bench?
questions.explanation: Subtraction is addition with the opposite vector, which is why the tip-to-tail construction still works after B is reversed.

showMeAction.label: Show subtraction as adding the opposite
showMeAction.observationHint: B flips first, then the same tip-to-tail construction builds the resultant.

questions.prompt: If the scalar becomes negative while A is not the zero vector, what must happen to the scaled version of A?
questions.explanation: A negative scalar reverses the direction of A, and the absolute value of the scalar sets the size of the scaled arrow. Magnitude does not become negative.

showMeAction.label: Show the flipped scale
showMeAction.observationHint: The scaled copy of A flips through the origin before the sum is taken.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a coordinate plane with two vectors from the origin, a scaled copy of A, and a resultant vector. Optional overlays can show the tip-to-tail construction, the resultant's component guides, and the scaled copy of A before combination.
Changing any component, the scalar, or subtract mode updates the plane, the algebraic readout, and the graphs together, so the geometric and component views stay synchronized.
One graph shows how the resultant's x- and y-components change as the scalar on A changes. The other shows how the resultant magnitude changes over the same scalar scan.
The highlighted point on each graph matches the current plane setup, so you can connect the current scalar setting to one exact resultant arrow.

variableLinks.description: Changes how far vector A points right or left on the plane.
Changes how far vector A points up or down on the plane.
Changes how much vector B contributes horizontally before addition or subtraction.
Changes how much vector B contributes vertically before addition or subtraction.
Scales A before it combines with B. A negative value flips A through the origin, and the absolute value sets its size.
Replaces B with its opposite, so subtraction uses the same tip-to-tail construction as addition.

overlays.shortDescription: Show the resultant's horizontal and vertical projections on the axes.
whatToNotice: The same resultant can be read as one diagonal arrow or as two perpendicular components.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links the plane picture directly to the component rule.

overlays.shortDescription: Move the effective second vector to the tip of the scaled first vector.
whatToNotice: Addition and subtraction use the same construction once B is kept or reversed.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes vector combination something you can see, not just a rule to memorize.

overlays.shortDescription: Show the scaled copy of A before it combines with B.
whatToNotice: A positive scalar keeps A's direction, while a negative scalar flips it through the origin.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates scalar multiplication from the later addition or subtraction step.

noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to keep the plane picture, the component readout, and the graphs describing the same vector event.

items.text: Drag A or B and compare the arrow on the plane with the ordered pair. They change together because they are the same vector in two forms.
Turn subtraction on and watch B reverse before it is moved tip-to-tail. The construction is still addition, but now you are adding the opposite of B.
Move the scalar through zero and watch sA flip through the origin. The magnitude graph never goes negative, because size and direction are different ideas.

predictionMode.title: Predict the change
predictionMode.intro: Predict what the plane and graphs should do before you change the operation or the scalar.

items.explanation: A negative scalar reverses A through the origin. Because the scalar's magnitude is still above 1, the scaled copy is also longer than A.
items.observationHint: Watch the scaled copy of A first, then see how the resultant and the graphs respond.

items.explanation: Subtraction is addition with the opposite vector, so the same tip-to-tail rule still works after B is reversed.
items.observationHint: Watch B reverse, then watch the resultant rebuild from the same construction.

challengeMode.intro: Use the plane like a vector lab, not a static diagram. The goal is to make cancellation visible while the individual vectors are still clearly nonzero.

items.prompt: Adjust the vectors until the resultant lands very close to the origin, but keep the scaled first vector clearly nontrivial so the near-zero result comes from cancellation rather than from shrinking everything.
items.successMessage: You created a near-zero resultant by cancellation, not by making the vectors trivial. The plane, the component readout, and the magnitude now agree.

setup.note: Use the tip-to-tail view and the component guides together. One shows geometric cancellation, while the other shows which x- and y-components are balancing.

hints: A near-zero resultant needs both the x-components and y-components to nearly cancel.
Keep the scalar away from zero so the first vector still matters.

paragraphs: A 2 by 2 matrix is easiest to read as a rule that transforms the whole plane, not as a detached box of numbers. This bench keeps the grid, basis vectors, unit square, and sample triangle on one coordinate system so stretch, shear, reflection, and mixed actions stay visible together.

Read the columns first. The first column is $M e_1$ and the second column is $M e_2$. Once you know where those two basis vectors land, every other point follows by the same linear-combination rule, which is why the entire grid moves consistently.

keyIdeas: The two matrix columns tell you where $e_1$ and $e_2$ land, so they already predict the new grid and the image of the unit square.
Every vector is built from x copies of $e_1$ and y copies of $e_2$, so after transformation it becomes the same combination of $M e_1$ and $M e_2$.
Stretch, shear, and reflection are different plane-wide outcomes of the same linear rule.

commonMisconception.myth: Each entry moves the sample shape separately, so the grid and basis vectors are just extra decoration.
correction: The entries matter because they set the two basis-image columns.
Once $M e_1$ and $M e_2$ are fixed, the unit square, the grid, and every other point follow automatically from the same linear-combination rule.

workedExamples.title: Read the matrix from its columns
workedExamples.intro: Use one shear case and one reflection case. In each example, read the columns first, then check the same story on the plane.

items.title: Read the basis images in a shear
items.prompt: For the shear matrix $M = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, where do $e_1$ and $e_2$ land, and what does that predict for the unit square?

steps.label: 1. Read the two columns
2. Match the first column to $M e_1$
3. Match the second column to $M e_2$

applyAction.label: Show the shear
applyAction.observationHint: Watch $M e_2$ lean right while $M e_1$ stays on the x-axis. The unit square follows those two columns into a parallelogram.

items.title: Track one point through a reflection
items.prompt: Where does $p = \langle 1.6, 1.1 \rangle$ land under the reflection matrix $M = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, and what does that mean geometrically?

steps.label: 1. Write the rule for any point
2. Compute the new x-coordinate
3. Compute the new y-coordinate

applyAction.label: Show the reflection
applyAction.observationHint: The x-coordinate changes sign while the y-coordinate stays the same, so the point mirrors across the y-axis.

miniChallenge.prompt: Build a matrix that shears the plane to the right without turning it into a reflection, and keep $M e_1$ close to the original x-axis direction.

miniChallenge.answer: Keep $M e_1$ near $\langle 1, 0 angle$ and move $M e_2$ to the right, for example by making the second-column x-entry positive.
miniChallenge.explanation: A right shear comes from changing where $e_2$ lands while leaving $e_1$ almost unchanged. That tilts the unit square into a right-leaning parallelogram without flipping the plane.

A reflection changes the whole linear action, so every point on the plane flips consistently, not just the sample triangle.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a coordinate plane with a transformed grid, two draggable basis-image vectors, a transformed unit square, a transformed sample triangle, and a tracked point. Sliders control the four matrix entries that determine where the two basis vectors land.
Changing an entry updates the basis images, the grid, the unit square, and the sample triangle together so the learner can connect the matrix columns to one plane-wide action.

One graph tracks the x- and y-coordinates of a fixed point while the current matrix is blended in from the identity matrix. The second graph tracks the lengths of the transformed basis vectors over the same blend.
Those graphs are tied to the same plane picture, so hovering them previews an intermediate transformation directly on the grid and shapes.

setup.note: Change the second column first. A right shear comes from moving where $e_2$ lands while leaving $e_1$ nearly unchanged.

paragraphs: The dot product is easiest to understand as a measure of alignment. This bench keeps two vectors, the angle between them, the signed projection of $\vec{B}$ onto $\vec{A}$, and the scalar $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}$ on the same plane so the picture comes first and the formula confirms it.

The key question is: how much of $\vec{B}$ points along the direction of $\vec{A}$? If that along-$\vec{A}$ part points with $\vec{A}$, the dot product is positive. At $90^\circ$, that part is zero. Past $90^\circ$, it points against $\vec{A}$, so the dot product becomes negative for a geometric reason.

keyIdeas: The dot product measures alignment by multiplying the size of $\vec{A}$ by the signed along-$\vec{A}$ part of $\vec{B}$.
Orthogonal vectors have dot product zero because $\vec{B}$ has no along-$\vec{A}$ component at all.
A negative dot product means the projection of $\vec{B}$ points against $\vec{A}$, not that either vector has a negative length.

commonMisconception.myth: A negative dot product means one of the vectors has a negative length.
correction: Vector lengths stay nonnegative. The sign comes from direction: once the angle is obtuse, the projection of $\vec{B}$ onto $\vec{A}$ points against $\vec{A}$.
Positive, zero, and negative dot products come from alignment, not from a new kind of length.

workedExamples.title: Read the picture, then the formula
workedExamples.intro: Use these as interpretation guides while you drag the vectors. Start by reading the angle and the amber projection, then use the formula to name what the picture is showing.

items.title: Read the dot product from alignment
items.prompt: How can you read the current dot product from the angle and the amber projection on the bench?

steps.label: 1. Use A as the reference direction
2. Read how much of B lies along A
3. Combine size with signed alignment

applyAction.label: Show a right-angle case
applyAction.observationHint: At $90^\circ$, the amber projection collapses even though both vectors are still visible, so the dot product drops to zero because there is no along-A part of B.

items.title: Read the along-A part of B
items.prompt: How should you interpret the amber projection vector on the bench?

steps.label: 1. Keep the projection on A's line
2. Use the sign of the along-A length
3. Rebuild B from parallel plus perpendicular parts

applyAction.label: Show an obtuse case
applyAction.observationHint: Once the angle passes $90^\circ$, the amber projection flips to the opposite side of the origin because the along-A part of B now points against A.

miniChallenge.prompt: Keep both vectors clearly nonzero, then rotate $\vec{B}$ until the dot product is almost zero. What must happen to the amber projection as the angle approaches $90^\circ$?
miniChallenge.prediction: Predict whether the projection shrinks to zero, keeps the same sign, or flips sign before you test it.
miniChallenge.answer: The projection must shrink toward zero because $\vec{B}$ is losing its along-A component.
miniChallenge.explanation: Orthogonal does not mean short. It means none of $\vec{B}$ points along $\vec{A}$.

quickTest.intro: Use the picture and the readout together. These checks are about interpreting sign and projection geometrically.

questions.explanation: The sign is about alignment, not about which vector is longer or whether the components match.
Orthogonality removes the along-A piece. That is why zero dot product does not require a zero vector.
Negative sign means the along-A part points opposite A. Length still stays nonnegative, and the perpendicular leftover can still be present.

paragraphs: The simulation shows two draggable vectors from the origin on one coordinate plane. An angle marker can show the angle between them, and an amber guide can show the projection of B onto A together with the dashed perpendicular leftover.
Changing either vector updates the stage, the readout, and the angle-response graphs together so the learner can compare geometry, sign, and projection on one bench.

One graph shows how the dot product changes as the angle between the current vector lengths opens from $0^\circ$ to $180^\circ$. The second graph shows how the scalar projection of B onto A changes over that same angle sweep.
Hovering either graph previews the same angle on the stage, so the response curve, the angle marker, and the amber projection stay synchronized.

equations.meaning: Packages alignment into one number: acute gives positive, right angle gives zero, and obtuse gives negative.
Gives the signed length of the along-A part of B.
Turns that signed length into a vector that lies on A's line.

overlays.shortDescription: Show the angle between A and B at the origin.
whatToNotice: The sign change happens when the angle crosses $90^\circ$, not when either vector becomes small.

overlays.shortDescription: Show the projection of B onto A and the perpendicular leftover.

overlays.whyItMatters: It turns the dot product into a visible decomposition instead of a hidden multiplication rule.

graphs.description: With the current vector lengths fixed, shows how angle alone changes the dot product from positive to zero to negative.
With the current vector lengths fixed, shows how the signed along-A part of B changes as the angle moves from acute to obtuse.

noticePrompts.intro: Keep the angle marker, the amber projection, and the sign reading on the same story.

items.text: An acute angle keeps a positive along-A part, so both the projection and the dot product are positive.
As the angle approaches $90^\circ$, the amber projection shrinks to zero even if $\vec{B}$ stays long.
Past $90^\circ$, the projection flips against A, so the dot product becomes negative.

predictionMode.title: Predict the sign

items.explanation: Orthogonality removes the along-A part of B. Length alone does not keep the dot product positive.

items.explanation: An obtuse angle means the along-A part of B points the opposite way, so both the signed projection and the dot product flip sign.

challengeMode.intro: Use the angle marker, the amber projection, and the response graph together. The goal is to make zero alignment visible without shrinking either vector away.

items.title: Make the projection nearly vanish

Use the angle marker first, then confirm with the amber projection.

paragraphs: Complex numbers become easier to trust when the algebra and geometry stay on the same plane. This bench keeps z, w, and the current result visible together so a + bi can be read as both the point (a, b) and the arrow from the origin to that point.

Addition should look like vector addition: add real parts and imaginary parts, or place one arrow head-to-tail with the other. Multiplication should look different: it changes size by multiplying magnitudes and changes direction by adding arguments, so multiplying by w scales z and rotates it at the same time.

keyIdeas: A complex number z = a + bi is one object with two views: the point (a, b) and the vector from the origin to that point.
Adding complex numbers adds their horizontal and vertical components, just like vector addition on the plane.
Complex multiplication multiplies magnitudes and adds arguments, so a multiplier near the unit circle mainly rotates while a larger or smaller magnitude also stretches or shrinks.

commonMisconception.myth: Complex multiplication is just a symbolic rule for components, so the geometric picture is optional.
correction: The algebraic rule and the geometric picture describe the same operation.
On this plane, multiplication by w changes the size of z by |w| and changes its direction by arg(w), which is why the product can be read as both a formula result and a rotated, scaled arrow.

workedExamples.title: Read the result on the plane
workedExamples.intro: Use the live plane instead of a detached worksheet. First read z and w as points or arrows, then read the result from the same geometry.

2. Scale and turn z

miniChallenge.answer: Choose a multiplier whose argument turns z into the second quadrant while its magnitude stays close to 1.
miniChallenge.explanation: In multiplication, the product direction comes from adding arguments and the product size comes from multiplying magnitudes.

Optional guides can show head-to-tail addition, a one-unit reference circle, and a rotation cue for multiplication.

One graph shows how the real and imaginary parts of z + w change as the real part of w changes. A second graph shows how the real and imaginary parts of z · w change under the same sweep, so addition and multiplication can be compared as two different geometric actions on the same plane.

paragraphs: The unit circle is easier to understand when one rotating point, its projections onto the axes, and the sine-cosine traces all stay visible together. This bench ties every view to the same live angle, so the graphs read as a record of the motion rather than as separate rules to memorize.

Because the circle has radius 1, the x-coordinate of the point is exactly $\cos\theta$ and the y-coordinate is exactly $\sin\theta$. When the point moves left of the y-axis, cosine becomes negative; when it moves below the x-axis, sine becomes negative.

keyIdeas: On the unit circle, angle $\theta$ selects the point $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$.
Cosine is the horizontal projection and sine is the vertical projection of the same rotating point.
Sign changes come from position on the axes: left gives negative cosine, and below the x-axis gives negative sine.

commonMisconception.myth: Sine and cosine are just graph rules to memorize, so the unit-circle picture is optional.
correction: The traces come from the same rotating point on the circle.
On a radius-1 circle, the x-coordinate is $\cos\theta$ and the y-coordinate is $\sin\theta$, so the graphs are recording those coordinates over time.

workedExamples.title: Read the live circle
workedExamples.intro: Use the current rotation state and read the geometry first. The same live angle sets the point on the circle, the axis projections, and the graph markers.

2. Read cosine and sine from the projections

2. Use the axes to predict the signs

applyAction.observationHint: Use the sign map to predict the signs first, then confirm them from the point and the trace values.

miniChallenge.prompt: If the point rotates counterclockwise from Quadrant I into Quadrant II, which projection changes sign first, and why?
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide which axis is crossed first and which coordinate that crossing changes.
miniChallenge.answer: Cosine changes sign first because the point crosses the y-axis first.
miniChallenge.explanation: Crossing the y-axis changes the x-coordinate from positive to negative, so cosine changes sign while sine stays positive.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the live circle, the projections, and the traces, not from a memorized list.

questions.explanation: On the unit circle, the x-coordinate is cosine and the y-coordinate is sine, so the projections match those two values.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a unit circle with one rotating point, projection guides to the x- and y-axes, an angle marker, a swept arc, and a sign map for the four quadrants.
One graph shows cosine and sine changing over time, and the other shows the angle changing over time. Both graphs are linked to the same moving point on the circle.

featuredSetups.label: See projections live

equations.meaning: Because the radius is 1, the coordinates of the point selected by angle theta are exactly cosine theta and sine theta.
With constant angular speed, the angle changes linearly in time.
The sign of $\cos\theta$ matches the sign of x, and the sign of $\sin\theta$ matches the sign of y.

variableLinks.description: Controls how quickly the point turns, so the angle graph gets steeper and the sine-cosine traces cycle faster.

controls.description: Set how quickly the point rotates around the circle. Faster rotation makes the angle graph steeper and the sine-cosine traces cycle faster.

whatToNotice: The same angle sets the point on the circle and the markers on the graphs.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the sign pattern for each quadrant visible.
whatToNotice: Left side means negative cosine, and below the x-axis means negative sine.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns sign changes into a geometry check instead of a memory trick.

overlays.shortDescription: Show the arc swept from the positive x-axis to the current point.

paragraphs: Polar coordinates are easier to trust when the same point stays visible in both polar and Cartesian language at once. This bench keeps r, θ, x, and y tied to one live point, so conversion is read from the geometry instead of treated like a separate worksheet.

Changing the radius should feel like sliding the point farther from or closer to the origin along the same ray. Changing θ should feel like rotating that ray around the plane while the x- and y-coordinates update as the horizontal and vertical projections of the same point.

keyIdeas: A polar point is determined by a distance r from the origin and an angle θ from the positive x-axis.
The same point has Cartesian coordinates x = r cos θ and y = r sin θ.
Keeping θ fixed changes the distance along one ray, while keeping r fixed moves the point around one circle.

commonMisconception.myth: Polar coordinates and Cartesian coordinates describe different things, so converting between them is just algebraic relabeling.
correction: They are two descriptions of the same point on the same plane.
The geometry explains the conversion: θ sets the direction, r sets the distance from the origin, and x and y are the horizontal and vertical projections of that same radius.

workedExamples.title: Read one point in both forms
workedExamples.intro: Use the reference-point preset so one ray, one angle, and one set of coordinate guides stay aligned. Start from the polar data, then read the same point in Cartesian form.

items.title: Read x and y from r and θ
items.prompt: For the reference-point preset, where does the current polar point land on the x-y plane?

steps.label: 1. Read the current polar values
2. Project the radius onto the axes
3. Write the Cartesian coordinates

applyAction.label: Show one point in both forms
applyAction.observationHint: Keep the guides on: the radius and angle locate the point, and the guides show that same point's x- and y-coordinates.

miniChallenge.prompt: Keep θ in Quadrant II, then move the point farther from the origin without changing the signs of x and y. What should you change?
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide whether changing r, changing θ, or changing both will keep the point in the same quadrant while increasing its distance from the origin.
miniChallenge.answer: Increase only the radius while keeping θ in Quadrant II.
miniChallenge.explanation: θ sets the direction and therefore the signs of x and y. Changing r only moves the point farther out along the same ray.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one coordinate plane with a point, a radius ray, an optional angle arc, dashed coordinate guides to the axes, and readout cards that report both polar and Cartesian values for the same point.

equations.meaning: Projects the radius onto the horizontal axis to give the x-coordinate.

paragraphs: This bench keeps one rotating point, its horizontal and vertical projections, and the squared-projection graph visible together. That way, trig identities come from one piece of geometry instead of feeling like detached symbol rules.

On the unit circle, every point is exactly 1 unit from the origin, so $x^2 + y^2 = 1$ is always true. When you read the same point as $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$, that becomes $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$.

As the point moves through different quadrants, raw sine or cosine can change sign, but the squared sum does not change because the radius does not change. In the first quadrant, complementary angles swap the horizontal and vertical projections, so sine and cosine swap places too.

keyIdeas: Every point on the unit circle satisfies $x^2 + y^2 = 1$ because the radius is fixed at 1.
Reading the same point as $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ turns that distance rule into $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$.
Quadrant changes can flip the sign of sine or cosine, but squaring removes the sign while keeping the same projection lengths in the identity.
In the first quadrant, complementary angles swap the horizontal and vertical projections, so $\sin\theta = \cos(90^\circ - \theta)$ and $\cos\theta = \sin(90^\circ - \theta)$.

commonMisconception.myth: Trig identities are just algebra facts to memorize, so the unit-circle picture is optional.
correction: The identities come from the geometry of one point on the unit circle.
If the point stays 1 unit from the origin, its horizontal and vertical projections must still satisfy $x^2 + y^2 = 1$, which becomes $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$.
A sign flip in sine or cosine does not break the identity, because the squared terms still come from the same fixed radius.

paragraphs: Inverse trig is easier to trust when you can still see the point that created the ratio. This bench keeps one polar point, its x- and y-coordinates, and the angle-recovery graph visible together so “angle from ratio” stays geometric instead of turning into a calculator trick.

For any point with $x 
e 0$, the ratio $y/x$ equals $\tan\theta$. But $\arctan(y/x)$ returns only a principal angle, not always the full polar direction. The signs of x and y still decide which quadrant the ray actually lies in, which is why $\operatorname{atan2}(y, x)$, or equivalent quadrant reasoning, is safer.

keyIdeas:
For a point with $x 
e 0$, the ratio $y/x$ gives $\tan\theta$.
$\arctan(y/x)$ returns a principal angle, so it may describe a reference-style angle rather than the full polar angle.
The signs of x and y tell you whether the point lies in Quadrant I, II, III, or IV.
Changing radius at a fixed angle scales x and y by the same factor, so the direction and the ratio $y/x$ stay the same.

commonMisconception.myth: If $\arctan(y/x)$ gives an angle, that number must already be the full direction of the point.
correction: The ratio $y/x$ does not uniquely identify the quadrant because Quadrant II and Quadrant IV can share the same tangent sign and value.
You still need the signs of x and y, or a function like $\operatorname{atan2}(y, x)$, to recover the actual direction safely.

paragraphs: A parametric curve is built by one shared clock t. At each moment, x(t) and y(t) together choose one point in the plane. This bench keeps those two coordinate values, the moving point, and the traced path visible at once so the equations stay tied to the geometry.

Keep two questions separate. First, what set of points gets traced out? That is the path. Second, how does the point move through those points as time passes? That is the motion. Amplitudes mainly set the size of the path, while frequencies and phase shift control how the point is timed along it.

keyIdeas: At each time t, x(t) and y(t) must be read together as one ordered pair (x(t), y(t)).
Amplitudes set the horizontal and vertical reach of the traced path.
Frequencies and phase shift change the timing between x and y, so they affect how the point moves through the curve and can also change the shape that gets traced.

commonMisconception.myth: If two settings trace the same-looking curve, the motion along it must also be the same.
correction: The traced path and the timing along that path are related but different ideas.
To tell how the point is moving, you need the time-based information such as x(t), y(t), or the speed graph, not just the picture of the curve.

miniChallenge.explanation: That is the key separation in a parametric curve. Amplitudes mainly shape the trace, while frequency, phase shift, and the current time help determine how the point moves through it.

One graph shows x(t) and y(t) together, so each time corresponds to one ordered pair on the plane.
A second graph shows the point's speed over time, which helps separate the shape of the path from how fast the point moves along it.

paragraphs: Start with two separate questions: how often do particles collide, and how many of those collisions actually react? This bench keeps the particle box, threshold cue, and live rate readout together so you can see both parts of the rate story at once.

Concentration mostly changes how often particles get chances to collide. Temperature changes both collision frequency and the fraction energetic enough to clear the barrier. A catalyst changes the story in a different way: it lowers the effective barrier, so more collisions succeed without needing hotter particles.

keyIdeas: Reaction rate depends on successful collisions, not just on total collision attempts.
Higher concentration mainly increases collision chances, while higher temperature also increases the fraction that can clear the barrier.
A catalyst speeds the reaction by lowering the effective barrier, not by heating the particles or crowding the box.

commonMisconception.myth: If the box gets more crowded, every extra collision should automatically count as a reaction.
correction: A crowded box mainly creates more collision attempts.
The reaction speeds up only when enough of those collisions also clear the activation barrier.

workedExamples.title: Worked examples on the live collision bench
workedExamples.intro: Use the same particle box, threshold cue, and graphs. The goal is to read rate as collision frequency multiplied by success, not as a single mysterious number.

items.title: Separate collision attempts from successful reactions

miniChallenge.answer: A strong solution keeps concentration moderate but makes a larger share of collisions clear the barrier.

questions.prompt: Which statement best matches the simulation?
questions.explanation: A reaction needs successful collisions, not just lots of collisions. A busy box can still react slowly if most hits fail the barrier test.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: This ignores the activation barrier. Collision attempts and successful reactions are not the same thing.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: Temperature can matter even in a moderately crowded or dilute box because it changes how many collisions clear the barrier.

showMeAction.observationHint: The particles are more energetic, and a larger fraction of collisions becomes successful.

questions.prompt: What does the catalyst change most directly on this bench?
questions.explanation: The catalyst lowers the effective barrier, so more collisions can succeed without changing the crowding.

showMeAction.label: Switch on the catalyst
showMeAction.observationHint: The barrier cue changes even if the particle motion cue changes only a little.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a particle box with reactants moving and colliding, plus cues for total collisions, successful collisions, and the barrier that collisions must clear. Sliders change temperature, concentration, and activation threshold, and a toggle applies a catalyst.
A readout reports the current temperature, concentration, threshold, successful fraction, and successful-collision rate so the learner can connect the moving box to the graphs.

One graph compares all collision attempts with successful collisions as temperature changes, a second makes the same comparison as concentration changes, and a third shows the successful fraction against temperature.
Reading the box and the graphs together helps the learner separate two ideas: how often particles collide and how often those collisions actually react.

sections.title: From collision theory to rate changes

featuredSetups.description: Open a crowded, low-temperature box and compare frequent collision attempts with the much smaller number of successful collisions.

setup.note: Start here to separate a busy particle box from a genuinely fast reaction.

featuredSetups.description: Open a lower-barrier setup and watch the successful share rise without needing hotter particles.

setup.note: Use this to compare lowering the barrier with simply heating the mixture.

pageIntro.definition: Use collision theory to predict whether a rate change comes from more particle meetings, more barrier-clearing hits, or both.
pageIntro.whyItMatters: It lets you explain temperature, concentration, and catalysts with one visible cause-and-effect model instead of memorized rate slogans.
pageIntro.keyTakeaway: A reaction speeds up only when collision attempts increase, the successful fraction increases, or both happen together.

equations.label: Reaction rate = attempts × successful fraction
equations.meaning: Separate how often particles meet from how often those meetings actually become reactions.

equations.label: Clearing the barrier
equations.meaning: Temperature helps by increasing the fraction of collisions with enough energy to get over the barrier.

equations.label: Catalyst lowers the effective barrier
equations.meaning: A catalyst changes the barrier side of the story without needing hotter particles.

variableLinks.description: Changes particle energy and motion, so it can raise both collision rate and the fraction that clears the barrier.
Changes how crowded the box is, so it mostly changes how often particles get chances to collide.

variableLinks.label: Activation energy barrier
variableLinks.description: Sets how much energy a collision needs in order to react.
Lowers the effective barrier, so more of the same collisions can succeed.

controls.description: Raise or lower particle energy in the box.
Change how often particles get chances to collide.

controls.label: Activation energy barrier
controls.description: Raise or lower the energy barrier collisions must clear.
Lower the effective barrier without increasing particle energy.

presets.description: Moderate temperature and crowding, with no catalyst.
Many collision attempts, but only a limited fraction clear the barrier.
Fewer particles than the crowded case, but a larger fraction of collisions clear the barrier.
Similar crowding, but a lower effective barrier lets more collisions succeed.

overlays.shortDescription: Show motion streaks so temperature changes stay visible in the particle box.
whatToNotice: Hotter particles move more vigorously before you even compare the graphs.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes temperature a visible cause of rate change rather than a hidden setting.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the barrier and above-threshold share visible.
whatToNotice: The successful fraction can rise even when the box is not especially crowded.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates collision count from barrier-clearing success.

overlays.shortDescription: Show all collisions and successful collisions separately in the vessel.
whatToNotice: The all-collision pulses can stay busy while the successful pulses remain much fewer.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the difference between more collisions and more reaction directly visible.

graphs.label: Collision attempts and successful collisions vs temperature
graphs.description: As temperature rises, compare the growing attempt rate with the growing number of successful collisions.

graphs.label: Collision attempts and successful collisions vs concentration
graphs.description: As the box gets more crowded, compare extra collision chances with the number that actually react.

graphs.label: Successful fraction vs temperature
graphs.description: Shows how heating increases the fraction of collisions that clear the barrier.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice in the collision box
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the particle box, threshold cue, and graph visible together. First ask how often collisions happen, then how many of them succeed.

items.text: When concentration rises, the all-collision pulses can increase faster than the successful pulses. A busier box does not automatically mean a faster reaction.
items.whyItMatters: Concentration mainly increases opportunities for collisions, not guaranteed success.

items.text: When temperature rises, look for more collisions to clear the barrier, not just for faster particle motion.
A catalyst can raise the successful share even when the motion cue changes little. The key change is the lower barrier, not hotter particles.

predictionMode.title: Predict what changes the rate
predictionMode.intro: Before you test a change, decide whether it changes collision frequency, barrier-clearing success, or both.

items.prompt: From the baseline mixture, you raise the temperature while concentration and barrier stay fixed. What is the best prediction?

choices.label: Collision attempts should rise, and a larger fraction should clear the barrier.
The rate should stay about the same because concentration did not change.
The successful fraction should fall because faster particles are harder to react.

items.explanation: Higher temperature makes particles collide more actively and makes more of those collisions energetic enough to clear the barrier.
items.observationHint: Watch the motion cue and the successful-fraction graph together after the change.

items.prompt: You switch on the catalyst while keeping the box at the same moderate temperature. Which explanation is best?
items.changeLabel: Same particle energy, lower effective barrier

choices.label: A larger fraction of collisions should succeed because the barrier is lower.
The particles must be much hotter, so the motion cue should show most of the change.
Only the concentration graph should change.

items.explanation: A catalyst changes the barrier story rather than the temperature story, so the successful share rises without needing faster particle motion.
items.observationHint: Look for a bigger change in the barrier cue than in the motion cue.

challengeMode.title: Challenge: speed it up without crowding
challengeMode.intro: Make the reaction clearly faster by improving collision success, not just by stuffing the box with particles.

items.title: Raise success, not just collision count
items.prompt: Build a setup where the reaction is clearly fast even though concentration stays modest. Keep the threshold cue and collision pulses on so you can show that the rate rose because more collisions succeeded.
items.successMessage: You produced a fast reaction without relying on a crowded box. The main improvement came from a larger successful fraction, not just more collision attempts.

setup.note: Keep concentration near the baseline. The cleanest solution uses temperature and/or catalyst to improve success.

hints: Stay near the baseline concentration instead of solving the task by packing in more particles.
Temperature and catalyst are the cleanest tools here because they raise the successful fraction.

targets.label: Keep concentration modest, between 0.9 and 1.15.
Raise the successful-collision rate to between 12 and 15.
Make the successful fraction strong, between 48% and 62%.

paragraphs: Dynamic equilibrium does not mean the reaction has stopped. It means the forward and reverse reactions are still happening while the overall amounts look steady. Keeping the particle view, rate bars, and time graphs together makes that balance visible.

A disturbance changes the current mix or the conditions, so the forward and reverse rates no longer match. The system then shifts until it reaches a new equilibrium. Le Chatelier's principle is easiest to understand here as a visible rebalancing process, not a slogan to memorize.

keyIdeas: At dynamic equilibrium, the forward and reverse rates are equal even though microscopic forward and reverse changes continue.
Changing the amounts or the product-favor setting disturbs equilibrium because the current mix no longer matches the equilibrium mix for those conditions.
Le Chatelier's principle describes the shift toward a new equilibrium after a disturbance.

commonMisconception.myth: If the amounts stop changing, the reaction has stopped or the reactants and products must now be equal.
correction: At equilibrium, the key condition is equal forward and reverse rates, not zero reaction activity.
Reactant and product amounts do not have to be equal at equilibrium. They only have to stay steady because the two rates match.

workedExamples.title: Worked examples on the live bench
workedExamples.intro: Use the live bench instead of a detached table. The same controls drive the particle view, the rate graphs, and the equilibrium target shown here.

items.title: Read the current rate balance
items.prompt: At the current moment, how do the forward and reverse rates compare?

steps.label: 1. Read the current reactant and product amounts
2. Compare the forward and reverse rates
3. Use the rate difference to judge how close the system is to equilibrium

applyAction.label: Load the product-heavy start
applyAction.observationHint: A product-heavy start can still move toward equilibrium. What matters first is whether the reverse rate is larger than the forward rate.

items.title: Read the equilibrium mixture for these conditions
items.prompt: For the current conditions, what product share is the system heading toward at equilibrium?

steps.label: 1. Read the current product share
2. Read the equilibrium product share for these conditions
3. Compare the current mix with the equilibrium target

applyAction.label: Load product-favored conditions
applyAction.observationHint: When the target band shifts upward, the concentration curves bend toward a more product-rich equilibrium mix.

miniChallenge.prompt: Create a clear disturbance, then let the system settle until the rates nearly match again under product-favored conditions.
miniChallenge.prediction: Before you touch the controls, decide whether you need to change the starting amounts, the product-favor setting, or both.
miniChallenge.answer: You need to move the system away from its old equilibrium and choose conditions that place the new equilibrium on the product-rich side.
miniChallenge.explanation: That shows both ideas together: equilibrium is dynamic because the exchange continues, and Le Chatelier's principle describes the shift toward a new balance after a disturbance.

quickTest.title: Quick check
quickTest.intro: Answer from the live rate-and-target story, not from the idea that equilibrium means the reaction is finished.

questions.prompt: What is the best description of dynamic equilibrium on this bench?
