questions.explanation: Dynamic equilibrium means the forward and reverse rates are equal. The reaction continues microscopically even though the overall amounts stay steady.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: A steady amount graph does not mean nothing is happening. The exchange can continue while the forward and reverse rates balance.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: If only the forward change continued, the product amount would keep rising instead of settling.

showMeAction.label: Load a steady-looking exchange
showMeAction.observationHint: The rate bars can nearly match while the exchange pulses still remain active.

questions.explanation: If the current product share is below the new target, the forward rate becomes larger and the mixture shifts toward more products until equilibrium is re-established.

showMeAction.label: Load the product-favored shift
showMeAction.observationHint: The target sits above the current product share, so products increase until the rates come back together.

questions.prompt: Which graph cue most honestly tells you the system is near equilibrium?
questions.explanation: The best equilibrium cue is that the forward and reverse rates are nearly equal, not that the amounts look equal or that both species are present.

showMeAction.label: Show a near-equilibrium moment
showMeAction.observationHint: Near the end of the run, the two rate curves nearly meet even though both are still non-zero.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a reversible reaction bench with reactants and products visible at the same time, exchange pulses for the forward and reverse reactions, rate bars, and an equilibrium target band. Sliders change the starting amounts and the product-favor setting.
A readout reports the current reactant amount, product amount, forward rate, reverse rate, and equilibrium product share so the learner can compare the moving scene with the graphs.
One graph shows reactant and product amounts over time, a second shows forward and reverse rates over time, and a third shows equilibrium product share against the product-favor setting.
Reading the first two graphs together helps the learner see the key pattern: after a disturbance, the rates separate, the mixture shifts, and later the rates come back together near equilibrium.

sections.title: From equilibrium to Le Chatelier shifts

equations.label: Net rate
equations.meaning: The sign and size of r_f - r_r tell you which direction the mixture is still shifting and how far it is from balance.

equations.label: Equilibrium means equal rates
equations.meaning: Dynamic equilibrium is reached when the forward and reverse rates are equal, not when the reaction has stopped.

equations.label: Shift to a new equilibrium
equations.meaning: When conditions change, the equilibrium composition changes, so the system shifts until the forward and reverse rates match again.

variableLinks.description: Sets the starting amount of reactant, which affects the initial rate imbalance and the path toward equilibrium.
Sets the starting amount of product, which affects the initial rate imbalance and the path toward equilibrium.
Changes which side is favored at equilibrium, so it moves the target mixture toward products or reactants.

controls.description: Change the starting reactant amount to disturb the current balance.
Change the starting product amount to disturb the current balance.
Shift the equilibrium target toward products or toward reactants.

presets.description: Starts far from equilibrium on the reactant side, so the forward rate leads first.
Starts far from equilibrium on the product side, so the reverse rate leads first.
Uses conditions that move the equilibrium target toward a more product-rich mixture.
Uses conditions that move the equilibrium target back toward reactants.

overlays.label: Exchange pulses
overlays.shortDescription: Keep the forward and reverse exchange pulses visible.
whatToNotice: The exchange pulses can stay active even when the amount curves have almost flattened out.
overlays.whyItMatters: It shows that equilibrium is dynamic rather than stopped.

overlays.label: Rate balance bars
overlays.shortDescription: Show the forward and reverse rates side by side.
whatToNotice: Near equilibrium, the two rate bars nearly match even though the reactant and product amounts do not have to be equal.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties the definition of equilibrium to rate balance, not to equal amounts or a frozen system.

overlays.label: Equilibrium target
overlays.shortDescription: Keep the current mixture and the equilibrium target visible together.
whatToNotice: The system keeps shifting only while the current mixture still differs from the equilibrium target for those conditions.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes Le Chatelier shifts visible as motion toward a new equilibrium.

graphs.label: Reactants and products over time
graphs.description: Shows how the reactant and product amounts move toward equilibrium after a disturbance.

graphs.label: Forward and reverse rates over time
graphs.description: Shows the rates separate after a disturbance and then come back together near equilibrium.

graphs.label: Equilibrium product share
graphs.description: Shows how changing the product-favor setting shifts the equilibrium mixture.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice about equilibrium
noticePrompts.intro: Read the rate bars, the current mix, and the equilibrium target together on the same bench.

items.text: The amount curves can flatten while both exchange pulses are still active. Equilibrium means equal forward and reverse rates, not a stopped reaction.
items.whyItMatters: This is the core idea of dynamic equilibrium: steady amounts can still come from ongoing forward and reverse change.

items.text: A disturbance matters when it makes the current mix differ from the equilibrium target. Watch the target band and the time graph together.
After a disturbance, the rates separate first, the mixture shifts next, and only later do the rates come back together.
items.tryThis: Apply one preset, then switch to the opposite favor setting and watch the rate graph and the concentration graph until a new balance is reached.

predictionMode.title: Predict the direction of the shift
predictionMode.intro: Before you test a change, ask how it moves the current mix relative to the equilibrium target for the new conditions.

items.prompt: Starting from the reactant-heavy case, you add even more reactants without changing the favor setting. What should happen first?
items.scenarioLabel: Add more reactants under the same conditions

choices.label: The forward rate should become larger first, so the system shifts toward products.
The reaction should stop because the mixture is too far from equilibrium.
The reverse rate should dominate immediately.

items.explanation: With more reactants under the same conditions, the forward reaction has the larger rate at first. The system therefore shifts toward products until the rates match again.
items.observationHint: Watch the forward rate pull ahead first, then watch the product amount rise toward the new equilibrium mix.

items.prompt: You change from product-favored conditions to reactant-favored conditions while keeping the same starting amounts. What should the equilibrium mixture do?
items.scenarioLabel: Change the conditions to favor reactants

choices.label: The equilibrium product share should decrease, and the reverse rate should lead during the shift.
The equilibrium product share should stay the same because the starting amounts did not change.
Both rates should instantly drop to zero.

items.explanation: Changing the conditions changes the equilibrium target. The system then shifts toward that new target, so the reverse rate can lead until the new balance is reached.
items.observationHint: Compare the equilibrium-share graph with the rate bars after the switch.

challengeMode.title: Challenge: disturb then rebalance
challengeMode.intro: Make a real disturbance, then give the system time to settle into a new equilibrium instead of treating equilibrium like an instant switch.

items.title: Create a shift, then let it rebalance
items.prompt: Start from a reactant-heavy mixture, choose clearly product-favored conditions, and wait until the forward and reverse rates nearly match again while the mixture stays product-rich.
items.successMessage: You created a genuine equilibrium shift and let the system settle. The mixture is product-favored, and the clearest equilibrium cue is that the forward and reverse rates are nearly equal again.

setup.note: Change the starting mix or the favor setting, then let the bench run long enough to approach the new equilibrium.

hints: Start from a reactant-heavy mixture, then choose conditions that place the equilibrium target on the product-rich side.
The challenge only counts once the system has had time to bring the forward and reverse rates close again.

targets.label: Set the product-favor setting between 1.28 and 1.4 so the new equilibrium clearly favors products.
Let the current product share settle between 58% and 70%.
Wait until the forward-reverse rate gap falls below 0.14 before judging the result.

paragraphs: Concentration is not just about how full the beaker looks. It tells you how much solute is present for a given amount of liquid. This bench keeps the particle view, amount-volume guide, and concentration readout together so you can connect the number to what the mixture looks like.

Dilution means adding more solvent while keeping the solute amount the same. That spreads the same dissolved particles through more liquid, so concentration falls. Adding solute can also change concentration, but for a different reason: there is more dissolved material, not just more liquid around it.

keyIdeas: Concentration compares solute amount with liquid volume.
Dilution lowers concentration by increasing solvent volume while keeping the solute amount fixed.
Adding solute can also change concentration, but that is not the same process as dilution.

commonMisconception.myth: If the beaker looks fuller, it must contain more solute.
correction: A fuller beaker can simply contain more solvent, so the same solute is spread out more.
To tell whether concentration changed, you have to check both solute amount and solvent volume.

workedExamples.title: Worked example: read the beaker first
workedExamples.intro: Use the live beaker to decide what is changing: solute amount, solvent volume, or both.

items.title: Read what makes this mixture concentrated or dilute
items.prompt: For the current mixture, what makes the concentration high or low?

steps.label: 1. Read the solute amount
2. Read the solvent volume
3. Use both to read the concentration

applyAction.label: Apply the same-solute dilution
applyAction.observationHint: The solute amount stays the same, but the particles spread through more liquid and the concentration readout falls.

miniChallenge.prompt: Make the beaker clearly less concentrated without removing any solute.
miniChallenge.prediction: Before you change anything, decide whether dilution means changing solute amount, solvent volume, or both.
miniChallenge.answer: Add solvent while keeping the solute amount fixed.
miniChallenge.explanation: That lowers concentration by spreading the same dissolved particles through more liquid.

quickTest.title: Quick check
quickTest.intro: Answer from the beaker, the amount-volume guide, and the concentration graphs.

questions.prompt: What does dilution mean on this bench?
questions.explanation: Dilution means adding more liquid so the same solute is spread through a larger volume.

questions.prompt: Which change raises concentration because there is more solute, not because the volume changed?
questions.explanation: Adding solute raises concentration by increasing the amount of dissolved material rather than by changing the amount of liquid.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a beaker with dissolved particles, a concentration readout, and controls for solute amount and solvent volume.
The overlays help the learner compare how much solute is present with how much liquid it is spread through.
One graph shows how concentration changes with solvent volume, and a second shows how concentration changes with solute amount.
Comparing the two graphs helps separate dilution from simply adding more solute.

featuredSetups.description: Open a concentrated case and notice many solute particles packed into a small solvent volume.
Open the same solute amount in more liquid and compare how the particles spread out as concentration falls.

equations.label: Concentration from amount and volume
equations.meaning: Use it as a reading rule: the same solute is less concentrated when it is spread through more liquid.

equations.label: Dilution keeps solute fixed
equations.meaning: During dilution, the solute amount stays the same while the solvent volume increases.

variableLinks.description: Changes how much dissolved material is in the beaker. More solute in the same volume means higher concentration.
Changes how much liquid the solute is spread through. More solvent with the same solute means lower concentration.

presets.description: Lots of solute packed into a smaller solvent volume.
The same solute amount spread through more liquid, so the concentration is lower.
A higher concentration caused by adding more solute to the same liquid volume.

overlays.shortDescription: Show how crowded the same solute looks in different volumes.
whatToNotice: When the solute amount stays the same, adding more solvent makes the particles look more spread out.
overlays.whyItMatters: It connects the concentration number to how crowded the dissolved particles really are.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep solute amount and solvent volume visible side by side.
whatToNotice: Adding solvent and adding solute can both change concentration, but they change different parts of the mixture.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you see why dilution means changing volume, not changing the amount of solute.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice in the beaker
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the beaker, the amount-volume guide, and a concentration graph visible together.

items.text: Increase solvent volume while keeping solute amount fixed. The concentration should fall because the same particles are spread through more liquid.
Increase solute amount while keeping solvent volume fixed. The concentration rises because there is more dissolved material in the same amount of liquid.

predictionMode.title: Predict before you dilute
predictionMode.intro: Predict what the concentration will do before you add solvent or solute.

items.prompt: If solvent volume increases while solute amount stays fixed, what should happen to the concentration?

choices.label: The concentration should drop because the same solute is now spread through more liquid.
The concentration should rise because the beaker is fuller.
Nothing should change.

items.explanation: Dilution lowers concentration because the same solute is spread through a larger volume of liquid.
items.observationHint: Watch the concentration readout fall while the particle view looks more spread out.

challengeMode.title: Challenge: dilute without losing solute
challengeMode.intro: Use dilution honestly: lower concentration by adding solvent, not by quietly changing to a different solute amount.

items.title: Lower concentration by adding solvent
items.prompt: Start from a crowded beaker and make it clearly less concentrated while keeping the solute amount close to the starting value.
items.successMessage: You diluted the mixture without switching to a different solute case. The concentration fell because the same dissolved material occupied more liquid.

paragraphs: Solubility and concentration are related, but they are not the same idea. Solubility tells you the maximum amount that can stay dissolved under the current conditions. Concentration tells you how much dissolved solute is actually present per amount of solvent.

Saturation happens when the dissolved amount reaches that current capacity. After that point, adding more solute does not keep raising the dissolved amount under the same conditions. The extra material stays as visible excess solid. This bench keeps dissolved amount, excess solid, and capacity in one place so those roles stay separate.

keyIdeas: Concentration describes the dissolved solute already in the solvent.
Solubility describes the current maximum amount that can stay dissolved under these conditions.
Once the beaker is saturated under fixed conditions, extra solute increases the excess pile rather than the dissolved amount.

commonMisconception.myth: If you can see more solid in the beaker, the solution itself must be more concentrated.
correction: Visible solid can mean the beaker has already reached its dissolving capacity.
Once saturation is reached, extra solute can increase the undissolved pile while the dissolved amount stays at the same limit.

workedExamples.title: Worked example: read capacity before saturation
workedExamples.intro: Use the live beaker to separate total solute, dissolved solute, and excess solid.

items.title: Read the current dissolving capacity
items.prompt: For the current beaker, how much solute can stay dissolved and how much must remain as excess solid?

steps.label: 1. Read the solubility limit
2. Use the solvent volume to find the current capacity
3. Compare the total solute with that capacity

applyAction.label: Apply the near-saturation case
applyAction.observationHint: The dissolved amount should sit close to the capacity line, and any extra solute should start appearing as a visible excess pile.

miniChallenge.prompt: Make the excess solid disappear without removing any solute from the beaker.
miniChallenge.prediction: Before you move a control, decide whether you need to increase capacity or decrease total solute.
miniChallenge.answer: Increase the beaker's capacity by adding solvent or raising the solubility limit.
miniChallenge.explanation: Both moves let more of the same total solute stay dissolved. Removing solute would solve a different problem.

quickTest.title: Quick check
quickTest.intro: Answer from the beaker, the dissolved amount, and the capacity limit together.

questions.prompt: What does saturation mean on this bench?
questions.explanation: Saturation means the dissolved amount has reached the current capacity. Any extra solute must stay undissolved.

questions.prompt: Which idea tells you the maximum amount that can stay dissolved under the current conditions?
questions.explanation: The solubility limit sets the current capacity. Concentration only tells you how much is already dissolved.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a beaker with dissolved solute, a visible excess-solid pile when saturation is reached, and controls for total solute, solvent volume, and the current solubility limit.
A readout reports total solute, current capacity, dissolved amount, excess solid, and concentration so the learner can compare the visual beaker with the numeric state.
One graph shows dissolved amount against total solute, a second shows excess solid against total solute, and two more show how capacity changes with solvent volume and solubility limit.
Together they show the key saturation pattern: dissolved amount levels off at capacity while excess solid begins to grow.

featuredSetups.description: Open a beaker that is still below its current dissolving capacity, so all of the solute can remain dissolved.
Open a saturated beaker where the dissolved amount has reached capacity and extra solute remains as visible solid.

equations.label: Current dissolving capacity
equations.meaning: The maximum dissolved amount depends on both the solubility limit and the amount of solvent present.

equations.label: Dissolved amount cannot exceed capacity
equations.meaning: If total solute is below capacity, it can all dissolve. If total solute is above capacity, only the capacity amount stays dissolved.

equations.label: Excess solid after saturation
equations.meaning: Any solute above the current capacity remains visible as undissolved solid.

equations.label: Concentration of the dissolved solution
equations.meaning: Concentration counts only the dissolved solute in the solvent, not the extra solid sitting in the beaker.

variableLinks.description: Changes the total amount trying to dissolve. Below capacity it raises the dissolved amount; above capacity it mainly builds the excess pile.
Changes how much liquid is available, so it changes the beaker's dissolving capacity and the concentration of what is dissolved.
Changes the maximum amount that can stay dissolved per unit volume under the current conditions.

presets.description: The total solute is still below capacity, so all of it can stay dissolved.
The beaker is close to saturation, so a small extra amount of solute would create visible excess solid.
The beaker is saturated, so extra solute remains as an undissolved pile.

overlays.shortDescription: Show the current capacity and how full that capacity is.
whatToNotice: As the beaker approaches saturation, the dissolved amount approaches a ceiling instead of rising forever.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes solubility a visible capacity limit rather than just a word.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the dissolved portion easy to distinguish from the undissolved solid.
whatToNotice: Concentration follows the dissolved portion, not the total amount poured into the beaker.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the dissolved solution from the excess solid that no longer fits.

overlays.shortDescription: Show the undissolved solid once total solute exceeds capacity.
whatToNotice: After saturation, extra solute builds the excess pile instead of increasing the dissolved amount.
overlays.whyItMatters: It shows why saturation is different from simply making a solution more concentrated.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice as the beaker saturates
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the beaker, the saturation gauge, and the dissolved/excess cues visible together.

items.text: Watch for the moment when the dissolved amount stops rising with added solute and the excess pile starts to grow.
items.tryThis: Start from room to dissolve, then raise the solute amount slowly until the first undissolved solid appears.
items.whyItMatters: That is the point where the solution reaches saturation under the current conditions.

items.text: Changing the solubility limit changes the beaker's capacity itself, not just the concentration number.
items.tryThis: Increase the solubility limit and watch the same total solute move farther below saturation as more of it can stay dissolved.
items.whyItMatters: It separates solubility, which sets the cap, from concentration, which describes the dissolved portion right now.

predictionMode.title: Predict the capacity shift
predictionMode.intro: Predict whether the beaker will gain dissolving room or move closer to saturation before you apply the change.

items.prompt: What should happen if you raise the solubility limit while keeping the same total solute and solvent volume?
items.explanation: A higher solubility limit increases capacity, so more of the same total solute can stay dissolved and the excess pile can shrink or disappear.
items.observationHint: Watch the capacity increase on the gauge and the excess pile shrink at the same time.

challengeMode.title: Challenge: remove the excess without removing solute
challengeMode.intro: Increase capacity honestly: make more of the same total solute stay dissolved instead of quietly deleting the extra solid.

items.title: Make the excess pile disappear
items.prompt: Starting from a saturated beaker, make the excess pile disappear while keeping the total solute amount the same.
items.successMessage: You increased the beaker's capacity enough for the same total solute to stay dissolved. The excess pile disappeared because the capacity rose, not because solute was removed.

paragraphs: Treat pH as a live reading of the mixture, not a detached fact to memorize. On this bench, the pH strip, acid-base bars, and sliders stay visible together so you can connect each change to the state of the mixture.

When the mixture becomes more acidic, the pH marker moves lower; when it becomes more basic, it moves higher. Adding water does not create more acid or base, but it spreads the same mixture through more liquid, so the pH can shift back toward the middle.

keyIdeas: pH tells you whether the mixture is currently more acidic or more basic and how far it is from neutral.
Increasing acid amount and increasing base amount push the pH marker in opposite directions.
Increasing water volume dilutes both sides, so the same mixture can become less extreme.

commonMisconception.myth: pH is just a number or color label to memorize, so the bars and particle view are only decoration.
correction: The pH reading summarizes the current balance between acid and base influence in the mixture.
The bars and motion cues help you see why the pH shifts instead of treating it like an isolated fact.

workedExamples.title: Worked example: read one mixture
workedExamples.intro: Use the live bench to read the mixture step by step instead of treating pH as a standalone number.

items.title: Read the mixture before judging the pH
items.prompt: For the current mixture, what does the pH tell you about which side is stronger?

steps.label: 1. Read the starting amounts
2. Compare the acid and base influence
3. Use the pH strip to summarize the balance

applyAction.label: Apply the near-neutral preset
applyAction.observationHint: The pH marker should stay near the middle while the two acid-base bars remain similar in size.

miniChallenge.prompt: Bring the mixture closer to neutral without simply dragging the stronger side all the way down.
miniChallenge.prediction: Before you move a slider, decide whether adding the opposite character or adding water will move the pH more effectively for this mixture.
miniChallenge.answer: Adding the opposite character rebalances the mixture, while adding water dilutes both sides and can soften the shift away from neutral.
miniChallenge.explanation: One method changes the balance directly. The other keeps the same ingredients but spreads them through more liquid.

quickTest.title: Quick check
quickTest.intro: Answer from what the pH strip and acid-base bars are showing right now.

questions.prompt: On this bench, what does the current pH most directly tell you?

choices.label: Which side of the mixture currently has the stronger acid-base influence.
Only the color of the pH strip.
The total amount of liquid alone.

questions.explanation: The pH reading summarizes the current balance: lower means the acid side is stronger, and higher means the base side is stronger.

questions.prompt: What is the most direct effect of adding more water while acid amount and base amount stay the same?

choices.label: It dilutes both sides and can pull the pH toward neutral.
It always makes the mixture more acidic.
It always makes the mixture more basic.

questions.explanation: The same acid and base are spread through more liquid, so the mixture becomes less extreme.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one container of solution, a pH strip, bars for acid and base influence, and sliders for acid amount, base amount, and water volume.
Changing a slider updates the current pH and the visible balance of the mixture.
One graph shows pH against acid amount, and the other shows pH against base amount.
Use them to compare how each control pushes the mixture away from or toward neutral.

featuredSetups.description: Start from a clearly acidic case and watch the pH marker sit below neutral while the acid-base bars show the acid side as stronger.
Start near neutral and compare how similar the acid-base bars look when the pH marker stays close to the middle.

equations.label: How pH follows the balance
equations.meaning: Read it as a rule for the bench: more acid influence pushes pH lower, and more base influence pushes pH higher.

equations.label: How water softens the mixture
equations.meaning: Dilution makes the same acid-base amounts less extreme, so the pH can move back toward the middle.

variableLinks.description: Increasing this strengthens the acid side of the mixture and usually pulls the pH lower.
Increasing this strengthens the base side of the mixture and usually pushes the pH higher.
Increasing this dilutes both sides, so the mixture can become less extreme.

presets.description: A clearly acidic mixture with the pH marker below neutral.
A mixture where acid and base influence are close, so the pH stays near the middle.
A clearly basic mixture with the pH marker above neutral.

overlays.label: Acid-base bars
overlays.shortDescription: Show the acid and base bars beside the pH strip.
whatToNotice: When the acid bar is larger, the pH marker should sit lower; when the base bar is larger, it should sit higher.
Adding water can reduce the size of the mismatch without changing which side is larger.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns the pH reading into a visible balance instead of a number to memorize.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the current pH marker visible as you change the mixture.
whatToNotice: The marker moves continuously as the mixture changes, so pH responds to balance rather than jumping between named categories.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you read pH as a live measure of how acidic or basic the mixture currently is.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the liquid animated so the mixture feels like a real moving system.
whatToNotice: The particles can keep moving even when the pH reading has settled to a steady value.
overlays.whyItMatters: It reminds you that pH describes the state of a mixture, not a frozen label.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice as you change the mixture
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the pH strip and acid-base bars visible, then change one control at a time.

items.text: Increase acid amount while base amount and water volume stay fixed. The acid bar should grow and the pH marker should move below neutral together.
Increase water volume without changing acid or base amount. The mixture can move toward the middle because the same acid-base amounts are spread through more liquid.

predictionMode.title: Predict before you change it
predictionMode.intro: Make the prediction from the pH strip, the acid-base bars, and the graph before you apply the new case.

items.prompt: If you add more base while keeping acid amount and water volume the same, how should the pH marker respond?

choices.label: The pH should move upward because base influence becomes stronger than before.
The pH should move downward even though base was added.
The pH should stay in the same place.

items.explanation: Adding base strengthens the basic side of the mixture, so the pH shifts upward toward the basic region.
items.observationHint: Watch the pH strip, the base bar, and the pH vs base amount graph together.

challengeMode.intro: Use the pH strip and acid-base bars to guide your choices instead of chasing the number alone.

items.title: Bring the mixture near neutral
items.prompt: Adjust the mixture until the pH sits near neutral and the acid and base bars stay close together.
items.successMessage: You brought the mixture into the near-neutral region while keeping acid and base influence closely balanced.

paragraphs: Start with this idea: a buffer does not stop acid-base chemistry. It resists a large pH change by using up reserve, so a stable pH can still hide active chemistry. Keeping the pH strip, reserve bar, and mixture cues visible together lets you see that hidden work.

This bench helps you separate three different effects. Neutralization directly cancels acid with base. Dilution adds water and softens the same mixture without cancelling it. Buffering absorbs some leftover push, so the pH changes less until the reserve begins to run out.

keyIdeas: Neutralization cancels acid with base, dilution spreads the same imbalance through more water, and buffering spends reserve to resist a large pH change.
A buffer keeps pH steadier by spending reserve, not by freezing the chemistry.
Once the buffer reserve is mostly used up, the same added acid or base causes a much larger pH shift.

commonMisconception.myth: If the pH barely changes, the mixture must have stayed almost unchanged.
correction: A buffer can keep the pH fairly steady while the mixture is still changing and using up reserve.
The reserve bar matters because stable pH does not mean the chemistry has stopped changing.

workedExamples.title: Worked example: read a buffered mixture
workedExamples.intro: Use the live bench to separate direct neutralization, remaining imbalance, and buffer reserve.

items.title: Read how the buffer is responding
items.prompt: For the current mixture, how much has been neutralized directly, and how much reserve is still protecting the pH?

steps.label: 1. Read the starting amounts
2. Separate direct neutralization from leftover imbalance
3. Read the reserve and pH together

applyAction.label: Apply the buffered acid pulse
applyAction.observationHint: Compare the falling reserve bar with the smaller pH change. The buffer is working even though the pH looks almost steady.

miniChallenge.prompt: Keep the pH close to neutral after an acid push while showing that reserve is being spent, not that the mixture stayed unchanged.
miniChallenge.prediction: Before moving a control, decide whether buffer, added base, or added water best matches the goal.
miniChallenge.answer: Use a buffer when you want pH to stay near neutral while the reserve bar shows the buffer doing work.
miniChallenge.explanation: Added base changes the chemistry by direct neutralization. Added water dilutes the same mixture. Buffering works by absorbing some leftover push until the reserve is used.

quickTest.title: Quick check
quickTest.intro: Answer from the pH strip, reserve bar, and what each action means on the bench.

questions.prompt: What does a nearly stable pH together with a shrinking reserve bar tell you?

choices.label: The buffer is absorbing the push, so the chemistry is changing even though the pH is resisting a large shift.
Nothing in the mixture is changing.
The pH strip has stopped working correctly.

questions.explanation: The buffer is being used to absorb the added push, so the chemistry is changing even though the pH stays steadier.

questions.prompt: Which action is the clearest example of neutralization rather than dilution or display-only change?

choices.label: Adding more of the opposite side so acid and base directly counteract each other.
Adding more water while leaving the same acid-base amounts in place.
Turning on the reserve overlay without changing the mixture.

questions.explanation: Neutralization happens when acid and base directly counteract each other. Adding water only dilutes, and turning on an overlay changes nothing in the mixture.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one solution container with acid-base character, a pH strip, a buffer reserve meter, and controls for acid amount, base amount, buffer reserve, and water volume.
A readout reports the current acid amount, base amount, buffer reserve, neutralized amount, and pH so the learner can connect the moving scene to the numeric summary.
One graph shows pH against added acid, and a second shows remaining buffer reserve against added acid.
Reading both graphs together helps show when reserve is being used before pH begins to shift strongly.

featuredSetups.description: Open the same acid push with buffer reserve available, and watch the reserve bar fall before the pH strip moves much.
Open a near-neutral mixture and notice that pH can stay near the middle while buffering room is still left.

equations.label: How much cancels directly
equations.meaning: Acid and base can only neutralize each other pair by pair, so the smaller side limits how much is cancelled.

equations.label: How reserve delays a big pH shift
equations.meaning: After direct neutralization, the buffer can absorb part of the leftover imbalance before the pH changes strongly.

variableLinks.description: Adds more acid push to the mixture. Some may be neutralized first, and any extra can start using buffer reserve.
Directly counteracts acid and, if there is excess base left, can push the mixture toward the basic side.
Sets how much leftover acid or base the buffer can absorb before the pH starts changing much faster.
Dilutes the same mixture through more water, softening the pH shift without directly neutralizing acid and base.

presets.description: The same acid push with no buffer reserve, so the pH changes more sharply.
The same acid push with reserve available, so reserve falls before the pH shifts much.
A near-neutral mixture that still has buffer reserve available for later pushes.

overlays.label: Acid-base bars
overlays.shortDescription: Show the current acid-side and base-side bars beside the pH strip.
whatToNotice: The pH can stay near neutral even while the reserve bar shows that the buffer is absorbing a real push.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links the pH reading to what is happening in the mixture instead of treating pH as a detached number.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the live pH marker visible while reserve is being used.
whatToNotice: A small pH change does not always mean a small chemical change. Check the reserve bar at the same time.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you read pH together with buffering rather than as a standalone label.

overlays.shortDescription: Show how much buffering capacity is still available.
whatToNotice: The reserve can drop a lot before the pH moves much.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the hidden action of the buffer visible.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the mixture moving while the readouts update.
whatToNotice: A nearly steady pH does not mean the mixture has stopped reacting or mixing.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the bench feeling like a live chemical system rather than a frozen display.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice when reserve is working
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the pH strip and reserve bar visible, then change one control at a time.

items.text: In a buffered case, the reserve bar often falls before the pH strip moves far.
items.tryThis: Start from the buffered acid pulse, increase acid amount a little, and watch reserve drop before the pH changes dramatically.
items.whyItMatters: This is the clearest sign that the buffer is absorbing change rather than freezing the mixture.

items.text: Adding water can soften the pH shift, but it does not neutralize acid and base the way adding the opposite side does.
items.tryThis: Change water volume first, then add more base and compare which move actually changes the neutralized amount.
items.whyItMatters: It helps you separate dilution, neutralization, and buffering instead of treating them as the same idea.

predictionMode.title: Predict the first visible change
predictionMode.intro: Before you apply the change, decide whether reserve, pH, or both should respond first.

items.prompt: Starting from the buffered acid pulse, what should you notice first if you add a little more acid while reserve is still available?
items.scenarioLabel: Small acid increase with reserve still available

choices.label: The reserve drops noticeably while the pH moves only a little.
The pH swings strongly at once, but the reserve stays about the same.
Nothing changes because the buffer stops the chemistry.

items.explanation: As long as reserve remains, the buffer absorbs some of the added acid. Reserve therefore drops first, while pH changes less than it would in an unbuffered case.
items.observationHint: Watch the reserve bar, pH strip, and graphs together after the acid increase.

challengeMode.intro: Use the reserve bar honestly. Keep pH near neutral while leaving the acid push visible, without solving the problem by simply diluting everything.

items.title: Hold pH near neutral during an acid pulse
items.prompt: Starting from the unbuffered acid pulse, add enough buffer reserve to bring pH near neutral while keeping the acid and base amounts in place and without relying on lots of extra water.
items.successMessage: You used buffer reserve to resist the acid pulse while keeping the same chemical push and visible reserve. The reserve bar shows the buffer is doing work, not making the mixture magically unchanged.

setup.note: Keep the acid pulse in place. Raise buffer reserve until pH is near neutral and some reserve remains; do not wash the mixture away with extra water.

paragraphs: Treat the stoichiometric ratio as a recipe for one full batch. If the card says 2 A + 3 B, one batch forms only when those packets are used together. This bench keeps the recipe card, supply bins, and product tray in one view so the ratio stays tied to what the reaction is actually doing.

To read the bench, count how many complete copies of the recipe fit into the current supplies. When the supply ratio matches the recipe ratio, both bins support the same number of full batches. When the recipe changes, the same supplies can support a different number of batches.

keyIdeas: A stoichiometric ratio tells you how many packets of each reactant belong together in one full batch.
Count complete copies of the recipe, not isolated packets on their own.
Changing the recipe card changes what counts as one batch, so the same supplies can support a different output.

commonMisconception.myth: The larger number in the ratio must mean that reactant always makes more product.
correction: The ratio numbers tell you how many packets are needed together for one full batch.
Product depends on how many complete copies of the whole recipe fit into the supplies, not on which number in the ratio is larger.

workedExamples.title: Worked example: count full batches
workedExamples.intro: Use the live recipe bench to count complete recipe copies instead of doing a detached proportion drill.

items.title: Count batches in the matched 2:3 run
items.prompt: How many full batches can 10 A and 15 B make when one batch needs 2 A and 3 B?

steps.label: 1. Read one full batch from the recipe card
2. Count how many copies of the recipe fit
3. Connect those copies to the product tray

applyAction.label: Apply the matched 2:3 run
applyAction.observationHint: Both cap lines land on the same batch count because the supplies match the recipe ratio.

miniChallenge.prompt: Adjust the bench so the supplies match the recipe and neither side caps the run first.
miniChallenge.prediction: Before you move the sliders, decide which supply ratio would make both batch caps land together.
miniChallenge.answer: The supply ratio must match the recipe ratio shown on the card.
miniChallenge.explanation: When both bins support the same number of complete batches, neither reactant runs out first.

quickTest.title: Quick check
quickTest.intro: Answer from the recipe card and batch caps, not from memorized ratio tricks.

questions.prompt: Which statement is the best reading of a stoichiometric ratio?

choices.label: The recipe ratio tells you how many packets of each reactant are needed together for one full batch.
The larger raw supply always makes more product, no matter what the recipe card says.
The recipe ratio matters only after percent yield changes.

questions.explanation: Stoichiometric ratios define one complete batch before limiting reagent or percent yield enters the story.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: Raw counts alone do not set the output. Complete batches still have to satisfy the recipe card.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: Percent yield matters later. The recipe ratio already matters when you count possible full batches.

showMeAction.label: Apply the matched 2:3 run
showMeAction.observationHint: The recipe card and both cap lines point to the same batch count.

questions.prompt: If the recipe changes from 2:3 to 3:2 while the supply bins stay similar, what should change first?

choices.label: The number of full batches can change because each batch now uses different packets.
Nothing changes because the supply bins did not move.
Only percent yield changes.

questions.explanation: Changing the recipe changes what one full batch uses, so the maximum batch count can change even before you move the supplies.

showMeAction.label: Apply the 3:2 recipe
showMeAction.observationHint: The new recipe changes the batch cap story even before the supply bins change much.

paragraphs: The simulation shows two reactant supply bins, a product tray, and a recipe card that states how many packets of A and B make one full batch.
A readout reports the current recipe, the maximum full batches, which supply is capping the run, the actual output, and any leftover reactant.

One graph shows maximum full batches against the A supply, a second shows maximum full batches against the B supply, and a third compares actual product with theoretical product as percent yield changes.
Use the first two graphs to see when a fixed supply becomes the cap and when both sides land on the same batch count.

featuredSetups.description: Open a run where the supplies form exact copies of the 2:3 recipe, so both sides finish together.
Switch the recipe card to 3:2 and watch the same supply story produce a different batch count.

equations.label: Supply ratio matches recipe ratio
equations.meaning: Both bins support the same number of full batches only when the supply ratio matches the recipe ratio.

equations.label: Maximum full batches
equations.meaning: The reaction can finish only as many full batches as the smaller recipe-supported supply allows.

variableLinks.description: Changing A changes how many complete recipe copies the A bin can support.
Changing B changes how many complete recipe copies the B bin can support.
Changing this redefines one full batch by changing how much A each batch needs.
Changing this redefines one full batch by changing how much B each batch needs.

controls.description: Keep this at 100% here so the page stays focused on recipe batches before yield is introduced.

presets.description: 10 A and 15 B make exact copies of the 2:3 recipe, so neither side caps the run early.
A new recipe changes what one batch needs, so the same idea leads to a different batch count.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the batch recipe visible while the supplies change.
whatToNotice: The same supplies can support a different number of batches when the recipe card changes.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the stoichiometric ratio a visible batch recipe instead of a detached ratio rule.

overlays.label: Batch cap cue
overlays.shortDescription: Highlight which supply currently sets the batch cap.
whatToNotice: When the supplies match the recipe, neither side becomes the lower cap before the other.
overlays.whyItMatters: It lets you compare matched and mismatched runs by watching the cap directly.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the ideal output marker visible for later comparisons.
whatToNotice: On this page, leave yield at 100% so the main story stays about recipe batches, not missing product.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the next idea nearby without mixing it into the main ratio story.

graphs.label: Possible batches vs A packets
graphs.description: Increase A and watch the batch count stop rising once the fixed B supply becomes the cap.

graphs.label: Possible batches vs B packets
graphs.description: Increase B and watch the batch count stop rising once the fixed A supply becomes the cap.

graphs.label: Actual and theoretical output
graphs.description: Useful later: here, keep yield at 100% so actual output matches the recipe-supported batch count.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice when counting batches
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the recipe card and a batch graph visible, then change one thing at a time.

items.text: When the supply ratio matches the recipe ratio, both sides support the same number of full batches.
Changing the recipe card changes what counts as one batch, so the same bins can support a different number of full batches.

predictionMode.title: Predict the batch graph
predictionMode.intro: Predict what the batch cap will do before you change the supplies.

items.prompt: If B stays fixed while A increases on a 2:3 recipe, what should happen after the batch count reaches the B cap?
items.scenarioLabel: Increase A while B stays fixed

choices.label: The possible batch count should flatten because fixed B now limits how many complete batches can form.
The possible batch count should keep rising because adding A always makes more product.
The recipe card should stop mattering once A is large enough.

items.explanation: Once the fixed B supply supports fewer full 2:3 batches than the growing A supply, extra A cannot make extra complete batches on its own.
items.observationHint: Watch the graph rise and then flatten when the B cap takes over.

challengeMode.intro: Build a matched run for a new recipe instead of only matching raw totals.

items.title: Match the 3:2 recipe
items.prompt: Set the bench to a 3 A + 2 B recipe and choose supplies that finish four full batches together.
items.successMessage: You matched the new recipe correctly. Both supplies support four full 3:2 batches, so neither side caps the run early.
hints: Change the recipe card first, then choose supplies that are exact copies of 3:2.
Four full batches of 3:2 need 12 A packets and 8 B packets.

paragraphs: Do not start by comparing raw packet counts. First ask how many complete recipe batches each supply can support. This bench keeps the supply trays, recipe card, batch cap, and leftover readout together so you can see which side runs out first.

For each reactant, divide the available packets by the packets needed per batch. The smaller supported batch count is the limiting reagent and sets the theoretical output. The other side is excess, so it is the side that can have packets left over.

keyIdeas: Use the recipe card to convert each supply into the number of full batches it can support.
The smaller supported batch count caps the product, even if that reactant does not have the smaller raw packet count.
After the last complete batch, the limiting side is used up and leftovers remain only on the excess side.

commonMisconception.myth: The reactant with the smaller raw packet count must always be the limiting reagent.
correction: Limiting reagent depends on the recipe as well as the starting supplies.
A reactant can start with more packets and still be limiting if each batch uses that reactant more quickly.

workedExamples.title: Worked example: find the limiting side
workedExamples.intro: Use the live recipe bench to compare supported batches before you think about leftovers.

items.title: Read an A-limiting run
items.prompt: In the 8 A, 15 B run with a 2:3 recipe, which reactant limits the reaction and what is left over?

steps.label: 1. Turn each supply into complete batches
2. Choose the smaller batch cap
3. Use that batch count to find leftovers

applyAction.label: Apply the A-limiting run
applyAction.observationHint: A gets the lower batch cap, so A is fully used and B still has packets left.

miniChallenge.prompt: Adjust the supplies so one reactant clearly sets the batch cap while the other clearly remains in excess.
miniChallenge.prediction: Before moving the sliders, predict which side supports fewer complete batches.
miniChallenge.answer: The limiting reagent is the side that supports fewer complete batches for the current recipe.
miniChallenge.explanation: Once you identify the smaller batch cap, the product limit and leftover side follow from that single comparison.

quickTest.title: Quick check
quickTest.intro: Answer from the recipe-supported batch counts, not from raw counts alone.

questions.prompt: On a 2:3 recipe with 8 A and 15 B, which reactant is limiting?

choices.label: A is limiting because it supports only four full batches while B supports five.
B is limiting because it has the larger coefficient in the recipe.
Neither reactant is limiting because both trays start with packets in them.

questions.explanation: Limiting reagent is decided by the smaller supported batch count, not by the larger coefficient or by how full a tray looks at the start.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: A larger recipe coefficient does not make a reactant limiting by itself. You still have to compare the current supplies through the recipe.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: Both trays can start non-empty, but one side still runs out first once the reaction completes as many full batches as possible.

showMeAction.label: Apply the A-limiting run
showMeAction.observationHint: A supports only four full batches while B could support five, so A is the cap.

questions.prompt: Which statement about leftover reactant is most accurate?

choices.label: Leftover reactant appears on the excess side because the limiting side is used up first.
The limiting side should always have the largest leftover because it matters most.
If anything is left over, then no reactant was actually limiting.

questions.explanation: The limiting reagent is used up by the last complete batch, so leftovers remain on the excess side.

showMeAction.label: Apply the B-limiting run
showMeAction.observationHint: B runs out first, and A is the tray that still has packets left after the run.

paragraphs: The simulation shows two reactant supply bins, one product tray, and a recipe card that states how many packets of A and B each full batch requires.
A readout reports the current recipe, the maximum possible batches, which reactant is limiting, the actual output, and how much of each reactant is left after the run.
One graph shows possible batches against the A supply, a second shows possible batches against the B supply, and a third compares actual product with the theoretical product as percent yield changes.
Use the first two graphs to see which supply sets the lower batch cap, then use the readout to confirm which side is left over.

sections.title: From batch cap to product and leftovers

featuredSetups.label: A limits first
featuredSetups.description: Open a run where A supports fewer full batches than B, so A runs out first and B is left over.

featuredSetups.label: B limits first
featuredSetups.description: Open a run where B supports fewer full batches than A, so B runs out first and A is left over.

equations.label: Supported batches from each reactant
equations.meaning: Divide each supply by its recipe requirement to see how many full batches that reactant could support on its own.

equations.label: Smaller batch cap sets the output
equations.meaning: The theoretical output is set by the lower of the two supported batch counts.

equations.label: Leftover on the excess side
equations.meaning: After the limiting number of full batches, the excess reactant keeps whatever packets were not used.

variableLinks.description: Controls how many full batches A could support before A runs out.
Controls how many full batches B could support before B runs out.
Changing this changes how many A packets each batch uses, so it changes A's supported batch count.
Changing this changes how many B packets each batch uses, so it changes B's supported batch count.

controls.description: Change how many A packets are available in this run.
Change how many B packets are available in this run.
Set how many A packets one full batch requires.
Set how many B packets one full batch requires.
Keep this at 100% here so the output cap comes only from the limiting reagent, not from incomplete yield.

presets.description: Both supplies support the same number of full 2:3 batches, so neither side is left over.
A supports fewer full batches than B, so A runs out first.
B supports fewer full batches than A, so B runs out first.
Useful comparison case: the limiting cap stays the same, but actual product falls below it.
Changing the recipe can change the limiting side even when the supply counts look similar.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the batch recipe visible while you compare the supplies.
whatToNotice: You cannot identify the limiting reagent from raw counts alone. You have to compare how many full batches each side supports.
overlays.whyItMatters: It anchors the limiting-reagent decision to the visible recipe instead of a memorized shortcut.

overlays.shortDescription: Highlight the reactant that sets the current batch cap.
whatToNotice: The highlighted tray is the side that runs out first once only complete batches are counted.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns the limiting reagent into a visible cap on product, not just a label.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the ideal output marker visible for comparison with percent yield.
whatToNotice: For this concept, leave percent yield at 100% so leftovers come only from the excess reactant, not from low yield.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the idea of limiting reagent from the later idea of percent yield.

graphs.label: Possible batches vs A packets
graphs.description: Increase A and watch the possible batch count rise until the fixed B supply becomes the cap.

graphs.label: Possible batches vs B packets
graphs.description: Increase B and watch the possible batch count rise until the fixed A supply becomes the cap.

graphs.label: Actual and theoretical product vs percent yield
graphs.description: Useful later: here, keep yield at 100% so actual output matches the limiting-reagent cap.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice while comparing batch caps
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the recipe card, limiting cue, and leftover readout visible together.

items.text: The limiting reagent is the side with the lower recipe-supported batch count, not simply the tray with fewer packets.
Leftover reactant appears on the excess side because the limiting side is fully used up by the last complete batch.

predictionMode.title: Predict the limiting side
predictionMode.intro: Predict which reactant will cap the run before you apply the new setup.

items.prompt: If the recipe stays 2 A + 3 B, A stays at 12 packets, and B drops to 9 packets, which reactant becomes limiting?
items.scenarioLabel: Lower B while A stays the same

choices.label: B becomes limiting because it now supports fewer full recipe batches than A.
A still limits because its packet count did not change.
Neither reactant can be limiting if the recipe card stays the same.

items.explanation: With a 2:3 recipe, A = 12 supports six full batches while B = 9 supports only three, so B sets the cap.
items.observationHint: Watch B become the lower batch cap and A stay on the bench as leftover.

challengeMode.intro: Build a run where the recipe makes B limiting and leaves A in excess.

items.title: Make B the limiting reagent
items.prompt: Keep the 2 A + 3 B recipe, but adjust the supplies so B limits the reaction and exactly 3 A packets remain after the full-yield run.
items.successMessage: You built a real B-limiting run. B is fully used first, and exactly 3 A packets remain on the excess side.
hints: Start by deciding how many full batches B should support.
Then use the recipe card to work out how many A packets those batches would consume and how many A packets must be left over.

paragraphs: Start by finding the theoretical output. Use the recipe card and the limiting-reagent cap to work out the maximum possible batches, then compare that ideal marker with the actual product tray.

On this bench, reaction extent is the fraction of those theoretical batches that really finish. Lowering percent yield does not rewrite the recipe or switch the limiting side. It only lowers the actual output below the same theoretical maximum.

keyIdeas: Find the theoretical output first from the recipe and the limiting-reagent batch cap.
Percent yield tells what fraction of that theoretical output actually forms.
When yield is below 100%, the missing part is a shortfall from the same theoretical marker, so more reactant stays unused.

commonMisconception.myth: A 75% yield means the recipe itself changed so each batch uses only 75% of the reactants.
correction: The recipe card stays the same. You still start from the same theoretical batch cap.
A 75% yield means the actual product is 75% of the theoretical output, so the tray stops short of the ideal marker.

workedExamples.title: Worked example: compare theoretical and actual output
workedExamples.intro: Read the theoretical marker first, then use percent yield to predict the actual tray.

items.title: Read a 75% yield run
items.prompt: With 12 A, 18 B, a 2:3 recipe, and 75% yield, what actual output appears and how large is the shortfall from theoretical?

steps.label: 1. Find the theoretical batch cap
2. Apply percent yield to the theoretical output
3. Compare the actual tray with the theoretical marker

applyAction.label: Apply the 75% yield case
applyAction.observationHint: The theoretical marker stays at 6 batches while the actual tray stops at 4.5 batches.

miniChallenge.prompt: Keep the recipe and starting supplies fixed, then lower the yield until the actual tray clearly falls below the same theoretical marker.
miniChallenge.prediction: Before you move a slider, decide which control changes the actual tray without changing the theoretical cap.
miniChallenge.answer: Percent yield changes the actual output, while the theoretical batch cap stays set by the same recipe and limiting reagent.
miniChallenge.explanation: This lets you separate theoretical yield from actual yield instead of folding both into one number.

quickTest.title: Quick check
quickTest.intro: Answer by comparing the theoretical marker with the actual tray.

questions.explanation: Seventy-five percent of 6 batches is 4.5, so the actual tray should show 4.5 batches.

showMeAction.label: Apply the 75% yield case
showMeAction.observationHint: The tray lands at 4.5 batches while the theoretical marker stays at 6.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes what percent yield changes in this model?
questions.explanation: Percent yield scales the actual output against the same theoretical batch cap.

showMeAction.label: Compare 75% with 100%
showMeAction.observationHint: The theoretical marker stays the same while the actual tray moves when you change the yield slider.

paragraphs: The simulation shows two reactant supply bins, a product tray, a theoretical-output marker, and a recipe card that states how many packets of A and B one full batch needs.
A readout reports the current recipe, the maximum possible batches, the limiting side, the actual output, and leftover reactant so the learner can compare theoretical and actual results.
One graph shows possible batches against the A supply, a second shows possible batches against the B supply, and a third compares actual product with theoretical product as percent yield changes.
Use the yield graph to see the actual-output line move while the theoretical marker stays fixed for the same recipe run.

featuredSetups.description: Open a matched run at 75% yield and compare the shorter actual tray with the unchanged theoretical marker.
Compare the same kind of matched run at 100% yield so the actual tray reaches the theoretical marker.

equations.label: Actual output from theoretical output
equations.meaning: Take the theoretical batch count first, then scale it by percent yield to get the actual finished output.

equations.label: Reaction extent as a fraction of theoretical output
equations.meaning: Reaction extent here is the fraction of the theoretical batches that actually finish.
The yield gap is the missing product: the difference between the theoretical marker and the actual tray.

variableLinks.description: Helps set the theoretical batch cap before percent yield changes the actual output.
Helps set the theoretical batch cap before percent yield changes the actual output.
Changes the fraction of theoretical output that actually appears on the product tray.

controls.description: Lower this to make the actual tray fall below the same theoretical marker.

presets.description: Matched supplies at 100% yield, so the actual tray reaches the theoretical marker.
The same matched supplies at 75% yield, so the actual tray stops short of the theoretical marker.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the recipe visible while comparing theoretical and actual output.
whatToNotice: The recipe card and limiting side stay the same even when percent yield lowers the actual tray.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the theoretical yield story from the later percent-yield adjustment.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the limiting side visible while yield changes the product tray.
whatToNotice: Changing percent yield does not change which side set the theoretical batch cap.
overlays.whyItMatters: It shows that percent yield acts on the same theoretical cap instead of creating a new one.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the theoretical marker visible beside the actual product tray.
whatToNotice: As percent yield drops, the actual tray falls farther below the theoretical marker.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns percent yield into a visible shortfall from the maximum possible output.

graphs.label: Actual and theoretical product vs percent yield
graphs.description: Watch the actual product change with percent yield while the theoretical marker stays fixed for the same recipe run.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice when yield changes
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the theoretical marker and the actual tray visible together.

items.text: The theoretical output stays fixed until you change the recipe or the limiting reagent. Percent yield only changes how much of that possible output actually appears.
Lowering percent yield widens the gap between the actual tray and the theoretical marker, so more possible product is missing.

predictionMode.title: Predict what yield changes
predictionMode.intro: Predict what percent yield changes and what stays fixed.

items.prompt: If a matched 2:3 run stays the same but percent yield rises from 75% to 100%, what should change?

choices.label: The actual product tray rises toward the same theoretical marker.
The recipe card changes to a different stoichiometric ratio.
The limiting reagent switches sides.

items.explanation: Percent yield changes the actual output. It does not change the recipe card or the limiting side that set the theoretical cap.
items.observationHint: Watch the actual tray rise while the theoretical marker stays fixed.

challengeMode.intro: Keep the recipe matched, then use percent yield to land the actual tray below the same theoretical marker.

items.title: Set a matched run to 75% yield
items.prompt: Use the matched 2 A + 3 B run and set percent yield to 75% so the actual output lands at 3.75 batches.
items.successMessage: You kept the recipe matched and changed only the yield. The theoretical marker stays at 5 batches while the actual tray lands at 3.75.
hints: First confirm that the matched run makes 5 theoretical batches.
Then take 75% of 5 to predict the actual tray.

paragraphs: Sorting is not just a before-and-after result. It is a step-by-step process of comparisons and writes that gradually removes disorder. This bench keeps the live list, active markers, and running costs visible so you can read what each algorithm is doing.

Start by comparing traces, not by memorizing a complexity table. The same sorted output can come from very different local decisions, and changing the input order shows why some algorithms do much less work than others.

keyIdeas: Sorting removes disorder step by step through comparisons and writes.
Different algorithms can produce the same sorted output while following different local patterns and costs.
Input order matters because some algorithms can exploit nearly sorted data much better than others.

commonMisconception.myth: If two algorithms finish with the same sorted list, they must have done roughly the same work.
correction: The same final answer can hide very different comparison patterns, write counts, and marker motion.
That is why the live list, overlays, and counters need to be read together.

workedExamples.title: Worked example: read a live run
workedExamples.intro: Use the current list, counters, and disorder readout to describe the run before changing anything.

items.title: Read one sorting trace
items.prompt: From the live trace, what kind of work is this run doing?

steps.label: 1. Identify the current setup
2. Read the cost counters
3. Check how much disorder remains

applyAction.label: Try insertion on a nearly sorted list
applyAction.observationHint: Watch the next key move only a short distance, then compare the small rise in writes with the fast drop in disorder.

miniChallenge.prompt: Build a list that is almost sorted, then choose an algorithm that still wastes work by making repeated full sweeps across the interval.
miniChallenge.prediction: Before touching the controls, decide whether the expensive part should come from the algorithm, the starting order, or both.
miniChallenge.answer: Keep the list nearly sorted, but choose an algorithm that does not use short local fixes efficiently.
miniChallenge.explanation: A nearly sorted list reduces the disorder, but the algorithm still determines whether that disorder is removed with short fixes or repeated passes.

quickTest.intro: Answer from what the bench shows, not from memorized slogans.

questions.prompt: Why is it useful to compare several sorting algorithms on the same list?

choices.label: Because the same sorted result can come from different step patterns and different costs.
Because each algorithm must produce a different final order.
Because sorting only matters when the list is already sorted.

questions.explanation: What matters here is not only the final order but how the algorithm gets there and how much work it uses.

questions.prompt: If two runs use the same algorithm and the same list size, what can still change the visible cost a lot?

choices.label: The starting order of the list.
Almost nothing, because list size alone determines the whole cost pattern.
Only the graph styling.

questions.explanation: The starting order changes the initial disorder, so the same algorithm may do much more or much less work.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a list as bars. Markers indicate the current comparisons, highlighted regions show which part of the list is still active or already settled, and the readout reports comparisons, writes, and remaining disorder.
One graph shows comparisons and writes increasing during the run. Another shows inversions remaining alongside settled items, and a third shows the fraction of disorder left falling toward zero.

featuredSetups.description: Open a nearly sorted case and watch insertion fix short local mistakes with very few writes.

setup.note: Use this to connect the small changes in the list to the low write count on the graph.

featuredSetups.description: Open a reverse-order case and watch bubble make repeated sweeps and swaps before the list settles.

setup.note: Use this when you want the repeated passes and rising cost to stay easy to see.

equations.meaning: Counts how many item-to-item checks have happened so far.
Counts how many times the algorithm has rewritten or moved values in the list.
Counts the out-of-order pairs that still remain.

variableLinks.description: Changes the rule the bench uses to sort the same list, so you can compare different local strategies.
Changes the starting order, which changes the initial disorder even though the final sorted answer is the same.
Changes how many items must be organized, which changes how much work can build up.

presets.description: A useful baseline case where repeated scans, swaps, and rising cost are easy to follow.
A low-disorder case where insertion usually fixes short local mistakes with very few writes.
A high-disorder case where bubble must make many passes before the list settles.

overlays.shortDescription: Highlight the part of the list the algorithm is still actively working through.
whatToNotice: Watch whether the unsolved region shrinks from the left or the right as the run progresses.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you connect the moving markers to which part of the list is still contributing to the cost.

overlays.shortDescription: Mark the items the current algorithm already treats as fixed.
whatToNotice: Insertion usually grows a settled prefix, while bubble usually grows a settled suffix.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes each algorithm's local strategy visible on the list itself.

overlays.shortDescription: Show the current scan positions on the live list.
whatToNotice: The markers move differently because each algorithm asks a different comparison question at each step.
overlays.whyItMatters: They reveal the decision process directly on the list instead of hiding it behind pseudocode.

noticePrompts.intro: Keep the live list and one graph visible together, then change only one control at a time.

items.text: With the same list size, a nearly sorted input can make insertion look efficient, while reverse order makes bubble do repeated sweeps and swaps.
All three algorithms reach the same sorted list, but the marker motion and counter lines reveal very different paths and costs.

predictionMode.intro: Before switching setups, predict which cost pattern should stay lower and why.

items.prompt: If the list starts nearly sorted, which algorithm should usually produce the fewest writes on this bench?

choices.label: Insertion sort, because most items are already close to where they belong.
Bubble sort, because settling the right edge first should remove most of the work.
Selection sort, because fewer comparisons automatically means fewer writes.

items.explanation: Insertion sort can exploit short local fixes on a nearly sorted list, so it often reaches the same result with fewer writes.
items.observationHint: Open the nearly sorted insertion preset, then watch the write count rise slowly while the disorder line drops quickly.

challengeMode.intro: Build a low-cost run for a reason, not by luck. Use the list and graph together.

items.title: Make insertion pay off
items.prompt: Create a run where insertion sort finishes a nearly sorted list with very few writes.
items.successMessage: You built the kind of case insertion sort handles well. Because the list began close to sorted, most items needed only short moves, so the write count stayed low.

paragraphs: Binary search only works on ordered data. The invariant is simple: if the target exists, it must still be inside the current low-to-high interval.
Because the values are sorted, one midpoint comparison tells you which half cannot contain the target. Move low or high inward, and the promise stays true.

Watch low, mid, high, and the highlighted interval together. The speed does not come from scanning faster; it comes from preserving the invariant while shrinking the live search space again and again.

keyIdeas: Binary search only works because sorted order lets one midpoint comparison discard a whole half.
The interval invariant is: the target, if it exists, stays between low and high.
Low, mid, and high mark the current search space at every step.

commonMisconception.myth: Binary search is just a quicker item-by-item scan.
correction: It does not improve a one-by-one search by a little. It changes the strategy: keep the target inside the active interval while each midpoint check removes half of what remains.
Without ordered data, that elimination step would not be justified.

workedExamples.title: Worked example: read the current interval
workedExamples.intro: Use the highlighted interval, midpoint marker, and counters to explain how the invariant survives this step.

items.title: Read one binary-search step
items.prompt: From the live scene, what does this step tell you about where the target can still be?

steps.label: 1. Mark the active interval
2. Compare the midpoint with the target
3. Compare binary search with a one-by-one scan

applyAction.label: Try the far-right target setup
applyAction.observationHint: Even though the target starts near the far edge, the interval-width graph drops quickly because each failed midpoint removes half of the remaining positions.

miniChallenge.prompt: Build a large ordered list with the target near one edge, then show that binary search still finds it in only a few midpoint checks.
miniChallenge.prediction: Before changing the controls, decide whether a target near an edge should matter more than the repeated halving of the interval.
miniChallenge.answer: Put the target near an edge in a large list and let each midpoint remove half of what remains.
miniChallenge.explanation: The target can be far from the first midpoint in position, but each midpoint preserves the correct half, so it is not far in the number of halving steps needed to reach it.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the interval, markers, and graphs, not from memorized vocabulary.

questions.prompt: Why does binary search need ordered data to keep the interval invariant true?

choices.label: Because only then can one midpoint comparison discard half the interval while keeping the target in the surviving half.
Because ordered data makes the midpoint closer to the target.
Because ordering changes how many items are in the array.

questions.explanation: Only ordered data lets one midpoint comparison eliminate a whole half without breaking the target-still-inside promise.

questions.prompt: What is the main reason binary search stays fast on the bench?

choices.label: The active interval keeps being cut roughly in half.
It secretly checks many items at once.
It works even better on unsorted data.

questions.explanation: Its efficiency comes from repeatedly shrinking the search space, not from reading individual items faster.

paragraphs: The simulation shows an ordered array. Markers show low, mid, and high, the active interval highlight shows which positions are still possible, and an optional ghost lane shows how far a one-by-one search would have progressed after the same number of checks.
One graph shows the interval width shrinking over time, one tracks the low, mid, and high indices, and one compares the number of binary-search checks with linear-search checks.

featuredSetups.description: Open a large ordered list with the target near the far edge, then watch the active interval collapse anyway.

setup.note: Use this to see that a far-away target in position can still take only a few checks when each midpoint removes half of what remains.

featuredSetups.description: Open a simple case where the midpoint lands on or near the target very quickly.

setup.note: Use this first to connect low, mid, and high to the highlighted interval before trying harder targets.

equations.meaning: Picks the index halfway between low and high, so the next comparison tests the middle of the live interval.
Counts how many positions in the current interval could still contain the target.

variableLinks.description: Changes how many ordered values are in play, so you can see how halving scales with list size.
Moves the target to a new place in the ordered list so you can test whether edge targets are still found quickly.
Shows how many checks a one-by-one scan would need on the same ordered list.

presets.description: A gentle first case where the midpoint lands on or near the target and the marker logic is easy to read.
A larger case where the target sits near the far-right edge but the interval still shrinks quickly.
A mirror case on the left edge, useful for checking that halving works in either direction.

overlays.shortDescription: Highlight the positions that could still contain the target.
