whatToNotice: After each midpoint check, one whole half disappears from the highlighted interval.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes halving visible on the list instead of leaving it as an abstract rule.

overlays.shortDescription: Show the current low, mid, and high positions on the ordered list.
whatToNotice: low and high mark the surviving interval, and mid is the one position you test next.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links each update of low or high to the visible search space.

noticePrompts.intro: Keep the ordered list and one graph visible together, then follow what low, mid, and high do after each check.

items.text: The key move is not reading faster. It is preserving the target-still-inside invariant while the midpoint rules out half of the remaining positions.
The midpoint only tells you which half to keep because every value is already in sorted order.

predictionMode.intro: Predict which half survives before you advance the search.

items.prompt: If the target value is larger than the current midpoint value, which side of the interval should remain active?

choices.label: Keep the right half, so low moves to just above mid.
Keep the left half, so high moves to just below mid.
Keep both halves, because one midpoint is not enough information.

items.explanation: In an ordered list, everything left of mid is too small when the midpoint value is already too small.
items.observationHint: Open the far-right target setup and watch low jump past mid while the interval window keeps only the right half.

challengeMode.intro: Build a case where halving preserves the interval invariant, instead of relying on luck, to explain the small number of checks.

items.title: Find an edge target fast
items.prompt: Create a large ordered list with the target near the far-right edge, but still find it in five checks or fewer.
items.successMessage: You built the right kind of scene. Even with the target near the edge, binary search stayed efficient because each failed midpoint cut the remaining interval roughly in half.

paragraphs: A graph should first feel like a map of direct connections, not a picture you have to understand all at once. This bench keeps one labeled graph, one active frontier, and one adjacency readout together so you can read the current neighborhood before the search language gets more formal.

Adjacency is the first useful question: which nodes can the current node reach by one edge right now? Once that local neighborhood is clear, breadth-first and depth-first search become two ways of organizing the same next-step options.

keyIdeas: The first useful graph reading is local adjacency: which nodes are one edge away from the current node.
The frontier is the set of discovered next candidates that are waiting to be explored.
Changing the start node changes the first neighborhood and the first frontier, even when the graph itself stays the same.

commonMisconception.myth: To read a graph, you need to take in the whole diagram at once and guess the path globally.
correction: The honest first move is local: read the direct neighbors of the current node.
A search grows from one neighborhood to the next, not from a full-map scan done in your head.

workedExamples.title: Worked example: read one neighborhood
workedExamples.intro: Read the first neighborhood from the bench before you name BFS or DFS.

items.prompt: On the layered campus graph, what does starting at A tell you immediately?

steps.label: 1. Read the direct neighbors
2. Turn those neighbors into the first frontier
3. Connect that local picture to later search rules

applyAction.label: Open the layered campus graph
applyAction.observationHint: Keep the adjacency cue and frontier cue visible together. First read which nodes touch A directly, then watch those nodes become the first frontier.

miniChallenge.prompt: Move the start node until the first neighborhood clearly becomes wider, then list the direct neighbors you used to decide.
miniChallenge.prediction: Before changing the start node, decide whether you need a hub node or an edge node.
miniChallenge.answer: Start from a hub node if you want the first frontier to widen immediately.
miniChallenge.explanation: A hub has more direct neighbors, so one step reveals more next candidates even though the graph itself has not changed.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the visible neighborhood and frontier, not from memorized graph jargon.

questions.prompt: What makes two nodes adjacent on this graph bench?

choices.label: One edge connects the two nodes directly.
The two nodes are drawn close together on the page.
One node has already been visited by the search.

questions.explanation: Adjacency is a direct edge relationship. It is read from the graph structure, not from visual spacing or visited state.

questions.prompt: What sets the first frontier most directly?

choices.label: The direct neighbors of the chosen start node.
The total number of nodes in the graph.
Whether the target is drawn on the right side.

questions.explanation: The first frontier starts with the chosen start node's direct neighbors, so changing the start node can change the frontier immediately.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one labeled graph. The current node, frontier nodes, and visited nodes are highlighted differently so you can tell what is being explored now, what is waiting next, and what is already done.
A readout card shows the traversal mode, current node, frontier size, and target, while a cue panel shows the current node's neighbor list and the order of the frontier.
One graph compares visited nodes with frontier size, a second tracks current depth against deepest reached depth, and a third compares new discoveries with repeat skips from already-visited nodes.
These graphs update with the same live traversal steps shown on the main graph.

sections.title: Turn direct neighbors into the next frontier

featuredSetups.description: Open a graph where A has only a small first neighborhood, so the first adjacency read is easy to follow.
Start from the hub so the first step immediately reveals several next nodes.

equations.meaning: Lists the nodes you can reach from v by one edge.
Counts how many direct neighbors v has.
The first frontier is exactly the start node's neighbor set.

variableLinks.description: Switches among graph scenes so you can compare how different connection patterns change the first neighborhood.
Moves the starting point, so the same graph can produce a different first neighborhood and frontier.
Marks which node the later search steps are trying to reach.
Changes how the waiting frontier is organized: breadth-first stays level by level, while depth-first follows one branch sooner.

controls.label: Graph layout
controls.description: Switch among graph scenes and compare how their connection patterns change the first neighborhood.
Choose where the traversal begins, then read that node's neighbors first.
Choose which node the later search is trying to reach.
Compare breadth-first expansion with depth-first branch-following on the same graph.

presets.description: A simple graph where the first frontier from A is easy to read.
The same graph, useful for comparing how DFS organizes the same neighbors differently.
A graph with cycles, useful for seeing why visited state matters.
A branch-first pass through the cyclic graph, where repeat avoidance still matters.
A hub start that makes the first frontier widen immediately.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the current node's direct neighbors visible.
whatToNotice: Before thinking about BFS or DFS, read which nodes the current node touches by one edge.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes adjacency a visible local fact instead of a vague graph term.

overlays.shortDescription: Show which discovered nodes are waiting to be explored next.
whatToNotice: BFS and DFS mainly differ in which waiting node they take next from this frontier.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns an abstract queue or stack into a visible set of next candidates.

overlays.shortDescription: Mark which nodes have already been fully explored.
whatToNotice: Visited marking stops already-claimed nodes from being added again, especially in cycles.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes repeat avoidance visible on the graph itself.

graphs.description: Compare how many nodes are already explored with how many discovered nodes are still waiting.
See whether the search stays near the start or dives deeper along one branch.
Separate first-time discoveries from edges that point back to nodes already visited.

noticePrompts.intro: Keep the graph and one history graph visible together, then read the current node before thinking about the whole map.

items.text: Start locally: which nodes are one edge away from the current node right now?
Starting from a hub widens the first frontier immediately because one node can discover several neighbors at once.

predictionMode.intro: Predict how the first frontier will change before you move the start node.

items.prompt: If you move the start node from an edge to the hub on the hub-and-detours graph, what should change first?
items.scenarioLabel: Move the start to the hub

choices.label: The first frontier should widen because the hub has more direct neighbors.
Nothing should change until the target node moves.
The graph should stop having repeat edges.

items.explanation: A hub node has a larger local neighborhood, so the first frontier gets wider on the very first expansion.
items.observationHint: Open the hub preset, read C's neighbors, then watch them enter the frontier together.

paragraphs: Breadth-first search explores an unweighted graph in layers. From the start node, it first reaches nodes one edge away, then nodes two edges away, and so on.

That layered pattern comes from the queue frontier. The oldest discovered node is expanded next, so shallow discoveries stay ahead of later deeper ones. On this bench, keep the graph, frontier cue, and depth history together so the rule stays visible.

keyIdeas: Breadth-first search reaches nodes in increasing fewest-edge distance from the start.
The queue frontier expands the oldest waiting node first.
The frontier can widen before it shrinks because one shallow node may discover several new neighbors.

commonMisconception.myth: Breadth-first search just means moving across the page in a wide-looking pattern.
correction: The drawing can be misleading. What matters is queue order: the oldest discovered node is expanded next.
That rule is what makes BFS finish one layer before moving to deeper nodes.

workedExamples.title: Worked example: read the first BFS layers
workedExamples.intro: Use the graph, frontier cue, and depth picture together to read what BFS should do first.

items.prompt: Starting from A on the layered campus graph, which nodes should BFS settle before it goes deeper?

steps.label: 1. Read the first frontier
2. Keep the queue order honest
3. Connect queue order to layer depth

applyAction.label: Open layered-campus BFS
applyAction.observationHint: Keep the frontier cue and visited-history graph visible together. Watch B and C enter first, then notice that deeper nodes wait until that shallow layer has been processed.

miniChallenge.prompt: Build a BFS case where the frontier becomes wide before the target is reached.
miniChallenge.prediction: Before you switch presets, decide whether you need a hub-like start or a narrow edge start.
miniChallenge.answer: Use a hub-like start so BFS discovers several same-depth neighbors before it moves deeper.
miniChallenge.explanation: When one shallow node has many direct neighbors, the queue fills with several nodes from the same layer before that layer is processed.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the visible queue, layers, and depth graph, not from memorized acronyms.

questions.prompt: Why does BFS reach shallow nodes before deeper ones on this bench?

choices.label: Because the queue frontier keeps earlier discoveries ahead of later ones.
Because BFS always teleports to the target node.
Because BFS ignores adjacency once the search starts.

questions.explanation: Queue order keeps earlier shallow discoveries ahead of later deeper ones.

questions.prompt: What is the key reason BFS is useful on an unweighted graph?

choices.label: Its layer order means the first time a node is reached, it is by a fewest-edge path from the start.
It always uses fewer total edges than the graph actually has.
It removes the need for a frontier entirely.

questions.explanation: Because BFS explores layer by layer, the first time it reaches a node is already along a fewest-edge route from the start.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one labeled graph with the current node, the queue frontier, and the visited nodes highlighted differently so the BFS layers stay readable.
A readout card reports the traversal mode, current node, frontier size, visited count, and target, while a cue panel shows the frontier order and the current neighbor list.
One graph tracks visited nodes against frontier size, a second tracks current depth against the deepest reached depth, and a third compares new discoveries with repeat skips.
Together they show how breadth-first search can widen the frontier and then settle the graph layer by layer.

sections.title: Read the queue frontier and graph layers together

featuredSetups.description: Use a clean graph where BFS finishes one shallow layer before moving to the next.
Start from the center so one shallow node makes the frontier widen before it narrows again.

equations.meaning: A newly discovered neighbor is one edge deeper than the node that found it.
BFS removes the oldest frontier node first, then adds its newly discovered neighbors to the back of the queue.

variableLinks.description: Switch graph scenes and compare how the same queue rule produces different frontier shapes.
Move the starting point and watch the first BFS layer change immediately.
Choose which node the search is trying to reach so you can see when BFS first reaches it.
Use DFS as a contrast, but notice that BFS is the mode that keeps the frontier in queue order.

controls.description: Switch graph scenes and compare how the frontier grows under the same BFS rule.
Choose where the traversal begins, then read that node's first layer.
Mark the node the search is trying to reach.
Compare queue-based BFS with stack-like DFS on the same graph.

presets.description: A clean graph where the first frontier and first two layers are easy to read.
A graph with cycles where BFS still settles the shallow side first and visited state prevents repeats.
A centered start that makes the frontier widen quickly at one shallow depth.
The same graph in DFS mode, useful for contrasting layered expansion with branch-first diving.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the current node's neighbors visible.
whatToNotice: Each BFS layer grows from the direct neighbors of the node that just left the queue.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links the layer pattern to actual edges in the graph.

overlays.shortDescription: Show which discovered nodes are waiting next in the queue.
whatToNotice: Earlier discoveries stay ahead of later ones, so BFS finishes a shallow layer before moving deeper.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the queue rule visible instead of leaving BFS as a slogan.

overlays.shortDescription: Mark which nodes have already been expanded.
whatToNotice: Visited nodes spread outward from the start in layer order.
overlays.whyItMatters: It shows the shallow-to-deep pattern directly on the graph.

graphs.description: Watch the frontier grow as shallow neighbors are discovered, then shrink as that waiting layer is processed.
See BFS finish shallower depths before the search reaches deeper nodes.
Compare new discoveries with repeat checks that visited state blocks.

noticePrompts.intro: Keep the graph and one history graph visible together, then follow the queue order after each expansion.

items.text: BFS does not go 'wide' because of the drawing. It goes layer by layer because the oldest discovered node leaves the queue first.
The frontier often widens first when one shallow node discovers several neighbors at the same depth.

predictionMode.intro: Predict which node should leave the queue next before you advance the scene.

items.prompt: On the layered-campus BFS run from A, which node should leave the frontier first after A discovers both B and C?
items.scenarioLabel: After A discovers B and C

choices.label: B, because BFS removes the oldest discovered node first.
C, because the newest discovery always goes next in BFS.
H, because the target jumps to the front of the queue.

items.explanation: BFS uses first-in, first-out queue order, so B leaves the frontier before the later discovery C.
items.observationHint: Run layered-campus BFS and watch the frontier cue after A expands. B stays ahead of C because it was discovered first.

paragraphs: Depth-first search keeps its frontier in stack order. The newest discovered node is expanded next, so the search usually follows one branch as far as it can before returning.

On this bench, keep the graph, frontier cue, and depth history visible together. That makes DFS easier to read: it is not random wandering, and it is not a shortest-path method. It is a branch-first rule applied step by step to the same graph that BFS explores layer by layer.

keyIdeas: Depth-first search expands the newest waiting node first.
DFS often increases the current depth quickly because it keeps following the active branch.
Backtracking begins when the current branch has no new neighbors, so the search returns to an older waiting node.

commonMisconception.myth: Depth-first search reaches the target fastest because it goes straight down one branch.
correction: Going deeper first is not the same as finding a short route. DFS can spend time on a long detour before it reaches an easier target on another branch.
The defining rule of DFS is stack order, not shortest-path efficiency.

workedExamples.title: Worked example: read the first DFS branch
workedExamples.intro: Use the frontier order and depth view together to read what DFS does immediately after the start.

items.prompt: On the layered campus graph, what does DFS do after A discovers both B and C?

steps.label: 1. Read which node is on top of the stack
2. Predict the next branch move
3. Connect that move to later backtracking

applyAction.label: Open layered-campus DFS
applyAction.observationHint: Keep the frontier cue and depth graph visible together. Watch C go next because it is the newest frontier node, then notice how DFS stays with that branch until it must backtrack.

miniChallenge.prompt: Build a DFS case where the current depth rises quickly before the target is reached.
miniChallenge.prediction: Before switching presets, decide whether you need a graph with one clear branch or one that spreads evenly.
miniChallenge.answer: Choose a graph with a clear continuing branch so DFS can keep following the newest path for several steps.
miniChallenge.explanation: DFS goes deepest when each new node reveals another unvisited neighbor on the same branch before the search has to backtrack.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the visible stack behavior and depth graph, not from memorized acronyms.

questions.prompt: Why does DFS usually feel deeper before it feels wider on this bench?

choices.label: Because the newest frontier node leaves first, so one branch keeps priority.
Because DFS deletes all the other frontier nodes immediately.
Because DFS ignores the graph's edges.

questions.explanation: The stack frontier gives one branch temporary priority, so the current depth often rises quickly.

questions.prompt: Which statement is most accurate about DFS on this bench?

choices.label: DFS is branch-first, but it does not guarantee the shallowest route to the target.
DFS always finds the shortest route because it moves quickly.
DFS removes the need for visited state.

questions.explanation: DFS chooses one branch first. That can be useful for exploration, but it is different from guaranteeing a shortest path.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one labeled graph with the current node, the stack frontier, and the visited nodes highlighted differently so the active branch stays easy to read.
A readout card reports the traversal mode, current node, frontier size, visited count, and target, while a cue panel shows the frontier order and the current neighbor list.
One graph tracks visited nodes against frontier size, a second tracks current depth against deepest reached depth, and a third compares new discoveries with repeat skips.
Together they show how DFS follows one branch, then backtracks when that branch runs out of new nodes.

equations.meaning: A newly discovered neighbor is one edge deeper than the node that found it.
DFS removes the newest frontier node first, then places newly discovered neighbors on top of the stack.

presets.description: A clear graph where the stack order and first deep branch are easy to follow.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the current node's direct neighbors visible.
whatToNotice: DFS still makes each move from the current node's local neighbors, even when the overall path looks deep.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the branch-first behavior tied to actual edges instead of making it look like a mysterious path choice.

overlays.shortDescription: Show which discovered nodes are still waiting on the stack.
whatToNotice: The newest waiting node sits on top, so DFS keeps giving the current branch priority.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the stack rule visible on the graph instead of leaving it as a definition to memorize.

overlays.shortDescription: Mark which nodes have already been fully expanded.
whatToNotice: Visited marking stops DFS from reopening old branches when the graph contains cycles.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes repeat avoidance and clean backtracking visible at the same time.

graphs.description: Watch older alternatives remain on the frontier while DFS keeps one newer branch active.
Track how DFS often increases the current depth quickly, then drops when backtracking begins.
Compare new discoveries with repeat edges that visited state prevents from reopening.

noticePrompts.intro: Keep the graph and one history graph visible together, then watch which frontier node stays on top after each step.

items.text: DFS often increases the current depth quickly because the newest branch stays on top of the frontier.
Backtracking works because older alternatives stay on the frontier, and visited state stops the search from reopening already explored branches.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the next stack move before you advance the scene.

items.prompt: On the layered-campus DFS run from A, which node should leave the frontier next after A discovers both B and C?
items.scenarioLabel: After A discovers B and C

choices.label: C, because DFS keeps the newest discovered node on top of the stack.
B, because the oldest discovery always leaves first.
H, because the target always jumps to the front.

items.explanation: DFS uses last-in, first-out order, so the newest discovered node C is the next branch to expand.
items.observationHint: Run layered-campus DFS and watch the frontier cue after A expands. C goes next because it is on top of the stack.

paragraphs: On a graph with cycles, frontier and visited state answer different questions. The frontier shows discovered nodes still waiting to be expanded. The visited state shows nodes whose neighborhoods have already been checked.

That separation turns a loop from a trap into a bookkeeping event. When an edge points back to a visited node, the search records a repeat skip instead of reopening that node, while the remaining frontier keeps the run moving forward.

keyIdeas: Frontier means discovered and waiting, not finished.
Visited means expanded already, so an edge back to that node becomes a repeat skip instead of new work.
Cycles stay manageable when the current node, waiting frontier, and finished visited set stay visually separate.

commonMisconception.myth: Frontier and visited state are just two names for the same set of nodes.
correction: A frontier node has already been discovered, but its neighbors may not have been checked yet.
A visited node has already been expanded, so edges back to it can be skipped instead of reopening the same cycle.

workedExamples.title: Worked example: separate frontier from visited
workedExamples.intro: Use the bridge-cycle graph to name what is waiting, what is finished, and what should happen when an edge points backward.

items.title: Separate the frontier from the visited set
items.prompt: On the bridge-cycle graph, how can you tell whether a node belongs in the frontier, the visited set, or neither?

steps.label: 1. Find the waiting work
2. Mark finished expansions
3. Read the repeat edge

applyAction.label: Open bridge-cycle BFS
applyAction.observationHint: Open bridge-cycle BFS, then pause at the first repeat skip: name one node that is waiting in the frontier and one node that is already visited.

miniChallenge.prompt: Create a run where a repeat skip happens while at least one other node is still waiting in the frontier.
miniChallenge.prediction: Before you switch presets, predict which graph shape makes a backward edge appear before the frontier is empty.
miniChallenge.answer: Use the bridge-cycle graph, then watch for a backward edge into a visited node while another discovered node is still waiting.
miniChallenge.explanation: That moment shows the two jobs working together: visited prevents the loop from reopening, and the frontier preserves the useful work that remains.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the visible frontier, visited nodes, and repeat-skip graph, not from memorized vocabulary.

questions.prompt: Which statement best separates the frontier from the visited state?

choices.label: Frontier nodes are discovered and waiting to be expanded, while visited nodes have already had their neighborhoods checked.
Frontier and visited always mean the same set of nodes.
Visited nodes are the only nodes that still need work.

questions.explanation: That waiting-versus-expanded difference is what lets the search skip repeats without losing unfinished work.

questions.prompt: Why are repeat skips useful on the bridge-cycle graph?

choices.label: They stop the traversal from reopening the same looped nodes over and over.
They force the traversal to ignore adjacency completely.
They remove the need for a frontier.

questions.explanation: Without repeat skips, a cycle could reopen old nodes again and again instead of letting the frontier continue.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one labeled graph. The current node, frontier nodes, and visited nodes are highlighted differently so you can tell what is being processed now, what is waiting next, and what is already expanded.
A readout card reports the traversal mode, current node, frontier size, visited count, and target, while a cue panel shows the frontier order and the current node's neighbor list.
One graph compares visited count with frontier size, a second tracks current depth against deepest reached depth, and a third compares new discoveries with repeat skips.
Together they show why cyclic graph traversal needs frontier and visited state to play different roles.

sections.title: Keep frontier and visited separate when cycles appear

pageIntro.definition: Use one cyclic graph to track the current node, frontier nodes waiting their turn, and visited nodes whose neighbors are already checked.
pageIntro.whyItMatters: That bookkeeping keeps graph search from circling forever or counting the same work twice.
pageIntro.keyTakeaway: Frontier means discovered and waiting; visited means already expanded, so repeat edges can be skipped honestly.

equations.meaning: Expanding the current node adds it to visited, while only newly discovered neighbors join the waiting frontier.
Counts neighbor checks that point to already handled work instead of producing a new discovery.

presets.description: A cyclic graph where discovered-but-waiting nodes must stay separate from already expanded nodes.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the current node's neighbor list visible.
whatToNotice: A repeat skip only makes sense when you can see which neighbor points back to earlier work.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties cycle handling to actual edges from the current node.

overlays.shortDescription: Show which discovered nodes are still waiting to be expanded.
whatToNotice: Being on the frontier means discovered and waiting, not finished.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps waiting work separate from completed work on graphs with cycles.

overlays.shortDescription: Mark which nodes have already been fully expanded.
whatToNotice: Edges back to these nodes become repeat skips instead of new discoveries.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes cycle control visible on the graph itself.

graphs.description: Compare how many nodes are already expanded with how many discovered nodes are still waiting.
Track whether the active node stays shallow or goes deeper while the frontier and visited sets continue to change.
Separate genuine new discoveries from checks that point back to already expanded nodes.

noticePrompts.intro: Keep the graph and one history graph visible together, then compare who is still waiting with who is already done.

items.text: The frontier is a waiting line of discovered nodes, not a list of finished nodes.
Repeat skips appear when a cycle points back to a node whose neighborhood has already been expanded.

predictionMode.intro: Predict what a backward edge should do before you advance the search.

items.prompt: On the bridge-cycle graph, what should happen when the current node has an edge back to a node that has already been visited?

choices.label: That edge should count as a repeat skip, and the search should keep the current frontier instead of reopening the old node.
The old node should go back onto the frontier every time that edge appears.
The traversal should restart from the start node.

items.explanation: Visited state stops the search from reopening already expanded nodes, so the backward edge becomes a repeat skip instead of new frontier work.
items.observationHint: Run bridge-cycle BFS and pause when an edge points back into visited work; the old node should stay closed while the frontier keeps its waiting nodes.
